title.rendered: Episode 769: I’ve Invited Everyone I Know (Classic)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 633, first aired on November 6, 2019.

Synopsis

Way back in Episode 11, Dr. Misner touched on where to find new members and how to invite people to meetings. In today’s podcast, he takes a look at three obvious but often-overlooked places to find potential visitors.

In the early days of BNI, when there were still only 20 chapters, Dr. Misner was responsible for inviting people to all the chapters. One day he was talking to another member and said “I’ve invited everybody I know. I got nobody else.”

The response was skeptical—and you can tell by the member’s questions just how long ago this was.

  • Have you gone through your Rolodex, name by name?
  • Have you gone through your business card box, card by card?
  • Have you gone through your checkbook register?

Today, you might not have a Rolodex or a checkbook. Instead, go through all the contacts in your email client and your CRM. Go through your online banking register. People you’ve already met and done business with are the best people to invite to BNI.

What unexpected places have you found people to invite to your BNI meeting? Let us know in the comments.

Complete Transcript of Episode 769/633

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer, Dr. Ivan Misner. 

Hello, Ivan! How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
This week I’m at the BNI International Convention in Warsaw, Poland. These international conventions are just amazing, Priscilla. If you’re listening to this podcast, make a note of the next convention because you’ve got to come. It’s like being at a United Nations event. There are people from all over the world. The back of the room has all of those cubicles with people that are translating different languages and there’s flags from more than 70 countries around the world, it’s an incredible experience. It’s truly a humbling experience and it really gives you a great idea of what BNI really is around the world. It’s fantastic! 

Priscilla:
I went to it once and I felt the same way. I just loved it, it was amazing.

Ivan:
Yeah, it really is amazing!

So today I’m going to be talking about “I invited everyone I know”, but before I do, I just want to share something with everyone; you’ve been mentioning that we have a new sponsor called Ivan’s Inner Circle (IvansInnerCircle.com) and I have said this and it’s up on Ivan’s Inner Circle website. It’s a website where I’m just really pouring into members of the Inner Circle on topics like networking, how to scale a business, on time management and many other topics. I mentioned that I am donating all of the proceeds to the BNI Foundation and in the future to other charities. So I got my first check, Priscilla!

Priscilla:
Yay! How much?

Ivan:
This is a check that will be going directly to the BNI Foundation that is for $4974.23.

Priscilla:
Fantastic!

Ivan:
So anybody who is participating in Ivan’s Inner Circle, I want to thank you personally because it does go to support charitable causes like children and education- which is what the BNI Foundation does. Thank you to everybody that is participating!

Alright, let’s jump in! Let me tell you what his topic is. I touched upon it briefly but before your time on the podcasts, Priscilla, and you jumped on to these podcasts early on in the process. This is episode 11 and I briefly touched on this topic but I want to go into more detail. It was towards the end of 1985, I had opened about 20 chapters in BNI and I was involved in inviting people to those twenty chapters. I said to one of the members in a chapter, “I’ve invited everybody I know. I’ve got nobody else.” And I have to tell you, this woman schooled me on whether I had actually invited everyone I know. She took me to school.

Priscilla:
How did she do that?

Ivan:
It was great because she really blew me away. She said, “Oh, Ivan. Really? You have not invited everyone you know.” I said, “Yes, I have!”  She said, “Okay. Have you gone through your Rolodex card by card?” Of course you and I know what a Rolodex is but there are a lot of millennials who might think I’m talking about an expensive watch. I’m talking about this little device that you would turn and it had all of these cards that you would turn over and hand write the name, address, phone number, and contact information of people that you have. There was even some where you slip business cards into the card and use the Rolodex that way. So again, she said, “Have you gone through your Rolodex card by card?” I said, “Well, no not card by card.” She said, “Have you gone through your card box?” Now in the 80’s we kept most of our contacts on a Rolodex or copies of their business cards in a card box- like many chapters use the pass around to get cards out of there. Let’s put it in 21st century terms, today I would say, “Have you gone through your database name by name?” 

So I said to her on both of those questions, “No, I haven’t gone through my business card box and I didn’t go through my Rolodex card by card.” She said, “Well, do that and then tell me that you invited anyone.” And then, she gave me a third suggestion that I thought was absolutely brilliant. Had I really been thinking about it, I would’ve come up with the first two, but it one was a BFO for me. Have I talked about BFO’s with you, Priscilla? 

Priscilla:
I don’t know.

Ivan:
BFO = Blinding Flash of the Obvious. It was a BFO and I hadn’t thought of this. She said, “Go through your checkbook register. Who are you paying?” Now, I’m the Founder of BNI and she was a Member! She totally took me to school. “Because the people you are paying would be great BNI members.” I was like oh my goodness, that is so obvious! Although I would’ve thought about the Rolodex and card box, I’m not sure I would have thought about a checkbook register. Now today, you might not have a physical checkbook register. Today, everything I have is online. I pay my bills online. So go through your online register or if you’re still paying on a manual check register, look through your checkbook register. Who are you paying money to? There are services that people are rendering and these are the perfect people to invite to BNI. 

So she recommended those three things to me and I was like, “Okay. I haven’t actually done that.” And I went back and went through my Rolodex card by card. I went through my card box card by card. Today I would go through my Outlook Contacts or if you have Sales Force or some database management like Sinduit, the company that handles Ivan’s Inner Circle. Go through your contacts on your database person by person. And I went through my check register, and Priscilla I found a bunch of people I had not invited. I thought, “Oh my goodness, how could I not have thought of this person?” You know it could’ve been six months to a year since I talked to this person, but I have a relationship with these people. I know these people. They would take my call and I can invite them. After going through everything, I had a bunch of people that I could go to and invite- and I did! I went back with my tail between my legs and told this member, “Thank you. That was great advice! I found all kinds of people that I had not invited in the past.” 

Priscilla:
Oh, that’s good. I do write checks, so I could look in my checkbook and find people.

Ivan:
I think it’s such a simple concept. Look, one of the ways that we invite people is to go to organizations, a chamber event, or some business event and you’re talking to somebody but you don’t really know them that well. That’s not the best invitation for a visitor because you don’t know if they are honest and ethical. But if you’ve done business with somebody or know somebody and have their contact information and just haven’t connected with them recently, then those are the best people to invite to BNI because there is a relationship. And when there is a relationship, there is a force multiplier in a chapter because you can say to people, “I know this person and have done business with them.” or “I’ve known them for a long time, you can trust them and I think they would make a great member.” I think that’s far better than doing that with somebody you’ve just met. Although, I do that too, but I still think this is the best approach. And this member, I wish I could remember her name because I would call her out by name but it was almost 35 years ago when she gave me this advice. “‘Look at your Rolodex… Look at your card box… Look at your checkbook register.”

It’s great advice and that’s what I recommend everyone that’s in BNI go back and do.

Priscilla:
Okay. I just want to insert here that having some visitors in the room makes all of the difference. 

Ivan:
Doesn’t it? It’s very important to always have some visitors in the room. It keeps everybody on their toes and they are making sure they follow the program properly. Visitors are the lifeblood of the chapter. They are what helps a chapter grow. So take this concept that we are talking about today and take it to the 21st century. I know I’m an old guy with grey hair over here talking about Rolodexes, but this is a completely effective technique in the 21st century by changing out the Rolodex for your database and your checkbook register to online. It’s the same concept. 

That’s everything I’ve got for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay! That’s terrific, Ivan. Thank you so much!

This podcast has a new sponsor! Go check out the great content available over at www.IvansInnerCircle.com where Ivan has created a wide array of topics and you’re invited to participate!

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to you joining us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Can’t think of anyone to invite to your BNI meeting? Go through your contacts and check register name by name to find potential visitors.

title.rendered: Episode 768: Class Reunions

content.rendered:
Synopsis

This week, Dr. Misner shares his answers to questions from a recent radio interview about networking at class reunions:

  1. How do you answer the question “What do you do?”
  2. How should you stand to subliminally invite people to come on over and to talk to you?
  3. How personal should you get?
  4. How drunk should you get?
  5. What should you do if you’re feeling nervous about going?
  6. How can you become an opportunity magnet at your class reunion?
  7. Should you ever try to sell your product or service?
  8. Should you update your LinkedIn page or your social media before you go to the event?
  9. How do you follow up with revived contacts after the reunion?

The short version: don’t get drunk, don’t try to sell, do update your LinkedIn profile, and make sure to get contact info so you can follow up.

Relevant Episodes

  • Episode 173: “What Are the First Words Out of Your Mouth?”
  • Episode 569: Body Language and Networking
  • Episode 630: Seven Rules for an Elevator Pitch
  • Episode 649: The 12 x 12 x 12 Rule
  • Episode 760: Get Specific about What You Do

Complete Transcript of Episode 768

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner.
Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, I recently did a live Ask the Founder for the [BNI] US Membership Drive winners throughout the United States. And this week, I am at the Transformational Leadership Council event, which happens to be in Austin for the first time, but it’s at the Miraval Hotel. And so I’m at the Miraval this week, hanging out with Jack Canfield and John Gray and some other amazing people who are part of the Transformational Leadership Council.

Priscilla:
I know you love that. So that’s really neat.

Ivan:
I do. It’s an amazing organization.

Priscilla:
Yeah. Okay, great. What are you going to tell us about these class reunions?

Ivan:
Well, many countries throughout the world have class reunions for high school and sometimes college. And I was recently interviewed on the radio by somebody who asked me questions about “What do you do at a class reunion? You know, how do you make it worthwhile for yourself?” So what I’m going to do, Priscilla, is something I don’t usually do. I’m going to share the questions I was asked, and my answer to each of the questions.

Priscilla:
Okay, that sounds good.

Ivan:
Yeah, I thought it might be interesting for this podcast, because it was a live interview, which you know, is not online, and nobody else can hear it. So I thought this might be of value.

The first question I was asked were some tips for the elevator pitch — when somebody asks, “What have you been up to? What do you do?”

And so, here were my answers.

First and foremost, don’t do an elevator pitch in an actual elevator. That’s not the idea with an elevator pitch. The idea with an elevator pitch is to tell people what you do in a very, very short period of time. And it’s important in that elevator pitch, or what we in BNI often call our weekly presentation, is to make it tight. Keep it simple, don’t try to say everything you do in a short amount of time. Don’t use jargon.

Share your Unique Selling Proposition, which I’ve talked about in previous podcasts. You want to talk about what makes you different and more important than anything else, you want to be very clear about what things will look like after you do what you do. Not just, you know, “I’m an accountant.” “I’m a lawyer.” But what do things look like after you do what you do?

And go back and look at some of the podcasts I’ve done on passing the “eyebrow test” because that makes a really good elevator pitch. The eyebrow test was an idea that was given to me by Sam Horn, who is a fellow TLC member and I’m seeing her this week. She wrote the book, “Someday is Not a Day in the Week.” And the eyebrow test is when you say something about what you do, do the eyebrows go up? Or do they go down? If they go up, you got their attention. So those are some of the things that you want to say in a good elevator pitch.

Ivan:
The next question I was asked was about body language. I was asked about somebody’s body language, is there a way that you want to stand, subliminally, that invites people to come on over and to talk to you or to have a conversation with you?

Most of you, I hope, know about the material I’ve written about Open Twos and Open Threes and open groups – that’s really, really important. You always want to be in a stance that is inviting. So if you have the “God” view and you’re looking down, you know you’re in an Open Two or an Open Three, you’re not in a closed group or a closed discussion with somebody where you’re perpendicular to the other person and nobody can step in. But there’s more to it than just the Open Two, Open Three.

Making good eye contact throughout the conversation is really important. I’ve talked about Richard Branson many times in my podcasts and one of the things I really am amazed about with him is he has fantastic eye contact. He’s not looking around the room for the next person that he wants to talk to. He is really focused on talking to you, and that is so important.

I think it’s important that you have arm movement that you know, you’re using hand gestures. Now, not jazz hands. I don’t know if you’ve ever heard of the expression, Priscila, “jazz hands” you know, where your hands are just like constantly moving. But you want people to know that you’re engaged in the conversation. And facial expressions are really important. You know, as you’re having a conversation with somebody, showing that you’re interested is really, really important.

The next question they asked me was, should you keep the conversation light? How personal should you get?

I thought that was a really interesting question from the hosts. My answer was that contextual intelligence is key here. How well you knew them in school should determine how deep you go in a conversation. If you knew each other well, it’s okay to get more personal than if you barely knew them. If you barely knew them, don’t try to go deep and go personal with somebody that you barely knew.

Now, of all the questions this host asked, the next one was the craziest. Here it is. How drunk should you get?

Priscilla: (laughing)

Ivan:
Yeah. My answer was, don’t get drunk at any event. As a rule of thumb, that’s dumb. Don’t get drunk at any networking event. Now, I’ve seen people do it and they don’t walk away with people being very impressed. So I know it’s a reunion; it’s a little lighter than a business event. But yeah, don’t get intoxicated, it’s always a bad idea at any kind of networking event.

And the next question he asked was, What should you do if you’re feeling nervous about going?
I thought that was a really good question. And probably a question that a lot of us might feel. You know, I think a lot of people get nervous about something like a reunion. And so my advice was, reach out to a couple of your friends from school. If you’re nervous about going, reach out to a couple that maybe you’ve kept in touch with, and see if they’re going. If so, make sure to meet with them as soon as you get there.

Priscilla:
Right.

Ivan:
And that’ll help you push past the nerves that you might have. Because if you meet somebody that you were a good friend with in high school or in college before you go to the reunion, then you’re going to feel more comfortable walking into the reunion.

Two or three more questions that he had that I think were worth going over here today. The next one was, if you’re looking to go to a networking event, how can you become an opportunity magnet at your class reunion?

My answer was pretty brief. It was: don’t sell to them. You know, it’s not time to have a sales seizure at a reunion. You don’t go there to sell to your past classmates. It’s like most networking events, you know, too many people use networking as a face-to-face cold calling opportunity. And you don’t want to do that. It’s an opportunity to reconnect with people.

Which leads into the next question he had, which was should you ever try to sell your product or service?

And the answer to that, at a reunion, is no. Absolutely don’t. Desperation is not referable; it’s not saleable. So don’t use a reunion as an opportunity to go sell to people. I’ve gone to class reunions where I’ve had people do that. It just doesn’t work. It’s great to reconnect. There was one person in particular, a college class reunion that I went to, where I reconnected with somebody and we ended up doing business together. But it was, I didn’t try to sell to him, he didn’t try to sell to me that night; it was just a matter of reconnecting and re-establishing the relationship which later led to working together on something, which was the perfect way to do it, in my opinion.

Ivan:
Two more questions. Should you update your LinkedIn page or your social media before you go to the event? And the answer to that was yes, absolutely. You should always keep your social media pages up to date because anyone who really wants to connect with successful people at the reunion are going to do a search on you to see how you’re doing and what you’re up to. And so I would definitely keep – whether it’s LinkedIn, Facebook, or whatever social media platform you use, I would keep it up to date, particularly before you go to a reunion.

And the last question that he had was, how do you follow up with revived contacts after the reunion? Do you reconnect on social media, ask for their phone, or what?

And my answer to that was, you definitely want to reconnect. I would recommend that you ask for their card or ask for their social media moniker — what’s their social media name that they use? — and connect with them either via social media or by email after you see them at a class reunion. It’s always good to reconnect with old friends. And to stay connected with old friends. I think that one of the advantages of technology today that we didn’t have 20 or 30 years ago, certainly when I graduated from high school and college, is social media because I’m able to stay connected with people that I went to high school with or went to college with that I would never be able to do had we not had the technology that we have today. So those were my answers about class reunions. Priscilla, any thoughts before we wrap up?

Priscilla:
I get spammed by classmates.com, I think it’s a website. And I always feel kind of guilty that I haven’t paid, you know, to be a member because they send me these notices that so and so just looked at your picture or whatever it’s like. Anyway, I don’t know if that happens to you or not.

Ivan:
When you get spammed by them, is it people that you know?

Priscilla:
No, it’s the company. The company does it. They’re constantly sending me…

Ivan:
Yeah. I’m not on classmates.com. It may be a great site. It might be a good service. I can’t speak to it effectively. But I’m not on it. I’m on LinkedIn, as I’m sure most members know. And on Facebook, of course. I’m very active on Facebook, Twitter, and Instagram. But I’m not familiar with classmates. I think honestly, I think from a professional perspective, LinkedIn is probably the place you want to be.

Priscilla:
Uh huh. Right.

Ivan:
You know, Priscilla, they always have this little thing at the bottom of the email that says unsubscribe.

Priscilla:
Right. That’s what I should do.

Ivan:
It’s a thought.

Priscilla:
Okay, Ivan. Good idea.

Ivan:
That’s all I have for today.

Priscilla: Okay, perfect. All right. Thank you so much for the great information and thank you for listening. This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material that’s available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: How do you network at class reunions? Use what you’ve learned in BNI, don’t get drunk, don’t try to sell, and do follow up.

title.rendered: Episode 767: Be a Big Thinker

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Think Big. Your vision controls your perception, and your perception becomes your reality. The size of your success is determined by the size of your belief.

If you want to be big, you need to start thinking big, and behaving big. Don’t let small minds convince you that your dreams are too big. Don’t be a product of your circumstances. Be a product of your decisions.

Has something in one of the podcasts helped you to Think Big? Share your answers in the comments.

Complete Transcript of Episode 767                                     

Priscilla:
Hello, everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner.
Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, I’ve been at the BNI Malaysia National Conference this week, and I recently did a European summit by video at the Big Thinkers Summit, which is the topic of today’s podcast.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. Well, you have to help us become a big thinker.

Ivan:
So I recently did this “Welcome to the BNI Big Thinkers Summit” in Europe, which was for BNI Directors around the world. And I love the concept of a Big Thinker Summit, and I was really proud of BNI for putting it together. Here’s some of the things that I shared with everyone at the Summit, and I’d love to talk about it when I’m finished here, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay.

Ivan:
What I’m going to say next is so much more than a soundbite to me. It’s a belief about thinking big. I really believe that our vision controls our perception, and our perception becomes our reality. I want to repeat that. Our vision controls our perception, and our perception becomes our reality. Don’t become so attached to a limiting belief that you can’t see past what is possible. I think it’s important to move past limiting beliefs and move towards meaningful beliefs.

Think Big. The size of your success is determined by the size of your belief. Think little goals and expect little expectations. Think big goals and smash other people’s expectations of you. You know, it takes the same energy to think big as it does to think small. Sure, if you think big, you’re sometimes worried about whether you can achieve those dreams. But when you think small, you’re disappointed in yourself because you didn’t achieve the success you wanted. I don’t know about you [who are] listening to this podcast, but I’d rather be a bit worried than disappointed in myself. Think big. It takes as much energy as thinking small, but you can end up in a much better place.

And don’t let small minds convince you that your dreams are too big. There are those voices that will stand on your shoulder and tell you why your dream isn’t realistic. I had that. Remember the story of my dream to have 10,000 chapters of BNI? I talked about it a couple of times; most recently was podcast episode number 753. I didn’t let that guy influence my dream – that guy who was saying, “10,000 chapters, really seriously? You know, you’ve only got 30. How can you have 10,000 chapters?” I didn’t let him influence my dream. Don’t let small minds influence your big dreams.

I’ve seen ordinary people do extraordinary things throughout my career. I believe anyone can do extraordinary things with the right mindset, a good plan, and a lot of effort. The last time I was on Necker Island, Richard Branson’s island, I heard Richard say that if people aren’t calling you crazy, you’re not thinking big enough. I think there was a little bit of truth to that. I’ve had people throughout my career say, “Are you kidding me? Are you crazy?” And I didn’t care. I didn’t care because I had a plan. I had tested it out, things were working, I knew it was possible.

To inspire others, you must first inspire yourself. If you want to be big, you need to start thinking big, and behaving big. Don’t be a product of circumstances. Be a product of your decisions. I’m going to repeat that. Don’t be a product of circumstances, be a product of your decisions. One of my greatest lessons in life was learning that I can alter my life by altering my beliefs, by altering my attitude, and by altering my behavior.

Long ago, I recognized that I might not be able to make a world of difference, but I could make a difference in the world. I could make that difference one person at a time, one chapter at a time, and then one region at a time. By doing that long enough, I think I’ve made a larger impact in the world. The big difference starts with making a difference for one person at a time. Start there and stay with it. You have the power to change people’s lives.

Think Big, my friends. Anything less is not a good use of that opportunity.

It was a short message today, Priscilla, but I think it was an important one for people to hear. Especially in these times when everything seems confusing and difficult and challenging. And it’s important, today more than ever, to Think Big and to think about what’s possible.

Priscilla:
Yeah, I think it’s great, Ivan. I love this podcast. I have a story. It’s kind of a little bit different, but it’s about the inner person, you know, that restricts you.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Priscilla:
It happened when I was a lot younger. I was studying martial arts and I was trying to get my black belt, which I eventually got. And the leader of my…

Ivan:
The Dojo, the Sensei?

Priscilla:
Yes, the style is Kuk Sool Won and he was the Grandmaster and he wanted me to fight in a competition. And I was there and I got scared. And I decided not to do it. And what I learned after that was I regretted that so much, that I made a promise to myself that if I ever had a choice between doing something that seemed too hard, and doing the opposite, or nothing, that I would always choose doing something that I thought might be too hard. Because I hated the feeling I had when I didn’t take on that challenge.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Priscilla:
It’s kind of like what you’re talking about. Not exactly, but it sort of relates.

Ivan:
Yeah, it does. And I think it’s important to take on those challenges, to think big. I know we’ve done a podcast in the past with Dr. Mark Goulston where he talks about stepping up and trying (BNI Podcast Episode 211). That if you give up, you’re never going to succeed. But if you keep trying, you have the opportunity to succeed. Never give up because if you give up, you’ll never succeed. But if you keep trying, you have the opportunity to succeed. And, you know, I did a podcast with him here years ago that he talked about that. And I think it’s a powerful concept.

Priscilla:
Yeah, I agree. Thank you.

Ivan:
Well, that’s it for today, Priscilla. I would love to hear from members. If there is something in any of the podcasts like this one that I have shared with you that have helped you to Think Big, and to help you in business. I would love to see it in your comments on this podcast. As I’ve mentioned many times, I read every comment that’s posted here on BNI podcast. And so if there’s anything I have ever shared in any of the podcasts that’s helped you Think Big, please share it here on today’s podcast. Thank you very much, Priscilla. Back to you.

Priscilla:
That’s so great. Thank you so much, Dr. Misner.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material that’s available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: If you want to be big, start thinking big, and behaving big. Don’t be a product of your circumstances. Be a product of your decisions.

title.rendered: Episode 766: Three Simple Steps to Grow Your Business

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Dr. Misner expands on the three steps to more referrals from Episode 764, which are

  1. Define your target market and describe your ideal customer or client.
  2. Describe the problems you solve for your target customer or client.
  3. Build your weekly presentation using this content.

To create an even better presentation, don’t just describe your ideal client as an individual. Ask yourself five more questions to situate that client within their business or industry.

  1. What is a business who is already a great customer or client for you?
  2. What line of business is that customer in?
  3. What’s the approximate size of that business? That’ll give you a sense of what kind of target business you’re looking for.
  4. Who within that business makes the buying decisions for your product or service?
  5. What are their primary goals, or what’s the problem they’re trying to solve when buying your product or your service?

You can find this content in The Connector Effect, co-authored with Robert Skrob and Graham Weihmiller.

Complete Transcript of Episode 766

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, I was at the BNI Taiwan China National Conference recently by Zoom, of course. And this week I’m doing a writing retreat. Okay, so this is a book that nobody, well, not many people know about. So keep it to ourselves. It’s called Garage to Global. It’s about how you take your business from your garage to a global enterprise. And I’m doing a writing retreat here at my house with my co-author, Graham Weihmiller, and a developmental editor who I’ve used on a number of projects, Heidi Giusto, who is a great editor. So that’s what I’m doing this week.

Priscilla:
Oh, that sounds great.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Priscilla:
So tell us a little bit about this topic.

Ivan: Yeah, I’m talking about a topic called “Three Simple Steps to Grow Your Business.” And this is content from a book that I’ve written with Robert Skrob and Graham Weihmiller, who’s going to do Garage to Global with me.

So you know, a lot of people, as I’m sure you know, Priscilla, listen to the podcast in their car as they drive, right? Don’t do that today. Okay, because I’m going to ask you to stop and write some things down. So you know, you can listen to it, but do not stop and write anything down if you’re listening to this in your car.

So I’ve got some ideas that I think are a value to BNI Members, and it’s from the material, like I said, from The Connector Effect book. So within one or two minutes, I want everyone to write down the answer to each of these questions that I’m about to give you. And don’t overthink each question or spend a lot of time writing just jot down the first two or three sentences that come to mind—as long as you’re not driving.

Step One

So here’s the first one. It’s about describing your best target market customer or client. So a lot of people, I think, in business fail, because they never define a target market, a target customer or client. Instead, they use words like “everybody” or “anybody.” And defining a target market is really never limiting. Rather, it gives referral partners a mental picture of the best customer to refer to you.

So, I’m going to give you FIVE QUESTIONS that I want you to answer about your Target Market. Here’s the first one. And when you’re listening to this podcast, answer this question. So pause the podcast and answer the question.

So, here’s the first one. Think of a person who is already a great customer or client for you. I want you to think about that person, or those people, and write that down.

Here we go to the second question. Where do they live? Is geography important in terms of your best customer or client? Yes or no? And talk about that.

Three. What’s their family status and profession? These things may or may not be important. Maybe it’s not important, maybe it is important.

Number four. How does their household income compare with the average? That one’s important because if you’re in a business that requires a little higher average income, you need to know that. If that’s not an issue, you need to know that.

Five. What are they planning, bragging about, or complaining about? What are things that are on their mind?

Answer those five questions about your customers or clients.

Ivan:
So here’s FIVE MORE QUESTIONS about your customers or other businesses that you might be working with.

One. Think of a business who is already a great customer or client for you. A business that’s a great customer or client.

Two. What line of business is that customer in?

Three. What’s the approximate size of that business? That’ll give you a sense of what kind of target business you’re looking for.

Four. Who within that business makes the buying decisions for your product or service?

Five. What are their primary goals, or what’s the problem they’re trying to solve when buying your product or your service?

These are great questions to be asking yourself when you’re trying to figure out your target market. The key in what I’m talking about, is to be specific. Being specific means that you’re serving your chapter members by giving them a clear idea of who your best customer is. The more specific you are, the more effective they will be in finding those referrals for you.

Step Two

Ivan:
The next thing to think about is what are the problems you solve for your target customer or client? All businesses exist to solve some kind of problem. Too many businesspeople talk about their products and services. And it not only sounds self-serving, but it actually is not. I don’t think it draws people into you. Talking about the problems that you solve makes you,

I think, more attractive as a potential service provider.

So here’s a handful of questions that we present, again, this is from the book The Connector Effect.

One. Write down — list three problems your good customer or client has that makes them ideal for your business. What are the kinds of challenges they have that make them ideal for your business.

Two. What is the worst thing that could possibly happen to your customer if their problem isn’t solved? What could go wrong for them?

Three. What’s the best thing that could happen to your great customer, once their problem is solved by the product or service that you have to offer?

Four. Do you have any evidence or client success stories to illustrate how you help your customers? These are customer testimonials, client testimonials. They’re as powerful, or almost as powerful, as a member standing up and giving you a testimonial at a chapter meeting.

Step Three

Build your presentation, your weekly presentation, using this content.

And here are the five elements of doing a great weekly presentation. I’ve talked about some of these in previous podcasts, but it’s important to review here.

One. Introduce yourself, provide your name and company name.

Two. State your professional classification.

Three. Tell a brief story about a problem you solved for a customer. Be specific. Specific is terrific when it comes to your weekly presentation. Don’t be generic. Don’t shotgun your presentation, sharp-shoot your presentation.

Four. And, when appropriate, request a referral. “Who do you know who is…”  – and then insert the target customer who suffers from the problem that you just described. Be specific.

Five. And then, of course, repeat your name and company name, particularly for those people who are new to the chapter or who are visitors.

That’s it. As a business owner, if you have a clear message as to who you want to attract, then you’re going to get more referrals, you’re going to get more customers. Too many businesspeople complicate their message by talking too much about themselves. And we want you to focus, in BNI, on a few simple elements to create a powerful marketing message. And once you learn how to create a presentation that really hones in on your target market, you’re going to be able to generate far more referrals than if you just stand up and wing it.

And I don’t know if you’ve seen this Priscilla, but I’ve seen people at BNI meetings who just – you know, they stand up and they’re thinking about what they’re going to say as they say it. They haven’t prepared, they haven’t thought it through. And they’re winging it. Or worse yet, in some ways, they’re thinking about what they’re going to say as the person before them is speaking. You need to go into a BNI meeting with ideas about what you’re going to say. And if you answer the questions that I’ve just given you, you’re gonna go in with great content, and you’re gonna have a ton of content over the course of the year that you can share at each and every meeting. And for those of you who want more information on this, again, go to The Connector Effect, the book that I did with Robert Skrob and Graham Weihmiller.

Any thoughts, Priscilla, before we wrap up?

Priscilla:
I think it’s perfect. In our chapter we were encouraging everybody to come up with their most valuable customer and create a Power Team to try and to bring somebody in that might have those skills or that business. And this is sort of an extension of all of that.

Ivan:
Yeah, absolutely. It’s really a matter of just thinking about it. You know, when I was a business consultant, before I started BNI, people would talk to me about, “Well, how do you create the best strategic plan for your business?” Well, the first thing is to sit down and think about your business. You’ve got to think about what you want, and how you want to get there. And the same thing applies to these weekly presentations. You’ve got to think about it. You’ve got to think about it within the context of the kinds of questions that I gave you, that makes it a lot easier. But we can’t do your presentations for you. You have to spend the time to think about what you do, what you bring to the table that other people can’t bring. And when you do that, you gonna nail your weekly presentation. And when you nail your weekly presentations, you’re gonna get more and more referrals. That’s everything I’ve got for today. Priscilla.

Priscilla:
That’s great. Thank you so much, Ivan.

Well, this podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs are going to provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation. So thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.
