title.rendered: Episode 765: 7 Rules for an Elevator Pitch (Classic)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 630, first released on October 16, 2019.

Synopsis

An elevator pitch is not the same thing as your weekly presentation. Your weekly presentation should be laser-specific and focus on a different product or service every week. An elevator pitch needs to tell people who you are and what you do in more general terms. If you’re doing an elevator pitch in your weekly presentation, you’re wasting your time.

Here are seven tips to help you create an effective elevator pitch.

  1. Don’t do your pitch in an elevator.
  2. Make it tight.
  3. Keep it simple.
  4. Don’t use jargon.
  5. Share your USP.
  6. Start with the “afters” (benefits).
  7. Pass the “eyebrow test.”

Complete Transcript of Episode 765 (630)

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m going to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am on my way to Cincinnati to do a BNI event and to go to my daughter’s wedding. 

Priscilla:
Oh, wow! That’s so great! You must be so excited.

Ivan:
We are really excited for her.

Priscilla:
So what is this podcast about today? What’s the elevator pitch?

Ivan:
I used to hate the expression “elevator pitch.” It just drove me crazy. But everyone is using it all over the world, so I officially give up and I’m going to go with expression developed out of the idea that you’re literally in an elevator with only a minute or less,  to say who you are and what you do. What would you say? I want you to keep in mind that your elevator pitch is not a sales pitch. It’s got to be creative and succinct. It’s a way to share who you are what you do to generate interest in the listener. This is a little different than the weekly presentation that we do in BNI. The weekly presentation should be laser specific every and you should talk about a different product or a different service. That is different than a elevator pitch which is going to be more general and it tells people who you are and what you do in general terms. If you do a basic elevator pitch every week in BNI you are wasting your time. Don’t do that. Listen to some of the podcasts that I’ve done about effective weekly presentations and being laser specific. 

With that in mind, here are the rules for creating an engaging elevator pitch to just tell somebody you don’t know who you are and what you do:

#1) Don’t do your pitch in an elevator. An unsolicited pitch in a elevator is basically face to face cold calling. I’ve been a victim of that. Don’t be a perpetrator. Unless someone asks you what you do in a elevator, just say “good day” to them. The elevator pitch is meant to be taken out of the elevator and into the right environment.

#2) Make it tight. It needs to be short. This is a quick pitch, not reading from “War And Peace.” The pitch should be more like a work of art instead of a science project. It should be succinct and expressive. It’s something you must practice carefully to be able to present yourself cohesively and professionally. You also need it to be natural. You want to rehearse but not sound rehearsed. So you want to avoid sounding staged and canned. 

#3) K.I.S.S. (Keep It Simple, Silly.) Don’t try to explain everything in the short time that you have, which breaks rule number #2 “Making it tight.” Or it’ll be too vague to be of any value. By keeping your elevator pitch simple, you have more of a chance to catch your listeners attention and engage them with your creativity and create interest in your product or service. 

#4) Don’t use jargon. If at any point someone has to say, “What does that mean?” You have officially lost them. Push the button for the next floor and exit now! Okay, I know you’re not really on an elevator, but you know what I mean. You’ve lost them, don’t do that.

#5) If you can, share your USP. (Unique Selling Proposition.) I’ve talked about this in other podcasts. It can serve you well in a elevator pitch. One example of how to create a crafty USP. is to compare a bland general statement such as, “I’m a coaching consultant.” Compared to saying something like, “I help people work leas, make more and create referrals for their profession.” Something like that is tighter and it’s their USP. It’s sort, powerful and informative. It’s the perfect combination for this part of your effective elevator pitch.

#6) When crafting your elevator pitch, consider starting out with precisely how your listener will benefit. Now this is an important one. My friend, Andy Bounds calls this “The Afters.” For your elevator pitch, it could be something as simple as, “I help people reach their sales by 33%, improving their closing ratio to 80%. Or double the number of clients they take on per month.” That’s “The Afters.” It doesn’t matter what he does because if you talk to any business person, they are going to say, “Wait a minute, how do you do that?” Focus on the after of the product or service that you provide. I think Andy’s concept of “The Afters” is the most one of the most powerful concepts to doing a elevator pitch. I highly recommend it. 

#7) This is something I have talked about in previous podcasts; Pass the eyebrow test. Another good friend of mind, Sam Horn, author of the book “Someday is Not a Day of the Week” and “Pop”, she writes about the eyebrow test. That’s where you give your elevator pitch, you watch the listeners eyebrows. If what you say in your elevator pitch causes the listener’s eyebrows to go up, you’ve got them. They’re curious. By doing this, you leave the listener wanting more and that precisely what you want the elevator pitch to do. On the other hand, if the listeners eyebrows scrunch down, you’ve confused them. Find a new pitch because that one is not working.

So don’t do a pitch in a elevator. Keep it tight. Keep it simple. Don’t use jargon. Share your USP. Focus on “the Afters” from Andy Bounds. When you test things out, do the eyebrow test. Look to see if their eyebrows go up or scrunch down. Keeping these seven rules in mind will help you create a elevator pitch that will set you aside from the crowd. So it’s time to press open the door, step on out and try out your pitch to people that you meet. I think they (the elevator pitch) are particularly at events like a chamber function where you don’t know people, and where in general terms you want to say who you are and what you do. Remember, an elevator pitch is not the same as s weekly presentation. The weekly presentation needs to be laser specific that needs to talk about one product or one service of what you do. Maybe, you use your elevator pitch in BNI on a visitors day. So it’s a little more general but doing an elevator pitch week in and week out, where it’s in general terms what you do, is not what you want to do in a BNI meeting. Your elevator pitch is different from your weekly presentation.

That’s all I got for today, Priscilla, unless you have something to add.

Priscilla:
Oh no, that’s great. It’s perfect. Thank you so much, Dr. Misner!

Ivan:
Thank you.

Priscilla:
This podcast has a new sponsor! Go check out the great content available over at www.IvansInnerCircle.com where Ivan has created a wide array of topics and you’re invited to participate!

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to you joining us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: An elevator pitch tells people what you do in general terms; your weekly presentation needs to be laser specific. Don’t confuse them!

title.rendered: Episode 764: Three Steps to Attract Referrals

content.rendered:
Synopsis

The entrepreneur with a clear message is the one who attracts the most customers or clients. Answer the questions in each of these three steps from The Connector Effect to create a compelling description of what you do and attract more referrals.

Step One: Describe Your Ideal Client or Customer

  • Who is already a great customer for you?
  • Where do they live?
  • What’s their profession?
  • What’s their family status if that’s important in your profession?
  • How does their household income compare with the average household income in your market?
  • What are they planning?
  • What are they bragging about?
  • What are they happy about?
  • What are they complaining about?

Step Two: The Problems You Solve for Your Client

  • What are three problems your ideal client has?
  • What is the worst thing that could possibly happen to your customer if the problem isn’t solved?
  • What is the best thing that can happen to your perfect customer or client once their problem is solved?
  • What will it look like after you do what it is that you do?
  • Do you have any evidence or client success stories to illustrate how you help your customers or clients?

Step Three: Build Your Weekly Presentation

  • Introduce yourself and your business.
  • Share ONE problem you solved for a client and how it helped (Step Two).
  • Ask for a referral to your ideal client. Be specific about who that person is. (Step One).
  • End with your company name and profession.

Write down you answers to the questions above and share your thoughts on how you’re going to apply this technique at your next BNI meeting in the comments.

Complete Transcript of Episode 764

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I’ve been Zooming all around, went to the Philippines and to Brazil via Zoom for events recently. So I’m continuing to do a lot of those.

Priscilla:
Perfect. Okay, tell us about these three steps.

Ivan:
Well, this is material from the book I wrote called The Connector Effect. And if you haven’t seen that book, it came out about a year ago. It’s called The Connector Effect: The Proven Way to Grow Your Business Right Now. And there are three steps in there that I thought would make for a great podcast. I’m going to give you a whole series of questions. And as I give you these questions, I want you to write down an answer to these questions that I’m about to give you. And so if you’re in a place where you can pause, pause and write it down, or go through the transcript of the podcast, look over these questions, and write down the answers to these. If you’re not in a place where you can write them down, like if you’re driving, don’t write them down; but think about them as I throw them at you, and then come back and write them down. And don’t overthink each question, or even spend a lot of time writing. Just jot down the first two or three sentences that come to mind.

So the Step One for the process is to describe your best target customer or client. So, many people that network really, I think, fail because they never really define their target market. Instead, they use words like ‘everybody’ or ‘anybody.’  By the way, I’ve never met anyone named ‘everybody’ or ‘anybody.’ Those are words you don’t want to generally use. And so you want to define your target market in a way that’s not limiting. Something that gives your referral partners a mental picture of the best customer or client to refer to you.

So here are the kinds of questions that you want to ask yourself and write the answers down for. Think of a person who’s already a great customer or client for you. Write that down. Who is that? Where do they live? Geographically? Where are they? What’s their profession? Or what’s their family status that might be important in your profession? How does their household income compare with the average household income in your community or in your market? What are they planning? What are they bragging about? What are they happy about? What are they complaining about? These questions are really important to understand in order to target your best customer.

Ivan:
So the second statement, the Second Step in the process that you want to address, is what are the problems that you solve for your target market, for your target customer, or your target client. All businesses exist to solve a problem. Too many business people talk about their products and their services. And this is, you know, first of all, it kind of sounds self-serving, and it’s not very effective. In actuality, talking about the problems you solve makes you much more attractive to people in the sense of getting referrals and being able to give referrals. You want to talk about the things that will happen after you do the work that you do. A friend of mine, Andy Bounds, talks about this. He talks about “The Afters.” Not what it is that you do, but what’s it look like after you do what you do. So here’s some questions that you need to answer. And again, if you’re driving, don’t answer these now, but come back and write these down. And if you’re listening someplace where you can pause it, pause and answer these questions as I go.

So, four questions. List at least three problems that your good customer has, that makes them ideal for your business. Three problems that a really good customer or client has, that is ideal for your business. By the way, those three problems can be three separate presentations that you would do at a BNI meeting. Second question, what is the worst thing that could possibly happen to your customer if the problem isn’t solved? That’s an important one. What could go wrong if that problem isn’t solved? For each one of those different problems? Third. What is the best thing that can happen to your perfect customer or client once their problem is solved? What will it look like after you do what it is that you do?

And do you have any evidence or client success stories to illustrate how you help your customers or clients? These are testimonials. And if you can get one of your fellow BNI members to share a testimonial at a chapter meeting, that’s the best. Otherwise, sharing written testimonials or links for testimonials, is also very, very good. And one of the things that comes out of these testimonials is you sometimes you just have no idea what kind of help you’re giving customers that you wouldn’t have thought of, or clients that you wouldn’t have thought of.

To me one of the best examples was a chapter that I went to, and we were talking about testimonials. And the attorney said, “I’m an attorney.” He did family law and divorces. He said, “Well, you know, what can I say? What’s a client going to say, that will help? That he did a really good job on my divorce?” And one of the members stood up and said, “John, you know, I told members here that you handled my divorce. You know what you did for me? I knew you were on the case and I could sleep at night. Because I knew you were handling the case.” Now what attorney would have ever thought of, you know, “I help my clients sleep at night?” It’s not what most people would have thought of. But that’s what she said. And so you never know where these testimonials will go, and you know what kind of endorsement you can get that can be really powerful for your business.

Ivan:
Okay, so let’s do the Third Step. And the third step is to build your presentation. So you have to pull the information that you wrote down in Steps One and Two. And you want to then give a presentation that is really narrow, that’s specific. Remember, specific is terrific in doing your weekly presentations. You introduce yourself, give your name, your company name, talk about your professional classification, then tell a brief story about a problem you solved for a customer. A problem – ONE. Talk about one thing that you did, and how it helped them. And then request a referral. Who do you know who might have this kind of challenge? Who suffers from this kind of problem? And be very, very specific. And, of course, then end with your name, company name, your profession. And that’s it. But the key part of this, it’s an ‘air sandwich,’ if you don’t state the problem and how you solved it. That’s the meat to the weekly presentation. And you don’t want to talk about three or four or five problems. [Talk] about one each week. That’s how you build a great weekly presentation.

I believe the entrepreneur or business person with a clear message is the one who attracts the most customers or clients. And to many marketers, many networkers, I think they complicate their message by talking too much about themselves. And if you focus instead, on a few simple elements, like I spoke about, you will create an incredibly powerful marketing message. And once you’ve learned how to create these kinds of presentations for your business, you can use this skill in any environment, not just in a BNI environment. But in BNI, this is the secret sauce to a fantastic weekly presentation. And that’s my content for this week, Priscilla. Most of these ideas, again, are from The Connector Effect book that I did with Graham Weihmiller and Robert Skrob.

Priscilla:
That was great, Ivan. Do you want to hear the best recording session that we ever did here at the studio?

Ivan:
Yes.

Priscilla: 
So there was a woman named Baba, and she was in her 50s when she discovered that she loved to sing. And it turned out she was pretty good at it, but she didn’t have any instruction really. She just started singing and she came into the studio, and she did a recording with a pianist and talked about following your passion. and being open to doing something you never thought was possible. And it was a beautiful recording. And sometime in the future, I don’t remember how long it was, if it was like six months or a year, she came down with this terrible cancer. And she couldn’t talk any more at all. And she was able to play this recording for the people that came to visit her. And it had all about, you know, her philosophy about following her passion, and also, her voice and her singing. And it was just fantastic that we had that, and she had that. And so that’s the best session I’ve ever done.

Ivan:
And you know, for you, what you do is you memorialize people’s ideas, or in her case, her voice, her singing voice, their content, and it can live on forever as a result of that.

Priscilla:

Right, exactly.

Ivan:
Well, I would love for you, if you’re listening to this podcast, to please write down the things that I talked about in the podcast. And then I’d love to hear your thoughts on how you’re going to apply this at your BNI meeting; at the very next BNI meeting. I want to hear how you’re going to apply this. I read every comment that gets posted up on BNI Podcast. So Priscilla, thank you for that story. And thank you for what you do here on BNI Podcast.

Priscilla:
You’re welcome. Well, thank you for listening. And I want to thank you for the wonderful ideas that you just told us about. So this podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: The entrepreneur with a clear message is the one who attracts the most clients. Follow these three steps to attract more referrals.

title.rendered: Episode 763: Global Diversity

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Claudia Thompson, head of BNI’s Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion panel, explains how BNI National Directors and Executive Directors outside the US and Canada can understand and address these issues in their own regions. The panel’s vision is to have BNI chapters reflect their local communities, and specifically their local business owners, in terms of race, ethnicity, gender, age, and ability.

Once you realize who’s missing from your chapter, reach out to organizations that represent those groups. Attend some of their public events and start to get to know people. Invite them to visit your chapter. And remember: diversity is a fact, but inclusion is a choice.

Related Episodes

  • Episode 694: Diversity, Equity, & Inclusion
  • Episode 672: Being Intentional about Diversity

Complete Transcript of Episode 763

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, I just recently did a Canadian presentation by Zoom. And I’m really excited that this week is our second ever Founder’s Day. And it’s where I will do an entire day of one-to-ones, 30-minute one-to-ones, like 15 or 16 of them, all day. And it’s the second annual this week.

Priscilla:
Wow, that’s interesting. How do you get to be one of those people?

Ivan:
You just have to sign up and get approved. We’ve had hundreds and hundreds of people apply for it.

Priscilla:
Sounds great.

Ivan:
So today, we’re gonna be talking about a topic that I spoke about not too terribly long ago, episode number 694, Diversity, Equity and Inclusion. And I invited Claudia Thompson, who is here again on this podcast. Claudia started her career on the BNI Director Team as a Launch Director. And she served as the Area Director in Boston, where she launched 23 chapters. She is the head of the Diversity, Equity and Inclusion Panel for the [BNI] US and Canada. And, I didn’t know this until I saw it at a conference, she is active in performing in a dance company in Boston, for Bollywood. So Claudia is a Bollywood dancer. And can I just say, Claudia, you’re awesome at it. Really good.

Claudia:
Awesome. Thank you.

Ivan:
When I saw you starting to do that dance at a [BNI] US Conference, I was like, no—really? You rocked the house.

Claudia:
Thank you so much. Yeah, the talents were discovered the evening before and then someone had asked me to jump on stage at the end of a talk. So I couldn’t say no.

Ivan:
You did a great job. So you head the Diversity, Equity and Inclusion committee. And this is such an important topic. And what we wanted to do today was to talk about how it applies. And we may use the abbreviation DEI occasionally. But how does that diversity apply globally? Beyond just the US and Canada? Beyond North America?

Claudia:  
Yes, thank you so much, Ivan, for asking me that. So I think you know, the last time that we spoke early last year, the panel had just gotten underway, maybe about six or seven months. And this was still pretty new, to BNI, to put together such an initiative, like at this level of diversity. And I think the vision that we had was that it would always expand beyond the [United] States and North America. And we really had looked at okay, everything that we’re looking at, you know, what are the different characteristics and the qualities? What diverse backgrounds can we look at? And so when we think of it from that perspective, it really meant to look at it at a global level. And I had actually had several [BNI] National Directors and EDs (Executive Directors) from across the world reach out once they heard about this panel.

And one in particular, were the folks down in South Africa. And so I think a lot of times the misconception is while BNI is in all these countries, like we’re diverse. And yes, on some levels, we are diverse in that aspect. But a lot of people were struggling with diversity within these countries. In particular, you wouldn’t think that South Africa would have those struggles, being that it is, you know, somewhat diverse on some levels, but the chapters weren’t as reflective of the communities. And so our panel Mission and Vision is just that: to have the chapters that are in these communities, be reflective of those as well of the population and the people. And so we’re starting to see that in other countries. So that’s just one aspect of it.

Ivan:
You say the population and the people. It was pointed out to me once in a diversity group that I created in BNI decades ago, that really, you have to start by looking at the population of the people who are likely to be candidates of a member as a member. In other words, you know, the number of entrepreneurs, the number of business professionals, that are representative of that ethnicity or gender in an area. Would you agree or disagree with that? That was pointed out to me and it made sense long ago.

Claudia::
Oh, absolutely. Absolutely. So like, you know, we did that here in the states [US] where we had communities, we had chapters in communities that might have been represented more by one particular gender, let’s say, or sorry, race. But yet the chapters themselves weren’t reflective of that community or city. Right. So we have a lot of those. And so South Africa is just an example. But we have places like that around the world, that it doesn’t always match. So it would make sense because these are the, you know, the business owners in these communities, so it would make sense that they would reflect the people that are there.

Ivan:
And it goes beyond race and gender, I think you would agree. What other kinds of things, and this certainly applies globally, what other kinds of things should be considered in terms of inclusion in BNI?

Claudia:
Oh, absolutely. Yeah. Because I think when you say diversity, people automatically shoot to gender, and race. And, yeah, I mean, there’s ability, right? Is someone able bodied or not? And even though we don’t talk politics and religion in BNI, there are still people of different religious backgrounds. And you know, age is another one. So all of these different identities that help shape really healthy, quality, diverse chapters, is there representation from multiple demographics?

Ivan:
Yeah. And by the way, I don’t know if you know, this, you probably do. I was just told about two different transgendered members that have been accepted to chapters throughout the world, which I thought was amazing.

Claudia:
Yes, I am very excited about, I’m very excited to hear that. That makes me so happy. You know, we do monthly talks every second Wednesday of the month at 3pm Eastern [US time], called DEI talks, and one of them that we did was on gender identity. And I think that was one of the most popular ones that we did, because people really did want to be inclusive of folks, like making sure their chapter was safe enough for people who might have a different identity from what they were assigned at birth. And so we wanted to make sure that there was a space that was inclusive, and a lot of people have watched that talk. So that makes me very happy to hear that we have transgender folks in chapters.

Ivan:
Yeah, I was pleased to see that. You know, for me, BNI has always been about being inclusive and giving people space to be themselves within the organization. And I think that’s important, if we want to grow into the future as an organization, that we hold that space open for people.

Claudia:
Yeah, definitely.

Ivan:
So how can we broaden our diversity, equality, and inclusion, equity and inclusion strategies, and our efforts past the US to become more global?

Claudia:  
Well, I think it’s great that people have reached out because I think initially, there has to be buy-in from the top. So a lot of these National Directors and Executive Directors have to want to see diversity in their spaces. So if it’s from the top, they’re going to be excited, they’re going to be sending it out in their newsletters. I think that that message comes much more well-received from the people at the top. And then of course, spilling down to the Directors. So I think that that matters.

I think, also like doing like a scan, you know, we don’t necessarily survey our members. So if each ED or ND does a scan of their region, and they can kind of see, you know, just based on if they’re visiting chapters and getting connected, and if they talk to their Director Consultants, they can find out, okay, where are we lacking? You know, is this chapter, you know, the age much higher? Are there mostly men? You know, what do we need to fill the gaps? And I think that those are some of the things, once you get started, then, you know, you can start using some of the tools and the resources that we have put together to make that happen. Then we can start, you know, engaging with other different groups, who focus on a specific demographic maybe, and try to collaborate in those areas and things like that.

Ivan:
So, you know, if a member is listening to this, you mentioned tools and resources, you know, what is available to a member that would help them address this issue more effectively?

Claudia:
Recently, somebody from the LGBT Chamber [of Commerce] that’s actually national in the US—they have different chapter regions—had reached out and said, “Hey, we want to have some of our chapters, our members… we don’t have something like BNI; we would love to be able to incorporate that. So collaborating with some of these folks, like they have different networking events, starting to get connected. Or maybe there’s a black business chamber, you know, different groups that you can talk to, and just have conversations about BNI. So that they actually are educated on what we do. Because many of these other networking groups don’t have that.

So I think starting to do, not official collaborations, but just having conversations, engaging their events. I know that you’ve mentioned that many times: going to other networking events and talking actually about BNI, as opposed to just talking about your business. Those are good places to start to have those conversations and see what other resources are available in each region. Because I know what’s available here. But across the world, I know there are many tools or many groups and organizations, they’re pretty vast when we’re talking about the entire world.

Ivan:
Yeah, you’re right, and of course, different languages as well. For those of you listening to this, go to episode number 672, Being Intentional about Diversity. And I have seven very specific things that you can do in your chapter today to be inclusive. And one of the things that, you know, we talk about in that particular podcast is that diversity is a fact: you either have it or you don’t. But inclusion is a choice. And being inclusive is a choice that leads to diversity. Is there anything else you want to end with here, Claudia, before we wrap up?

Claudia:
No. For our members or anyone listening, just continue to network in places that you normally would not, and invite those folks to your social gatherings, to your chapter meetings. And that’s how we’re going to really have inclusive and diverse chapters. So just be open to anyone that visits and that’s pretty much it. And listen to podcast number 672.

Ivan:
Yes. 694 and 672 are two great podcasts on this topic. Claudia, thank you for everything you do. I really appreciate you. Your Instagram is claudiaconnects, I believe.

Claudia:  
Yes, it is.

Ivan:
All right. Well, listen, thanks for being on and as we have more information to share with members about this really important topic, will you come back again?

Claudia: Of course. Absolutely. Thanks for having me.

Ivan:
Thank you very much, Claudia. Over to you Priscilla.

Priscilla:
That was great. Thank you so much, both of you. That was a great podcast to listen to. This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. And these audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Claudia Thompson explains how BNI chapters around the world can understand what diversity means for them and how to achieve it.

title.rendered: Episode 762: Soft Skills Make Strong Networks (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
Synopsis

This is a rebroadcast of Episode 614, first aired in June 2019. Offers may no longer be valid.

Dr. Misner is a firm believer in the power of the Law of Reciprocity: the deep-rooted psychological urge to do something nice for someone who did something nice for you.

There are several scientific theories that support the Law of Reciprocity, such as Nash’s Equilibrium Theory, which states that the best result comes when everyone in the group is doing what is best for themselves and the group.

Reciprocal Altruism involves an equitable balance between collective altruism and personal need.

Enlightened Self-Interest is another concept that aligns with the Law of Reciprocity. This idea, that you can do well by doing good, underlies Givers Gain.

Social cooperation is rewarding to our brains. No matter what you call it, reciprocity is about serving others. That kind of networking is where individuals enter and communities emerge.

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to the official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you today?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic, PRisilla, and I have a topic today. My PhD might be slipping out a little bit because it is a little academic but I think people will find it interesting.

Priscilla:
Okay. Tell us about it.

Ivan:
People who know me would tell you that I am not much of a new age kind of guy but as an author and businessman who started BNI, and we are in 70 countries around the world, I really believe in the immense power in the law of reciprocity. This is a part of networking theory, social capital theory. It is a concept that touches upon the deep rooted psychological urge to do something nice for someone who did something nice for you. So for people like me who understand the value of soft skills but who would also like to have some evidence behind why those soft skills work, I can tell you there is actually a lot out there to support the law of reciprocity, beginning with a concept called Nash’s Equilibrium Theory. 

Ironically,  Nash’s Equilibrium Theory uses the acronym NET, which I think is funny, but it basically states that the best result will come when everyone in the group is doing what is best for themselves and the group. It is a form of reciprocity and the optimal outcome of the situation is one where no individual has an incentive to deviate from their chosen strategy after considering the other participants’ choice. If this sounds at all familiar, you may have seen it in the movie Beautiful Mind. That is Nash and his theory is the Equilibrium Theory and he won a Nobel Prize off of this concept, but there are others, other theories that help support the Law of Reciprocity. 

One is Burrell Altruism. It’s another form of reciprocity. It involves an equitable balance between collective altruism and personal need. Collective altruism looks at the needs of the group but doesn’t give strong consideration to the needs of the individual, whereas reciprocal altruism attempts to consider both the needs of the group and the needs of the individual. 

Even in early writings by Socrates and Tocqueville about ethics and government, the concept of enlightened self-interest was all about reciprocity. Enlightened self-interest is a philosophy which states people who act to further the interest of other or a group or groups that they belong to, ultimately serve their own self-interest. In other words, it is possible to do well by doing good. Let me repeat that: it is possible to do well by doing good. 

In BNI, I incorporated the use of the term Givers Gain into the lexicon or operations from the very beginning of the organization. The underlying foundation of this truth is built on the concept that what goes around comes around.  But it is more complex than that. Networking is about relationship building. I found that the best way to build a relationship with someone quickly is to help them first.

If you can help someone, I don’t mean in person–giving them an introduction and information, an article–anything that serves their need–you will begin a professional relationship with them and creating a relationship also helps build trust. And trust is the cornerstone of effective networking, so that when you practice Givers Gain often enough, you will be on the road to a powerful personal network predicated on trust built through helping to serve someone else. So some of this is a little deep, but I think it is important for BNI members to understand that there is some philosophy, there are some theories behind what we teach with Givers Gain and the Law of Reciprocity. The concept of whatever team you choose to use serves as a bridge between individuals and a community of people for collaboration of all kinds. It fuels individual and professional growth, not to mention increased growth which is what BNI is about.

Research actually has shown that social cooperation is rewarded in to our brains, Priscilla. It is rewarding to our brains. Cooperation increases. The frequency of Dopamine release within the brain. Interestingly, dopamine decreases without social cooperation..

So each of the strategies I have talked about are about cooperation and collaboration and each can increase dopamine production in the brain. Some time ago, I received an email from a BNI member in India, Amruth. Amruth sent me an email with a great philosophy or a great metaphor for this philosophy. He said words to the effect of imagine that you have two spoons. A small spoon and a large serving spoon. Which one are we using the most?

The small spoon serves only us, but the large spoon allows us to serve others. The more that we all use the large spoon, the more we ill all have plenty for the small spoon. I love this metaphor. 

A networking group that uses a large spoon for everyone has amazing success for all. In the book The Go-Giver, co-written by my friend Bob Burg, who I have had on the podcast several times, they say, “The Income is determined by how many people you serve and how well you serve them.” You can call it Nash’s Equilibrium. You can call it reciprocal altruism. You can call it Enlightened Self-Interest. You can call it Givers Gain, all of which I gave a philosophical premise to each of those theories here in this podcast.  

But I believe that reciprocity is really about taking off your bib and putting on your apron. It is about serving other people. This kind of networking is where individuals enter and communities emerge. Those are my thoughts for this week’s podcast, Priscilla. Any observations before we wrap up?

Priscilla:
I do love that reference to the small and large spoon. And I know that – you know, even people who have problems  and get depressed, feel withdrawn sometimes, they talk about the advantage of donating your time or seeing if you can be of help to somebody. It is healing. So I think that what you are talking about is essentially the same thing. The Dopamine increase, it is really helpful to give. One of the things I love about BNI is is just creates better members of society, because you are just always looking at how you can help somebody else.

Ivan:
I agree and I think sometimes people look at this and go “Yeah, but what’s in it for me?” and I think that anyone who really does any research into the concepts that I talk about, Nash’s Equilibrium, Reciprocal Altruism, Enlightened Self-Interest or Givers Gain. If you do any kind of research, you find that what’s in it for you is that what goes around comes around. By giving you feel great and oh by the way, it comes back to you, and it comes back to you many times over. It’s 2x, 4x, 10x. That is the power of building community which is what BNI does. BNI builds communities all around the world where people support, get to know and trust one another.

Priscilla:
That’s right. There aren’t enough of those kind of infrastructure communities in modern culture so it is really important. 

Ivan:
Absolutely, so soft skills makes strong networks and I really believe that BNI is where individuals enter and communities emerge. I would love for education ordinations to share this with chapters. It is a little deeper than some of the things that I talk about on some of the podcasts, but I think it gives the philosophical construct behind why Givers Gain works and why it works do well. That’s all I have for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. Ivan, thank you so much. For more of Ivan’s content go to IvanMisner.com. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Whether you call it Nash’s Equilibrium, Reciprocal Altruism, Enlightened Self-Interest, or Givers Gain, reciprocity is about serving others.

title.rendered: Episode 761: Three Steps to Receiving a Roomful of Referrals

content.rendered:
Synopsis

James Grima, BNI Director for Melbourne East, joins Dr. Misner on the podcast this week to explain how to get more referrals as a result of your feature (spotlight) presentation.

Start with the End in Mind

When you create your presentation, work backward from your call to action. Everything in the presentation should lead to that.

Divide the Presentation into Four Stages

  1. Engagement
  2. Problem
  3. Solution
  4. Call to Action

Make sure to allow a full 25% of your presentation time for your call to action.

Have the Next Step Set Up

Provide clear instructions for what to do next. If you’re on Zoom, you can put a link in the chat and ask them to click it, or just ask members who would like to follow up to type “Yes.” At an in-person meeting, you can distribute printed sign-up forms.

You can reach James Grima through BNI Connect or his LinkedIn Profile.

Complete Transcript of Episode

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Hi Priscilla. I’m at my home office in Austin, Texas this week. And today’s topic, I’ve got a guest, who’s a BNI Director, who I’m going to introduce in a moment, but let me just say right up front, we struggled with the title for this. And we ended up with “Three Steps to Receiving a Roomful of Referrals.”

The other possible title was “Yes to the Chat”, which you’re probably raising your eyebrows going ‘what is that?’ Well, we’ll get to that. And both titles are completely accurate for this presentation. And it’s a great topic. James, do you pronounce your last name Grima? (gree-mah)

James:
Yes, that’s correct. Hi, Ivan.

James:
Great to have you on. James Grima has been a BNI Director in Melbourne East for five years. He’s been an entrepreneur for 30 and has grown several companies into a multi-award winning, profitable powerhouse in hospitality and in the education industries. James coaches CEOs, executives, and solopreneurs to grow their business and themselves personally and professionally via one-on-one coaching, workshops, seminars, and speaking. And he specializes in the development of systems and tools to help business owners succeed. He’s, as I said, has also been a BNI Director in Melbourne. James, welcome to the BNI Podcast.

James:
Thank you for having me, Ivan.

Ivan:
It is great to have you and just for the record, it’s five o’clock in the morning for James. That’s the commitment that he has for doing this podcast today. Thank you. I appreciate that.

James:
Well, you’re welcome. It’s all part of Givers Gain.

Ivan: Thank you. So I’ve got a couple of questions and I’ll let you run with this as we go. Deciding in advance what the one clear next step is, for the listening members, does what? Why should one do that?

James:
Well, it’s a great question. We’re talking about, obviously, the spotlight presentation. And just like Stephen Covey says in the book, “The Seven Habits of Highly Effective People,” you want to begin with the end in mind. So, you mentioned that I’ve been a [BNI] Support Director for five years. And each week, I see about four spotlight presentations.

Ivan:
Because some countries may use a different term, we’re talking about the longer presentation [at a BNI Meeting].

James:
The longer presentation, yes. And so that means I’ve seen about 1000 of them over the five years. And there’s often a problem. And the problem is time. So a member speaks, and they usually share a few stories and photographs of their family. They talk about the problem; they talk about the solution and how their product or service solves that. And they get to the part that we call the call-to-action.

And typically one of three things will happen. They’ll run out of time. They’ll have a very weak call-to-action. Or alternatively, they’ll have a complex call-to-action, which might have five steps: call this number, take these series of steps. And of course, we know that life gets in the way. As soon as that member has finished their presentation, well, we’re all off into our lives. And we’ve almost forgotten about what that call-to-action is.

So beginning with the end in mind almost means to reverse engineer your presentation, starting with the call-to-action. Asking yourself, what is the call-to-action that you would like your audience to take at the conclusion of the presentation?

Ivan:
Again, you mentioned spotlight presentation. In most countries it’s the feature presentation. It may be a different term, but it’s the longer one. So one of the things you recommend is chunking the presentation down to four specific stages. Talk about that.

James:
Yeah, absolutely. If you think about it timewise and you allow… So in some countries, I know that there’s 10 minutes, eight, six—whatever that time is—

Ivan:
—It tends to be based on the size of the chapter, by the way, for those of you listening.

James:
—dividing it into four stages. So the first stage, to increase the engagement of your audience, you’re often talking about who you are: showing some photos of your partner, a pet, a passion. Then the second stage is talking about the problem, the typical problem in the marketplace that your product or service encounters or solves. The third stage is the solution. How do you go about solving that? And then finally, the fourth stage, the call-to-action that is usually has no time left. My suggestion is to allow one quarter of the presentation to speak to the call-to-action. That is to be specific and clear about what it is that you would like your audience to do at the conclusion of the presentation.

And I often use dating, as an example. When you go on a first date, if you would ask the person that you’re on a first date with to marry you, you’re probably not going to have a second date. But if the call-to-action is a clear, simple, relative or proportional step forward, then you’re likely to have a lot of second dates. And really, at the conclusion of your presentation, you’d like to have as many dates as possible. That’s what I would think that you would want to do to begin with the end in mind.

Ivan:
So on the third thing that you mentioned, is to have the next step already set up. Tell us what that means.

James:
Well, if you think about what it is that you’d like your audience to do. For example, many members will finish by saying, “If you’d like more information, contact me for one-to-one.” And some members would have no clear instruction at all, expecting the audience to simply know what to do and how to refer that person. So I believe it’s a very necessary step to have a, like a stepping stone, a second date, so to speak. So of those 1000 presentations that I’ve seen, the ones that I feel that are most successful, are those that have a stepping stone that is something like, “click this link,” if you’re on Zoom, “to register for a webinar.” Or it might be, “If you’re interested in more information right now, just type ‘yes’ in the chat.”

Ivan:
And there’s your, “Yes in the chat.” comment.

James:
There’s your “yes in the chat.” Because if you’re on a, if your meeting is virtual, and the call-to-action is, “Ivan, would you like some more information? If so, just go ahead and type ‘yes’ in the chat.” That’s a very easy second date, would you agree?

Ivan:
Yeah, absolutely. And so having that kind of follow up in place is… it makes all the difference in the world. I wrote an article on this years ago about doing presentations at service clubs, and I definitely had a very specific the call-to-action, and people would come up afterwards and grab the material, which then led to new clients for my consulting business. The call-to-action is critical.

James:
Yes, I had a personal example, I delivered a presentation at BNI, my feature presentation. And there were members and a couple of visitors in the room, a total of 28. And this was during COVID. And so we’re meeting on Zoom, and 22 of the 28 audience typed ‘yes’ in the chat. The question: type yes in the chat if you’d like to attend a webinar, to receive even more information, so that you can better refer to me –  21 out of 22 attended the webinar, and most importantly, 20 out of 21 either referred themselves or passed a referral.

Ivan:
Wow, that’s really impressive. Now, what do you do with the visitors where you may not have their contact information readily handy to you?

James:
Well, in most instances, the visitor information is either available through BNI Connect when they’ve registered…

Ivan:
Yeah, okay.

James:
Or alternatively, reaching out to the member who’s invited them to obtain those details.

Ivan:
Excellent.

James:
Yeah. In this particular instance, Ivan, the meeting was on Zoom. So the member was able to post their information into the chat.

Ivan:
Yeah.

James:
Members might be wondering, well, what do you do if the meetings not on Zoom or electronically? An equivalent of ‘yes, in the chat’ could be having on the member tables, a form, a pre-filled form, that just says, ‘tick this box if you’d like more information’ and then the member can collect those forms off the tables at the end. It’s an equally equivalent technique.

Ivan:
Excellent suggestion. We’re almost out of time. If you can answer this last one quickly. Have you ever witnessed presentations that rushed through and ended up with no call-to-action at the end?

James:
Absolutely. I would say in 70% of instances, what happens is the member says… They’ll get some kind of warning from the timekeeper that says, you have two minutes left. They’ll very quickly finish off their solution and click through… They’ll say something like, “Oh, don’t worry about those last couple of slides,” click click, click, “by the way, reach out to me for a one-to-one.” So, I emphasize again, to start with the end in mind, to start with your call-to-action, to allocate time for it, and reverse engineer so that you’ve allowed at least one quarter of the time for your presentation for the call-to-action.

Ivan:
Well, James, thank you so much for being on BNI Podcast today. We will include your LinkedIn URL in this podcast transcript. So anybody that wants to connect with you directly, they can do so.   https://www.linkedin.com/in/jamesgrima/    
And thanks for being here, James.  I appreciate you.

James:
Excellent. Thank you for having me.

Ivan:
Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. Thank you both for the great information that was really nice. This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudio programs.com. And these audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and use promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation. Thanks so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: James Grima shows you how to focus your feature presentation on your call-to-action to get more referrals from your chapter.

title.rendered: Episode 760: Get Specific About What You Do

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Many people that are new to networking make the mistake of thinking that referral marketing is about telling everyone they meet everything they do. In getting your message across, less is more. The more specific you are about the benefits of your service, the better your results will be.

Focus on the things you do well and document those things in a way that you can communicate to others effectively. This will help you teach your networking partners who they can refer you to.

Listen to Episode 630: 7 Rules for an Elevator Pitch, for more about the eyebrow test.

Have you ever found that being specific helps your networking results in BNI? Use the comments to tell us how you got laser specific and what a difference it made in the results that you have in the chapter.

Complete Transcript of Episode 760

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, I’ve recently been to the Latin American National Conference that represented nine different countries for BNI. And I hosted a BNI Foundation fundraiser. It was the person who won the live auction for a wine tasting and dinner at my house, Michele Bedwell won that. And her and Kevin Barber came to my house for a wine tasting and evening dinner, and we cracked open some great wine from my cellar.

Priscilla:
Oh, wow, that sounds delicious.

Ivan:
Anybody can do it. Just the next conference that it’s offered as a [BNI] Foundation item fundraiser, bid on it.

Priscilla:
Uh huh. Okay, well, that sounds good. So tell me a little bit about what this new topic is for today.

Ivan:
Yeah. Well, this is a topic that I have talked about on a number of occasions—getting specific about what you do. We all know, the most frequently asked question that is heard at networking events, business mixers, seminars, and even BNI meetings, is a common phrase. We’ve probably asked it ourselves and have had others ask us many times, “What do you do?” We’re so accustomed to the question that we hardly give a thought to how we answer it. It’s not enough simply to tell your contacts your job description, “I own and operate a sporting goods store,” or whatever it is you do.

You have to remember that effective business networking is about building relationships. And to deepen those relationships, you have to talk about what you do in a way that communicates the magic of your vision expressed through your words. So you have to get specific when you talk about what you do.

To get referrals from your networking efforts, particularly in BNI, people must know about your business. They need to understand it a little better than you might think. They need to understand it in a way that helps them identify potential referrals for you when they’re going about their daily lives. Or they’re talking with other people that you don’t know yet.

Many people that are new to networking make the common mistake of thinking that referral marketing is about telling everyone they meet everything they do. And I’ve seen that so much, you know, that people go to a BNI meeting and they try to say everything they do in the short time that they have for their weekly presentation. And they think that getting more referrals is simply a matter of talking to more people and telling them everything they do, when in fact the opposite is true. In getting your message across, less is more.

Ivan:
You want to be specific with people that you build relationships with. Your message should be specific without using industry jargon. You want to state it in terms of benefits to the client, not features. Remember, customers choose a product or a service based on its benefits, not its features. The features are simply the facts, the elements or significant parts that make up the product or the service. The benefits are its value to the potential customer, how it’ll solve their problems and make their life better. And I know it may seem odd, but the more specific you are, the more receptive the listener will be and the better your results will be. And again, you want to talk about the benefits.

My friend, Andy Bounds from the UK, calls this the “Afters,” the “Afters.” It’s not what you sell, or what you do. It’s what things will look like after the person buys what you’re selling, or they use the service that you’re providing. What will their experience be like AFTER? Those are the benefits. So look at the “Afters” and be crazy specific.

So you have to keep this in mind when you create your Unique Selling Proposition or USP. I’ve talked about USPs a number of times. You can go back to the podcast [website] and do a search on USP—unique selling proposition—and you’ll find where I talk about it a few times. Your USP is a brief description of the purpose of your business stated in the most concise and compelling way in order to help others understand the unique value of what you do.

So let’s look at a way to go beyond the previously mentioned job description of “I own and operate a sporting goods store.” One could say instead, “I deal in sporting goods, and I specialize in team sports. I’ve outfitted most of the high school football teams in the district, and I can order custom fitted shoulder pads and helmets for all players at a substantial discount and I deliver within five days.” Then go on to talk about the fact that you’ve sponsored the local youth football teams.

So you want to get as specific as possible in the time that you have. Now, it’s really important that your unique selling proposition passes what my friend, author and speaker Sam Horn, calls the eyebrow test. And I know I’ve talked about this a number of times in my podcast; you can go back and listen to it: the eyebrow test [Episode 630]. She says when you give your USP to someone listen to what they have to say. But most importantly, watch the reaction they have. Sam says if their eyebrows don’t move, it means they’re unmoved. If their eyebrows scrunch down and furrow together, you’ve confused them. However, if their eyebrows go up, Sam says your USP has succeeded. They’re engaged, they’re curious, and they want to know more. So see if your USP passes the eyebrow test when you talk to people. And if it doesn’t, you might want to keep working on your unique selling proposition to tighten it up and really gain people’s attention.

The bottom line here is that specific gets results. And this is particularly true in BNI. Too many business professionals and companies try to be all things to all people without being specific. And telling all the things that they do their message, or I should say, without being specific and by telling people all the things they do, their message is diluted. And I think it’s easily dismissed or ignored. So I recommend that you focus on the things you do well, and document those things, and your vision, in a way that you can communicate to others effectively. And this will help you teach your networking partners who they can refer you to. Ultimately, that is what effective business networking is all about.

It’s about building trust, mutually beneficial relationships that result in business opportunities for referral partners. And the way to get there is by being laser specific about the kinds of things that you do.

So I would ask those of you listening to my podcast today: Have you ever found that being specific helps your networking results in BNI? And if so, share your story here. Talk about how you got laser specific and what a difference it made in the results that you have in the chapter. The more people can get examples of what it is that I’m talking about, the easier it is for you to get granular on your weekly presentations. Because over the course of a year you have a chance to give a lot of content. Don’t keep saying the same thing over and over. Instead, get specific and change it up week, over week, and you’ll find you’re going to get a whole lot more referrals that way. Any thoughts, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
No, I think that’s a great point. And I think that it makes your presentation more interesting.

Ivan:
Yeah, that’s it. If people are hearing the same thing week in and week out, they just tune out.

Priscilla:
Exactly. But also, they can identify possibly with what you’re talking about. And it can inspire them to search for work for you. So that really works.

Ivan:
Yeah, it’s worked for me many times, you know, people will talk about something really, really incredibly specific. And then I started noticing it out in the community. I mean, I can even tell you the very first time it happened. It was 1985, [the] first time I heard somebody get laser specific. And now you know probably 90% of the people that hear me tell this short story will go “what is that?” But I’ve been around long enough that I know what this is. So 1985—he stood up at a meeting and he said, “When you go to somebody’s office look for fat wires, big fat cables to the telephones. And if they have fat wires, they could use my service and product.”

Now, for those of you who don’t know, the old phone systems had wires that were incredibly thick, I mean, probably a half an inch thick. From you know, if you cut it in half, it’d be a half inch across at least. And he set people up with new digital phones rather than old analog phones. And I remember going into offices and going, Oh, that guy’s got fat wires. Hey, they have fat wires, you know, they need they need my friend who does phone systems. And so that was the very first time I heard someone get laser specific and observed just how incredibly well it worked.

Priscilla:
Right. That’s a great point. Well, is there anything else you’d like to share?

Ivan:
Nope. I’d love to have members share something that they learned about being specific and how it benefited them. That’s the key. How did it benefit you? Did it work? Thanks a lot, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
You’re welcome. Thank you, Dr. Misner. Well, that’s it for this week. This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and use the promo code IVAN50 [this code is good for a limited time] for 50% off of everything. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thanks so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.
