title.rendered: Episode 759: What to Do When Things Go Wrong

content.rendered:
Synopsis

When things go wrong, an excuse is not nearly as good as a solution. Finding solutions to unexpected problems is a strength for any business. Dr. Misner shares four lessons from a wedding catastrophe that also apply to your business.

  1. Remain calm during a crisis and project that sense of calm to your clients.
  2. Believe in your team, which means you need to have a good team.
  3. Don’t focus on the problem, focus on the solution.
  4. Act quickly to implement that solution.

If you have an amazing story where something went wrong and you witnessed great customer service, or something went wrong and you provided great customer service, share it in the comments.

Complete Transcript of Episode 759

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I’m still Zooming around the world. I’ve been recently to the BNI Japan National Conference and the BNI Italy National Conference. And we’ll be continuing to Zoom around and starting to actually visit places in person.

Priscilla:
Great. So tell us a little bit about what you’re going to share with us today.

Ivan:
So today, my topic is what to do when things go wrong. So I know you know this, Priscilla, maybe some of the members know this. And people who follow me on Facebook know this. But recently my eldest daughter, Ashley, got married. And the wedding was at a little venue that was nestled in Southern California near the ocean. And I experienced something during the wedding that I think is a great lesson for entrepreneurs everywhere.

The sign of a good team is when they do their job well when everything around them is going right. The sign of a great team is when they do their job well when something out of their control goes completely wrong. And yet they find a solution. And finding solutions to unexpected problems is a strength for any business. So a couple of hours before my daughter’s wedding, a vehicle took out a transformer in the neighborhood and the venue lost all power.

Priscilla:
Oh no.

Ivan:
This is a disaster in the making for sure. However, the manager jumped in his car, drove to a hardware store and actually bought several generators, which his team hooked up to the venue just in time for the wedding.

Priscilla:
Oh great.

Ivan:
So while the wedding party was totally frenetic over the loss of power, the manager and his team remained really calm, at least in front of us. I’m sure behind the scenes they were frenetic. But they remained calm and positive that all would be right in the world and that they would take care of this. And his confidence in his team flowed over to the wedding party, including me, the father of the bride who paid for all this.

So there’s much to be learned from an entrepreneur or a manager can respond to a serious problem while maintaining their composure in the midst of frantic clients during an ensuing crisis. Now the venue brought the power back up with the generators that they procured. So everything seemed ready to go. And power was on within minutes of the time that the wedding was to start. The guests were all in their chairs. The music played for me to escort my daughter down the aisle; we were going to turn a corner and go down a very, very long aisle. Then everyone stood up to see the bride just before I began to turn the corner to escort her to her awaiting room. And the power went out again.

Priscilla:
Oh, no.

Ivan:
I looked to my right and I watched the team of people from the venue run to the generators and fix the latest challenge within moments. And the power was up once again. And with that the music also started playing again. And the people were still remaining standing. They had stood and were waiting; the power went out and then came back on so they were still standing. The venue went above and beyond to get all of us to this moment. Now I had to escort a very frenetic bride-to-be down the aisle. Any bride, I think, would be frenzied at this point, and my beautiful daughter was certainly in that headspace.

So as I walked her down the aisle, a really long aisle, I did what any father might try to do—make her forget about her nerves. I could literally feel her arm trembling as we took our first steps and turned the corner.

So I leaned over and I whispered in her ear, I said, “Hey, honey, have you ever wondered why do you park on a driveway and drive on a parkway?” That was obviously so random a statement to make that she looked at me really curiously and said, “What?” I said, “Or why is it that you send cargo by ships and shipments by car?” The sheer randomness of these questions started to make her chuckle. So I went on to say, “And why do you think they call them cookies, when you actually bake them?”

Priscilla:
(chuckling)

Ivan:
Now she’s actually laughing. And people had to be wondering, you know, what the heck? What the heck is he saying to her to make her laugh? And as we got closer to the groom, I ended with one last question, “Why are they called apartments when they’re all stuck together?” At this point, she was no longer frenetic. She had a huge smile on her face, I kissed her cheek, told her I loved her and handed her over to her groom. She was beautiful, calm, and very, very happy.

Ivan:
It’s hard enough to do everything right, when things are going smoothly. The test of a team’s ability to perform is how they handle things when serious challenges are going on all around them. The lessons in this story are at least four.

One: remain calm during a crisis and project that sense of calm to your clients. The venue really did that.

Two: believe in your team, which means you need to have a good team. And the venue had that.

Don’t focus on the problem. That’s number three: don’t focus on the problem, focus on the solution. They were totally focused on the solution.

And four: act quickly to implement that solution.

And if it happens, if all of this happens just before you walk your daughter down the aisle to get married, make sure to take her mind off the chaos and have some fun. That’s the last bit of advice that I would like to share. That actually works in many situations throughout your life and throughout your business.

Priscilla:
That’s great. I can’t believe that you came up with all those little comments and questions you had tucked away somewhere.

Ivan:
Yeah. All right. Full disclosure?

Priscilla:
Uh-huh.

Ivan:
I’ve been to enough weddings, to see that things don’t, things never go perfectly, right. Ever. I mean, I have never been to a wedding where everything worked exactly as planned. And so I thought, okay, just in case Ashley is a little frenetic as she’s likely to be, even if things have gone well so far, you know, what can I say that would just get her to start chuckling? And so yeah, I did have these ideas, kind of in my back pocket, waiting to throw out just in case she was nervous. And wow, I’m so glad I did.

Priscilla:
That was perfect. Just perfect.

Ivan:
Yeah. And I think, you know, it was… what I did was fun and funny. But the real kudos have to go to the venue. Because I think what they did is a great example of what we as entrepreneurs need to do. Things go wrong. And an excuse is not nearly as good as the solution. And they were: they spent no time on the excuse. I mean, it was like, “This is what happened, we’re now going to deal with this.” And they weren’t pointing fingers. They, you know, it wasn’t inexpensive to go and buy multiple generators, which, you know, they may never need again for years. But they had them, and they were willing to get them. And so they were focused on the solution and they implemented it quickly. And so those are some of the lessons that I think we can all learn from what happened. If there’s any lesson in what I did it was you know, try to have a little fun while you’re dealing with the chaos that’s going around. But the kudos go to the team at that venue.

Priscilla:
And be prepared. That’s what you were, you were prepared.

Ivan:
Well, that’s a good point. Yeah. Yeah, go in with a little preparation. I always like to say, I plan my spontaneity. You know, I try to think about what can go wrong, what can’t go wrong, and try to have something in my mind to address that, whenever possible. And that was my contribution to helping to make that day an amazing day. And ironically, everything after that was perfect. Nothing went wrong for the rest of the entire event. So it was all good after that.

Priscilla:
Oh great. I’m glad.

Ivan:
Well, that’s what I have for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, thank you so much. That was a great story.

Ivan:
Hey, you know what? I’d like to add one thing. If you have an amazing story of customer service, where something went wrong, and you witnessed great customer service, or something went wrong with your service and, and you provided great customer service, share it here on the podcast. I think that would make some great stories. Back to you.

Priscilla:
Okay, thank you so much. This podcast is sponsored by misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and use the promo code IVAN50 [this code is good for a limited time] for 50% off of everything. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thanks so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Dr. Misner shares four lessons from his daughter’s wedding to apply when something goes wrong in business.

title.rendered: Episode 758: Innovation vs. Repetition (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 612, originally published on June 12, 2019.

Synopsis

After recording Episode 610, with its emphasis on timed repetition as one of the keys to being successful, Priscilla wondered where creativity comes in. Ivan had a strong response to this question, and decided to share it with the listeners.

Businesses do need innovation as well as repetition for success, but most small businesses try to skip the repetition. If you encourage BNI chapters to innovate, they try things random—often things that have already been tried, and failed.

Innovation has to be planned. Boredom is not a reason to change. BNI does innovate, but they test their changes one at a time, one chapter at a time, before making that change worldwide.

There’s plenty of room for innovation and creativity in your BNI meeting. Get creative with your weekly presentation and feature presentation. Change them frequently, but don’t change the process.

If you have an idea that you think is going to change the BNI meeting, talk to your BNI director. Work out a system where it gets tested. Change for the sake of change is not innovation. It’s just bad business.

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Complete Transcript of Episode 758/612

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic. I am on my way to San Antonio. It is my wife’s birthday tomorrow. And I promised to take her to San Antonio. I have never taken her there, so we will be leaving today for San Antonio’s Texas. 

Priscilla:
Great. Have a wonderful time. Would you like to introduce this topic?

Ivan:
I would like you to introduce it, but I will preface it a little. You said something at the end of one of our podcasts and I went on a rant. We have never done a podcast where it is completely unprepared. I am just going to do a rift based on a comment that you made and a discussion that we started to have because I have a lot of energy on this. I would love for you to share what you brought up and then I will jump in.

Priscilla:
Okay. I will be happy to do that. We did a podcast recently and the guest brought up a concept of repetition, doing things the same for long periods of time because he is very much into simplicity and he feels that repetition is a very important part of being successful. I was thinking where does innovation fit into that? If you are always doing the same thing over and over again, where does creativity come in and how do you make more products and become better at what you do? You had a strong response to that.

Ivan:
Yes I did, didn’t I? I had a very strong response to that. I will try to tone it down for this podcast. But I did have a strong response, and here is where I am coming from. I think that you need both repetition and innovation, but here is the rub: as soon as you say that you need innovation, what I find in BNI is that people just start changing stuff. “Oh, Ivan says innovate.” And they start changing things. They change things that we did before, stupid things that didn’t work and don’t make any sense at all. I said I wouldn’t get too excited, but I am getting excited. They start doing things that we have done, and it is just a bad idea. 

Innovation has to be done with a plan, and it has to be planned innovation. That seems counterintuitive, but you can’t just innovate willy-nilly, especially if you are talking about a global organization with almost 9,000 chapters. So if I have to lean between repetition and innovation, I think the biggest mistake that most small businesses make is they don’t do the repetition. Timed repetition is one of the reasons why BNI is as successful as it is. It is that getting together each week, doing that weekly presentation, having a speaker who is a BNI member, passing referrals every single week. These are all things that are repetitive. 

Now, you can do things that are innovative within it, as in doing better weekly presentations, doing meeting stimulants. The Education Coordinator, that was an innovation in BNI. The Visitor Orientation was an innovation in BNI. So there are many things that are added. Certainly, BNI Connect is an innovation in BNI. 

What I see done really badly, though, with businesspeople is they just want to change stuff because they get bored. Boredom is not a reason to change. Boredom is not a reason to just change up everything you are doing. You can make a change in an element of what you are doing, but I would urge business people — and I have said this many times — do six things 1000 times, not 1000 things six times. 

When BNI innovates, we innovate by trial and error and not by saying to 9000 chapters, “Hey, here is an idea. Try this out.” No, we try it in one chapter. If that works, we try it in five chapters. If that works, we try it in 20 chapters. And if that works, then we might try it in a region. And if that works, then we try it in a country. And if that works, we try it globally. So innovation takes place through trial and error, not willy-nilly, and my fear in telling people be innovative is that they might do things that have been done and failed. Does that make sense?

Priscilla:
It does make sense. It is a very disciplined way of doing innovation. I’m coming from the perspective of improvising because I play jazz, and it is something that we are taught to do, which is come up with new ideas within a structure. It does have a structure, but there is something to be said for creativity and spontaneity, so how do you respond to that?

Ivan:
Well, listen, I wouldn’t compare jazz to a BNI meeting. You know, they are substantially different and spontaneity in jazz is a whole different ball game than spontaneity in a business meeting. But I believe that you can incorporate some spontaneity in a BNI meeting through your weekly presentations. They should not be the same week in and week out. As a matter of fact, the worst ones are the ones that are the same every week. You want to change those. That is a way to incorporate innovation into the structure that exists. 

What I am talking about is just changing the structure, changing the process without some research and development. BNI has a pretty thorough research and development program where we test things out before we do it. Every now and then — and there will be handful of directors who will give me a call when they hear me say this — but every now and then I get some crazy BNI Director who says, “I have this great idea.” In 34 years, this has happened at least two dozen times, Priscilla. “I have this great idea. We are going to start doing mixers in our chapter. Everyone will invite everybody to the mixer and then we will invite them to the BNI meeting. We invite everybody to the mixer and then we invite them to the BNI meeting. This is going to be it. I think this is going to be a game-changer for us!”

I tell them, “Oh please, don’t do that.”

“Why not? This is different. It is new. It’s exciting. It’s fun. “

“Well, I have seen it two dozen times. And out of two dozen times, I have seen two dozen failures.”

“No, no. I have done it. I did it once. It was great.”

“Yeah, it will be great for two, three, four months, but after five or six months, you are going to stop doing it and you are going to call me and say I am right.”

“No, no. I am going to keep doing it.”

Priscilla, it’s been 34 years. I have never seen anyone do it past nine months, ever. People like change and there are certain behavior profiles, high “I”s, in particular, who are very people-oriented and they love change. My wife is a high I. She loves change. But the truth is, the most successful businesses are the ones that understand that you have to find a system and you have to be like a dog with a bone. If you want to establish creativity in that system, that’s okay, but you do it within the structure and the confines of the system, so you make a great weekly presentation, a great feature presentation and change that up. Don’t change up the process.

We talk about this in the Givers Gain book, by the way. When I wrote Givers Gain, one of the things that I wanted to do was talk about my mistakes in that book. The reason I talked about my mistakes was that I wanted to show people that this did not come out fully formed as an organization. I screwed up a fair amount throughout the process. I figured it out as I went, so why do we reinvent the wheel? If you have a great idea that you think is going to change the BNI meeting, talk to your BNI Director. Work out a system where it gets tested. Don’t just make changes, because change for change’s sake is not innovation. It is just bad business. Innovation comes through testing and experimentation, trial and error, with a process in place to measure the results. That is the key, measuring the results. That is the way that you do innovation, not just to change things up and make them different.

So I believe in innovation. It is one of our core values, but you have to do it with a plan. You can’t wing it. Does that makes sense?

Priscilla:
Yeah, it does, and you are one of the most disciplined people I have ever met and you consistently do something. You refer to yourself as a dog with a bone, and I believe that. I have witnessed it over the period of time that we have been working together. So I think that I hear what you are saying and there is a lot of validity to it. I like that you at least acknowledge that there is something to be gained from being creative within the structure. 

Ivan:
There is. If you look at BNI today, 34 years after I started it, there are a lot of differences. BNI Connect and the mobile app, and BNI University — these are all things that are innovative to our process, but they are an innovative way of doing the things that we have been doing for a long time. Take a look at BNI University. What is it about? It is about lifelong learning. Oh wait, that is one of our core values. What’s on BNI University? Things like Member Success Program. Oh wait, we have been doing that for decades. It is just a new way of doing something that we have been doing for a long time. So it is another way of being consistent, but it is riding the way of technology.  So that kind of innovation makes sense to me. But changing things just to change things is just stupid. That is the technical term. It’s just bad business. 

So don’t change things just to change things. Change things with a plan and a system and a process to measure results. That is the key. So let me step off my soapbox. Here I go, Priscilla. We did this whole podcast. It was completely off the cuff. Any last thought that you have before we wrap up?

Priscilla:
No I think you put it perfectly well. I think it is a great thing to think about and I liked how it got laid out.

Ivan:
Listen, I am really glad that you brought the topic up because I think it is an important topic. I am a believer in innovation, but if I had to lean anywhere, I would be leaning towards that timed repetition and that consistency, and you integrate that innovation into that consistency. When you do that, you have a one, two punch. It is the reason that BNI is – I had a radio show host who said, “BNI is the Mt. Rushmore of networking organizations.” It’s Mt. Rushmore for a reason. It’s because we do six things 1000 times. Thanks, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. Ivan, thank you so much. For more of Ivan’s content go to IvanMisner.com. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

excerpt.rendered: Want to change your BNI meeting? Talk to your BNI director first. Change for the sake of change is not innovation. It’s just bad business.

title.rendered: Episode 757: The View from Your Windshield

content.rendered:
Synopsis

There’s a reason your windshield is larger than your rearview mirror: it’s important to have clearest view possible of where you’re going. You also need to know your destination and route to get there. Moving faster doesn’t help when you’re going the wrong way. Your business goals are your destination, and BNI is the route you take to get there.

Sometimes weather is so bad, you need something to help clear things off your windshield. Other times you need even more help than that to move ahead safely, like someone to look out the side window on a narrow mountain road. But even when you proceed slowly for safety, you need to focus on what’s in front of you and keep the windshield of your life clear of obstructions. Things that frustrate us now can lead us into the future successfully. Focus on where you are going, not where you’ve been.

What have you done to be looking through the windshield as opposed to the rearview mirror? Share your experiences in the comments.

Complete Transcript of Episode 757

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you today?

Ivan:
I am doing great. And I’m still Zooming all around the world. You know, I forgot to mention, I just did a large Zoom presentation in [BNI] China, which went over really well. I’m still getting all about the world by Zoom.

Priscilla:
Uh-huh, great.

Ivan:
And luckily, we’re starting to actually, you know, go to events. So I’m looking forward to going to future events.

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
So the topic today, you said you liked the title for this one?

Priscilla:
Yes, I do. It’s very intriguing.

Ivan:
So there’s a reason why your windshield is larger than your rearview mirror. It’s important to have the clearest view possible of where you’re going. As a result, your windshield is substantially larger than your mirrors. Now, don’t get me wrong, it’s important to know what’s behind you and to learn from where you’ve been. However, if all you are looking at is your rearview mirror, it’s because you’re going backwards. When you do that in life, you’re not living in the present, and you’re not aware of what may be ahead of you.

And I bring up this topic because I hear members who, you know, want to go back to the good old days. They’re gone. Things change. We have to be looking through our windshield and be prepared. And you know, Adam Markel’s presentation, podcast, a few weeks ago, it was really perfect about this and how we prepare for change is so important. Sometimes in life, we do need to go backwards a little bit. However, as soon as possible, we need to put that car in drive and move forward to our intended destination.

And to get to that destination, we should have a map or an app to take us where we intend to go. In BNI, it’s BNI. That’s your roadmap. And in it, we can set our goal and identify the route that we want to take through referrals to reach it. Sometimes we have to take an alternate route because of heavy traffic or accidents along the way. You know, examples of that might be you have to bring in visitors to help you get through all of the meetings that you have, like we talked about recently with Daniel [Haverman]. But these are metaphors that I’m sharing with you that can apply to BNI. Basically, you need a general idea of where it is that you want to go, and BNI is a great way to help you get there. How we are doing it is changing. You know from strictly in-person to strictly online to now, in many places, a hybrid – some online, some totally in-person. But we have to be looking forward to what works for us in our countries all around the world.

I know people who work hard, they have a fast-paced life, and they’re always busy, but they don’t set life goals and some don’t even set business goals. From my perspective, most of these people are lost. They’re lost because they don’t know where they want to end up and going faster won’t get you there quicker if you don’t know where you’re going.

Ivan:
Windshield wipers are another great metaphor for life. Sometimes weather is so bad, you need something to help clear things off your windshield. Other times you need even more help than that. Years ago, I was driving with my family up to our lake house in Big Bear, California, and suddenly we hit a patch of fog that was so incredibly thick, I could not see the road in front of me at all. Worse yet, we were driving up a mountain road so I couldn’t pull off any place. There was no place safe to move the car off the road because I didn’t know where that would be; we could go right off the mountain.

So I actually rolled down the window, I was doing about five miles an hour, I rolled down the window and stuck my head out and literally drove ahead looking down at the little white ceramic dots. They’re called Botts’ Dots that are on so many highways throughout the country. By the way, they’re called Botts’ Dots after Elbert Botts who invented them back in the 50s. I drove very, very slowly staying in alignment with the dots, while my wife looked ahead to warn me if she saw any lights of a car in front of us, thankfully she didn’t. And after a mile or two, the fog cleared enough for me to roll up my window and simply drive ahead slowly.

To me, the metaphor for this is that sometimes we may need an assist to get where we’re going. It may be our life partner, or business associates, a mentor who helps us see more clearly. The key is we generally need to continue to move forward, safely, of course. And in order to do that, we need to understand that your windshield is larger than your rearview mirror. Looking out the side of the window, you may think doesn’t meet that metaphor, but my wife was still looking out the windshield to tell me if everything was safe ahead of me.

Ivan:
And I think all too often, Priscilla, people live in their past. The way things WERE.  Not the way things ARE, but the way things were. One last observation and then I’m happy to chat about this a little more if you want, Priscilla. Don’t let objects obstruct your view out of your windshield. There will be people in life that will get in the way of where you’re heading. Don’t let them. There will be people who want you to stop or to keep you from looking forward. Don’t let them; don’t let them do that either. Keep the windshield of your life as uncluttered as you should keep the windshield of your car.

Your rearview mirror is to see what is behind you. What you already passed and what is in the past. With the crazy times that we live in, it’s more important than ever to remember that your windshield is larger than your rearview mirror for a good reason. From this moment forward, know that it is the view out your windshield that will take you where you want to go in life. What are your thoughts, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
That’s great advice, Ivan. I find myself sometimes ruminating on decisions I made in the past and regretting them. And that is not helpful, I can tell you.

Ivan:
Yeah, it really is. I see people, especially in the middle of the changes, get really frantic and fearful. And I think I’ve said this on a previous podcast. Today is the tomorrow we were worried about yesterday. And it’s funny, you know, because at some point, we’re going to be in this new normal that we have, whatever that is, and we’ll be looking back in the past and going, “Well, actually, this ended up working out pretty well.”

You know, I remember I looked back at the past and the pushback that I got on certain things years ago with BNI. The pushback on getting Directors to get fax machines many, many years ago was like, “I don’t want a fax machine.” Well, we have to communicate. And then it was the Internet and email. Oh my goodness What chaos, Priscilla, when I forced all of my BNI Directors to get email. They were so mad at me, “We don’t need email, we can pick up the phone, it will work.” And you know, and then it was BNI Connect. People just don’t understand how much pushback I got, and the team got, on getting BNI Connect developed worldwide, which now is a game changer. You heard Daniel and I talk about how he came up with the data, or the team came up with the data, on substitutes. And that data came from BNI Connect. And so the things that right now are very frustrating for us, can lead us into the future successfully. And I think it’s really important to accept the changes that we see that can be positive. Look for the positive that is taking place in the windshield and stop living in the rearview mirror.

Priscilla:
That’s great. That’s great advice. Really good.

Ivan:
I’d love to hear anyone’s suggestions or thoughts about their experience with looking through the windshield versus the rearview mirror. It might have to do with us going online, it might have something to do specifically with your business. What have you done to be looking through the windshield as opposed to the rearview mirror? Share it with me here on BNI Podcast. I read all of the posts that go up here on the podcast site. Priscilla, thanks so much, again.

Priscilla:
Great, Ivan. Thank you so much. Well, this podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience and help you boost your business. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com. And then use the promo code IVAN50 [this code is good for a limited time] for 50% off all audio programs. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thanks so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: The reason your windshield is larger than your rearview mirror is to give you the clearest view possible of where you’re going.

title.rendered: Episode 756: BNI’s International Board of Advisors

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Since 1986, every member policy in BNI has been made or approved by the International Board of Advisors (IBOA). BNI members who serve on the IBOA are not compensated by BNI. They are regular BNI members who have a good working knowledge of the BNI policies, guidelines and processes and provide a member perspective to the BNI corporate. The IBOA does not implement policy: it provides input and recommendations to the company.

Daniel Haverman from BNI South Florida returns to the podcast to share his goals as this year’s IBOA president.

  • Have an IBOA representative for every BNI Member in every country around the world.
  • Increase Member awareness of the IBOA.
  • Implement a suggestion box system so Members can reach out to their IBOA representative with questions and suggestions.

Complete Transcript of Episode 756

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you today?

Ivan:
I am doing great. I continue to do Zoom meetings all around the world but doing fantastic. I’m in Austin, Texas today. And I have a long-time friend here on the BNI Podcast. He did a podcast with me, Episode 624, back in 2019. That’s Daniel Haverman. Daniel is a Board-Certified Civil Trial Lawyer. He’s also a Palm Beach County Traffic Magistrate. I wish I lived in Palm Beach, Daniel, you know, just in case. I haven’t gotten any tickets a long time. But you know, it’d be nice. Dan, not that you would do anything by the way; I know that you wouldn’t.

Dan’s been in BNI for over 20 years. And he’s served in every Leadership Team position. Currently, Dan is VP of his chapter, and Ambassador of the Palm Beach in South Florida regions and is also the President of the [BNI] International Board of Advisors, which is what we’re talking about today. Dan, welcome back to the BNI Podcast. And I was just kidding about the traffic thing you would never.

Daniel:
I know.

Ivan:
Yeah. Okay.

Daniel:
Thank you for having me back.

Ivan:
Yeah, it’s great to be here. You know, so I wanted to start with the International Board of Advisors, just a really brief history. It was started in 1986 to help me look at the policies that I created. I happen to create a couple of really bonehead policies, and so I wanted Members to really look at it. We met at a member’s home, we met at my home in La Verne where I started BNI, and we looked at policies. One bonehead policy, in particular, that I created was that you could be a member of two chapters of BNI, as long as they were a significant distance from each other. I thought the significant distance would be the issue, that wasn’t. Just being in a second chapter made people go apoplectic. And so that was, obviously, one of the first things that we looked at and the Board changed that policy and said you can’t be in two chapters of BNI or in two organizations like BNI, because it’s the same thing. And that came from the Board of Advisors. Since then, every policy of BNI, every member policy, has either been created or approved by the International Board of Advisors (IBOA). And we have here with us today, the President of the International Board of Advisors, Dan.

Ivan:
Dan, tell me what are your goals as President of the IBOA?

Daniel:
Well, before we get to that, I just want to make the Members to understand that we on the IBOA are not compensated by BNI. We’re not Directors, Area Directors, Executive Directors; we’re just regular Members who have a good working knowledge of the BNI policies, guidelines and processes and provide a member perspective to the BNI corporate. My goals as President of IBOA is… Well, I think you’ll agree with me Dr. Misner, that this group, the IBOA, probably has more practical knowledge about what is happening at the Member level than any other part of the BNI Global Support Team.

Ivan:
Yeah, for sure. Certainly, where the rubber meets the road at the chapter level. No question.

Daniel:
One of my goals as President is to have global diversity in the IBOA. I want to have an IBOA representative for every BNI Member in every country around the world. We currently know how many BNI Members we have in each country, and we know how many IBOA members there are in each country. And we’ve actually determined what we believe is an optimal representation. Some countries might be overweighted and some underweighted. And I’m confident that I will be able to accomplish this goal of having IBOA representation for all members before the end of my term.

Ivan:
Well, I think that’s a really important goal. Having a good representation worldwide is, I think, critical.

Daniel:
Another goal of mine is to increase Member awareness of the IBOA, and that is why I’m so excited to talk about the IBOA with you on this podcast. Because I know thousands of BNI Members listen to your podcast every week. And when my two initial goals are met, my plan is to implement a suggestion box system so Members can reach out to their IBOA representative with questions and suggestions about our policies, guidelines and processes.

Ivan:
That’s a great idea. And I’m sure you’ll get a lot of feedback on that one. Anything else? Any other goals?

Daniel:
Well, I was prepared to talk about some of the projects we’ve worked on in the past and some of the things we’re working on currently.

Ivan:
Tell me, what are some of the projects you’ve done in the past?

Daniel:
Three or four years ago, you will recall, the question was asked, “Should we change the substitute language from being only a guideline, to BNI policy?”

Ivan:
Yeah.

Daniel:
You’ll recall that the guidelines said that a Member may substitute up to three times, may have a substitute up to three times, during a six-month period. And the question that we asked was should we change it from the “may” to “must not” – you can’t have a sub more than three times, making it a policy. So, you set up an IBOA Substitute Policy Task Force, and you’ll recall that we discussed what a good sub is, which is a sub that’s prepared with a well-timed commercial; a bad sub, which is subs just trying to drum up business for themselves; and then remember what we called super subs, which is those guys trying to get a free ride. And most importantly, we talked about how many is too many.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Daniel:
So you asked Andy Hart to run and develop some analytics. And we looked at over 136,000 BNI records and countries where more than six subs were allowed. And do you remember how surprised you were when we received the analytics, Dr. Misner optimizer?

Ivan:
I was, absolutely. And Dan, please call me Ivan. Yes, I was very surprised. Go ahead.

Daniel:
Just there was no statistical evidence to show that more than six substitutes affected a member’s ability to get qualified referrals. Use of more than six subs did not decrease Thank You For Closed Business, and it did not affect chapter growth or retention. In fact, [in] the current Leadership Team Manual, there was no mention of a maximum number of substitutes. It simply says, if a member cannot attend, they may send a substitute. This will not count as an absence.

Ivan:
I think the key there though, is… and that was a surprise to me. And I think the key though, is that they be a legitimate substitute; not a super sub, not someone just trying to promote their business. Would you agree with that?

Daniel:
Totally. I mean, a well-prepared sub represents the Member, not themselves. That’s BNI 101.

Ivan:
Yeah. And that really did surprise me, that data. And the truth is, you know, the data rules, and I’ve always felt that way. You know, I may have some experience, that varies, that differs. But the data is the most important thing. And we took a really good look on it, based on the data that we were able to collect from BNI Connect. So that’s a project that you did work on. What’s a project you are currently working on as of the date of this interview?

Daniel:
Well, before I talk about that, I just want to make sure that the BNI Members understand that the IBOA does not implement policy. We are not responsible for the recent dues increase, we only provide input and recommendations to the company.

Ivan:
By the way, you mentioned dues increase. Dues increases vary all around the world and don’t happen at the same time. That may apply to someone listening to this, it may not apply to someone.

Daniel:
Okay. Projects that we’re working on. Well, we’re very active, Dr. Misner, Ivan; we’re very active. When you approved Sandy Donovan’s nomination of me to the IBOA, we were meeting, you’ll recall, two times a year. We currently have two sessions a month. And we are providing input on many existing projects. One of them that I think is near and dear to your heart, is we are involved in a Thank You For Closed Business revision. We want to make it easier for Members to know how to accurately turn in their closed business. And as you know, banking and finance has always been a challenge.

Ivan:
Yeah, and real estate.

Daniel:
And real estate, exactly. One thing we’re considering is, instead of looking at the type of income produced such as, Did you make the money on commissions? We’re trying to look at specific professions from which the income was generated with a goal, again, of the Thank You For Closed Business project is to have better Member participation, documentation, and accuracy.

Ivan:
Well, that’s a good one. That’s a big, that’s a big topic. And when we come to a conclusion there, we’re going to definitely need to do a podcast to update our previous podcasts on this topic.

Daniel:
We also looked at the General Policies. In 2019, there were 14 BNI General Policies. And again, there’s a difference in policies and guidelines. And I think most people that are listening to this podcast, understand BNI policies versus guidelines, and we can talk about that. But we had 14 policies. Now we’re down to 11. And that’s because the IBOA and BNI’s corporate people felt that some of the policies could actually be handled as guidelines or even at the Leadership Team level.

For example, there was a policy in 2019 that said members are required to bring bona fide referrals and the chapter may establish a minimum. Well, the IBOA felt that that’s the kind of policy that actually could be handled at the Membership Committee level. And another one that is, I felt was a great improvement for the company, was the changes to the leave of absence policy. The leave of absence policy always said you cannot take a leave of absence except for medical leaves. And that we found to be a little bit too tough. And we changed it to leaves may be granted for certain major extenuating circumstances at the discretion of the Membership Committee. So basically what we did was, we tried to tighten up the policies to make them more 21st century. Excuse me, if I said that.

Ivan:
Yeah, no, it’s fine. And you know, as policies get changed, some people might be going, “Really, that’s a new policy?” It takes a little while to trickle down to all of the countries and all of the regions and all of the cities that BNI is in. So you know, what we’re talking about are changes that take time to implement worldwide, just in case some of this may be new to you.


Daniel:
Another exciting change that has come and is still being developed and furthered is obviously the mobile app. It’s something you’ve been pushing for years and now finally, everyone has it on their phones, the BNI mobile app. But now we have something called BNI Referral Tracking. And that means that you can actually go into your BNI mobile phone, your app, open it up, look at a referral that you gave, and you can see if the person that you gave the referral to—Did they follow up on it? Did they make contact with the source? Did they close the deal? And did they turn in Thank You For Closed Business? This can all be done with your telephone now. And so it’s just so much more convenient for Members to… Listen, if you go on your phone, and you find out that the person never turned in the Thank You For Closed Business, there’s nothing wrong with calling that Member and saying listen, what happened with that referral?

Ivan:
Well, Dan, we’re out of time now. How does a BNI Member get to be on the IBOA?

Daniel:
BNI Members that are interested in joining the IBOA must contact their Executive Director or National Director. Leadership Team experience is required. And you must have a good working knowledge of BNI policies and guidelines. Your Executive Director will give you an IBOA nomination form. And if you’re accepted, when you’re accepted, you’ll sign a confidentiality notice, and we’ll add you to the group.

Ivan:
And in some regions, it may be a Managing Director or Regional Director, there are different terms. But find your Director to ask if you can be on the International Board of Advisors. Daniel, thank you so much for being on BNI Podcast the second time. Anyone that’s interested in information on Daniel and his business, it’s www.havermanlaw.com. That’s HAVERMANLAW.com. Daniel, thanks again for being on the BNI Podcast.

Daniel:
Thank you for having me.

Ivan:
Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, thank you so much. That was great information. This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. And these audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience and help you boost your business. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 [this code is good for a limited time] for 50% off of all of the audio programs, and all the proceeds are going to go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Daniel Haverman joins the podcast to share his goals as president of BNI’s International Board of Advisors (IBOA).

title.rendered: Episode 755: Becoming Change Proof

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Resilience is not about our ability to bounce back, but our capacity to bounce forward. Adam Markel joins Dr. Misner on the podcast this week to introduce his book Change Proof: Leveraging the Power of Uncertainty to Build Long-Term Resilience. To become resilient, we need to develop rituals of recovery to regenerate our energy in four specific zones:

  1. Mental
  2. Emotional
  3. Physical
  4. Spiritual

A ritual is a conscious habit that you create for yourself, based on something you can change in that quadrant, such as taking time to sit quietly first thing in the morning instead of checking your phone, or reading fiction instead of watching the news after work when you need to unwind.

Find out more about Adam at AdamMarkel.com. Get your copy of Change Proof at changeproof.com. Take the Resilient Leader Assesment at www.resiliencerank.com and discover resources for resetting yourself on a ritual basis to regain and recapture energy.

Complete Transcript of Episode 755

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, I just took a little vacation in Park City, Utah, where I had a chance to meet a lot of BNI Directors at a special meeting, and this week I’m at the U.S. National Conference in San Diego. A real live in-person meeting so it’s gonna be exciting, in San Diego, to see everybody again.

Priscilla:
Who do you have with us today?

Ivan:
I have a friend and special guest on BNI Podcast today, Adam Markel. You know, people are hard-wired to resist change and to seek certainty, but that really leaves people ill-equipped to thrive in a rapidly changing world. And as I say, with me today is best-selling author, keynote speaker and resilience researcher, Adam Markel. Now Adam is the author of a new book, Change Proof – Leveraging the Power of Uncertainty to Build Long-Term Resilience. He is also a fellow member with me in the Transformational Leadership Council started by Jack Canfield. So, Adam, welcome to the BNI Podcast. It is great to have you on.

Adam:
Oh, it’s great to be here with you, Ivan, and Priscilla.

Ivan:
So let’s start with the idea of change proof, Adam. What’s it mean to be change proof?

Adam:
Oh, that’s a great question. Change proof is, it’s kind of a made-up term, at least it was at the start. Now we’re seeing quite a bit in use because there’s so much change that is part of everybody’s life. And I think before the pandemic, that was certainly the case. But I don’t know that people felt it as much as they do now. I think the level of change and disruption is really profound. And it is causing great, great levels of anxiety for many people; many people are exhausted and even nearly burned out. And the concept of being change proof is really being able to leverage the uncertainty as you said in the book subtitle. It’s about the power of uncertainty and using and leveraging the power of uncertainty as a catalyst for our long-term growth.

For so many people, they define being resilient as simply being able to endure, bounce back from setbacks or hardships. And having researched this topic now for several years, more than 3000 leaders from organizations across the globe, from Fortune 50 companies to startups, what we found is that resilience is not about our ability to bounce back, but actually our capacity to bounce forward. It’s not about endurance, as again, many many people really feel it is, it’s actually about how it is that we recover. How it is that we reset, and we regenerate our energy in four specific zones that we can talk about today.

Ivan:
You know, I just wrote a piece about the future and I call it Predictable Unpredictability. And that’s really what I think the future is, that we have to be prepared for unpredictability. I studied, I don’t know if you know this, under Warren Bennis at the University of Southern California, who was in his day, the world’s leading expert on leadership. And one of the things that he talked about leadership is adaptive capacity, the ability to be resilient, to adapt to changing situations. And I think that’s exactly what you’re talking about here, Adam. Now, you talk about rituals for resilience. Tell me about that.

Adam:
Yeah. So when it comes to how it is that we develop greater resilience, we have to think of it as something that’s holistic. It’s not one sort of big, amorphous thing, it’s actually baked into four different areas of our lives: mental, emotional, physical, and spiritual. And the key to developing resilience is again, not about endurance. It’s about how we create recovery in situations where we feel stressed or where we’re being challenged by uncertainty or by the unknowns or you know, a pandemic that doesn’t seem to end or supply chain issues or any number of things that are just the constant challenge or disruption du jour. We have to develop rituals to recover mentally, emotionally, physically, and spiritually. And if we are able to do that, if we’re successful at creating and crafting, curating specific rituals for that recovery, then we’re able to have the energy, the insight, the creativity, the capacity to be able to outperform any of those challenges.

And that’s certainly been what I’ve seen in my own life, starting at age 19, as a Jones Beach lifeguard, which is where I typically begin the journey when I’m speaking to groups of folks, right up through 18 years as a practicing attorney, and now in the work that I do. In all that time, I’ve never seen a time when change was not the great constant. And I’ve also never witnessed the time when people weren’t in some way, shape, or form, resistant or resisting and fighting change. And certainly now is no exception.

Ivan:
Can you give us an example of a ritual for resilience?

Adam:
So let’s look at mental for example. I sometimes use a case study of a woman that came to me shortly after I’d published a TED talk, and I, in this TED talk, told the story of how I end up in the emergency room, thinking I’m having a heart attack. Really believing, frankly, that I wasn’t going to see my kids again. My wife and I have been married since college and have four healthy kids. And I had this frightening morning, Saturday morning in the hospital, only to find out, fortunately, that I was having a panic attack, an anxiety attack, but my heart was fine. That was the good news. That was really, really what brought me to tears that day, realizing that I was healthy. But yet there were things in my life that were happening that were really a cause for concern. So I tell this story and eventually shared it on a TED stage. You know, it was embarrassing to me at the time as a lawyer. I didn’t feel like I could be that vulnerable about what I’d been experiencing; I didn’t know what to do with it.

And this woman who’s the CEO of a hospital in the Southeast [U.S.], she gets in contact with me after watching the TED Talk. She says this exact same thing happened to her this past weekend and she didn’t know what to do about it. And I told her, well, one of the lessons that I learned 12 years earlier, when this happened to me was that she needed to tell her leadership team, she needed to share this with other people. And that it might well be the most important thing she does as a leader or has ever done as a leader. It took some doing but she did agree to follow that advice and shared it with her senior team and was amazed to find out how many people not only were in support of her and wanted to help her, but also saw great strength in her transparency. Great trust was established because she’s willing to be that vulnerable. And she also learned that people were really suffering; they were having just as tough a time.

So we worked on what we call the recovery map with her. And the first element of it was to find something she could change mentally to create a new ritual for herself mentally. And what we established pretty quickly was that her ritual in the morning was to wake up, she was being woken up by her phone. And then immediately she would touch her phone and start to look at, you know, what was going on. She’s the CEO of a hospital. I mean, she has a lot of things that could go wrong while she was taking her six or seven hours of restless sleep. So the ritual that we created for her was a morning ritual, a rising ritual, where she couldn’t touch her phone for the first 30 minutes of the day, which was really difficult. And then the first 10 minutes, in particular, was a time that she was going to sit in stillness. That stillness could be gratitude practice, it could be prayer, it could be meditation, but that she wasn’t going to do anything, wasn’t going to watch anything on TV, etc. So that became one of four new rituals that she created.

And for folks that might be concerned or wondering, what’s a ritual? How’s it different than a habit? A habit is, to me, something that we do unconsciously, and like brushing your teeth with the same hand every day, that kind of thing. And a ritual is something we do with great consciousness, with great presence. And that’s the only difference. It’s not something religious, even though sometimes that word is associated with spiritual things. It’s just a conscious habit that you start, you create for yourself, and you ultimately can create these new rituals in each of these areas: mental, emotional, physical, spiritual. And the good news is they don’t have to be big changes. They can be tiny, tiny things. So for this CEO we’ll call Denise, in the emotional quadrant, she stopped watching TV when she got home from work at night. She wanted to unwind so instead of watching TV or specifically watching the news in which to unwind, which was triggering her emotionally, she just started to do something she didn’t have time to do, which was to read for pleasure. So we just replaced, it was just a habit replacement there.

Ivan:
I love that one. And what I’ve started doing is listening to books while I’m making dinner or doing something. But it’s a great ritual to read a book while I’m doing something that doesn’t take any thought, like cooking.

Adam:
Yeah, you can stack rituals as well. So for me, I too wanted to read more books and I was finding it difficult. But one thing that I was already doing was going to the local YMCA and swimming, because I’m a swimmer and that’s my main form of physical release. So I decided to bring a book with me and I would read for 10 or 15 minutes before I would go in the pool, and I’d read for 10 or 15 minutes afterward. And I was just tearing through books because I stacked one ritual against another. And anybody can do that. You take something you know you’re already doing, and then add something to it.

Ivan:
We’re out of time. Can you give me a quick misnomer about resilience before we wrap?

Adam:
Well, I think the greatest misnomer, again, is that it’s about how it is that we endure hardship or stress or difficulties. And you can reframe resilience to know that it’s really about how it is that we are resetting ourselves on a ritual basis to regain and recapture energy. And most often what we start people with is a baseline assessment of how they’re doing resilience wise. And people can go to www.resiliencerank.com, www.resiliencerank.com. It takes about three minutes, that’s the best part of it. And as I say, we’ve done it with more than 3000 leaders across the globe. And they’ll not only get their scores in those four areas, but they’ll also get a whole host of resources for creating new rituals that they can try on for size and see how many of them stick.

Ivan:
resiliencerank.com That’ll be in the transcript notes. Also for Adam: www.AdamMarkel.com and www.changeproof.com. Adam, thank you so much for being on the BNI Podcast. I really appreciate you.

Adam:
Oh, I so appreciate this conversation, Ivan. Thank you.

Ivan:
Thank you. Back to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience and help you boost your business. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and use the promo code IVAN50 [this code is good for a limited time] for 50% off all of the audio programs. And all of the proceeds are going to go to the BNI Foundation. So thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Resilience is not about our ability to bounce back, but our capacity to bounce forward by developing rituals of recovery in four quadrants.

title.rendered: Episode 754: I Want Referrals, and I Want Them NOW! (Classic)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 596, published on February 20, 2019.

Synopsis

Strong referral relationships are like close personal friendships. It takes about 50 hours of interaction to move a person from “acquaintance” to “casual friend.” It takes a total of 90 hours to become “real friends,” and 200 hours to become close friends.

When BNI members reach the 90-hour mark, they start receiving more referrals. When those same individuals cross the 200-hour mark, they generate an average of five times as many referrals as they did in the first year.

The time-confidence curve illustrates the way your fellow networkers need to gain confidence that referring people to you will not hurt their reputations.

Here are four questions to ask yourself to gain perspective on your efforts and results. Keep asking them until the answers become obvious.

  1. Am I being realistic about the amount of time it will take in my profession to gain the critical level of confidence I need?
  2. Am I regularly making stimulating, educational presentations to my fellow networkers about the value I provide my clients.
  3. Am I doing business with others in my network so I can give them dynamic testimonials and steer business to them so they will return the favor?
  4. Am I meeting regularly with my networking colleagues to learn about their businesses so I can confidently refer my contacts to them?

It takes time to build friendships. The best way to speed up the process is to spend time in the process.

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Complete Transcript of Episode 754/596

Priscilla: 
Hello and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today with the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you today?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic, Priscilla. I have what I think is a fun topic: I want referrals and I want them now! I love the way you said it with an exclamation point. Now! I run into people and they are almost desperate for referrals. Desperation is not referable. I am sure this isn’t for every listener. When you hear this, I am not talking about you. I am talking about those people who come across as desperate for referrals.

So you want referrals and you want them now. Well, you want referrals and you want them now. You can’t have them unless you have built meaningful relationships with your referral partners. 

Like I said, I am often asked “How long does it take for people to receive referrals from their network? How do we speed this process up?” That is the one that I love. “How do you speed this process up?”

Building a referral-based business is all about building a powerful personal network. If your network is a mile wide and an inch deep, you will never get any kind of referrals that make a difference in your business. This means that you have to go deep in building a number of strong relationships. From my experience, strong referral relationships are a lot like building close personal friendships. Now, Facebook has redefined what a friend is, but I am talking about truly close friendships.

In a study that I recently read that was published in 2018 by the Journal of Social and Personal Relationships, it was found that it takes about 50 hours of interaction to move from being an acquaintance to becoming a casual friend. It takes a total of 90 hours to become what they called real friends and – get this, Priscilla – a total of 200 hours to become close friends. 

Priscilla:
Hmmm, that’s a lot.

Ivan:
That’s a lot. And according to this study, friendship status was examined as a function of hours together, shared activities, and regular conversation. Now, this fits the BNI experience so well because people who are in BNI for a year, that is when things oftentimes start clicking for them, and at two years, they are really, really strong and generating, on average, more referrals. That is that 200-hour mark in that second year. So when I read this, I was like oh my goodness, they could have written this about BNI. So you know, when it comes to how long does it take to build a close relationship, it is somewhere between that 90 and 200 hours. That sounds right to me. This matches up perfectly with what I have seen in BNI.

When BNI members hit that 90-hour mark, they almost always begin receiving more and more referrals. Based on an independent study published in 2012 for BNI, when those same individuals cross the 200 mark, they generate an average of five times the number of referrals they did in the first years. Yes, you heard that right: more than 500% more referrals when they built a strong relationship with their referral partners. That is just a shocking number. 

So the best way to speed up the process is to actually spend time in the process of building relationships with the people who you are networking with. Networking truly is—and you have heard me say this a lot—more about farming than it is about hunting. This is hard data that helps support that. It is about building relationships and friendships with other business professionals. 

Now, it is important to note that there is a confidence curve that you have to negotiate before doing business with someone. We talk about the time confidence curve in our Member Success Program Training and in other places, but I am going to include it here in the podcast just to remind you. If you take that curve too fast, you will flip your reputation and end up in a very awkward position. Now, when it comes to getting referrals from your network, confidence is a vital component. Not your confidence, but the confidence that your fellow network member has in you. None of them want to risk their personal reputation by referring business to someone that they do not know, like, and trust; that they are not yet at credibility with in the VCP Process®.

So even though you may have known many of your networkers for some time, until they have gained a certain level of confidence that referring contacts to you will not harm their reputation with their clients and friends, you are still a referral stranger unless you have hit that point. So, the time confidence curve is here in the podcast. It is also in my book, Networking Like a Pro. It illustrates the dynamics of the process. Your success in getting referrals depends partly on your competence, of course, but also on how far up the confidence curve the referrer’s confidence in you has progressed. If you are at a point in the relationship where you are just starting, you are not going to get many referrals, but if you are at a higher level in the relationship and you have known each other for a while but you haven’t quite achieved the necessary confidence level with this person to get the referral from them, then you are still not going to get referrals. It is only when you hit that critical confidence level on the curve where people now feel comfortable with you. And by the way, the time confidence curve varies with the profession. For a florist it may be really quick, but for a financial planner it is really, really long. 

One way to expedite this process, which is the question that I am often asked, is to do regular 1-2-1s with people and go deeper in knowing how to refer them as well as teach them how to refer you. It is not always easy to know how far you have progressed up your confidence curve. Many networkers spend a lot of time and effort trying to build others’ confidence in them and then when they are on the brink of success, they get discouraged and they stop going to the networking meetings they were going to or they stop coming to BNI.

Oftentimes, they stop right at that point where things are going to kick in. I remember I once heard a story about a guy who had been in two years and certainly made more than his investment back, but it wasn’t the big numbers he was hoping for. He almost didn’t do that third-year membership. Three weeks into his third year, he got the biggest referral of his life. Not just from BNI, but from all referral sources. It was three weeks into that third year. That was a great example. He was in one of those professions that takes time. He was a financial planner. So, you know, if you drop out, you are going to lose those types of connections.

Here is what you can do to gain perspective on your efforts and the results that they are producing. Ask yourself the following four questions and keep asking them over and over until you have attained success and the answers become obvious. Here are the four questions:

  1. Am I being realistic about the time it will take in my profession to gain the critical level of confidence that I need? 

  2. Am I regularly making stimulating educational presentations to my fellow networkers about the value that I provide to my clients? I recommend that you go back and listen to the podcast I did with Charlie Lawson. I think it was entitled “Don’t Bore Me to Tears”, which is a great title. He talks about how you do a really good weekly presentation.

  3. Am I doing business with others in my network so that I can give them dynamic testimonials and steer business to them so that they will return the favor?

  4. Am I meeting regularly with my networking colleagues to learn about their businesses so that I can confidently refer my contacts to them?

So if you are following these simple tactics and are asking yourself these questions, then you are well along the road to getting the referrals from others in the networks that you participate in, BNI in particular. So I will leave you with one last thought and then if you have questions or want to talk some more, Priscilla, I am happy to do that. 

Here is my last thought: remember it takes time to build friendships – unless you are in kindergarten, then you can do it in about five minutes. 

Priscilla:
Right. Well, I do have something to say which is I am a big proponent for using each other’s services. I think that can really move along the time frame. If you give a testimonial for somebody that you just used their services, if you loved what they did, that can really change the timeline. 

Ivan:
Yes.

Priscilla:
I just had it happen to me. I used a personal organizer in our group. It had been a long time before I used her services. She was wonderful. Now I can just truly say that to everybody and to anybody I refer her to. You know, it changes everything. So I think it is a great – I know you are not supposed to sell to your fellow members, but I really think it helps for fellow members to use each other’s services so they have the personal experience. 

Ivan:
I am not sure I have ever said to anybody that you shouldn’t sell to your fellow members. I think you don’t want to target your fellow members exclusively. I agree with you, Priscilla. I think using each others’ services is brilliant because then you can come back and give those testimonials which will impact your referrals. I am a fan, but I just caution people not to exclusively give internal referrals. Some chapters call them internal referrals, which is member-to-member. An external referral is an outside member to a member. Some chapters call them Tier 1 and Tier 2 referrals. Whatever you call it, you want to transition to the latter over time. You want to transition to those outside referrals.

But it is hard to transition to outside referrals if you can’t speak about the person personally, which is your point, I think. Yeah?

Priscilla:
Right.

Ivan: 
I couldn’t agree more. So to me, it is not either / or. It is both / and. It should begin with the internal referrals and graduate over time to the external referrals. Good point, Priscilla, thank you.  That is everything I’ve got today. 

Priscilla:
Okay, well that sounds great. Thank you, Dr. Misner. I really appreciate it. So for more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast. 

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Results take time. BNI members average 5 times as many referrals after 200 hours in a chapter as when they started.

title.rendered: Episode 753: You’re Going to Need a Bigger Boat

content.rendered:
Synopsis

A “Brody Moment” is where you have a shockingly unambiguous realization that the current resources are no longer a viable option to achieve the results that you’re looking for. What you do as a result, and how fast you do it, can turn a Brody Moment into a defining moment.

Dr. Misner had a Brody Moment about BNI in 1986, when BNI had 30 chapters and he realized it could get to 10,000 chapters—if he created a plan to scale the business. BNI reached 10,000 chapters in 2020.

What are some of the epiphanies that you’ve had in business? Do you have a plan to scale your business? Share your thoughts in the comments.

Complete Transcript of Episode 753

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I’m still Zooming around the world, Priscilla. I’ve been to a number of states in the U.S. including Vermont. I’ve been to India, Canada, Europe. I’m really looking forward to going out and seeing people in-person. And I know we have the [BNI] U.S. Conference coming up and I plan on being there live and in-person really soon.

Priscilla:
When is that conference?

Ivan:
That is the week of April 28 through 30th. So there might still by the time this podcast comes out, there might be an opening or two if you’re listening and you want to go to the U.S. Conference.

Priscilla:
Okay, great.

Ivan:
So today’s an interesting topic, You’re Going to Need a Bigger Boat. In the 1975 hit movie, “JAWS,” Martin Brody, the police chief of a really small summer resort in the Northeastern U.S., utters probably one of the most quotable lines in film history when he gets his first close-up look of the great white shark. Remember that moment, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
I think I do. Yeah.

Ivan:
And as soon as he sees it, he slowly backs into the wheelhouse and he says to Captain Quinn, “You’re gonna need a bigger boat.” And this is what I like to call the prototypical Brody Moment, with Martin Brody, the police chief. It’s where you have a shockingly unambiguous realization that the current resources are no longer a viable option to achieve the results that you’re looking for.

And so let me explain how my Brody Moment came about with BNI. And I’ve talked about this a little bit in different podcasts, but I’m going to put it all together here. But the most important thing for you to think about as I talk about my Brody Moment is your Brody Moment, as you listen to mine. You know, understanding your Brody Moment can help you think about your motivations, and move you forward successfully with your entrepreneurial endeavors. You’re thinking about what’s it going to take to have a bigger opportunity to move your business or scale your business along.

My Brody Moment for BNI came at the end of 1985. After about 11 months, 11½ months, of opening up BNI groups, I’d opened 20 chapters. And I’d really done it by accident, without a plan, without even trying. And that’s when I realized that I had struck a chord in the business community. I was a management consultant in Southern California, and I needed referrals for my consulting practice. I needed referrals for my own business, and I’d hoped that I would be able to refer some of my friends. So I put together a group where I could start passing business to them. And hopefully, they would do the same for me.

I had previously gone to networks, and I’ve mentioned this briefly in some other podcasts, where they were incredibly mercenary – everyone was trying to sell to me. And I went to other groups that were totally social, happy hour, and hors d’oeuvres, and there was no business happening. What I wanted was something to focus on business without being mercenary. It was relational, not transactional, something that at the same time wasn’t totally social, because I wanted the relationship building aspect that led the business. Therefore, I created a network that I hoped would satisfy both of those considerations. I wanted to merge that focus on business with the relational aspect and the glue that would hold it all together is our principal Core Value of Givers Gain® — the idea that if I help you, you help me, we can both benefit one another by working together.

That one networking group led to another and another until there were 20 chapters within 11 or 12 months. And that first year, you know, I was method-acting my way through the process. I was trying to figure it out as I went. I was a young 28-year-old business professional. And I really thought that most businesses had this figured out. The thing is, I learned that nobody had it figured out because we don’t teach this in colleges and universities anywhere in the world. What I didn’t expect to find was that everyone has this challenge. And that was my Brody Moment.

Ivan:
To me, BNI was an example of necessity being the mother of invention. And it helped a lot of businesses. At the end of 1985, I figured out that I was going to need a bigger boat. And this way of business networking was going to be much larger than I anticipated. So I sat down, and I created my business plan to scale the company. And I touched upon this, I think, in a previous podcast, but to be more specific, it was June of 1986. And I had a goal in mind as to how big BNI could be someday and I went to the library to gather information on populations. Now 1986 – remember, at that time, there was no such thing as Google. And so after extensive research and many calculations, I felt that BNI could someday have 10,000 chapters.

And shortly after that, I told a friend of mine that I think that BNI could have 10,000 groups someday. He said, “10,000.” I said, “Yeah, I think that’s possible”. And he said, “Well, and how many groups do you have now, Ivan?” And this is like June of 86. I said, “30.” He said, “You think you can have 10,000?” I said, “Yeah, I think it’s possible.” And he said, “Well, it’s good to have goals, Ivan.” And that was a BIG goal.

Every year near the end of December, and I’ve talked about this in a lot of my podcasts, I take time to reflect. That particular year I read “The E-Myth” by Michael Gerber early on and I used it as a baseline strategy. I looked at what was working well and what didn’t work at all. And each year, I adjusted my plan and revisited the small goals that were all striving towards that one big goal of scaling my company into a global enterprise. In December 2020, in the middle of the craziest years I have ever seen in business, BNI achieved that big goal. We had grown to 10,000 chapters, and we continue to grow, helping BNI members around the world, do business through referral marketing.

Ivan:
As a business leader, you’ve probably experienced a few Brody Moments over the course of your career. And you’ve probably got a few more coming. What you do as a result, and how fast you do it, can turn a Brody Moment into a defining moment. And so, I ask you to think about what are some of the epiphanies that you’ve had in business? Comment on them here in this podcast.

And if you haven’t had a Brody Moment like I’m describing, sit down and think about your business and what’s it going to take to scale your business. How are you going to do it? How are you going to build that bigger boat to help you scale your business? And it doesn’t have to be global.
