Certainly, when I was thinking about BNI back then, I wasn’t thinking of it in global perspective. I was thinking of it in a national perspective. International came later. But I still had my Brody Moment. And I still realized that I could create a plan to scale my business. And that’s what we all need to do, even if it’s scaling it locally, or regionally. Scaling your business is what entrepreneurship is really, to a large extent, about. And that’s my message for today. Priscilla, do you have any thoughts or questions?

Priscilla:
Do I have any thoughts or questions? I have thoughts, definitely. But I was trying to think if I had a Brody Moment, and I don’t scale my business, Ivan, it’s one recording studio. But I did change what we’re doing here at the studio and focus on something a little bit different, which is doing a narration recording and audio books primarily.

Ivan:
Yeah, well, those are ways to scale your business. You’ve also transitioned from, you know, doing a lot of things by telephone to, we’re using Zencaster to record these now. And you can tell the quality is, I think, substantially better. But taking on different products like, you know, voiceovers and reading. You said, book material; you’re doing book readings?

Priscilla:
We’re doing audio books for lots of publishers.

Ivan:
Audiobooks, yeah. So those are things that you can do to scale your business. And the more all of us can sit down and think about – how do we change during these changing times? – the more effective we’re going to be at scaling our business and pivoting our business. You know, it was back in 2018 that I wrote my first public article, I’d been talking about it for two years before that, but I wrote my first public article that the future of in-person networking was online. And, you know, the pushback on that concept was dramatic back in 2018. But I knew it was inevitable. And what I also believe to be true, is that it will also help to scale BNI even further. Because creating it as an online platform, even in a hybrid form, or fully online form, is going to make it easier and easier and easier for business people to participate.

And so the kinds of things that you want to think about, not just you, Priscilla, but anyone listening to this podcast, are how can you do things that utilize current technology that enable you to have a bigger impact on your business? And you can have one location and still be able to scale to a larger extent in that one location. So that’s what I ask everyone to think about. And certainly, I think, decent food for thought, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
I think that’s a great thing to be considering. And I appreciate it. It’s a great topic.

Ivan:
That’s everything that I have today.

Priscilla:
Okay, then. Thank you so much, Ivan. This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience and help you boost your business. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and use the promo code IVAN50 [this code is good for a limited time] for 50% off of all the audio programs. And all of the proceeds are going to go to the BNI Foundation. So thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Turn a “Brody Moment” into a defining moment by creating a plan to get the resources you need to scale your business.

title.rendered: Episode 752: Who to Have a One-to-One With

content.rendered:
Synopsis

The key to intentional One-to-Ones is to have them with the right people. Shirley Towne recommends focusing your energy on two people every month.

Have a One-to-One with a New Member

  • Show them how to conduct an intentional One-to-One.
  • Set up a meeting between the new member and the other member they most want to meet.
  • Ask for their Bragging Point, Key Situation, and Golden Goose Referral Partner.

Have a One-to-One with a Huge Giver

  • Schedule a One-to-One with the member who passes the most referrals.
  • Ask them who and what they look for to be able to give so many referrals.
  • Ask for their Bragging Point, Key Situation, and Golden Goose Referral Partner.

Keep Them Top of Mind

For 30 days after these One-to-Ones, post the Bragging Point, Key Situation, and Golden Goose Referral Partner for each of these members in your home or office. Review them before every call or meeting. Tell everyone you can about them. Do everything you can to become a Creator for them.

Helpful Reports

  • Membership Roster
    PALMS Report
  • GAINS Exchange

Share your own One-to-One success stories in the comments.

Complete Transcript of Episode 752

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, this week, I am at a BNI meeting in Charlotte, our global support headquarters. And I have been Zooming around the world still, recently in Taiwan and other places. So it’s great to have back on the BNI Podcast Shirley Towne for her fourth visit, and we’re going to talk a little bit more about One-to-Ones. I mentioned last week that Shirley has been in BNI since 2014 representing ADT, and she’s been both the president of a chapter of BNI, and is now the Executive Director of BNI, since 2018. And she has increased the membership in the region from 400 members to over 850. So Shirley, it’s great to have you back on BNI Podcast.

Shirley:
Yeah, happy to be here.

Ivan:
Not too many people have been on four times. Congratulations!

Shirley:
I know, I know. That’s exciting.

Ivan:
So tell me about the two types of members that you want to meet with and why.

Shirley:
All right, sweet. So I had somebody coach me on this and the more I started thinking about it, the more they gave me, like one suggestion, then it led to the other suggestion. They said, you know, the very first thing you need to do is have a One-to-One with every new member. Because, you know, selfishly and giving, but selfishly, there are a whole new set of contacts. You know, they have their own family, they have their own co-workers, they have their own networks. And it’s a whole new world that you could become a part of. But you know, the giving part is, we need to have an intentional One-to-One with them to kind of coach them on how to have one. So I said earlier, I went a whole year without having effective One-to-Ones. It’s my goal for our members to get an effective One-to-One right off the bat so they know how to have them. And so that’s really the big thing about that. And then also, too, I think new members need a quick win.

So you know, the retention of our new members is always a focus of ours. And I don’t think there’s a better way than to make them feel really comfortable, and to introduce them. One of the things I love to do with a new member is to show them the roster and say, “Hey, who on this roster would you like to meet? I’ll be glad for the three of us to have lunch together next week.” And then I kind of help them through that One-to-One and ask some questions to kind of get that going. So they can get some really good referral partners in the room right off the bat.

Ivan:
Shirley, I’m going to ask you a question that we didn’t talk about. So sit back in your chair, hopefully it’ll be an easy answer for you. Tell me something about security, because you have worked with ADT home security for many years. What’s something that’s like really obvious that most people just don’t get about home security?

Shirley:
Okay, so my passion with home security is the fire part of it. It’s not what everybody thinks about a security system. So I am passionate about making sure that we put heat detectors in the attic. And that will save a home if a fire starts in the attic. So my passion is fire.

Ivan: This is a perfect example. Heat detectors, fire system — probably one of the biggest problems I’m guessing.

Shirley:
Yes.

Ivan:
Okay, so this is one of those things where it’s obvious to you. But not obvious to your clients.

Shirley:
Correct.

Ivan:
Okay, One-to-Ones are so incredibly obvious to us, but not always obvious to the new member. And what you’re talking about are things that are so obvious to us that if you don’t do what Shirley is talking about, you are not going to have success in BNI. So Shirley’s got a couple of other thoughts and pay attention. Whether you’ve been around for a long time and haven’t done One-to-Ones, or whether you’re new in BNI, pay attention. And if you’ve been in BNI and you’ve done One-to-Ones, comment about why these are so important. Anything else on that first step before we talk about reports?

Shirley:
Yeah, you know, you just made me think of something else that is a huge deal. I never thought about this, but I was meeting with a financial advisor and he was like, “You know, every time I meet with a spouse that has lost their significant other, typically a female that’s lost a male, they want a security system.” And he’s right. Like, that was a huge part of my business, but I’ve never talked about it. And he said, “I am going to just say this, ‘Hey, you know, when you lose your spouse, sometimes the icemaker makes a noise that you’ve never heard before, because you don’t have somebody sleeping beside you. I just want you to know, if you are uneasy, and you would like a security system, I have somebody that will take extra special care of you.'” I in no way would have ever thought to ever think of that. And he literally transformed what I asked for from that point on, because that is one of the segments of my market that I love to help.

Ivan:
And that’s what we’re trying to do with our members on One-to-Ones here.

Shirley:
Absolutely.

Ivan:
It’s to get you to think about the things that maybe you hadn’t thought about before. Anything else on the first step to figure out the two types of members before we talk about reports?

Shirley:
Yeah, the next one is the second type of member that you want to meet with is a huge giver in BNI. So you figure out from the summary PALMS report, the Thank You For Closed Business, who the huge givers are in the room. Most of us know who those are but have a One-to-One with them. Most people automatically think I want to have a One-to-One with a huge giver, because I want a piece of the pie that they’re giving. But I actually take a little bit of a different point to it. I want to pick their brain as to who they’re giving to: How are they giving to them? What do they say? What key situations do they look for? What kind of golden goose referral partners are they referring? And so you kind of get the opportunity to learn from the best of the best, right?

Ivan:
Yeah.

Shirley:
So they’re teaching you how to give and if you learn how to give, it could open up a huge referral partner for you.

Ivan:
Yeah, great suggestion. Really good suggestion. There are a couple of reports that people should look at in terms of One-to-Ones. You want to talk about them?

Shirley:
Absolutely. So the very first report is the chapter roster. It’s going to show you a few stats, but it’s going to give you the [business] classification of every new person. But this is where I want you to evaluate your members. Are they an Advocator, or are they a Promoter, or are they a Creator for you? And then on the other side of the name, I want you to write what you are to them. So I’m going to look and I’m going to say okay, Priscilla. Priscilla has given me one to two referrals in a six-month period, she’s an Advocator for me. I’m giving Priscilla six to eight in a six-month period, I’m a Creator for Priscilla. I bring her up in conversation whenever I possibly can. So I want to do that to figure out who I want to have One-to-Ones with. If I’ve got an uneven relationship, I want to learn what I need to do to fix that. But I’m looking for who is a huge giver, and who is a brand-new person in my chapter.

And every month, I’m going to pick those two people and I’m going to focus on them for 30 days, I’m going to fill out the GAINS exchange, I’m going to fill out the three add-ons: the bragging point, the key situation, and obviously, most importantly perhaps, the golden goose referral partner. And I’m going to make three copies. And Ivan, this sounds really, really nerdy, but hang on with me. I’m going to put one where I brush my teeth; I’m going to put one on my desk, and one in my car.

And before I meet with any client, I’m going to read over it. And by the end of 30 days, so I tell my people when I have a One-to-One with them, “For 30 days, you’re my focus. What can I do for you on social media? How can I love on you? What can I do to help you? Is there somebody in the chapter I can introduce you to? Who are your golden goose referral partners?” I’m getting all that information. For 30 days, I focus on that person, just with the utmost intentionality.

And then at the end of 30 days, I’m going to pick two more people, a brand-new person and a huge giver; every month I’m picking two people. At the end of six months, I’m promoting and creating for six brand new members and six huge givers. The last thing we ever want is a huge giver to leave because they’re not receiving. So I always ask them, “How is BNI to you?” And so that’s the second part of the huge giver.

Ivan:
All right. Now I know that Priscilla has a question about the three types of people that you were talking about. I want to make sure that we have time for that; we’re almost running out of time. Can you tell me about the summary PALMS pretty quickly and then the international One-to-Ones?

Shirley:
Okay, so the summary PALMS is where you’re going to get the Thank You For Closed Business from. So it’s going to show the referrals that have been given to that person and you know, or the referrals that person has given and how they’ve turned it into Thank You For Closed Business. So that’s where you figure out who your big givers are. And then you want to look at the P for present under the PALMS to figure out who the new people are in your chapter depending, you know, if you don’t know that right off, or if you are a new person. New people can meet with new people, that’s still effective. So that’s really what we’re looking for there.

Ivan:
And international One-to-Ones.

Shirley:
So, you know, this doesn’t just stop in your backyard and it could be across the country, and then it can be in a different country. How many times have we heard about some people making contacts, and they’ve just gone crazy with their business? There are all sorts of situations out there where you can have an intentional One-to-One, you know, somebody that might be a golden goose referral partner, in another country.

Ivan:
Yeah. Excellent. And, Priscilla, you had a quick question for Shirley about the types of people she was referring to.

Priscilla:
Right. I was just reviewing my notes and I forgot what the difference is between the Advocator and the Creator.

Shirley:
Okay, so there’s three. So the Advocator waits for the phone to ring, and you’re literally just advocating for them. So somebody calls and they ask for a power washer, and you’re like, “Yeah, I know a guy. Here’s a bragging point, here’s his information.” So they called you. You didn’t work very hard for it; it could still be a great referral.

The second one is the Promoter. The Promoter waits for the key situation to happen in order to bring you up in conversation. So we train our folks what their key situations are — a storm comes through town. I once knew of a jewelry lady that gave $751,000 worth of roofs, because a storm came through her neighborhood. So that’s the kind of thing that we’re talking about with a Promoter.

And then lastly, there’s the Creator. The Creator just can go into a room, they could be in a dinner party, and they just start talking about the guy that did their taxes. So that’s just a random one. But you just know, like, and love that person so much that you don’t need anything to happen in order for you to bring them up in conversation.

Priscilla:
Right. Okay, good.

Ivan:
Excellent. Shirley, I know a lot of this material is up on [BNI] Business Builders and I urge members of BNI to go there. Is there any last thought? We’re out of time. Any last thought before we wrap?

Shirley:
You can get started today and you will impact your chapter forever. Every single month, pick two people – brand new person and a huge giver. Start today. Why would you wait? Be intentional about your One-to-Ones. Don’t just “date” anybody; pick who you want to ask out on a One-to-One and make it count.

Ivan:
Excellent. Shirley, thanks again for being back on BNI Podcast. I appreciate you.

Shirley:
My pleasure.

Ivan:
Back to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, thank you. That’s it for this week. Great information from both of you. Thank you so much. This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience and help you boost your business. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and use the promo code IVAN50 [this code is good for a limited time] for 50% off all of the audio programs. And the proceeds are all going to the BNI Foundation. So thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Pass more referrals by scheduling an intentional One-to-One with a new member and a huge giver every month.

title.rendered: Episode 751: An Intentional One-to-One

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Shirley Towne returns to the podcast this week to explain how conducting intentional one-to-ones can dramatically increase referrals.

First, review the chapter member roster and identify your referral partner level for each person.

  • Advocator: Refers you when someone asks for a referral.
  • Promoter: Volunteers referral information when they hear about a key situation.
  • Creator: Reaches out to their network to promote you without waiting for prompting.

The idea is to learn how you can become a Creator for them and show them how they can become a Creator for you.

Start with the GAINS Exchange and ask for three more things:

  1. Bragging Point: Two short sentences to use when describing the member’s services. Knowing this helps you become an Advocator.
  2. Key Situation: What is the trigger event that causes people to need their services? Knowing this helps you become a Promoter.
  3. Golden Goose Referral Partner: What person or profession could pass them endless referrals? Knowing this helps you become a Creator.

Episodes Mentioned Today

  • Episode 5: The GAINS Exchange
  • Episode 208: Behind the GAINS Profile
  • Episode 404: Standing in the Middle of Referrals

Complete Transcript of Episode 751

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am still Zooming all over the world. I’ve been to Mexico; I’ve been to the United Kingdom. I’ve been to Brazil. I did an Inner Circle for U.S. Core Members, which are the [BNI] regions run by [BNI] Global Support. And I took a vacation for about 10 days. So you know, I’m allowed a little bit of time off. I went to Utah for a vacation. So things are fantastic. And it’s great to be doing these podcasts. You know, it’s been 15 years, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Really? Wow, that is amazing.

Ivan:
Well, I have a guest with me today. This is her third appearance on BNI podcast. Her name is Shirley Towne, and Shirley joined BNI to represent ADT. And I think I told you this, Shirley — my home security service [is ADT]. I’ve used them for many years.

Shirley:
We appreciate that.

Ivan:
Yes, well, they’ve done a great job. And she joined BNI in 2014. She was looking for BNI to replace the leads that she was paying for – $5,000 a month to the Yellow Pages. You’ve got to check the previous podcasts. I don’t know what they are right off the top of my head. But you know, the theme for this one, as I recall was “If BNI cost you $5,000 a month, how would you treat it differently?” [BNI Podcast Episode #730] And I think that’s just brilliant. So she became [BNI Chapter] President in 2016, Executive Director in 2018. And she’s grown her region from 400 members to over 850. And it is fantastic to have you back on the BNI Podcast, Shirley.

Shirley:
Thank you. I’m happy to be here.

Ivan:
So we’re going to cover a little bit about an intentional One-to-One. And I love this topic. So what should one focus on in terms of the GAINS Exchange, and some game changers, in an intentional One-to-One, Shirley?

Shirley:
So the GAINS Exchange is really what we all consider kind of the outline of what the BNI One-to-One should be. I’m embarrassed to tell you, Ivan, I went an entire year without knowing what the GAINS Exchange was. And I was about to leave BNI because I was not being very effective in what I was receiving. And somebody said you’re not doing a good job at One-to-Ones, or you would definitely be getting more business. And so they sat me down and talked about the GAINS Exchange and I never heard about it before. And it was a game changer. So I did not leave in that first year. I only closed one deal in my first year. And in my second year I closed 68.

Ivan:
Wow.

Shirley:
And I really believe it was all about getting intentional about One-to-Ones. The GAINS Exchange – there’s so much good stuff in there. But it’s just you know, I want to know your Goals. I want to know, what you want to get and how I can help you achieve those. I want to know what you’ve Accomplished. Because honestly, I gotta be able to brag about you in order to get you the introduction if somebody needs your goods and services. Interests, we all connect on interests; like we do that naturally. But to do it intentionally just makes it another level. And then Networks, this is one I’ve really learned a lot about. You know, if I go to a bridal show as an exhibitor, who all can I refer that day? And if you get intentional about that you can really go next level. Networks — you know, huge deal. And then obviously Skills. What are you skilled at? What differentiates you from other folks? ALL good stuff that, if you talk about, you’re definitely going to become a better referral partner for each other. What I found though, is we spend so much time doing One-to-Ones where we just get to know each other, that we don’t ever land the plane. Does that make sense?

Ivan:
Well, it does. That’s the reason I did the GAINS Exchange because it ended up becoming, you know, kind of chit-chatty as opposed to focusing on some things where you can find overlapping areas of interest.

Shirley:
Yes, 100%. And as soon as I got that, I’m going to say ‘intentionality’ about the One-to-One, it transformed my BNI experience. And then I really went to school with it and really studied everything I could get my hands on about power teams. And at first, we were like, spending so much time trying to figure power teams out. And then I’m like, you know, there’s some stuff that we could just incorporate into our One-to-Ones that kind of come from power teams. And why wouldn’t we?

And so those are the three add-ons that I talk about. One kind of overlaps with Accomplishments. It is, you know, just your bragging point. But it’s more concise, in that if somebody asked for your service on social media, give me two sentences of what you want me to say; hopefully getting you access to get in front of that person. So the bragging point is just two clear, concise statements.

Ivan:
And for those of you who want more information on the GAINS Exchange, you can go all the way back to Episode #5, that’s right, FIVE, back in 2007, the GAINS Exchange. And Episode #208, and I talk about the GAINS Exchange there.

Now, Shirley, tell me about the three levels of referral partners: Advocators, Promoters, and Creators. This is new to me, tell me about it.

Shirley: Yeah, so I actually learned this from another podcast where Tim Roberts talks about this in the BNI Power of One Podcast, #248. (Available to BNI Members in BNI Business Builder.)

Ivan:
I forgot about that, give me the numbers again.

Shirley:
BNI Power of One Podcast 248.

Ivan:
Got it.

Shirley:
So this is the next thing that really elevated my experience in BNI. He really just talks about three levels of referral partners. The Advocator. And so the Advocator, you call them up and you ask them, I need a power washer, and they give the information. They’re willing to advocate for you, just like it sounds. The Promoter waits for a key situation in order to bring you up in conversation. So the key situation is a trigger, it could be somebody getting married, somebody having a baby, a loss of a loved one, somebody’s buying or selling real estate, a storm comes through town, who could benefit from a storm coming through town — four or five different BNI members. So the Promoter waits for the key situation to happen. And then lastly, the Creator. The Creator is the status that we all want to get to. This person knows, likes and loves, and trusts you so much, that they’re just going to open up their network to you, and really make an effort to bring you in. Not waiting for the phone to ring, not waiting for a key situation, just making it happen all on their own.

So this will often involve an introduction to what we call a golden goose referral partner. I can give an insurance agent one referral, or I can hook them up with a mortgage person that can refer to them continuously, and vice versa. And they eat for life instead of just for one day. And so it’s kind of like that old adage, we always say, give a person a fish and they eat for a day, kind of the same thing. So those are the three add-ons that we kind of like to do to take it next level. So you know, the Advocator or the Promoter or the Creator. You’ve got to decide what you want to be for people in your chapter. And then you’ve got to figure out how to coach them to get above Advocator status to become a Promoter, and then maybe one day become a Creator.

Ivan:
Excellent. So in evaluating where you are with your members in your chapter, where do you want to go from that? Let’s talk a little bit about, you know, how you can transform your BNI experience with this.

Shirley:
Yeah, so the first thing you gotta do is print a copy of your roster and kind of figure out where you are with people. Do you advocate for them? Do you promote for them? Or do you create for them? The other thing you’ve got to do is flip it around. Do they advocate for you? Do they promote for you? Do they create for you? So it’s both sides — what are you to them and what are they to you? And that’s the first step in figuring out the next step. The next step we’re going to cover in another podcast. But this is really the first step to kind of figure out where you are right now.

Ivan:
Excellent. And yeah, we’re gonna have a follow up podcast to this next week. And we’ll come back again next week for more information on this. Because I think One-to-Ones are so important. And we discovered this, and you may not know this, Shirley, we discovered this actually at BNI conferences, where Directors were getting together and they had spare time. And so we started doing these One-to-Ones and they worked so well, we thought, “Gee, why aren’t we incorporating these into BNI meetings?” And that was back in 1990.

Shirley:
Nice. Oh, my goodness. I never knew that. That’s amazing.

Ivan:
Yeah, it came from doing Conferences in the U.S. where we transitioned it to doing One-to-Ones with members. And the GAINS Exchange came out of a book I wrote back in the 90s, called “Business by Referral,” that was where it first came out. It’s now been in many of my books. Alright, we’ve got a couple more minutes. Tell me about the reticular activating system in our brains and how this relates to One-to-Ones.

Shirley: Yeah, absolutely. So when I have a One-to-One with somebody, I’m trying to figure out everything I can about them to really be able to refer to them. And so how many of you have ever purchased a vehicle and you drive it off the lot, and you realize that everybody has the same make and model and color that you now have? But you never saw one before you purchased it.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Shirley:
That is actually your reticular activator working. It’s now on your radar. So you start paying attention to it. When you have a One-to-One and you learn all these little tidbits about the person that you’re wanting to become a referral partner with, it’s the same thing. You drive off the lot, you leave that One-to-One, and you go out into the world and you start looking for opportunities for them. You’re looking for their make model and the color of the “car” that they need. And that is really just making somebody really on your radar. And I like to pick, and we’ll talk about this next week, but I like to pick one or two people every month and making them my primary focus. And that allows you to get that level of focus where you’re looking for their make, model, and color.

Ivan:
Yeah, great information. And we’re gonna have another session, with Shirley next week on BNI podcast. I’d like to refer everyone to Episode #404, which is all about the reticular activating system. So go listen to 404 after you listen to this episode, and you’ll get a lot more information on how powerful the reticular activating system is. Shirley, thank you very much for being on the BNI Podcast. I appreciate you.

Shirley:
My pleasure.

Ivan:
All right, back to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, that’s it for this week, Dr. Misner, and thank you so much, Shirley, that was some great information. This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience and help you boost your business. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and use the promo code IVAN50 [this code is good for a limited time] for 50% off all of the programs. All of these proceeds are going to go to the BNI Foundation. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Learn how conducting intentional one-to-ones and adding 3 steps to the GAINS Exchange can dramatically increase referrals.

title.rendered: Episode 750: Competing in the New World of Work

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Kian Gohar, co-author of Competing in the New World of Work, shares three keys to success in a hybrid work environment and how they apply to BNI.

Create Emotional Connectivity with Colleagues

Apply this principle during the Open Networking part of your BNI meeting and during One-to-Ones.

  • Spend the majority of your time in breakout groups of 3-4 people
  • Have each member answer the question “What is sweet and sour in your life?” (What’s going well and what isn’t.)

Maximize Collaboration with Asynchronous Discussions

This can be a great tool for your BNI chapter’s leadership team and membership team, or for members of a power team, as well as within your own business.

  • Create a shared document and write down what the problem is, what potential solutions to it can be, and who should be involved in this conversation.
  • Give your team members a week to add their thoughts to the document.
  • Call a meeting to discuss the specifics.

Maximize Your Team’s Resilience

BNI chapters have had to weather some pretty big changes in the past two years. It’s important for the leadership team to know how members are handling it and to support them.

  • Ask your team members to rate their energy level from 1 to 10.
  • Enforce taking breaks so team members can recharge their energy.
  • Model providing support when a member has lower resilience than usual.
  • Even when things are tough, remember to point out what’s going well.

Learn more about Kian’s book and get your own copy at www.leadersguide.org.

Complete Transcript of Episode 750

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you doing today? And where are you?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic. And I’m home in Austin this week, and today I happen to be with a mutual friend of my guest, Sam Horn. Sam is a wonderful lady. She wrote the books, “POP!” and “SOMEDAY Is Not a Day in the Week.” And we do Writing Wednesdays, which honestly are more like “talking” Wednesdays to be truthful, because we talk about writing a lot. And so she’s a good friend of both myself and Kian.

My guest today is Kian Gohar. He is the Founder and CEO of Geolab, an innovation research and leadership training firm in Los Angeles. He’s a co-author of the bestselling Harvard Business Review book, “Competing in the New World of Work,” with his co-author, also a friend of mine, Keith Ferrazzi. I’ve been to a couple of Keith’s parties that he talks about in “Never Eat Alone.” And, just for everybody in BNI, BNI is mentioned on pages 131 and 132 of their new book, “Competing in the New World of Work.” I highly recommend the book; please go out and get it.

Kian is a former executive director for the XPRIZE Foundation and has coached CEOs and leadership teams of Fortune 500 companies for many years. Kian, I think I’ve told you this, but, at a TLC, most of my BNI members, I’ve talked about TLC, they’re probably familiar with it – The Transformational Leadership Council, at a TLC event you did a presentation called “Your Moonshot.” You know, what’s your BIG idea that you want to get to? And in January of 2020, Kian, and I think I’ve told you this, in January of 2020 I said, “I think BNI needs to really be thinking about moving online as much as possible, because that is the wave of the future.” In January of 2020! Well, after that TLC meeting, guess what happened? BNI moved 10,000 groups, not because of technology, which I thought it was going to be, but because of COVID, we moved 10,000 groups to online. So your moonshot concept became a reality for me.

Kian:
I love to hear this. And I’m so grateful that I had a little bit of insight to share with you that allowed you to move mountains and go online. And now you’re able to connect with your network so much more broadly using digital tools and I’m so grateful for that.

Ivan:
Yes. Well, it’s great having you here on BNI Podcast. Welcome to the Podcast. You have three points that you wanted to talk about. One was to create emotional connectivity with colleagues when working in an hybrid environment, which is exactly what BNI is doing. Talk about that a little bit.

Kian:
Yeah. So we know that the future of work is going to be hybrid, and whether some of us go back to the office or some of us work virtually, we still have to manage how to lead teams in a virtual and hybrid environment. And it’s really hard to do that when you are constantly on Zoom. And maybe sometimes we haven’t even worked with people face-to-face yet, but you’ve been working with them remotely. And so I recommend doing two things to create emotional connectivity with your colleagues in a hybrid setting. Whenever you have a virtual meeting, let’s say 60 minutes, devote only the first 10 or 15 minutes to a larger session, and then immediately go into a breakout. Spend the majority of your time in breakout sessions. And these breakouts are going to be groups of three or four people maximum. And the whole idea is that you create a smaller group where people have psychological safety to share their opinions and stop self-censoring themselves in conversations that they might ordinarily have in larger meetings.

And so the first step is to go immediately to small breakouts of three or four people. And then ask them to ask each other this very simple question. What is sweet and sour in your life? And what sweet means is something that’s going really well in your life, whether personally or professionally, and something that’s sour or something that isn’t going as you had hoped. And the whole point of this really basic question is to allow people to get to know each other in these smaller virtual breakout rooms of three or four people so that they understand the context of what’s going on in their lives. And  even if you don’t get to talk to them at the watercooler, you can better understand what’s going on in their day-to-day on an everyday basis.


Ivan:
That’s a great suggestion. For those of you listening to this, let me put this in a BNI context. This would be a great thing to do before a BNI meeting starts, in the Open Networking with breakouts, ask this question. It’s also great for One-to-Ones. And guess what? The next two weeks’ sessions that we’re going to be doing on BNI Podcasts are all about One-to-Ones. So apply some of these concepts that Kian is talking about here to the One-to-Ones that you’re doing. Kian, so how do you maximize team collaboration? Oh, there is one other thing. We you’re doing meetings with your Power Teams, do what Kian is suggesting here. That’s a great concept. So Kian, how do you maximize team collaboration and innovation in a post-pandemic world?

Kian:
Yeah, so I think the pandemic made us reassess all of our assumptions about how we live and socialize and work. But it didn’t reassess our assumptions of how we collaborate. And there is this myth that we collaborate by first calling a meeting and everybody get everybody into a meeting room and decide how to innovate and collaborate. That’s a myth. So I want to tell your BNI listeners, if you want to maximize collaboration, don’t start with a meeting first. Instead, actually create an asynchronous meeting. And what that means is, you create a document on the cloud, whether it’s on Google Docs, or SharePoint, or wherever service you use. And on this document, I want you as the team leader, or the problem owner, to write down what the problem is, what potential solutions to it can be, and who should be involved in this conversation, to bring more ideas to the table. And then I want you to share this cloud document to your team and give them a week to ideate individually on this document and add all their thoughts to it.

And then you, as a team owner, can see the whole universe of what the various problem sets are that you’re trying to solve for. Circle that and say, “Okay, let’s call a meeting around this particular problem that I hadn’t thought of.” And so to maximize innovation and collaboration, start first with an asynchronous meeting, identify all the various problems sets, then team out to your individual team, and then even beyond your team, to potential partners and vendors, to get more ideas into how do you get the best ideas into this particular concept. And then once you know what they are, then call a meeting, whether that’s virtual or hybrid or in-person. So the most powerful teams of the pandemic era started with an asynchronous collaboration mindset first. And just because we’re going back into this new world of work that’s partially hybrid, partially in person, don’t go back to the old ways of how you did work, use these tools of asynchronous collaboration to get the best ideas first.

Ivan:
So let me put this in BNI context again, for those of you listening. If you’re on the Leadership Team, or you’re on a Membership Committee, what Kian just suggested is perfect for collaborating on issues relating to your chapter. In a Power Team, these are things that are perfect. And of course, what the book is primarily focused on is working within your company. And so these suggestions certainly apply within your business context. But you can also apply these concepts within a BNI context, through your Leadership Teams, your Membership Committees and your Power Teams. Okay, great suggestions, Kian. So how do you make sure your team is fully resilient to adapting to the future? And here, remember everyone, this book was written with a sense of your business, you as an entrepreneur. And we’ll try and make some of this fit within BNI, but a lot of this that Kian is talking about is with you as an entrepreneur. Kian.

Kian:
Yeah, so we interviewed over 2000 entrepreneurs and executives over the last few years to crowdsource all the best practices of all the teams from around the world who really succeeded. And that’s where the research from this is coming that we did with Harvard Business School on this book. And in terms of maximizing your team resilience. Obviously, we all come to work with different energy levels. Some days, you have a lot more energy levels, because maybe you have different financial or personal resources, and others have more social support. And so we come to work every single day with different levels of energy. And as a team leader, as a business owner, it’s your responsibility to identify what your team’s energy level is as a whole. And not just think that resilience is an individual activity; your responsibility is really to the entire team.

So the first step is to diagnose your team’s resilience level on a consistent basis so you know where the baseline is. So once every two weeks or once every three weeks, just do a really simple survey, whether it’s on Zoom or whether it’s on virtual, and ask your team to rate from 1 to 10, 1 being low, 10 being high, what their level of personal resilience is. And you’ll see a baseline over the course of the next few weeks and months. Let’s say everybody’s like a five or six. And then sometimes one person says, “Hey, I’m a two.” Now you know there’s something going on in that person’s life so then you can try to figure out how you can unblock that. And the next thing is really for the leader, and the entrepreneur who’s leading this team, to role model, that behavior of how you want to see the team practice. And that sometimes means enforcing breaks and trying to really create a space for individual resilience so you can recharge your energy levels. So that way, when you are trying to solve the problem as a team, you’re able to do that and support each other and cross the finish line together, even though sometimes we come to work with different levels of energy. So building these resilient teams is really critical to make sure that we can power through and succeed incredible goals, even if we are coming to work with different levels of energy on an individual basis.

Ivan:
So what Kian is talking about here actually applies to an entire BNI chapter really, really well. Surveying a chapter on how things are going is a very effective technique. And Kian, you may not know this, but Mark McKergow, who wrote the book, “The Solutions Focus,” he’s a past TLC member, one of the things he likes to do when he does a survey and somebody comes in with a low score, you know – out of 10, I’d give it a 4,  one of the techniques that he uses, which I think is brilliant, is he says to somebody, “Why so high?” And they’re like, what? that’s low. Yeah, but why did you give it a four instead of a three or a two or a one? What’s being done, right, that you do like? And then you get people focusing on what’s working within the group. And then when you get people focusing on what’s working, you can really achieve success much easier. I don’t know if you’ve ever heard that, but check out his book, Kian, and BNI Members, check out his book, “The Solutions Focus” [by] Mark McKergow.

Kian:
I love that example. I’ll definitely look.

Ivan: 
Yeah, it’s a great, great example; Mark was a great member of TLC. Alright, so this one applies not only to you in your business, but also within a BNI chapter. It’s time to wrap up, Kian, so can give us quick two or three business lessons you’ve learned from the last two years of pandemic?

Kian:
Well, I would just say, you know, we’re lucky that we have an opportunity to take all the lessons from the last couple of years and not go back to the way things were. We’re never going to go back to work the way it was. So we have the opportunity to learn and create a new world of work, which is what the research and this book is all about. And thank you so much, Ivan, for having me today on this show to share some of these lessons with your audience and BNI.

Ivan:
Truly my pleasure. And the book that we’re talking about, the material that we’re talking about, is from the book, “Competing in the New World of Work.” It’s written by Kian Gohar, Keith Ferrazzi, and Noel Weyrich, you want to pronounce his last name for me, Kian?

Kian:
Way-rich

Ivan: 
Way-rich. I highly recommend a book. I got an advanced copy. It is fantastic. You can get information on the book, of course, at bookstores, Amazon, and there’s a website, www.leadersguide.org. That’s www.leadersguide.org. Any last thought before we wrap?

Kian:
I am excited for this new opportunity and of the future. And I really invite your audience to reconsider the assumptions that made them succeed over the last few years and how they can continue to reassess those so that they’re always keeping up with constant change.

Ivan:
Good advice, Kian. Thank you very much. And thanks for being on BNI Podcast.

Kian:
Thank you so much for your time and friendship.

Ivan:
My pleasure. Back to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, perfect. Thank you both so much for the great information. This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience and help you boost your business. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50, [this code is good for a limited time] and you can get 50% off of all of the audio programs. And all of the proceeds are going to go to the BNI Foundation (www.bnifoundation.org). Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

excerpt.rendered: Kian Gohar shares 3 keys to success in a hybrid work environment and how to use them to build a better BNI chapter.

title.rendered: Episode 749: How Do You End a Conversation? (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 595, which was published on February 13, 2019.

Synopsis

How do you end a conversation at a networking meeting? Here are four techniques.

  1. Say “It was really nice meeting you. Do you have a card so I can have your contact information? Thanks.” This is the simplest option and the one Dr. Misner uses most often.
  2. Recap the part of the conversation you liked most, and then use #1. Ask permission to write a note on the back of the card.
  3. If they say something that makes you think of someone else at the event, make the introduction on the spot.
  4. Invite them to go to another networking event that you go to–like your BNI meeting.

Keep it simple. Keep it honest. Whatever you do, don’t Seinfeld it. Be polite and friendly. Don’t make excuses. The real key is how you follow up.

Tell us how you end your networking conversations by leaving a comment at the end of this podcast.

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Complete Transcript of Episode 749 (595)

Priscilla: 
Hello and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today with the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer, Dr Ivan  Misner. . Hello, Ivan. How are you today?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic, Priscilla, and I think we should mention that we are using a new recording system, and I hope everyone likes it. I believe it will level out my piece of the conversation. So we would love to hear. Last week’s podcast and this week’s podcast are on the new system, so we would love to hear what you guys think.

Priscilla:
So what is this topic that you have?

Ivan:
The topic is how do you end a conversation? As the Founder of BNI, I often get asked about the best way to end a conversation in a networking situation. Candidly, I really think the answer is pretty simple. So I am going to start this podcast with the simple solution. Then, for those of you who love to overthink things, I will give you some other options.

Here are four techniques: One, say something simple like, “It was really nice. Meeting you. Do you have a card so I can have your contact information? Thanks.” That’s it. No fuss, no muss, no big deal. Don’t apologize because you have to go network with somebody else. Definitely don’t say you see someone else you need to talk to. Those are the things that make it feel awkward. Simply thank them very much and move on. 

I was doing an interview not long ago, before you give you two, three and four. It was with someone body who knew me through BNI. I told him that and he said, “Oh my goodness. You did that to me. It was so smooth. I never thought about it. I felt like you listened to me. We spoke. We had a conversation. It wasn’t really long, but I really felt like we had a conversation. Then you said those very words. ‘It was really nice meeting you. Do you have a card so I can have your contact information? Thanks a lot.’” He said it was really, really smooth. 

So this is where I say don’t overthink it. I really believe that this is the easiest of the four techniques and it is the one that I generally use because I am there to network. I do meet a lot of other people. Many times, they are BNI events and some of the BNI members want to meet me. So it is an effective technique. 

Here is number two: frame what you liked about the conversation or recap part of the conversation that you found most interesting and then do number one. I have done that a fair number of times as well because there may have been something the person said and I want to anchor it in my mind. I want to remember it. So I will ask them. Or I will talk to them about about it. “So let me just recap what you said. You said [this and that]. That was really amazing. Thank you for sharing that with me. It was a real pleasure meeting you. Can I have one of your cards?”

Now, what I may do in that case is I will say to them, “Is it okay if I write on the back of your card a note or two?” Now, this is important because I know that it is inappropriate to write on the back of cards in some cultures, particularly in Asian cultures, so I never do that in Asian countries. I carry a little notepad with me rather than write on the card. But in most countries, it is fine. Don’t even ask in [Asian] countries. But in most countries, where you know it is okay, ask if you can write on the back. It is one way that I can remember the conversation. That is number two.

Number three: if they say something that. Makes you think of someone else that they should meet, tell them. And tell them that you promise to make the introduction. If the other person is there at the event, make the introduction on the spot. Being a connector at a networking event is always a good thing. So that is another great way to end the conversation. 

Here is number four: invite them to participate with you at  another networking meeting that you go to. They may want to get out and meet more people. You stand a greater chance to connect to their network of individuals, particularly in BNI. Invite them to your BNI group. It gives you the chance to meet them again. “It was really interesting talking to you. I would love to chat with you again sometime. I am really active in another networking organization, BNI. I would love to invite you to come visit it. I would love to learn more about what you do.” That is a great technique.

Now, for those of you who want more, I have read all kinds of exit lines, and unless they are absolutely true, I don’t recommend most of them. Keep it simple. Keep it honest. If you want some of those exit lines, here are a few, but I don’t like them But I have read about them and they may resonate with you. Here is one that I read from another “expert” at networking:

“It was great meeting you. I have to get home by X:00 to have dinner with the family. I hope we connect again.” Then you step away, but you better leave the place because if you are there another 30 minutes talking to other people, it is the liar, liar pants on fire sort of excuse.

Here is another one: “It has been nice meeting you. I need to run to the restroom, but thanks for talking.” Again, if you do need to go, you better go. 

Here is another one that I read. Again, these are not things that I use. I don’t think they are that strong, but I have read them and I think some people might think they resonate with them. “I have a deadline on a project. I really need to take off, but it was great meeting you.” And leave. I honestly think that is too much information for people. Just tell them that it was nice meeting them. That’s great. 

So I have a couple more thoughts and then I would love to hear your thoughts on it, Priscilla. Those first four are what I recommend. The last three are okay, but don’t fib. Don’t make it up. If you really have to do something, tell them. Otherwise, simply doing what I say in the first one is great. Whatever you do, don’t Seinfeld it. That is a phrase I use in my latest book, Who’s In Your Room? Don’t Seinfeld it. One of the really funny things on the old TV series is Seinfeld and the characters and how they go off on some crazy, complicated rouse and end up in more trouble than if they had just been candid to start with. Be honest and be direct. Seinfelding it almost always fails and both you and the other person feel uncomfortable. 

Remember to keep it polite and friendly. Don’t make excuses. politely move on. The real key is how you follow up, it then that is a different podcast that I have done before, using the follow up after you network. So what do you think, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
I really like your first four examples and I don’t think that the last three that you brought up- it’s too much information. You don’t need to provide it to the person. You don’t have to be embarrassed that you need to end the conversation. And then you are stuck saying or having to do whatever excuse you used to get out of talking, which might not suit what you need to do next, which might be meet other people. So I am in agreement with you.

Ivan:
I have always found that ending the conversation is really not that complicated. I really feel that people overthink it. Obviously, I lean towards number one. However, if I have the chance to do particularly the one where I can connect them to somebody else in the room, I love that even more. I don’t always have that opportunity, not with everybody I meet. I would say that almost every networking opportunity that I go to, I do use that technique at least once. There is someone in the room who says something where I say, “Hey, I know somebody in the room that does that or that you might want to talk about that issue. Let me look around. Oh, they are right there. Would you like me to introduce you?” Then you walk them over. I think that is a fantastic technique. You just can’t do it to every single person you meet. 

Priscilla:
Right, right. 

Ivan:
So that is it. Now, what I would love for you to do is try a couple of these things out and come back to my podcast and tell me how they work. Or if you do some of them now, tell me how they work. Or even if you use those three that I said I don’t particularly like, if you use it and you like it, tell us specifically in the podcast how you use that in ending the conversation and how it has worked out for you. I am open to hearing the good, the bad and the ugly. Ending a conversation should be pretty easy if you mean what you say to the person you are talking to and you are polite and professional. That is everything that I have for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay. Great, Ivan. Thank you so much. For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: How do you end a conversation at a networking event? Thank the person, get their contact info, and make an introduction if you can.

title.rendered: Episode 748: The Willing Conversation

content.rendered:
Synopsis

To receive more effective referrals from the members of your BNI group, it’s really important to help them understand your business and your target market enough to identify a good referral. To do that, you need to make sure they know these four things.

  1. The Needs Assessment
  2. Your Unique Selling Proposition
  3. Why Are You in Business?
  4. What’s Your Emotionally Charged Connection?

Listen to Episode 451 and read “Why You Do What You Do Is Your Key to Success” for more about Emotionally Charged Connections.

What have you shared with your network that has helped you gain willing conversations with prospective customers? Share your answers in the comments.

Complete Transcript of Episode 748

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan: I am at home in Austin, Texas this week. And, you know, most of our podcasts, almost all of our podcasts, land a post on Wednesday morning. And many of you may not know this, but for the last couple of years, every Wednesday, I have Sam Horn come over in the morning. Sam wrote the book, “POP!” where she mentioned BNI, and that was before I knew Sam. And she also wrote the book, “Someday is Not a Day in the Week.” I talk about Sam and quote her in a number of places. She comes over every Wednesday morning after this podcast goes live. And we do what we call “Writing Wednesday.” But honestly, Priscilla, it’s oftentimes ‘talking’ Wednesday. We talk. We talk a lot about writing, but we talk a lot. And today’s topic is The Willing Conversation.

Priscilla:
Uh huh.

Ivan:
And so it’s about talking to people. Do you remember, Priscilla, when you were a kid how fun magnets were to play with?

Priscilla:
Yes.

Ivan:
Yeah, I loved magnets. And depending on which way you turn the magnet, they were either attracted or repelled by one another. And as an adult, we may find ourselves feeling six years old again when we make a phone call to a referral, who turns out to not be a referral at all. And similar to a magnet that’s turned the wrong direction, you’re not being embraced in that conversation, you’re being resisted. So the referral you were given that should have been a warm introduction quickly turns into a cold call.

We all want good referrals, people who want to talk to us. We want to give and receive referrals that are willing conversations about the products and services that we have to offer. And to receive more effective referrals from the members of your BNI group, it’s really important to help them understand your business and your target market enough to identify a good referral.

So here are four tips that you can follow that will help you with those willing conversations.

The first tip is The Needs Assessment. It’s really your responsibility to be very clear; it’s our responsibility – I’ll talk about it with me as well. It’s our responsibility to be very clear and specific with our referral partners about what constitutes a good referral. And this is a combination of an ideal prospect profile and the problems that we can solve for them. Because it’s not just about what you do, it’s about what solutions you have for the people that you’re working with. It’s about what Andy Bounds calls the AFTERs —what are things going to look like after you’ve worked with them.

So, the needs assessment really is an example of you clearly defining your target market. So for a corporate coach, as an example, you might say that a small to medium sized company with fewer than 100 employees, closely held business – family owned business, regional with locations, you know, local in a state or two, and that they as a coach provide themselves on higher than average retention, getting companies to have higher than average retention for employees. And they may be in a competitive industry and as an executive coach, you can help them gain an advantage. So, you know, you want to communicate your target market very clearly to the people in your BNI group.

Ivan:
You also want to describe your Unique Selling Proposition. So do you have dozens or hundreds of competitors in your marketplace? You probably do. And that means that your unique selling proposition, or USP, is really important because it allows you to stand out among your competition. Your USP is a brief description of the purpose of your business. It’s stated in the most concise and compelling way possible in order to help others understand the unique value of what it is that you do. So your unique selling proposition tells people the type of client you like to work with and the benefits that you can provide for them.

And what you want to say is what makes what makes you stand out and why that makes you stand out. What do you do that your competition can’t touch? At the very least, figure out what you do better than your rivals and go beyond simply saying ‘good customer service.’ That’s really part of your unique selling proposition – what it is that you have that your competition doesn’t.

The third thing is Why Are You in Business? What’s your passion? Why do you go to work? Unfortunately, one of the most popular answers to the question is ‘to make money.’ That’s the worst answer a business professional can ever give. You’ve got to be able to describe why it is that you do what it is that you do. Why are you in your profession? How do you change lives? You know, that’s what the referral partners in your business group, in your BNI chapter, that’s what they want to know. Remember, passion really is referable. You need to go deep and identify your why if you want to truly connect with people on a personal level.

The fourth thing is, what is your Emotionally Charged Connection? Your emotionally charged connection, or ECC, is a phrase leading to a story that your referral partners can recite when referring to you. So for those of you who want to know what my ECC is, what my emotionally charged connection is, go to episode number 451: Why Do You Do What You Do? And in that episode, I shared my emotionally charged connection and why that was so incredibly important to me, and why I do what it is that I do in business. It really stems from that podcast, 451, the experience that I have, from the podcast that I talk about.

We all have an ECC, it was something that happened to you, often during childhood, that lays the groundwork for who you are as a person. It can be positive; it can be negative. Many people are not consciously aware of their emotionally charged connection yet, it’s the reason that we get up in the morning and do the things that we do every day. It’s driven by the heart, not by the checkbook, or by the head; there’s a big difference. And I’m going to also include a link in here where you can read about my emotionally charged connection. I did a blog on the same topic and so I’ll have a link here for that blog as well. The bottom line is that the better you become at sharing the information in these tips that I’ve just given you with your BNI group, the more likely you are to feel like the magnet that attracts, instead of the magnet that repels. Your referral partners will be able to give you good referrals that lead to the willing conversation.

Well, I have a question for everyone when we’re done here. Priscilla, do you have any thoughts before we wrap up?

Priscilla:
No, but I haven’t heard this before and I really like what you’re talking about. I don’t remember hearing about the emotionally charged connection. Is that what it is?

Ivan:
Yeah, well, you were there for me. It was episode number 451.

Priscilla:
I just made a note. I’m gonna go back and listen to it.

Ivan:
Go back and listen to it. It was the story — you’ll remember it as soon as I give you a little heads-up. There’s a story about my teacher, Mr. Romero, and asking who wants to be on the student council.

Priscilla:
Aha, yeah, I remember that.

Ivan:
Yeah. And that was my emotionally charged connection. Because after that experience, which I’m not going to restate here on this podcast, go back and listen to 451. After that experience with this teacher, who, by the way, changed my life in many ways. After that experience, I realized many years later, that almost every business I’ve ever been drawn to and ever started was about inspiring people or providing people with an opportunity to be their best. And that’s really what BNI is, isn’t it? It’s about giving people an opportunity. You know, you joined BNI, you gotta work.

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
And I talked about this in the podcast, you know, in 451, it’s not like it was just a gift and I walked away and became successful. It was a gift that I had to work at. It gave me an opportunity, the gift was an opportunity, and that opportunity I had to work really hard at. And from that opportunity, I achieved the level of success in high school. And from that, I realized many, many years later, not at the moment, that that experience led me to try to give other people that experience, to try to give them an opportunity to achieve success. And that’s really what BNI is about. So, go listen to episode 451 everyone.

Priscilla:
Yeah, I plan to.

Ivan:
It’s a powerful story. It’s a true story. So for those of you who are listening to this podcast, let me ask you a question. I read all of the questions that get posted here, or comments that get posted here on BNI podcast.

My question is, what have you shared with your network that has helped you gain willing conversations with prospective customers? Share them here on the BNI podcast. That’s everything I’ve got, Priscilla, over to you.

Priscilla:
That was great. Thank you so much, Ivan. This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience and help you boost your business. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 and you’ll get 50% off all of the audio programs [this code is good for a limited time]. And all of those proceeds are going to the BNI Foundation. So thanks so much for listening.

This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: To receive more effective referrals from the members of your BNI group, help them understand your business and your target market.

title.rendered: Episode 747: The Power of One Conversation

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Pete Cullen from the Santa Cruz Business Builders chapter joins Dr. Misner this week to talk about the power of one conversation. One conversation, one invitation to a BNI meeting, spreads out like a ripple in a pond.

Pete’s BNI journey started with one conversation at a chamber of commerce mixer. At the end of that conversation, the woman he was talking to invited him to visit her BNI chapter. He felt so welcome at that meeting that he wanted to join, but his slot in the chapter he visited was already full, and the person in it was someone he knew.

So he had a conversation with that other realtor, and she suggested that he call the executive director for their area about starting his own chapter in Santa Cruz. And because that conversation went so well, that’s just what he did.

Because of the experience he had as a visitor, he always emphasized welcoming visitors in his chapter. He hosts a special visitor welcoming breakout room for online meetings during the general networking session.

How far has one conversation taken you in BNI? Share your answers in the comments.

Complete Transcript of Episode 747

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am so excited to tell you where I am this week. It has doesn’t have a lot to do with BNI. Although I’m going to try and do a BNI event while I’m there. I’m in the Los Angeles area. My eldest daughter is getting married. And I am so excited to walk my eldest daughter down the aisle, so I’m really happy.

Priscilla:
That’s great, you should be.

Ivan:
I have a guest on my podcast this week. Pete Cullen. Pete is a realtor and broker with Bailey properties in Santa Cruz, California. He’s an immediate past president of the Santa Cruz County Association of Realtors and served on the Board of Directors for the California Association of Realtors and friends of the Santa Cruz public libraries. Pete’s been a BNI member for over three years and he’s a founding member and past president of the BNI Santa Cruz Business Builders chapter which was launched in 2019. Pete’s a fan of local wines. Yes, Pete. You know, I’m a fan of wines. At this moment, at least, that’s still my banner on my Facebook page, my private Facebook page, my wine cellar. And so he’s a fan of local wines from Santa Cruz Mountain, which is known for growing and producing world class pinots. I love pinots! Chardonnay, I like chardonnays;  I don’t drink a lot of chardonnays, and Rhone style varietals. Pete started his personal growth journey by reading The Seven Habits of Highly Effective People. Pete, welcome to BNI Podcast.

Pete:
Thank you. It’s a pleasure to be on the podcast. I really appreciate the opportunity.

Ivan:
Yeah, truly my pleasure. And we’re going to talk today about the Power of One. Tell me about the power of one conversation and one invitation and the pebble in the pond dynamic that happens from that.

Pete:
So I had an opportunity to share this story at a BNI Leadership Summit in San Francisco. And it goes a little bit like this. I was at a business mixer, a chamber of commerce mixer, a young lady came up and introduced herself to me and we had a nice conversation. We had a chat. And at the end of that conversation, she said, you know, Pete, I’m part of this business networking group and we have breakfast every Wednesday morning. And I’d love to invite you to breakfast, would you like to come? And I said, “Absolutely.” So I got an opportunity to join her at her BNI Trendsetters [Chapter] meeting.

And, you know, I was just super impressed. I felt very welcomed at the meeting. And then when we actually sat down for the meeting, I was pretty much blown away by the structure, by the introductions,  by the whole way the meeting was run. And so after the meeting, we chatted it up a bit and they invited me back for a second week. I went back that second week and as I found out, there could only be one realtor in that group. And I actually knew the realtor, a local gal. And she said, “You know, Pete, you ought to think about starting your own BNI chapter.” And I thought, you know, I should think about that. So they said you need to talk to Ed Craine. He’s the [BNI] Executive Director, and he can give you some guidance on this. And so I remember it to this day. It was a November afternoon, I went and parked overlooking the ocean and got on the phone with Ed and we had an hour-long conversation. And it was just amazing how much we both resonated with each other. I’ve been in coaching with a real estate coaching company, Buffini & Company, for a number of years.

Ivan:
Oh, listen, can I just say, I love Brian Buffini. I highly recommend his program. I’ve been to his offices; Brian is a close associate. And I highly recommend him, particularly for real estate agents.

Pete:
Well, I count Brian among my mentors, as I count you among my mentors. And, you know, just to go back to that meeting a little bit, the very first meeting, you know, a few things that really stuck out to me were not only how structured it was, but the Toastmaster component – how people really had just honed their presentations, and then all the referrals that were being passed. So Ed and I came up with a plan to start a new chapter in Santa Cruz. And from the very get go, I really focused on culture. I focused on creating a welcoming culture for visitors, and really paying attention to the visitors when they walked in the room. And you know, it’s interesting, we’re in this Zoom world now. And we have, you know, there’s some hybrid Zoom stuff going on, we have our chapter going on with all Zoom. And one thing we found super valuable is to do a breakout room for visitors in the very beginning that I actually host as the past president and give them a little bit of a history of the chapter and overview of the chapter, and then set the expectations for what the meeting is going to be about.

Ivan:
Oh, can I just say, I love that idea. And I think that making a visitor feel welcome, is so important, particularly on Zoom, because, you know, it’s very hard to feel welcome on an online platform. And so that kind of personal attention is critical. I love it.

Pete:
Awesome. Well, we’ve had a great deal of success with that. And the difference with visitors that have come without the breakout room and with the breakout room is tremendous.

Ivan:
So just for people listening, Pete, let me ask you, how do you do this? Do you send them right into the breakout room when they come on before the meeting? Or how does it happen?

Pete:
Yes, yeah, we do actually; we pretty much put them right into the breakout room. What ended up happening, initially, was that everybody’s catching up on the Zoom call, oh, you know, I just got back from here, I’m doing this and the visitor feels kind of like left out and like, they’re not part of that conversation. So we put them right into the room as soon as they come in, I welcome them, I ask them to introduce themselves. Just, you know, what’s your business, and then I share with them sort of my BNI journey.

Ivan:
I think that’s brilliant; I would recommend that other chapters try it. And by the way, I also recommend that most chapters don’t do just the normal Open Networking, on a Zoom as you would in a live meeting. I would recommend that you do a lot of breakout rooms with the members, you know, maybe the first few minutes, but then move them into breakout rooms. Otherwise, you know, a handful of people basically control the Open Networking. But throwing visitors into one room where they get an overview is brilliant. I’ve never heard that idea. I love it.

Pete:
Awesome. Well, I appreciate that. And, as you know, inviting is critical, right? To chapter growth and to maintaining a healthy chapter. And you know, I get a lot of flack from my chapter members, because my mantra is invite, invite, invite. I just continue to drum it into everyone in our group because it’s just critical. And what’s amazing is out of this one conversation, and Nicola Balmain actually was the president of the Trendsetters [Chapter] at the time, and out of that one conversation, you know, we have created this incredible chapter. And when COVID hit, we really came together. But here’s a really just incredible story. I don’t know if you remember the CCU fires that hit Santa Cruz.

Ivan: Yeah, of course.

Pete:
About a year and a half [ago]. And you know, we lost 900 homes. But here’s what happened. We had a lot of our chapter members evacuated from their homes, and they were welcomed into other chapter members’ homes to stay during those fires.

Ivan:
Wow.

Pete:
And I mean, you talk about, you know, creating a culture, you talk about a really tight-knit group of people that really love each other, and it just, it showed up so powerfully with those fires. So yeah, I mean, I’m just such a huge advocate of BNI, Ivan. I look at the power of BNI with the resources that you guys provide us, the support you give us, and I am a student of leadership. You know, I’m committed to personal growth and professional development, as you talked about in the beginning. And I’m on a leadership journey, myself. And I have just found so much support throughout the whole BNI platform and the BNI family. It’s really a pretty incredible thing that you’ve put together.

Ivan:
Well, thank you. I appreciate that, Pete. I think BNI, it’s taken 37 years at this point, but BNI has reached the point where I had hoped that it could be someday, where we could provide content to people globally. You know, when I started BNI, everything was by telephone. And anything past about 20 miles was a long-distance phone call. And so, you know, the idea of having content online and the idea of having a podcast, or a blog, or training online was unheard of. And so we have continued over the last 37 years to add value and content to the membership to help make sure that people are more prepared in business and particularly in their networking. So I really appreciate your thoughts.

Any closing comments before we wrap up? I have a question. How can chapters leverage this message to grow their chapter?

Pete:
Well, I think it’s just really leaning into that invitation, that conversation, and really try and keep it top of mind. You know, one thing that I think we get out of our meeting Wednesday morning, and we’re on fire for a day or two, but then three or four days goes by, and that’s the last thing on our mind. So what one thing we’re doing is, we are actually touching base with our chapter members on Sunday night, just to give them a quick reminder and send them a link to the Zoom meeting.

Ivan:
I like it. Alright, well, listen, Pete, thank you so much for being on the podcast today. I really appreciate your time.

Pete:
It’s been absolutely my pleasure. And thank you for everything you do.

Ivan:
Listen, I love what I do. I absolutely love what I do. I can’t think of a better business to be in, so thank you. For anyone that wants more information on Pete, you can go to his website, www.petecullen.com. And that’s his real estate website. Pete, thanks for being on BNI Podcast.

Pete:
My pleasure. Thanks again for the opportunity.

Ivan:
You bet. Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
