Okay, great. Thank you both. Thank you, Ivan. Thank you, Pete. That was great. This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. And these audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience and help you boost your business. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code Ivan50, [this code is good for a limited time] and you’ll get 50% off all of the audio programs costs. And all of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: One invitation to a BNI meeting spreads out like a ripple in a pond. Every conversation is an opportunity to make a visitor feel welcome.

title.rendered: Episode 746: The Unnatural Promoter

content.rendered:
Synopsis

“Unnatural Networker” Charlie Lawson joins Dr. Misner this week to talk about his second book, The Unnatural Promoter: How anyone can blow their own trumpet without feeling awkward.

If you hate talking about yourself even when you know you provide a top-notch service and your customers love you, you might be an unnatural promoter. So how can you build business?

The secret is simple: get other people to talk about you. If you can build up the great strong relationships with people around you, they’ll go out and promote you.

If you feel self-conscious about the way you sound on audio or look on video, remember: you’re the only person who thinks you look or sound wrong. To everyone else, you look and sound just like you always do.

Visit unnaturalnetworker.com to learn more about Charlie and order the books.

Complete Transcript of Episode 746

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you today?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic and I just did some calculations. You know, at the end of the podcast, you mention Misner Audio Programs, and I donate all of the proceeds, all of the profits from that, to the BNI Foundation. And we just donated over $4,000 USD to the BNI Foundation from the Misner Audio Programs downloaded in the last year.

Priscilla:
Oh, that’s great!

Ivan:
So thank you, everyone. For those of you who are downloading programs from there, I donate all the profits from it to the BNI Foundation.

And I’m really excited to say I have a guest who’s been on the show once before. His name is Charlie Lawson. I consider Charlie a friend, and he is the author of The Unnatural Networker. And you know, most experts talk about networking as though it’s easy to talk to strangers, and Charlie helps other unnatural networkers, he considers himself one as well, to gain confidence by putting networking across from the point of view of someone who’d rather avoid it completely. And that book was released in 2014; it’s helped a lot of business owners to network effectively. His second book, The Unnatural Promoter, was released in 2021, and it focuses on helping people that don’t like to blow their own trumpet to be their best selves in promoting themselves. Charlie is a highly regarded keynote speaker. He’s the National Director of BNI in the United Kingdom and Ireland. And in his spare time, he’s mostly out swimming, biking, and running triathlons. God bless you, Charlie, good for you. Welcome back to the BNI Podcast, Charlie.

Charlie:
Thank you for having me. It’s fantastic. I mean, when you just read out the start there, Episode 746, was it? I knew it was a big number, but I didn’t realize it was quite that high. It’s amazing. Fantastic. Love it. Good to be on.

Ivan:
Thank you, I appreciate that. I’ve been doing this just about every week. I mean, we’ve put in classics in between, and we close for at least a week around New Year’s, but I’ve been doing this since 2007.

Charlie:
Yeah, amazing. Fantastic.

Ivan:
Priscilla, you’ve been with me since what? 2008?

Priscilla:
No, I was with you, well, I’m not sure. It was like somewhere around in the 30s.

Ivan:
Yeah, so it would have been late ’07 or ’08. 

Priscilla: Yeah.

Charlie:
I’m still gonna say that’s quite a high number. Priscilla, you might not have done the first 30. But that’s still impressive.

Priscilla:
I know. Consistent. That’s what we both are.

Ivan:
Yes, indeed. So, Charlie, tell me, what is The Unnatural Promoter?

Charlie:
What is an unnatural promoter? Well, I am definitely an unnatural promoter just in the very same way I’m an unnatural networker. I love the word unnatural. I guess I can say that. Now, what is an unnatural promoter? Look, you’re great at what you do, you deliver an amazing product, top notch service, and your clients or customers seem to love what you do. So why do you feel that you’re not getting the opportunities your rich talents deserve? Why do other people get all the breaks, even though you know they’re not as good as you? I don’t know if that statement resonates with anyone, but I know it does.

For me, I don’t find it easy to talk about myself; I don’t I find it easy to shout about myself. And that comes in both a business and a personal context, honestly. You know, sometimes you just don’t feel very confident about going out there and shouting about yourself. So I’ll give you an example from the personal context. You know, we all have WhatsApp on our phones and constant conversations going on. Sometimes I feel a bit nervous about putting something out with a group of friends. So people that I know the relationship is strong. I know they’re not going to be judging me a lot, if you like, or wondering what I’m talking about. I still find it quite hard sometimes to put that message out there. So yeah, there’s a lot of people out there in a big wide world who are unnatural networkers, maybe find it a little bit daunting going into a networking scenario, or a networking event and going and mingling. But there’s also people who just don’t really feel comfortable shouting about themselves at all.

Ivan:
So what’s the best way for an unnatural promoter to generate business for themselves?

Charlie:
That’s a great question. I mean, honestly, the best way to do it, I think you probably would recognize the answer, is to have a group of people around you, a group of people that you’ve got good relationships with, and they, once they have a good relationship with you, will want to help you want to shout about, want to help you move forward. So yeah, networking, generating great relationships, building those relationships to the point where you want to help each other, you can trust each other, and then getting people, other people, to go out there and effectively do your promotion for you. It’s much easier that way, you don’t have to shout about yourself quite so much. I mean, you still got to educate them, you still got to make sure they know what you’re what you’re after, and how to shout about you, effectively. But once you’ve educated them, they’ll go off and do it. Think about, you know, your best friend, you’ll help them if they’re in need, wouldn’t you?

Ivan:
Yeah, of course.

Charlie:
If you can build up the great strong relationships with people around you, they’ll go out and promote you. In a nutshell, that’s what’s in the book, honestly. I mean, there’s a lot more to it than that, clearly, you’ve got to have lots of strategies and techniques to make that happen. But summing it up in a single concept – get your people around you, build a relationship with them, and they’ll go and promote you for you.

Ivan:
Third party endorsement has always been the most powerful way to promote yourself. I mean, even in advertising; some of the best commercials really are somebody else who’s actually used a product or service talking about the product or service in the advertisement.

Charlie:
Exactly. Yeah.

Ivan:
And so if it works in advertising, which, by the way, I’m not opposed to. Many people think I’m opposed to advertising, I’m not. I just think that referral marketing is another form of advertising, and you need to plan it, like you plan your other forms of advertising. And that third party testimonial is really, really powerful.

Charlie:
Yeah, I think that’s the key, you know, to go from just being, I’m not going to be able to go network with someone straightaway, get to know him a bit, and they’ll suddenly go and promote my business to the hilt and just make sure everyone knows about me – it’s not going to work like that. What you’ve got to do is take a strategy to it, make sure you’re building a network or building a relationship with the right people, get the right people in vaguely similar professions, your contact sphere around you, all the good things that we know make it work. But yeah, if you don’t feel comfortable chatting about yourself, get other people to do for you. That’s the basic principle of what an unnatural promoter should do.

Ivan:
Yeah, you know, in my first book, my first major book, World’s Best Known Marketing Secret, I talk about the fact that people are more likely to talk about you when they’re upset with you, than they are happy with your services. And so what you need to do is mobilize those people who are in fact happy with your services and train them then to talk about you and how to talk about you effectively. And when you do that, that’s when you get those third-party testimonials.

Charlie:
It’s such a great, great point. And so many people don’t do it, you know. They’ve got a happy client, they’ve got someone who’s very satisfied with their services, and they don’t go and use that and leverage that all the time. Yeah, absolutely. If you can get those people shouting about you, that’s what to do.

Ivan:
Yep. So give us a specific tip from the book on why people don’t like to promote themselves.

Charlie:
Yeah, there’s lots of different ways in which we can get into that. The one I was going to focus on is actually moving away slightly from networking, I mean, good networking practices, good self promotion practice, in many ways. But the one I’ll focus on is slightly different; I’m going to talk about video. Because so many people, well, you look at any social media feed, and you’ll see videos all over the place, you know, it’s everywhere at the moment. It always will be. It’s such a powerful way of getting things across, you know, if you think of words going to a picture, that’s a huge step up, when they go from picture to a video, there’s so much content that can be shared in that.

And a lot of people don’t like seeing themselves on video. And there’s actually a bit of science behind it. And I was fascinated to look into it. There were studies done where people looked at pictures of themselves, and then a picture of themselves inverted. So just the other way, flips 90 degrees, but just the other way around. And without knowing that they were in this, this was just a controlled experiment, people will look at those pictures and say, you know, I like one of them more than the other, but I can’t really tell why. And the thing is, when you look at yourself on video, what you’re actually seeing is how the world sees you. So if you are talking just face to face, obviously in the same room, you know, you can, you’ll just see that person differently from how I see myself. Why? Because we only ever see ourselves in the mirror. And a mirror by the way it works, it inverts you.

So in that study, people were tending to like the pictures of themselves [that] were inverted, but preferred the pictures of other people where they were seeing the true picture. But they couldn’t figure out why, it’s too subtle a difference. It’s just the face isn’t exactly, doesn’t flip exactly the same each way. And it was just a subtle difference. So when it comes to a video, so many people say like, I hate the way I look on video, I look like such an idiot when I see myself on video. Or they’ll say, I don’t like the sound of my voice, it’s a similar principle there. When you hear your voice on a video, you’ll hear it coming straight from the speaker of your phone, or the device or whatever it is you’re listening to. When you hear your own voice in your head, there’s a more scientific way of expressing this, but effectively, your voice goes through your head, through bits of brain and bone and whatever else, and ends up in your ear. So it’s distorted.

Ivan:
I totally agree with that. I have so many people who tell me they listen to the podcast, and I have such a calming voice. And I’m like, no, no, I listen to my voice. It’s not that calming. Interesting how we perceive our voices different than other people.

Charlie:
Exactly. And you know, if you weren’t listening to this video, I’m sorry, this podcast is audio recording, I’m hearing your voice and you sound exactly like Ivan, that I’ve always known and love.

Ivan: (chuckling)

Charlie:
You’re hearing me and I sound exactly like I do, and you sound like you. But to each other, it’s different. So yeah, with that little flicker of you know, the challenge on both seeing ourselves because of that inverted issue, and the way our sound comes, so many people don’t want to do it. Now, I can’t tell you, I can’t try and solve that, click my fingers, and suddenly you’re going to look like…

Ivan:
Yeah, so how do we use that information?

Charlie:
Well, that’s the thing. I think you have to just sort of realize that it is only yourself that thinks that. It really, genuinely is only yourself thinks that because it is personal. No one else is hearing me in the same way because they’re hearing my voice come from my mouth and go to their ears. And no one else is seeing that, you know, everyone else is just seeing the normal version of me that they always see. So yeah, I can’t flip a magic wand and suddenly, it’s all sold. But in your mind, we’ve just got to tell ourselves that as an unnatural promoter wanting to get out a video, is a great tool to use, you’ve got to use it. But you’ve just got to tell yourself, it really, genuinely is only you that’s thinking that.

Ivan:
All right. Well, we’re out of time. Is there any last thought before we wrap up?

Charlie:
No, look, I think the main thing about being an unnatural promoter is get a good network around you. If my two books were fiction, they’re not, but if they were fiction, it would almost be that “The Unnatural Promoter” is the prequel to “The Unnatural Networker.” So what “The Unnatural Promoter” says is look, don’t feel comfortable going out and promoting yourself? – get out there and network. Then go and check out “The Unnatural Networker”, that will tell you how to do it. So that’s how the two fit together. But you know, if I can be of any help to anyone with their unnatural networking or promoting tendencies, I’d love to do so.

Ivan:
Well, that’s great information, Charlie. And thanks so much for being back on BNIpodcast.com. For anyone who’s interested in Charlie’s books, “The Unnatural Promoter” or “The Unnatural Networker”, you can go to www.unnaturalnetworker.com for information on the book. And I think, Charlie, the books are both available on Amazon. Yeah?

Charlie:
Correct, they are. Yeah.

Ivan:
Fantastic. Well, thanks for being on again, Charlie. I appreciate you.

Charlie:
Thank you for having me.

Ivan:
Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, perfect. Thank you both for the great information. This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience and help you boost your business. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off all of the audio programs [this code is good for a limited time], and all of the proceeds are going to the BNI Foundation.

So thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: How can you build business when you hate to talk about yourself? Build relationships and get other people to promote you.

title.rendered: Episode 745: Networking Up, Revised (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 568, released on July 25, 2018.

Synopsis

In Episode 530, Dr. Misner talked to Mike Macedonio about “networking up.” That episode focused on hanging out where successful people are and how to connect with them. Today, Dr. Misner wants to share some additional suggestions about how to network above your weight class.

  1. Embrace discomfort. If you’re not uncomfortable connecting with someone, you’re not aiming high enough.
  2. Don’t sell to them. Nothing will shut down your chance for a real connection faster.
  3. Don’t complain to them. You don’t want to be remembered for complaining.
  4. Don’t be a sycophant. It’s fine to acknowledge their work, but don’t overdo the fandom.
  5. Work within the context of the meeting. Connect what’s happening at the moment
  6. Find out what they’re currently interested in. Do your homework before meeting them–or at least look them up on your phone at the event.
  7. Add value. Offer help with something they care about.
  8. Don’t assume they remember your name. They meet hundreds of people at every event. Remind them of your name and how they met you.
  9. Don’t sell to them! It’s the biggest mistake if you’re networking up, and it’s worth repeating.

Brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube.

Complete Transcript of Episode 568

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast which is brought to you by The Networking For Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Misner and many other networking experts. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan! How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am in Montreal, Canada this week attending a TLC event and also speaking at a BNI event.

Priscilla:
Great! That is a beautiful part of the world right there.

Ivan:
It really is. It’s absolutely beautiful! I’ve been here several times. I love it.

Today I’m talking about Networking Up part 2. So, we did a podcast- it’s podcast number 530, where I talked with Mike Macedonio about Networking Up. We’ve all heard the advice “you become the people who you hang out with,” which means that you not only need to surround yourself with successful people or however you define success, but you also need to continually network up and raise the bar for yourself over time. Now, Mike and I mostly talked about hanging out where successful people are and learning how to connect with those people. But what I want to do today is give you a number of points, about half a dozen or more, in addition to learning how to hang out where successful people are. I want to give you some other points that I think can really make a difference. You know, I’ve always believed that there’s generally plenty of room at the top, but it’s the bottom that’s really crowded. So, here are some suggestions on how you can network above your weight class and move up into networking with stronger people.

In addition to hanging out where successful people are, Podcast 530, the next thing is to embrace discomfort. If you’re not uncomfortable connecting with someone, then you’re not aiming high enough. I’ve been there and I understand this feeling. However, you need to get past that and go talk to them. So if you meet somebody or see someone at an event, it’s really important to go on up and talk to them. Your discomfort may be a sign that this is the exact person you really should be talking to.

Here’s another one: Don’t sell to them. I can’t tell you how many times I’ve met someone for the first time and they start selling to me. I’ve seen the same thing happen when I’ve been with other business people who are far more successful than me. Don’t do it! The old adage, “It never hurts to ask, right?” It’s completely wrong! When you’re networking up, especially if you’re networking up with someone for the first time, a lot of people do that. Don’t be one of the crowd. Don’t sell to them. Don’t say, “This is my one
opportunity!” Because it’ll be your last opportunity with that person if you try to sell to them.

Here’s one that surprises people that other people even do this: Don’t complain to them. If you’re networking up with somebody more successful, don’t complain to them. I know that sounds obvious, but I have been both the victim of it and I’ve seen it done to other people. I was with an incredibly successful businessman some time ago, he was meeting people in a crowd and someone he just met went on a rant about some problem with the man’s company. This guy absolutely stood out and was quietly escorted out. You do not want to be remembered for that. If you’re talking to somebody really successful, don’t
lead with a complaint. If it’s more important to complain than it is to make a good connection, then do whatever you want. But if your goal is to really network and build relationships with people, don’t do that.

Here’s another one: Acknowledge their work, but don’t be a sycophant. Plenty of people will flatter them, but don’t puppy-dog-lick somebody to death. When you meet somebody that you’re just so excited to see them, don’t puppy-dog-lick them to death. Successful people, you know they are still people, they
appreciate knowing that their work makes a difference. Just don’t overdo that. Be cognizant.

A really important recommendation: Work within the context of the meeting. If at all possible, find a way to connect what’s happening at the moment, something interesting in your discussion or setting. For example, I met a well-known international thought leader for the first time at the book signing for speakers at a conference. The problem was the audience was still at dinner and nobody was at the  signing. So, I shared a story with him about me at a book signing that I did where the ONLY person who showed up was my mother. Priscilla, you may have heard me tell this story before. She acted like she was
a fan and made such a big deal of the signing that people started crowding around the table where I was signing. I had this guy laughing so hard that it helped him remember who I was and what I did, and then he invited me to join an organization of his where I had the chance to network up with many, many people. As a matter of fact, I’m doing that here in Montreal right now with those people.

Here’s another point: Find out what they are currently interested in. This is a critical item. If you know that they are going to an event, do some internet research. If you meet them at an event or you see them at an event, get on your mobile device and just look them up. See what they are working on so that you can have a conversation on that topic- which leads me to the next suggestion:

Add value. If you don’t sell to them, what can you do? Add value to them. Here’s a great real-life example, one of the times that I met Richard Branson on Necker Island, I really wanted to do a video with him- that I put up on IvanMisner.com. I wanted to put it up there. I wanted to say, “Hey, Richard, will you do a video with me?” Instead, I asked about something he was working on, which was The B Team- which was about how business can make a difference in local communities. And he just lit up, he was very excited! I said,  How can I help you with that? What can I do to help you with that?” He said, “Well, you’re in a network, can you get the word out?” I said, “Yeah! Would you like to do a video on it?” And he said yes. So there it was! I’m adding value to him, rather than asking for a favor. And by adding value, it makes that person really want to do that, and I was able to do that. You can go to IvanMisner.com and do a search on Richard Branson and you’ll see the video that I did with him on Necker Island.

Here’s the last thing and then I want to repeat one other: If you meet them a second time, you’re networking up and you’ve already met them once, don’t assume they remember your name. That’s so important. If you meet them and connect with them again, never ever assume they remember you. Always help them out by giving them context of how you know each other or met. Really successful
people tend to meet hundreds of thousands and thousands of people, so giving them context can help them jog their memory. So if you meet them in person, give them a reminder of where they met. For example; when I’m networking on an email communication and I’m networking up, I’ll often times send a copy of a photo that may have been taken of the two of us at the event and say, “Hey, we met at this event. Not sure if that jogs your memory but here’s where we met and I just wanted to reconnect.” And that helps people. Don’t ever assume they remember you.

And the last thing I want to share: Don’t sell to them! I call it “sales Tourettes.” People can’t help themselves, they just burst out trying to sell. It’s the biggest mistake if you’re networking up.

So there are about eight different techniques in addition to what we talked about in the last Networking Up podcast. Feel free to share this in your chapters. And if you have something that you want to add through my networking up scenario, please add it here on BNIPodcast.com. I read all of the posts that go up. That’s it for me, Priscilla, unless you have a question or want to add anything.

Priscilla:
No, I think that’s fascinating. I like all of your points. Especially the uncomfortable barometer as evidence that you’re doing the very thing that you’ve promoted here.

Ivan:
Yeah, a lot of people may think, “Oh, that’s Ivan. He can just introduce himself to anyone.” Well, you know what, I suffer from the same issue. I’m networking up and I’m uncomfortable. You just have to work past that. You’ll never have a chance to meet them if you don’t step up and introduce yourself.

Priscilla:
Right! And I love that you suggest reminding them of your name because it’s just really difficult to keep those names at the top of your head- especially if you haven’t seen that person in a while or maybe you’ve only met them once, it’s very hard to remember their name.

Ivan:
Exactly. I had somebody get really mad at me a couple of years ago because I didn’t remember his name. And here’s the kicker, he said, “You don’t remember me?” And at that moment, I was at a PTA meeting. I’m thinking, “I’ve never met this guy at a PTA before. Is he in BNI?” So I ask him if we met at a BNI event and he said, “No. I went to school with you 20 years ago at Gladstone High School.” I’m like seriously? I’m at a PTA meeting, I run a large organization, and this is just completely out of context. And had he said to me, “Hey, we went to school together at Gladstone-” the brain matter would have gone to the right file and opened that. So, there’s just this assumption that people will remember them, even in a different context. Don’t make that assumption.

Priscilla:
Okay, cool. Well, thank you, Dr. Misner! I think that’s great information. For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

 

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: The most important thing to remember when making connections with people who are more successful than you are is “Don’t sell to them!”

title.rendered: Episode 744: Networking Is a Marathon, Not a Sprint

content.rendered:
Synopsis

It’s International Networking Week, which makes it a great time for a reminder that if all your networking efforts are focused on visibility, you’ll never get to profitability. No matter how many networking events you attend and how many people you meet, if you don’t take the next step and start building relationships with those people, you won’t get any new business.

When it comes to networking, sometimes you have to slow down in order to go fast. Go to fewer events and spend more time getting to know the people you meet there. In BNI, that means increasing your One-to-Ones. If you go from one a month to one a week, you DOUBLE the amount of referrals that you receive and give.

You can also schedule check-in calls with your contacts to ask “How are you doing? How’s business? What can I do for you? How can I help you?” They don’t have to be long, but leave yourself time to respond thoughtfully when they answer.

Complete Transcript of Episode 744

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice. I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you today?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic. And I want to welcome everyone to International Networking Week. I started International Networking Week in 2007. And we have had it every year since then, all over the world. And it’s really been a great opportunity to get exposure for BNI and exposure for the concept of business networking.

Priscilla:
Now that sounds great. You have to tell us about it a little more.

Ivan:
Yeah, well, you know, again, I started it back in ’07, because I wanted a way of really highlighting the power of business networking. And, you know, BNI alone generates billions of dollars’ worth of business for members all around the world. And we’re making a big difference in local communities, in the success of small business. And so I wanted a way of really highlighting that with the media and with communities. And that’s why I started it back then.

Priscilla:
Uh huh.

Ivan:
But my topic today is, I think, a good topic when you’re talking about networking, and that is that the process of networking is in fact a marathon, not a sprint.

Years ago, I met a woman who was known as the consummate networker. She had hundreds, if not thousands of contacts, gave her a really broad network made up of people from all walks of life. She was really well known in the community. She was the go-to person if anyone needed anything. Then one day during a conversation she was having, she pulled me aside and dropped a bombshell with me. She said that her networking efforts weren’t really paying off for her. And she went on at some length about all the groups that she went to and the people she met and how she had made so many good contacts, and was continuing to make more all the time, but wasn’t actually getting any solid business from her efforts.

When I asked her how many networking events she went to each week, she said she was going to at least five. At least five, and sometimes she went to more. And I asked why wasn’t she seeing real results? And despite her amazing talent for making contacts and gaining visibility, she was never really getting to the heart of what networking is all about, which is building relationships.

She was so busy running around and making appearances that she wasn’t ever learning how to actually work the networks that she had created, in order to build deep relationships with people and develop credibility with them. It’s true that she was ‘visible’ in the community, very visible, problem was that she viewed activity as an accomplishment when it came to her networking efforts. Her network was, in fact, a mile wide, but only an inch deep. And she had not taken the next, and most important, step with many, many people in her wide-reaching network. She never devoted the time to develop the kind of rapport with any of them, that would allow them to really get to know her, like her, trust her, and to want to pass referrals to her.

Ivan:
And I think this is the perfect topic for International Networking Week. I recently saw the same thing with someone I’ve known for a few years, he made a consistent habit of going to every single networking meeting and event that he could go to, and he was incredibly visible. And not only was he always at networking meetings, but he was always full of energy and enthusiasm from the time he arrived, to the time he left. Again, the problem was in no way due to the lack of activity, to effort, or to enthusiasm, because he was putting himself out there and meeting new people, which is good. The problem was that he was running around so much that he never stopped long enough to spend the time necessary to establish the kind of long-term roots that lead to an ongoing reciprocal relationship. The end result was that he got a severe case of networking burnout. He went from going everywhere and meeting everyone possible, to going nowhere and meeting almost no one, and a year later his business went under.

See, this is one of the reasons why I set up BNI to meet weekly, to meet every week so you can go deep and build relationships with people. It’s not just the number of contacts you have, it’s the number of connections you create, the number of relationships that you build. Networking is a marathon, not a sprint.

If your goal is to significantly grow your business by simply making as many contacts as possible, you’ll never build a powerful personal network. If you’re networking with this approach, you’re almost guaranteed to get burned out, networking as a sprint. Constantly bouncing around from event to event is exhausting, and I think it’s ineffective. There needs to be a balance between the visibility creating aspect of your networking efforts, and the credibility creating aspects of your networking efforts.

Ivan:
One way to accomplish this is to schedule regular one-to-ones with people you’ve met, in order to go deeper in understanding the services that they offer, and how you might be able to refer them. Most importantly, it’s a way for them to do the same for you. And if you’re in BNI, you know that I promoted this for years. I’ve got a number of podcasts talking about the power of doing one-to-ones and how, when you double the number of one-to-ones you do, you increase… Actually, if you go from one a month to one a week, in one-to-ones, you DOUBLE the amount of referrals that you receive, and that you give. And so one-to-ones are very powerful in actually building relationships.

Case in point is a third person, who was real estate agent in California, who shared with me that her business grew dramatically during a major recession in the past, because she decided to develop a network that was both wide and deep in some places. She took my advice and did at least four one-to-ones a month with people in her network. She focused on building relationships. And once she was convinced that she had found a good business opportunity for some of her contacts, she would actually phone the contact on the spot and put them together with the person that she was meeting with. And this created powerful introductions that led to business. So from this activity, she ended up giving twice as many referrals to other people as she had in the past, which matches the data from the research that we did in the previous podcast I’ve mentioned a number of times.

But more importantly, she received twice as many referrals as she had from people in the past. She was treating the process more like a marathon than a sprint. And this is a great example of how networking is more about farming than it is about hunting. It’s about building relationships with key people. What’s amazing about this story is that it happened at a time when businesses were dealing with a massive recession, and the fallout from that recession. More importantly, the business woman was in a profession that was hit hard, as hard or harder than the overall majority of businesses. And despite the conditions around her, she used her relationship building skills to create long-term referral relationships.

When it comes to networking, sometimes you have to slow down in order to go fast. Let me repeat that, because I think it’s powerful. And it’s appropriate during International Networking Week to talk about this. When it comes to networking, sometimes you have to slow down to go fast, certainly to go far, and to get there in a timely manner. It’s really treating networking like a marathon and not like a sprint. What are your thoughts, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
I was thinking about something kind of specific, which was about making phone calls. You know how people tell you to make five phone calls a day and all of that. And I have this resistance to doing it because then you have to be available to receive five phone calls a day in return, you know. So I was thinking about building your network and, you know, practical ways to do it. And that’s what came up for me. Do you have anything to say about that?

Ivan:
Yeah, well you know, I was never a believer, necessarily, of a certain number of phone calls that I had to do every day. I mean, when I was first writing books and getting them out there, I did have a certain number of phone calls that I would make every day. And then I left time open to take phone calls. But again, the key as you build your business over time is about going deep rather than going wide. And so the number of phone calls is maybe slightly less important than the depth of the phone calls that you make or connections that you make. You know, you can schedule phone calls, you know, rather than just cold call people. And you know, if you just call somebody, I know it’s not a cold call if you know them, but to just call someone cold, even if you know them, they may or may not have the time at that moment to really have a deep conversation with you. So I’m a real believer in scheduling calls.

Priscilla:
Uh huh.

Ivan:
So reaching out to somebody and asking, “Hey, are you free tomorrow at a certain time? I’d love to catch up with you.” To me, that works better.

Priscilla:
Yeah, that sounds like a great idea.

Ivan:
Yeah, and, you know, do a few of those. I mean, when I was running the day-to-day operations for BNI, I made a list of all my executive directors and regional directors and national directors. And I broke up the list, you know, by a handful of people every day, where I would reach out to them, usually by email, and say, “Hey, I’d love to connect sometime this week,” and then do these connection calls, where I would just check in. They’re check-in calls. You know, “How are you doing? How’s business? What can I do for you? How can I help you?” And I mean, I did that pretty consistently for many years when I was running the day-to-day operations of BNI. And that’s a big-time commitment when you’re also managing the business.

Priscilla:
Right. It is a big-time commitment.

Ivan:
It is, but it’s a good relationship builder. And that’s a great example of networking being more of a marathon than a sprint.

Priscilla:
Right.

Ivan:
Well, that’s everything I’ve got for today, Priscilla. I hope everyone enjoys International Networking Week and I’d love to hear your comments right here on BNI Podcast. I read every comment that is posted here.

Priscilla:
Well, thank you, Ivan. I think that was very revealing and interesting, and I look forward to learning more about International Networking Week.

Ivan:
Thank you.

Priscilla:
This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience and help you boost your business. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of all the audio programs [this code is good for a limited time], and all of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Networking is a marathon, not a sprint. You have to slow down in order to get results. Take time to build relationships with new contacts.

title.rendered: Episode 743: Be Passionate, Not Pushy

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Jonathon Chua, Head of Operations at BNI Global, joins Dr. Misner this week to talk about passion and enthusiasm as elements of making a referral. Sometimes we go overboard with our enthusiasm, and then being passionate can come across as being pushy.

Whether you’re inviting a visitor to a BNI meeting, making a referral for another member, or telling a prospect about your own goods and services, remember to keep the focus on helping the other person and meet them where they are. That way you won’t come across as pushy, even if the other person is not ready to take the next step.

Passion is priceless. 21 members of a BNI chapter in Singapore who believe in inspiring and empowering women around the world got together to write a book called Venus Rising. Their passion fueled a donation drive of US $10,000 to the BNI Foundation.

Order the book Venus Rising: the Phoenix Awakens at venusgivesback.com.

Complete Transcript of Episode 743

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you today?

Ivan:
I am doing great. And I’m going to share something I don’t think I’ve ever shared on any of the podcasts. Every Wednesday that I’m not traveling, I do what’s called Writing Wednesday, and I generally get together with Sam Horn, wonderful lady, she’s written a number of books and has promoted BNI a lot. And we do Writing Wednesday, and that’s what I do just about every Wednesday I’m in town. So, I’ve seen Sam today, and they should be called “Talking” Wednesdays because we end up talking a lot, but we talk about writing.

Priscilla:
Oh, that’s great. Okay.

Ivan:
I am really pleased to say that I have a guest today, Jonathon Chua. Jonathon is from Singapore. And he started his BNI journey as a member in 2017. As chapter president, he led his chapter to win Chapter of the Year in 2018. And since then, has launched over 10 chapters and conducted launch trainings for Executive Directors in China. Currently, he supports 1200 members, and his mission is to know every single member by name and trade. That’s impressive. His vision step: 5000 Members in Singapore and generate 500 million Singapore Dollars of Thank You For Closed Business in the Singapore economy by 2030. Jonathon, welcome to BNI Podcast. It’s great having you here.

Jonathon:
Thank you very much for your kind introduction, Dr. Misner, and the opportunity to be here this morning today.

Ivan:
Well, we’re glad to have you here, and you’re going to talk about a topic that I like a lot, and that is be passionate, not pushy. So talk a little bit about passion and enthusiasm as the components of making a good referral or a good sale.

Jonathon:
Yes, passion and enthusiasm are key components to any successful sale or business. And being passionate may sometimes come across as pushy, because we want the best for the other person. I believe that our intention plays a huge part. When we come from a space of love, which is looking to impact lives, and service, adding value to the other person, the other person will get our pure intention. If I don’t believe in the value I provide, my pitch is going to sound forced or salesy.

Ivan:
I like it, I like it. How do you apply that within the BNI context? Let’s take a little spin on terms of inviting people. Because I see the same thing with inviting people that people are so passionate, that sometimes others may feel it’s pushy, when in fact, the members are passionate about inviting someone. I know it’s a little different than what we’re talking about, but I think it’s relevant.

Jonathon:
Definitely. Because sometimes we are so enthusiastic, we say, “Hey, you have to come to this chapter, you have to come and meet this particular member”. And we go a bit overboard. So it’s a fine line. When we say, “Hey, our intention is to get them connected,” the focus would be on the other person, how we can add value by having him here, we’re going to connect him to a particular person that’s going to benefit him or her in his business.

Ivan:
So how you communicate – and let’s go back to referrals and sales. How do you communicate in such a way that doesn’t come across as pushy, but comes across as passionate about what you do?

Jonathon:
Okay, so when we have that belief in our products or services, we add value to them. Correct? We don’t come across [as pushy] because we can talk about it with enthusiasm and with energy. Everything in the universe is about energy. And our passion is priceless. It’s just like this book I’m going to share, it’s called “Venus Rising”, where 21 entrepreneurs from a [BNI] chapter come together to write this book. They believe in inspiring and empowering women around the world. And their passion drove a donation drive of US $10,000 to the BNI Foundation, and it became the first chapter in the world to become a Misner Diamond Fellow. I think their passion drove this donation drive so fast, within two months, in fact.

Ivan:
Yeah, that’s an amazing story. And you know, I haven’t thought of this, but I should invite one or two of them on to BNI Podcast to talk about how they did that, because I’d love to see more chapters become Fellows of the BNI Foundation Endowment, which we started about two years ago, and now has, as I understand it, almost $600,000 in the Endowment. So that’s a great story and I appreciate you sharing that. Name the book again, Jonathon.

Jonathon:
It is called Venus Rising.

Ivan:
And these are ladies that are absolutely passionate not only about what they do, but passionate about BNI, and passionate about empowering women in business. And, of course, passionate about self.

Jonathon:
Yes, definitely. And I also recall, Dr. Ivan Misner, when you shared that when you started BNI with just a piece of paper, but a ton of passion.

Ivan:
Yeah, yeah, I didn’t have much material, but I had a lot of passion. I was the poster child for ignorance on fire being better than knowledge on ice. And it’s just grown amazingly well. So talk to me a little bit about speaking people’s language, speaking other people’s language, when you’re marketing or selling to them.

Jonathon:
One of the ways is to speak their language and meet the clients or the customer where they are. You know, sometimes they may not be emotionally, mentally, or even financially ready now. But just plant a seed of harvest. And this, in fact, is in line with the philosophy of Givers Gain; even if the person is not ready now, we leave him or her in a better place, in a better knowledge, than before we met them. And I’ve also developed a way to crack the networking personality code, where in five minutes, I’m able to identify their networking personality, and connect on a deeper level, by speaking their language.

Ivan:
Okay, you can’t throw that out without giving a little bit more information. How do you get their personality code in a short period of time?

Jonathon:
So one of the ways is, we understand their motivation behind them. So everyone has a different motivation; motivation in terms of their business, and their success in how they view success in life. And from there, we can fine tune them into four different types of personality, networking personality, and from there, we can see – well this person is like an examiner, he’s very, I would say, strategic person, and you can speak in that language.

Ivan:
Yeah, it sounds like you’re talking about some of the material that was actually mentioned last week by Becky Isbell, “Room Full of Referrals”, the behavioral styles, is that what you’re talking about?

Jonathon:
Yes.

Ivan:
Yeah, great. Well, that’s a great book I worked on with Dawn Lyons and with Tony Alessandra. Really, they contributed massively to putting together that book. Well, that’s fantastic. Jonathon, where can people get copies of “Venus Rising”, the other book we were talking about today?

Jonathon:
All they need to do is just type in Venus Rising, the book will be online. Yes, on sale, yes, that’s right. I intend to send a few copies over to you and the rest of the team U.S. and so that will be part of a small gift from them.

Ivan:
Well, thank you. That’s very nice of you. I appreciate it. And Jonathon, I thank you so much. Any closing thought before we wrap up here?

Jonathon:
Well, that’s all I have today. And those that like to reach out to me can reach out to me at JonathonChua.sg. And that’s all I have for today, Dr. Ivan, and thank you for everything you have done to build this powerful network. We wouldn’t be here without you, or your passion to change the way the world does business.

Ivan:
Aw, thank you, Jonathon. I appreciate you and I’ve enjoyed my visits to Singapore. Sitting on my desk right now is a plaque that was given to me almost 20 years ago, in one of my visits to Singapore, so I have it here proudly every day.

Jonathon:
We look forward to welcome you to Singapore. Dr. Ivan Misner.

Ivan:
Great. Well, thank you very much. Back over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, that was great. Thank you both so much for the great information. This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience and to help you boost your business, so check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of all the audio programs [this code is good for a limited time], and all of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Your passion won’t come across as pushy as long as you keep the focus on helping the other person and meet them where they are.

title.rendered: Episode 742: Building Relationships at Home, Work, and Community

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Becky Isbell joins Dr. Misner on the podcast this week to talk about the three places you need to build relationships: at home, at work, and in your community. Relationships are one of BNI’s core values, and it’s important not to take them for granted.

At Home

Do things to let your spouse and family know you appreciate them. Say “Thank” you. Do something nice for them unexpectedly.

At Work

When someone asks you to do something at work, don’t say “That’s not my job.” Always think about how you can be helpful. Do something nice for a colleague when they’re having a rough day. Say “Thank you” often.

In Your Community

Get involved in a service project or service organization in your local community. Where can you contribute your time, your talent, and your treasure to make your community a better place to live? If you don’t have a nonprofit that you support locally, please get involved with the BNI Foundation and the Business Voices project.

What are you doing to build relationships in these three areas? We’d love to hear from you here in the comments section.

Complete Transcript of Episode 742

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, I’m still Zooming around the world. As you know, I got back from Antarctica last month, and that’s my seventh continent that I’ve been on. And I gotta tell you, Antarctica truly is a desert. You hear people say that, but when you’re there, it’s amazing; there is literally no vegetation anywhere in Antarctica. But I’m Zooming around. I’ve had a chance to go to the United Kingdom on their 25th anniversary by Zoom and still traveling around the world through Zoom mostly.

Priscilla:
That’s great. And I hope we’re going to hear about Antarctica.

Ivan:
Yes, I’ll be writing about Antarctica for sure. Okay, so I’ve got a guest today. Becky Isbell, and Becky was raised in southeast Oklahoma. She’s proud to call Texas her home for the last 12 years. She’s currently married to what she calls her AMAZING husband, James. And they have three children and one grandchild. You’ve got a grandchild, Becky?  You know, I’ve found that mentioning it to your children that you don’t have any grandkids doesn’t go over well. I’ve discovered that the hard way.

Becky:
Yeah, it doesn’t help.

Ivan:
So in 2009, Becky joined the BNI organization on a whim and that whim turned out to be the best business decision she and her husband ever made, she says. And Becky quickly became a support director for BNI Central Texas and was promoted to Area Director in 2012. And in February 2020 was offered the position of Regional Area Director for all of Texas, Arkansas, Missouri, Colorado, and most recently her home state of Oklahoma. Becky, it is awesome to have you back on BNI Podcast, welcome.

Becky:
Well, thanks Ivan, I’m so excited to be back. I love doing these podcasts with you and getting to talk to you about my favorite core value, which is building relationships. And I know we have seven core values. And I’m told often, like you shouldn’t say building relationships is your favorite. But how many times have you also heard you’re not supposed to have a favorite child?

Ivan:
Well, you’re not. And I would stand firm by the fact that I don’t. However, I think it’s okay to have a favorite core value. And we talked briefly about this in a previous podcast, I think it was episode 714, about building relationships and how to turn referrals into results, didn’t we?

Becky:
We absolutely did. And so today, we’re just gonna take a deeper dive into how to build those relationships – at home, in your business, and in our community. And going back to that favorite child, I do have a favorite child and I will proudly put this on a recording, that favorite child is my grandchild. So, it’s okay to have that favorite.

Ivan:
Yeah, yeah, I’ll give you that. I’ve asked my kids which one they think is the favorite, I’ve asked them individually, and they all name another one of the three kids.

Becky: Isn’t that funny how that works out? They never want to say, “I’m the favorite.” But they’ll say, “So-and-so is the favorite.”

Ivan:
That’s right. So I think I’m doing my job.

Becky:
Yeah, I just make it very clear that the grandchild is my favorite.

Ivan:
Yeah, that works.

Becky:
So let’s talk about relationships at home. I know you have traveled a lot here lately, and I’ve been traveling quite a bit. But prior to even fourth quarter of 2021, there wasn’t a lot of travel going on. We were all at home, right?

Ivan:
Right.

Becky:
And we’re spending more time at home, and I think oftentimes we forget about building those relationships at home. Thank you for introducing my husband, James, as the amazing husband because he is. Ivan, you’ve been around me for over 10 years, you know I’m a lot – I’m a lot to handle. But my husband does that. He does it with kindness and grace. So at home, what are we doing to build relationships at home? Because like I said, sometimes we just put this on the back burner, and I say “we” meaning me. I take those relationships for granted. Oh, my husband will always be there. My children will always be there. But one thing I learned during 2020 and the first part of 2021, is those are the people who I need the most, my relationships at home. And so, what are we doing to build these relationships? Ivan, I know you’re a fan of the book “The Five Love Languages”, right?

Ivan:
Yes.

Becky:
And I will tell you this book was transformational in my relationship with James and learning what that person’s love language is. And it goes back to everyone in our house, not just our spouse, but our children and our neighbors, and learning how to talk to them to build that relationship. And you probably have heard of this book, he’s kind of a big deal, Dr. Ivan Misner wrote a book called Room Full of Referrals. It talks about the same thing, learning how to talk and communicate with other personality types. My love language, as you know, Ivan, is words of affirmation. I tell my husband, like I am so easy. All you have to do is tell me I look pretty, and the unofficial love language of feed me. So feed me and tell me I look pretty, and I’m golden. My husband, on the other hand, is not a words of affirmation person, his is acts of service. So I can tell him all day long what an amazing husband he is, and it doesn’t mean anything to him. But if I go out and throw some dirt around with him, he’s happy as a clam. Like, just makes him happy.

So think about this at home, what are we doing to develop those relationships at home? Things that you can do at home? It’s pretty simple. I say, say thank you. You know, when’s the last time you said thank you to someone in your household? Maybe it’s thank you for doing those dishes. Thank you for taking the trash out. And then do the unexpected. You heard me mention earlier that I just got back from traveling quite a bit, back and forth to see my family in Oklahoma, and just different trips. But when I came home, my husband had changed the sheets on the bed. He knows I love sleeping in a clean bed. He didn’t have to do that, but he did it. So thank you and just, you know, showing the people at home how much we appreciate and need them goes such a long way.

Ivan:
Very true. I like it. Elisabeth’s was gifts and words of affirmation, in that order.

Becky:
Right, yeah. What is your love language, if you don’t mind sharing?

Ivan:
It would be acts of service.

Becky:
Acts of service. Yeah. So you know, giving you a gift doesn’t mean a lot. But if it’s acts of service, let me do something for you. Let me go help you with something, or just be with you goes a long way. And I’m just gonna plug again, the book Room Full of Referrals. It is my absolute favorite book that you’ve ever written. And because it’s not only business, but we can take it back to our family. And we’re gonna talk more about family and community later, but it applies to everyone across the board.

Ivan:
All right, so let’s talk about work. That’s your second category.

Becky:
So work. All right, so I was just curious. On average, how many hours do you think the average American spends at work?

Ivan:
If you’re asking me, I would say probably, on average, close to 40. I mean, you have people who spend more than that. But you also have people who work part time, so it’s hard to say.

Becky:
So on average, it’s about 40 hours a week, which turns into about 2000 hours a year.

Ivan:
2080.

Becky:
Yep. Wow, you’re really good at the math and quick with the facts.

Ivan:
No, I ran a company long enough.

Becky:
So we spend a lot of time at work. And sometimes we spend more time at work than what we do at home. And so we need to make sure that we’re building those relationships at work. And so what are we doing at work? We are, you know, doing again, the acts of kindness. Are you going above and beyond? I will tell you one thing that will throw me over the edge is if somebody says ‘That’s not my job.’ It gets under my skin more than anything. And my very first job that I had to start, was working at a vet clinic when I was 14 years old. And that was so unacceptable with my boss at the time. And we were trained, and it was expected that if the front staff was done with their job, they would go back with the kennel staff because nobody got to leave until the last person was done working.

And I think about that just even today, because we live in a Zoom world now, virtual, and I’m looking at the people still working online. And I’m saying ‘Hey, you know, Jen, what can I help you [with]? I see you’re still online.’ She’s like, ‘Hey, I really need somebody to proof this for me.’ So going back to the acts of service — what can I do to help?

And then again, I’m just wanting to say again, say thank you. When’s the last time you said thank you to someone you work with? And say thank you, I really appreciate all the work you do. For someone who words affirmation is their love language, that goes above and beyond anything else that you can do — just say thank you, thank you for your time, thank you for doing that with me. And then again, do the unexpected. You know, maybe it’s send an Uber Eats driver to send that person lunch, especially if you’re working in a virtual world. You know Ivan, I told you food is my sixth love language.

Ivan: (chuckling)

Becky:
And somebody sent lunch to me the other day. I was in the weeds with work, it was like two o’clock and I said something about I hadn’t had lunch, and somebody sent lunch to me via Uber Eats. And I will tell you, it just made my day and made me feel so good that a co-worker that lives in a different time zone than I do thought enough of me to send me lunch.

Ivan:
Nice.

Becky:
It’s building that relationship. So, and then the third, let’s bounce into the community, which is my favorite thing to talk about. I know, you’re probably saying, well it’s your grandkid and your dog.

Ivan: (laughing)

Becky:
I love the community I live in; we’ve been here almost 13 years. And it’s kind of a joke that everyone here calls me the networking nerd. Because when they need something, I know a guy. You know, we talked about this before, I know a guy, I’m the guy who gets things, right? But I absolutely love our community. And I love seeing what our community does, and we really pull together when times get tough. You know, I’ve seen restaurants who were struggling during COVID, and businesses would get together and order a big lunch order from them, or buy gift cards from them, to just help keep them afloat through COVID. And that’s how great our community is.

But I’m going to ask our listeners, How involved are you in your community? Are you involved in a service project, a service organization? Ivan, I know you were involved in Rotary at one time; I think you were actually president of your club, weren’t you?

Ivan:
No, I wasn’t, but I was in [Rotary] for 16 years.

Becky:
Yeah. And so, you know Rotary does a lot of good service in the community. And I love our Rotary Club, but it doesn’t have to be Rotary. Are you involved in your garden club, your food care center? Something. Are you involved? Get involved with that nonprofit, maybe it’s a nonprofit in your chapter. You know, we encourage nonprofits to join BNI because I have found, and Ivan you probably have a lot more experience in this than I do, but in the past 12 years, I’ve been a member and a director, I found [that] BNI members are very giving, and they want to help.

And so we encourage those nonprofits to join BNI so we can help. And it’s not just with money; you know, Beth used to tell us all the time: we have three things that we ask for in our community, that’s your time, your talent, or your treasure.

And so I’m going to ask our listeners, what are you giving back to the community? Are you giving one of those three? And if you’re not, I’m going to encourage you to get involved because kind of like our number one core value of Givers Gain, when you give back to the community, it comes back to you tenfold. And, of course, I’m going to plug… Ivan, you know I couldn’t go a whole podcast without mentioning the BNI Foundation…

Ivan:
And I’m glad.

Becky:
Because in case you didn’t know, it’s my favorite nonprofit. And if you don’t have a nonprofit that you support locally, please get involved with the BNI Foundation and our Business Voices Project because this is such a great way that you can get involved with your community, especially our children. And I’m going to quote Beth because she said one time when she was speaking, that “Children are 20% of our population, but 100% of our future.”

So I’m asking our listeners, if you don’t know how to get involved, please reach out to myself, Ivan, we’ve got a BNI Business Voices crew that we’ve got on the ground, we’d love to help our listeners get involved to get involved in our community. Because like I said, it just gives you that warm fuzzies when you’re involved in the community.

Ivan:
Absolutely. And I appreciate you mentioning the Foundation. For people that are interested in more information, they go to www.bnifoundation.org. We are out of time. Becky, is there any last quick thought that you have before we wrap?

Becky:
Again, just thank you, Ivan for your time today. I appreciate it. If you have any questions, please reach out to me. I still love doing one-to-ones because again, those one-to-ones build those relationships in BNI. And that’s really where the magic happens. You know, we talked about the VCP process. So Ivan, thank you for your time today. I appreciate you and I appreciate your time.

Ivan:
Thanks, Becky. I appreciate you. And for those of you listening, we’d love to get your feedback. What are you doing in these three areas? We’d love to hear from you here in the comments section. Along the line of words of affirmation, Becky, you are amazing. You radiate Givers Gain in a way that I rarely see to the extent that you do. It’s really amazing. And I’m so pleased that you’re part of the organization.

Becky:
Well, thank you very much. I appreciate you, Ivan.

Ivan:
Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, I think that’s it for this week. Thank you both so much for the great information and for that podcast. This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience and help you boost your business. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50, for 50% off all of the audio programs [this code is good for a limited time], and all of the proceeds will go to the BNI Foundation. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: The key to building relationships at home, at work, and in your community is saying “Thank you” and helping others.

title.rendered: Episode 741: Premature Solicitation (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
Note: this is a rebroadcast of Episode 147, which originally aired in March 2010.

Synopsis

Have you ever been solicited for business or for a referral by someone you didn’t even know? Asking for a referral before there’s a relationship is premature.

Someone Dr. Misner had never met once asked him for an introduction to Sir Richard Branson so he could market a product to the British entrepreneur.

Priscilla sometimes gets calls from aspiring musicians who want to be connected to multi-platinum performers who recorded at the studio years ago.

This is not networking. It’s direct selling. When you give a referral, you give part of yourself away. You don’t want to put your own reputation on the line for a stranger.

Dr. Misner would like to invite Education Coordinators to bring up this topic at their meetings.

Brought to you by Networking Now.

Complete Transcript of BNI Podcast Episode 741 (147)

Priscilla:
Hello everyone, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the Net for networking downloadables.

I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the founder and the chairman of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner.

Hello, Ivan. How are you?

Ivan:
I’m doing great, Priscilla. Well, thank you very much. I want to open up with a question.

Priscilla:
Okay.

Ivan:
Have you ever been solicited for a referral or for business by someone you didn’t even know?

Priscilla:
I have been solicited by people that I do know to refer them to somebody who’s kind of important and they didn’t know that person. It seemed inappropriate.

Ivan:
Yeah, but has anybody that you didn’t know them at all and they’ve asked you to give them business or give them a referral?

Priscilla:
I think I have in the past, yeah.

Ivan:
Yeah, most people have. And frankly, it’s a little bit annoying when somebody you don’t know is asking you for a good referral or for business; it’s very frustrating.

Priscilla:
Right.

Ivan:
Well, I met a BNI member in Miami, Michelle Villalobos, she’s a BNI member in Miami, and she calls this premature solicitation. Now you say that fast three times and you might get into a little trouble. But I love the expression, and I think it’s very apropos to some of the things that I’ve seen. I completely agree with Michelle with the idea that sometimes when you’re asking for referral before there’s a relationship, it’s absolutely premature, and I think many people have been a victim of this concept of premature solicitation.

I was recently speaking at a business networking event, and before my presentation, someone literally came up to me and said, “Hi. It’s a real pleasure to meet you. I understand that you know Richard Branson. I offer these really specialized marketing services, and I’m sure that his Virgin Enterprises could benefit from what I provide. Could you please introduce me to him so that I can show him how this would assist his companies?”

Priscilla:
Right.

Ivan:
Okay, so I was thinking – now, what I was thinking was, “Are you completely insane? I’m going to introduce you, someone I don’t know and don’t have a relationship with, to Sir Richard, whom I’ve only met a few times.” I’ve talked about it in my blog if you’re interested in the story and how we connected, go to NetworkingNowBlog.com and look at the blog back in September 2007, or do a search on this podcast because I talk about it in a podcast here; just do a search on Richard Branson, and it will pop up.

“So I’m going to introduce you, somebody I don’t know,” this is what I’m thinking, “to Sir Richard so that you can proceed to attempt to sell him a product or service that I don’t know anything about and haven’t used myself? Yeah, right! That is never going to happen.”

Now, I’m really pleased to say that, with a lot of effort, I was able to keep that little monologue inside my own head as an internal dialogue.

Priscilla:
That’s good!

Ivan:
Instead, I had a little bit more subtle response to this guy. I looked at him and I said, “Hi. I’m Ivan. I’m sorry; I don’t think we’ve met before. What was your name again?”

Priscilla:
Yeah, right.

Ivan:
That surprised the guy so much that it made him realize that his solicitation might have been a little bit premature. I then went on to explain to him that I regularly refer people to my contacts, but only after I’ve established a long term, strong relationship with the service provider first. What was interesting was that he basically said, “Thanks,” and he moved on to his next victim. So I mean, I wasn’t even completely sure, at least at that point, that he even got it.

And so I just wanted to share that story in this podcast, because I’ve said many times that networking is not about hunting, it’s about farming, it’s about cultivating relationships with other business professionals. Don’t engage in premature solicitation. You’ll be a better networker if you remember that. And to me, this is a classic example of how somebody was hunting instead of farming, instead of trying to cultivate their relationships. This is not, in my mind, networking; this is direct selling, and I would argue that it’s bad direct selling.

Priscilla:
I totally agree with you. I sometimes get phone calls from people. This is a recording studio, and we’ve had some famous people here. And I’ll get a phone call from a songwriter or singer who wants to be connected to a multi-platinum artist that’s been here years ago, and it’s always very funny to me that they would even ask, but it’s similar to that.

Ivan:
You know, if it’s somebody you have a relationship with and you trust and you’ve done business with and you know them and they know you, I’m happy to put people together. That’s what my business is all about. That’s what much of my professional life has been about, is connecting people I know and trust with someone who needs a product or service. I’m all over that.

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
What I’m not okay with is connecting people I don’t know, I don’t trust, I’ve never used their product, I’ve never used their service, I don’t know people very well that have used their product or service, and I’m being asked to connect them with other people who I do know well. I’ve often said when you give a referral, you give a little bit of your reputation away. If you give a good referral, it enhances your reputation. If you give a bad referral, it hurts your reputation, and so you don’t want to give those kinds of referrals away.

To be honest with you, I think BNI members get this more than the average person, because we meet every week, it’s all about building relationships. But I think a lot of people that haven’t engaged in the kind of networking that we do where you’re going deep in relationship building, they’re out there hunting, they’re out there looking to bag the big one, and they don’t understand how this process works. And it’s a numbers game, and they just keep hitting people until they hope to stumble on someone who’s willing to it. The thing is, most people aren’t willing to do it, so I don’t know why they try.

Priscilla:
Yeah. Well, I think that’s really great information, and I think it’s commonly done. I think that is happens often.

Ivan:
Yeah, it really does, so I’d love to have the Education Coordinators share this one at your chapter meetings. Be careful as you name the topic; don’t say it quickly. I think it’s a real hoot, and I want to thank Michelle Villalobos for the phrase, because I think it’s one of those things that really helps you remember something that you want to steer away from, that you don’t want to try to do.

Priscilla:
Okay, great.

Ivan:
Thanks, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
You’re welcome. Thank you, Dr. Misner.

I think that’s it for this week. I’d just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the Net for networking downloadables. Thanks so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we hope you’ll join us next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Asking for a referral before there’s a relationship is not networking. It’s direct selling, and it’s never welcome.

title.rendered: Episode 740: Adding Subscription Revenue to Your Business

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Robert Skrob joins Dr. Misner this week to explain how small businesses can expand their revenue exponentially through subscriptions. Customers have become increasingly amenable to buying subscriptions to all kinds of goods and services.

Subscriptions help your local business because

  • You lock the customer in for repeat business.
  • Subscriptions add a revenue stream that it doesn’t cost anything extra to fulfill.
  • Businesses that have subscription components sell at a higher valuation.

Not sure how subscriptions fit into your business? Download Robert’s free ebook at www.9subscriptionmodels.com to learn about the nine different subscription models and the four key growth drivers for each. (If the link times out, click the button below.)

Download Ebook

Complete Transcript of Episode 740

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I have been Zooming all around the world. I’ve been to China; I just recently did a region in northern California. And you know, I love meeting in person. But being able to Zoom around the world is actually pretty cool, and I’ve been enjoying it.

Priscilla:
Wow, that sounds good.

Ivan:
Today I have one of my most frequent guests on BNI Podcast, and that is Robert Skrob. Robert co-authored, with Graham Weihmiller and myself, the book, The Connector Effect. It’s a book focused on helping BNI chapters grow by demonstrating the value of members to becoming a Master Connector. Robert is also the author of Retention Point, and a consultant for Fortune 500 companies within the US, and companies around the world, in growing their subscription revenue.

Plus, he has a brand new book that teaches any business how to add subscription revenue to their business. The book is called The 9 Proven Models for Exponential Subscription Growth. And you may be wondering, how does this apply to you? And we’re going to talk a little bit about that. Robert, welcome back to BNI Podcast.

Robert:
Oh, it’s an absolute honor. And my goodness, congratulations on 37 years, it is a remarkable achievement to see what BNI has become from one chapter to chapters all the way around the world. Really, you’ve been an inspiration and leader for thousands of businesses. And I just want to say congratulations and thank you.

Ivan:
Oh, thank you, Robert, I appreciate that. You know, it’s been Mr. Toad’s wild ride. You know, I think only people of a certain age probably recognize that as a ride on the old Disneyland. But it’s been pretty crazy. To tell you the truth, it’s humbling to see what BNI has become. And I appreciate your comments very much.

Robert:
Thank you. I really had a passion of writing this book, The 9 Proven Models for Exponential Subscription Growth for small businesses around the world. Seeing how customers are increasingly looking for subscription offers and are more amenable to buying a subscription to a restaurant or to a professional service or to a cleaning service or to the types of businesses that they would otherwise frequent.

And for the small business person, this could be a very lucrative opportunity so that rather than getting a customer to sell a single transaction, they’re able to get a customer that pre-approves a series of subscription transactions, which allows them to increase their customer value, and often really disrupt their industry because they’re able to offer customers at a great price, a tremendous value, because it’s a subscription and has a longer lifetime value, that other businesses can’t. So this is certainly a unique topic that kind of departs from some of the other things that we’ve talked about, but I absolutely appreciate you having me on to share this book with all the members.

Ivan:
