My pleasure. I think most businesses don’t really think of themselves as having the opportunity to do a subscription model. But I think you would argue that in fact, most businesses do have that ability. Do you want to talk about that for a moment?

Robert:
Yeah, I think that it’s a tremendous opportunity. And you see a lot of large companies offering subscriptions. And when you look a little closer, you’ll see that small businesses everywhere are beginning to offer them as well. A lot of businesses use a VIP model, even like a restaurant – that would be a great example where, VIP members or subscribers are able to come during certain hours to the restaurant that non-VIP members are not invited to. Or VIP members get exclusive access to certain tables that others have to wait in line for the rest of the tables. Or they’re able to get dishes or meals or something that non-subscribers aren’t able to get access to. And that’s just one type of model.

But you know what a subscription can also do is, if you have a pre-approved supplier business where you are providing goods or services, you can invite your customer to have a subscription to that. Similar to what Dollar Shave Club did. And Dollar Shave Club was able to completely disrupt the razor business. And now you see a whole lot of copycats in a lot of different niches, that instead of buying razors for several dollars, you were able to buy razors for a lot less. And the reason was that because they didn’t have the distribution system, and because they’re going direct to customer, and also because they were having it on a subscription where the customer had pre-approved several orders and thus they had a higher lifetime value, Dollar Shave Club was able to make that work and disrupt an entire industry.

And you know, why not, in your own business, is there a way to reward your most reliable customers to give them an opportunity to upgrade to a VIP status, or to invite your customers to pre-order where they’re able to receive a certain amount of goods on a regular basis that’s pre-approved and going to be delivered, to kind of avoid the hassle and back and forth. It kind of is one less thing they have to deal with, and for your business, it generates an ongoing subscription revenue. And that’s just two of the nine models, but they’re completely accessible for any type of business.

Ivan:
Well, let’s talk about that for a second. What businesses — I mean, you know, BNI well; you wrote the book about BNI with Graham and I — what businesses do you think would say, “Well, I don’t see how I could have a subscription?” What businesses do you think would say that, that in fact, do have the opportunity to do a subscription model?


Robert:
Yeah, it’s kind of interesting, because actually, quite a few businesses already do, you know, from landscaping to pest control; they’re already a regular service business. But even CPAs, I actually have worked with a number of CPAs to have an audit defense subscription, where, particularly in the USA, you pay an annual fee and then if you’re ever audited, they come to your defense and that way you don’t have the expense of paying them per hour at that time. Even roofers could have an annual gutter cleaning services, sold together with their with a roof or as an add on. I’ve worked with air conditioning businesses and plumbers, that added a subscription offer so that when they came out to the home and gave an estimate, they said, well, it’s this price in order to do the repair. Or if you would like to become a member, you can enjoy the member discount and then that also entitles you to X,Y and Z.

Ivan:
Can I just say I have a BNI Member who does AC and plumbing, and that’s exactly what I have — a subscription with them where they come out automatically every quarter and check my AC units and check the plumbing and if there’s any problem then they you know, go deeper with it, but they come out and check regularly because of the subscription. So that’s a great example.

Robert:
Yeah, once you think in these terms, for that AC contractor, what it does for them is, you’re not gonna call anyone else, they have lock-in because regardless of what’s going on with the AC and plumbing, Ivan knows exactly who he is going to call for that. So there’s no searching around. You lock your customers in.

Number two, that subscription revenue adds another revenue stream that didn’t cost that business money to fulfill. Ordinarily, you’re buying services or you’re buying a part. Whereas with membership, they’re buying kind of a promise that they’re going to be connected. So it’s a very high margin revenue stream.

And then third, businesses that have subscription components are more valuable when you go to sell them. Local businesses often have a valuation of two to five times the EBITA — earnings before interest and taxes. Whereas businesses that have subscription revenue are often five to even 30 times EBITA when they go to sell. So it’s a huge impact on customer value and the value of the business.

Ivan:
Most members may not know what EBITA is, go check it out on Google, it’s earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization. We’ve only got a couple minutes left, we have a couple of other topics. Real quick, what stops most businesses from succeeding when they attempt to add subscription revenue?

Robert:
This was really the reason why I created the book, and why I really want to give it away to as many businesses that want it, is I’ve seen so many people try to create a subscription, but they swipe this idea from one and then they swipe another and they end up with this Frankenstein-like model that combines a bunch of different things that just doesn’t work. And so they reach this, what I call this subscription growth ceiling, it just becomes frustrating, and it doesn’t go anywhere. And so what I wanted to help businesses see is that for each different subscription model, there are four key growth drivers that will allow them to become successful.

Ivan:
Any last thoughts before we wrap up today, Robert?

Robert:
You know, I really love BNI, and what you’ve done, and the members. And I wanted to make the book completely free to BNI Members. So there’s a URL at www.9subscriptionmodels.com. That’s the numeral nine, and then subscription models.com. And you can download the electronic version of the book there and take a look and see what types of subscription businesses you could add to your own business. And I think it could be a huge new revenue opportunity for you in the new year.

Ivan:
Well, Robert, thank you so much for offering this as a free download for BNI Members and we will put the link in the podcast. It’s www.9subscriptionmodels.com. Robert, thanks so much for being on BNI Podcast again, I appreciate you.

Robert:
It’s an absolute honor. And congratulations again on 37 amazing years! You have built a legacy and every day are practicing Givers Gain by helping small businesses around the world.

Ivan:
Thank you, my friend. I appreciate it. Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, perfect. Thank you so much to both of you for the great information. This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs are going to provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience and help you boost your business. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com Use the promo code Ivan50 for 50% off all of the audio programs [this code is good for a limited time], and all of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

excerpt.rendered: Robert Skrob joins Dr. Misner this week to share how small businesses can expand their revenue exponentially through subscriptions.

title.rendered: Episode 739: Networking: A Matter of Life or Death

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Terry Hamill from BNI Portugal and Corporate Connections joins Dr. Misner this week to share what he teaches master’s degree students about networking at the Nova School of Business and Economics in Lisbon. Here are a few of his recommendations.

  • Develop clarity about your goals and the tenacity to stick with them.
  • Tell your story in a way that captures attention and links to what you do now.
  • Ask your fellow BNI members for feedback on your story and help connecting it to your goals.
  • Set up your own network of people that you want to be around, and find common ground with them.
  • Listen and ask questions to connect on a personal level.

Complete Transcript of Episode 739

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you today?

Ivan:
Well, I have been all over the place in the last week or so, Zooming around the world. I recently did a live Inner Circle for U.S. Executive Leadership Teams and continue to visit regions via our online platform.

I’m really excited to have a good friend, longtime friend and BNI Director, as a guest today, Terry Hamill. Terry has been part of BNI for 22 years, and he’s from the UK, but now lives in the beautiful coastline of Lisbon, as the National Director for BNI Portugal. He’s also Co-National Director for Corporate Connections, which is an affiliate, a subsidiary of BNI. And one of Terry’s passions is helping young people through speaking engagements and seminars and teaching them how to network and build their career strategy. And he was invited to become a faculty member of the prestigious SingularityU in Portugal. And that’s where this title comes from. Terry, two things. First, welcome to the BNI Podcast. Second, why this title?

Terry:
Well, thank you, Ivan, for having me. It’s only been 22 years since the invitation that I got a chance to do a podcast with you. And I’m delighted to share this story because I think the journey of this delivery inside the university is a really funny one.

Well, let me answer your question, first of all, about the title because right now I’m working in Nova University, which is the most prestigious university in Portugal. And they teach economics, finance, and management. So, you know, those students get a chance to go to another seminar to inspire themselves. And I thought, you know, it’s got to be a sexy enough title that they choose to go there.

So I thought, wait a minute, your network and a matter of life and death, let’s spice it up. And I can tell you every single time it’s listed, it sells out within two hours, just because of the title. I think they are upping the numbers to an extra 100 students per session, because they can’t cram enough people into the two days. So it’s really cool. It’s really cool.

Ivan:
Well, I want to, at some point here in this podcast, have you answer how it’s a matter of life and death. But I know there’s a couple of other things that you want to talk about. I know that you’re not the typical university professor. Do you want to talk for a moment about that before we get into the other topic?

Terry:
Yeah, I mean, Ivan, I was a dropout at 15; I didn’t qualify for anything. I think I do have one qualification, which is the half-length of a swimming pool, which I sewed the badge to my shorts when I was a kid. But I was a dropout, became a hairdresser, and that was the beginning of relationship building and networking. When I go to the University, everybody there addresses me as the professor. And that’s the first thing that I play down. I say, “Look, I’m not a professor, but I’m going to teach you networking so that you get the career of your dreams.” So it’s a really interesting dynamic, someone coming from a non-academic background, working with such brilliant minds. Because this is the top 5% of students in the country; it’s a really, really smart university. So it’s an interesting gig for me.

Ivan:
That’s got to be exciting for you to do. I’m really pleased for you. One of the things that you wanted to talk about was storytelling. And I’m obviously a big believer in storytelling in business. How do you apply that in what you teach?


Terry:
Yeah, so a lot of the construct of the kind of two days that we have is, first of all, really understanding who they are as individuals. And remember, you’re dealing with two different groups of people right now: Masters students who can be from 19 to maybe 30. And they’re really finding their way, they’re finding their careers. And so the storytelling power is for them to talk in a situation of networking that captures people’s attention, and really asking the right questions and connecting with people on a more personal level. Because a lot of them are looking for systems and processes. But in fact, I’m teaching them how to really step back and work together in teams and say the right things.

So a big part of the two days is about their personal story. Every single student comes to the front and does a narrative about who they are, where they’re going, and the audience gives feedback to them. So it’s the first time they have the chance to really get that, almost like a gymnasium, feel of speaking in front of an audience because there’s sometimes 100 or 150 people there.

I use a really interesting tool where it’s a collaborative on the screen, and everybody shares their stories on the screen, so they can all see together, everyone’s individual stories. And we start to map out and they learn from each other. It’s a big part of it; I’d say probably 50% of the two days is about how they say it, what they say, when they say it, and with whom they say, obviously, about who did they network [with], where are they going, how do they leverage with other people and things? That’s a big part of the session.

Ivan:
So one of the topics that you wanted to talk about is networking up. Is this something that you teach? Or how does that fit into what you’re teaching in the course?

Terry:
Yeah, and in fact, I talk about the whole opportunity of being in the university from a personal story of myself. So it was around about six years ago, I started to—you know, Ivan, you taught me a lot through your books. And so you know, your whole book about Who’s In Your Room?—and I started to have another room and I met the most incredible guy called Tim Vieira, who’s a South African Portuguese entrepreneur, like a Shark Tank guy. And I told him my own personal story, which came from some trauma as a child, and he connected with me instantly, because he was already passionate about helping young people.

We then together, created the most incredible social club; expanded it from 22 people to 650, called the Cascais Curry Club, which is a social club, but it’s got three rules: number one – drink beer; two – drink wine;  three – eat curry; no more rules. And that was a place that people could come together and communicate. And that was where I met the Deans of the universities, the CEOs of large companies, but I would never, ever sell to them.

Because I was the leader of that group, and I was the person that everybody had seen, I was the person who looked after them, I was a person that poured more wine in their drink. They’re like, what do you do? I said, “Well, I inspire young people.” “How do you do that?” they said. “Well, I can come and talk to you about it.” So I got access to the direct line of the Deans of universities, because of a networking up situation.

So I talk about that with the students. Don’t just go through a traditional route, set up your own network, set up your own tribes of people that you want to be around with, and find common ground. And then you get this relational stuff going. And it’s been incredible for me, with the SingularityU gig, I teach vulnerability now with large corporates. In fact, I just did the largest oil company in Portugal, a session on vulnerability, all because of the social networking up Curry Club that I did six years ago.

I teach it, I give them the structure of how to do it, oh and also, I invite them to the Curry Club. Because the students learn a lot from being around a lot of seasoned people. There’s a lot of CEOs that sold their companies, a lot of really good talented people there, and they network with them. I guide them through it, and I get them to be less stressed and more conversational. So I use it as a big part of the session.

Ivan:
For BNI Members who listen to this podcast, what’s one message that you think a BNI Member could benefit from in terms of what you teach at the university?

Terry:
I think certainly clarity; knowing clarity of exactly what they want and be tenacious to stay with that particular idea or topic or goal. Because so many people have, I mean, my kind of brain works at a million miles an hour and I have a million ideas, but I suppose my maturity says that if you’re clear about what you want, stay with that, because the audience get really unclear if you keep chopping and changing all the time.

I think another amazing trait of great networkers is to have a story that’s yours and it’s personal, coming from positive or negative background, it doesn’t matter, linking that to what you’re doing now. So people understand the connection between where you come from, to what you do, but then have a specific ask. You know, ask people say, “Hey, do you know anybody at the university I can work with?” Oh, yeah I do.

So I think it’s about being less vocal sometimes, more clear, listening a little bit more, and then asking for help, because I think people genuinely want to help each other in BNI. But sometimes they’re too busy doing it, they don’t hear the question.

Ivan:
Yeah. We’re almost out of time. Is there any last thought that you have before we wrap up?

Terry:
You know, I would love it if we could roll out this whole networking narrative in all universities. Tim and I have actually just started a school called the Bravegeneration Academy, which is teaching students from 13 to 17 [about] networking, relationship building, visioning, you know, all those sort of tips.

I think there’s an opportunity for all members, if you speak well, if you have passion, knock on the university’s doors and say, “Hey, why don’t we start to talk about these?” because the universities are desperate for speakers to talk about this narrative, and no one’s doing it really well. And of course, I’d love to travel all over the world, but it’s not my thing anymore. I’m 50, living by the ocean, I want to be here. So if anybody can pick up this whole idea of talking to universities, it would be great.

Ivan:
Well, I’d love to see more universities teaching this. The problem is that they don’t recognize, most universities, don’t recognize the value and the power of networking. So I’m really impressed and pleased that you’ve been successful at this, Terry, and I thank you very much. And by the way, I’m happy to be a guest lecturer at one of your events if you would like me to.

Terry:
Really? Because I use your material and references and play some video. So it would be nice to have you live there one day.

Ivan:
I will be happy to do that for you, my friend.

Terry:
Okay, fantastic.

Ivan:
Thanks for being on the podcast. And for everyone who is listening to this – this is the anniversary week for BNI. It is our 37th Anniversary for the organization. And I look forward to many more years in BNI. And I look forward to many more years with you as part of BNI, Terry, thank you so much.

 Terry:
Thank you, Ivan.

Ivan: 
Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
I think that’s it for this week. Thank you so much to both of you for the great information. This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience and help you boost your business. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and use the promo code Ivan50 for 50% off all of the audio programs [this code is good for a limited time]. All the proceeds are going to go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Terry Hamill explains that to network up, you need clarity, tenacity, and personal connection to the network you build yourself.

title.rendered: Episode 738: Richard Branson on Legacy

content.rendered:
Synopsis

In November 2021, Dr. Misner made his fourth visit to Sir Richard Branson on Necker Island and asked him what legacy he feels he will leave behind.

Branson emphasized that your children are your most important legacy, but also mentioned living a full life and being driven by a higher purpose, as well as the responsibility that people of extreme wealth have to invest and create jobs.

Dr. Misner believes that your life is your legacy. The best legacy we can leave is to make a difference in the lives of the people around us, and you don’t have to be a billionaire to do that. So go out and do something special for someone.

Complete Transcript of Episode 738

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, I’ve been Zooming around the world still; recently did a Facebook Live in the United Kingdom for their 25-year anniversary. And I’m just back from Antarctica, and I will share stories with everyone about my trip to Antarctica and planting the BNI flag on the seventh continent of the world, on a future day.

Priscilla:
Wow. Okay, that sounds great.

Ivan:
Yep, it should be fun to talk about. Today I’m talking about my visit to Necker Island back in November. I had an opportunity to go back, it was my fourth visit to Necker, and I spent the better part of a week talking to Richard Branson. And I asked my followers on social media to suggest questions that I could ask Richard, and the one that I chose to focus on was about his legacy. Specifically, what is the legacy that he feels he will leave behind in the world?

So first of all, my impression of Richard is that he’s not really one to mull over his legacy. That said, he is one to contemplate the impact that he has had on the people around him. Surprisingly, the first thing that he spoke about when I brought up the topic was his family. “In the end,” he said, “it’s about your children and your family and how you brought your children up. And how they live on in your name is an important part of your legacy.”

I asked him for an example of how he did that with his kids. He said when they were young, he spoke at family dinner, they had dinner table discussions. And he said it’s so much more interesting if you can throw out a discussion with kids and have a dinner table debate on an issue. And he really wanted them to learn about what’s going on in the world. And he said, “Most people don’t do that. Most people don’t have those kinds of discussions with their children.” So for him, it was a way to guide and coach his children about issues in his community in the world, and that learning is an important legacy to carry on. And frankly, I’ve seen that with him firsthand. In my visits to Necker, he talks a lot about family. Family is very, very important.

My experience of Richard is that he absolutely lives his life to the fullest. And every day, every day on the island, he would swim a lap, or kite sail around the entire island. And then he would go to a nearby island to ride his bike, he would do that often. And in fact, he just had a nasty bike accident about a week or two after my visit to Necker, and really hurt himself but he’s recovering well. He’s very, very physical. I private messaged him on our WhatsApp group from visiting Necker, and I told him that I was really glad he was okay. And I suggested he slow down a bit, but I added that I don’t think that phrase is in his vocabulary. And he agreed, it’s not in his vocabulary. As did the rest of the private WhatsApp group, they seem to agree with that assessment, it’s not in his vocabulary to slow down and he’s, I think 70 years old.

He once told an interviewer that, with extreme wealth comes extreme responsibility, and “The responsibility,” he said, “for me to invest in creating new businesses, create jobs, employ people, and put money aside to tackle issues where we can make a difference, is very important.” Richard said that you must be driven by a purpose that is greater than yourself. And he said, “I’d like to think I’ve lived a full life and made a difference. But in the end, it’s about how you brought up your children.” He went back to the children, and how they live in your name.

You know, Priscilla, the older I get the more I’m asked about my thoughts relating to legacy. It’s like, the more gray hair I get, the more people ask me this question. And to that I answer that your life is, in my opinion, your legacy. Your life is your legacy. How you lived and how you showed up in the world is the legacy that you leave behind. And I think this is a great topic for the end of this year and moving into the next year. In college, I read something that had a major impact on my worldview. And I’d love to talk about this for a couple of minutes before we wrap up today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Great.

Ivan:
I read something that had a really big impact on my worldview, and I’ll paraphrase it here. It’s based on a quote by Jean-Paul Sartre. He says: We all die too soon, or too late. And yet, our life is complete at that moment, with a line drawn neatly under it, ready for the summing up. We are our deeds in life, and little more.

And that’s why I believe my life is my legacy. All of our lives are our legacy. How we show up in the world and what we do throughout our life is the legacy that we create – good, bad, indifferent. It’s how we show up and the things that we do. It’s the sum of our behaviors, the sum of the things that we do throughout our life that really creates our legacy. What are your thoughts, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
Yeah, I totally agree with you. I think it doesn’t appear to be anything grandiose, you know, it’s the small things that are important. It’s like how you help other people in just the daily way that you live your life. And it’s hard to see it as something important, but it’s all the little transactions, you know, all the little experiences that you have with people that make a difference.

Ivan:
Yeah, I think that’s a great way of putting it, it’s the little transactions that make a difference. I’ve said, and I know I’ve said this in a podcast before, that we as individuals may not make a world of difference, but we can make a difference in the world. And we do that through those individual transactions. And I think that’s so important. And, you know, people think that only billionaires are able to make a difference in the world. And I would disagree, I think we can all make a difference. We do that with the people we interact with, the actions that we take. You know, we all have people that are in our story, people that have made a difference in our life. But the thing that’s more important than anything, in my opinion, is whose story are we in? Whose life have we changed? How have we helped somebody? I think that’s the best legacy that we can all leave in the world.

Priscilla:
Yeah, I totally agree with you. And it’s also the most accessible, you know, because everybody has the opportunity to do that.

Ivan:
Everyone has the opportunity, yeah.

Priscilla:
And not everybody has a lot of money to throw around or donate, but they definitely can be a good person to their neighbor.

Ivan:
Yeah. Look, I know people that have no money that have made a bigger difference in the world, than people with a lot of money. And I think that’s an incredible legacy – is making a difference in people’s lives. And I see BNI Members all around the world who make a difference for each other, who support each other and help each other. They’re there, not only with giving referrals, but they’re there to give emotional support, which has been really important over the last couple of years and help one another. And that, to me, is the idea of, you know, a line drawn neatly under it ready for the summing up, is those activities, those things that you do for other people.

Priscilla:
Yeah, well, I just want to say that BNI is a beautiful way to build community and be able to do those things for each other because you get to know each other on kind of a deeper level. And, you know when somebody needs you and when somebody needs help, so I really appreciate that about BNI.

Ivan:
Thank you. I appreciate you saying that. And everyone listening to this podcast, go out and do something special for someone. It’s the end of the year, it’s a perfect time to, to pour into somebody else, and to support somebody else in the transition to the New Year.

So, with that I would like to say Happy New Year, Priscilla, and I really enjoy working with you.

Priscilla:
Oh, thank you, Ivan, and I feel the same way. And Happy New Year to you.

Ivan:
Alright, well, we’re gonna be dark for one week and we’ll start up again on the first of the year, which will be during our anniversary week for BNI – our 37th Anniversary. Thanks so much, Priscilla. I really appreciate you.

Priscilla:
Okay, thank you, Ivan. Well, I think that’s it for this week. This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience and help you boost your business. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and use the promo code Ivan50 for 50% off all of the audio programs [this code is good for a limited time]. And all of the proceeds are going to go to the BNI Foundation.

So thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: The best legacy we can leave is to make a difference in the lives of the people around us, and you don’t have to be a billionaire to do that.

title.rendered: Episode 737: Creating a Life Plan (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 487, which first aired on December 14, 2016.

Synopsis

Every year at this time, Dr. Misner takes time off to do some reflection. Since this is the last podcast for this year, Dr. Misner wanted to talk about creating a life plan. Create a list of what you hope might happen. Wherever you are in your life, this has value for your future.

Here are the goals Dr. Misner had for each decade of his life when he first undertook this exercise:

  • Twenties: Learning Years
  • Thirties: Establish Career
  • Forties: Growth Years
  • Fifties: Top of Game
  • Sixties: Elder Statesman

This year Dr. Misner wants to set another thirty-year plan. Take a day and reflect on what you want to be doing and who you want to be doing it with.

Brought to you by Networking Now.

Complete Transcript of Episode 737 (487)

Priscilla:
Hello everyone and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast, brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I have been in Austin, Texas all month. We are headed towards the end of the year, and I have done a number of podcasts here on BNI podcast over the years talking about how I take a little bit of time off before the end of the year and do some reflection. As a matter of fact, BNI exists because I did this over 30 years ago, and I took at look at where do I want to be in the future and how did last year go? I was like oh my goodness. What happened last year? It was that moment that gave me an opportunity to really think about making BNI a global organization.

So I take this time off every year and since this is that last podcast for this year and we are moving into the new year, we are going to go on holiday for a couple of weeks here. I wanted to talk about creating a life plan. So this podcast is more personal than it is about BNI. It is personal and you can use it in your personal life but also in your business life. And, of course, you can do some planning, no doubt, in BNI with this.

I have always been a believer in setting goals and targets. Literally in my 20’s on one of these times that I took days off between the holidays and New Year’s, I assembled my plan of where I wanted my career to be as I matured throughout my professional years.

I know. I know. It never turns out the way you plan it, but try telling that to a 20- something who has this overwhelming ambition to blaze his own trail, which was me when I was in my 20’s.

So in my 20’s right after college, I sat down and I wrote out my vision of where I hoped to be as my career progressed. I am suggesting that wherever you are in your life, whether you are towards the end of your career or the beginning of your career, this has value for your future.

I happened to have done it in my 20’s. You might imagine as a recent college grad with a diploma in hand and ready to take on the world, I kind of tossed and turned at night wondering that I was going to do and where I might end up.

I created a list of what I hoped might happen. Here is what I came up with:

In my 20’s I wrote down that these would be my learning years. These would be when I pay my dues. Be prepared to work crazy long hours and be a sponge for learning.

In my 30’s I wanted to establish myself in my career. I wanted to pick a professional lane and move forward. That is what I wrote down in my plan.

In my 40’s these were my growth years, build my business, create a reputation, continue with lifelong learning. You will notice that’s always important. Lifelong learning. That happens to be one of the core values, lifelong learning while accelerating the growth of my business.

In my 50’s, I wanted to be at the top of my game, the top of my game professionally. I hoped to have built- this is what I was envisioning- hoped to have built a postitive, professional reputation and a successful business.

In my 60’s, and I just hit 60 this year. In June of 2016, I turned 60. In my 60’s and beyond, I wrote that I would like to be the elder statesman, spend time – I didn’t use these words back then, but now I would call it working in my flame, doing the things I love doing and providing strategic leadership in my business.

I am here today in my 60’s just having turned 60 and I have arrived at this later phase of my list. I have to say that I am pretty excited about it. This life has taken me places I never really dreamed I would go and I met people that will forever change the course of my life. I have seen things that would alter my perspective in profound ways and I have learned to expect the unexpected.

To this day, I am still amazed at how setting a vision helped create a reality in my life. My goal now is to set another – I am going to shoot for a 30 year plan. When I am 90 I will see if I have any more goals and objectives. But my intention this year is to set another 30 year plan and see where I am at in the next 30 years.

Now, you know, if you are my age, some of you may be at the beginning of your career. It doesn’t really matter where you are. If you are my age, you know, no matter what age you are, setting five to 10- year goals, I think are really, really important. The best time to do it is right now.

There is this great Chinese proverb. I know I have shared it in the podcast before. The proverb goes like this. It says, “When is the best time to plant an oak tree?” The answer is 20 years ago. When is the second best time? The answer is “today”.

So that is why I say no matter what age you are at, now is a good time to start creating a vision for where you want to take your life. It may or may not end up where you are anticipating, but I am here to tell you that it is a lot easier to know what direction, what paths to take when you have a direction in mind.

Sometimes the directions may change. What I thought I would be doing in my 20’s is not what I was doing by my 30’s but still the general theme of my 20’s was learning, be prepared to work crazy long hours and be a sponge. And that I did. And in my 30’s I picked a professional lane and moved forward in that lane.

It is not a matter of really having a crystal ball and knowing exactly where you are going to be. It is a matter of having a roadmap to try to take you where you want to go. You can do this at anytime in your career.

I urge you if you are listening to this podcast, do it now. This is the end of the year. Take at least a day before the new year. This is perfect timing. Sit down and create at the very least a five-year plan. What do you want to be doing? Who do you want to be doing it with? My wife and I, every year, create family goals and those family goals may be one year goals, five year goals, ten year goals.

I don’t think I have ever shared this on the podcast. We literally had the name of our children written down. I have that one page summary. The names of our children written down on our first year goal sheet 27 years ago. We knew roughly how many kids we wanted. We knew what their names would be.

There are a lot of things on that goal sheet that we didn’t do. We changed our mind. But there are a lot of things on that goal sheet that we have done. We go back to it every now and then. Certainly in the early days, we said do we still want to do this? If so, what are we waiting for? We want to do this. We haven’t done it on our goal sheet. It is powerful to set a vision and then move towards it.

That is my advice to everyone listening to this podcast. What are your thoughts, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
Well, I love this idea. I am the kind of person that is constantly making lists and checking them off, thinking in advance. On my list for today, was setting goals for next year so that is pretty funny. I just find that, like you, if you write it down and you think about it and you let it go, and you come back to it, it is amazing how many things you may have accomplished that is on that list. It really makes you feel good about your work.

Ivan:
It does make you feel good and to be able to look back and see how reasonably close I came to sort of my life vision is humbling. It makes me feel just fantastic. You know, in my 60’s I wanted to be the elder statesman and spend time working on the things that I am most passionate about. This is it. Podcasts, interviews, writing, speaking- those are the things that are my flame and the things that I want to do. That is what I am doing here. This podcast is a great example of it.

That’s all I have for today, Priscilla. If you are listening to this podcast, please take a few minutes. Take a few hours probably and before the first of the year, sit down and create your vision of where you want to take you life at least over the next year, if not the next five to ten years.

Thank you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay. Thank you, Ivan. I have to say that you do that very well. What you do in your flame is excellent so thank you so much for all that you give us through the podcast and all the other places that you speak and write.

Ivan:
Thank you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
So I think that is it for this week, and I’d just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we hope you will join us next week or maybe in three weeks actually for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: You can create a life plan no matter where you are in your life or your career. What do you want to be doing in the coming decades?

title.rendered: Episode 736: How to Leverage BNI International Connections

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Jay Changlani from BNI Auckland joins Dr. Misner to share what he learned at the 2021 BNI Global Convention about how a small business can leverage big global network connections all over the world. Jay identifies the convention’s value to him with BNI’s core values:

  • Givers Gain: Connect and give referrals internationally.
  • Building Relationships: People-to-people connections and the opportunity to build meaningful relationships.
  • Lifelong Learning: Industry best practices to implement locally and internationally.

If you’re looking for international business, you want to be a member of an organization that does international business. For Jay, it’s the India New Zealand Business Council, which helps him serve not only his own business but those of his BNI family.

Be sure to visit Jay’s website at https://www.orb360.co.nz. And feel free to share your own takeaways from the BNI Global Convention and favorite international organizations in the comments.

Complete Transcript of Episode 736

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan. How are you and are you still in Antarctica?

Ivan:
Yes. This is a pre-recorded session. I am still in Antarctica. I hope to be back really soon; it was a long trip. I have a guest from Down Under. Jay, what time is it for you at this recording? 7:30 in the morning?

Jay:
Yeah. It is 7:30 In the morning, Ivan.

Ivan:
Well, thank you for getting on to do this session with me. I really appreciate it and, and Jay, um, you know, if I mess up your last name, you can call me miz-ner from now on. I believe it’s Changlani.

Jay:
That’s right. Changlani is perfect. Mr. Misner.

Ivan: 

Great. Okay, good. So listen, thanks for being on here. For everyone who’s listening to this podcast, Jay specializes in the franchise sector. And he has commenced this franchise journey as a chartered accountant for both franchisors, and franchisees,  providing strategic and accounting advice. Jay focuses his attention on servicing the franchise sector in New Zealand, across all aspects of franchising through his franchise advisory brand and Franchise Ready. So Jay joined BNI in 2014, as a member in New Zealand, he was a nervous public speaker, and now he’s doing a podcast – I love that, and had zero clients. He started his accounting business in 2015, leaving a big corporate accounting firm. His BNI family members pretty much gave him the confidence and support to start his business. And today he employs 22 across three offices, and he gives full credit to BNI for his business and personal growth. Currently, Jay is a BNI Director Consultant serving two chapters in Auckland. Jay, welcome to the BNI Podcast.

Jay:
Thank you so much, Ivan. First of all, I just want to acknowledge and say thank you to Graham Southwell, who’s the BNI [New Zealand] National Director, and Mac Srinivasan from [BNI], Global Markets, President MFAs and APAC CRCs for the great opportunity to speak to you on this podcast.

So today, I want to talk about this international networking side of BNI. You know how small-to-medium businesses like myself can leverage the international side of BNI. First of all, I must say, congratulations for a very successful BNI Global Convention 2021. This was my first time and I loved it, absolutely value for money. This week, because of the BNI Convention, I’m actually catching up. I’m doing BNI one-to-ones with my BNI family based in Canada, Philippines and Singapore. The Convention gave me so much insight to understand and learn about how a small business based even in New Zealand can leverage the big global network connections across all over the world.

Ivan:
Can I just jump in for a second? You know, [for] our future [BNI] conventions we plan on you know, we don’t know for sure, nothing is certain. But it’s our goal to continue to offer them online and in-person. So we hope our next one can be in-person. But we do intend to also continue to offer it online, which makes it so much easier for everyone to participate.

Jay:
Absolutely. I think, Ivan, what I see from this is it’s a great opportunity for a small business for such a tiny amount to contribute or to invest in, and to connect with the global leaders. And what I really gained from that for myself, the small business like myself, out of the BNI values and it resonated very, very clearly with me because of this convention, I looked at the Givers Gain. And I thought this BNI Convention pretty much gave me an opportunity to connect and give referrals internationally now. I can now connect with suppliers, my clients who are looking to grow internationally.

The other thing was around another BNI value, and that was Building Relationships. This has actually given me a strong opportunity to build meaningful relationships with people, all over the world. People-to-people connection, that’s what I’ve got out of it and the capability of sharing some ideas.

And the Lifelong Learning side of it, I thought there was so much of ideas that we shared, we learned. In fact, I personally connected with a lot of accountants and learned what other businesses were actually doing, and what’s the best industry practices we are learning. And we can actually implement that in our businesses here and international trade policy.

So for me, personally, the BNI Global Convention 2021 was a great eye-opener, to understand and learn about the systems, the process, the values, the culture, and traditions. It was great.

Ivan:
Can I just jump in for a second? I think most people recognize it as a Lifelong Learning opportunity, but they don’t always recognize it as a Building Relationships opportunity. I think members sometimes don’t connect the dots. Many members say I would like to do business internationally, or I would love referrals from international sources, but they don’t connect the dots that actually participating in an international convention will help you do that. And that’s one of the beautiful aspects of international conventions, anybody that wants to do business internationally, or even if you want to just get business through international referrals. I mean, I’ve seen dentists get business referrals; I’ve seen a landscape architect get business from international connections that were made locally. And so I think the building relationships piece is connecting the dots for BNI. effectively. What do you think?

Jay: 
Absolutely, I think, Ivan also I think this technology, the world has changed so much now, like I’ve got a situation in New Zealand. I’ve got a person who does a greenhouse, and he’s developed a fantastic sort of, you know, the technology. Now, that can easily be replicated in Southeast Asia. Especially I’m looking at an organization in India, looking to scale that and taking the technology and expanding that all over India and other parts of the world. So what that has done is first I think, end of the day, people do business with people, you know, the connection. And once that resonates, then everything else follows. So that’s what I’ve seen from this Global Convention. I actually want to build that deep meaningful relationship with people I want to connect with, and then the business will follow.

Ivan:
Yeah. So Jay, tell me about your experience working as a Board Member of the India New Zealand Business Council.

Jay:
The India New Zealand Business Council is a premier trade organization in New Zealand, which has been working since 1988. And what it does is it builds relationships between India and New Zealand, and also other countries across Southeast Asia. We work very closely with the Indian and the New Zealand Government policymakers in the bilateral trade space. I’m a Board Member and also, I’m Treasurer of the Business Council.

What I have seen is, by actually engaging at that Board level, I can actually serve my BNI international family more closely. What I can do from that is I can actually promote the international business — they want to come to New Zealand? I can help them understand and implement government compliance and policies, help them to set up their businesses in New Zealand, and connect the distributors. What happens a lot of times, is that people don’t know how to connect with the distributor, how to sell their products and services. So this could be a good opportunity for me to serve my BNI family. And lastly, you know, look at facilitating the international investments or any joint venture people might have. So yeah.

Ivan:
And so, you know, I think the lesson there is engagement in organizations that give you the kinds of contacts that you’re looking for. Which is something I’ve been saying for decades is you got to go out. Networking is a contact sport; you have to diversify the kinds of networks that you belong to. And so if you’re looking for international business, you want to go to something like the BNI Convention; you want to be a member of an organization that does international business. For you, it’s the India New Zealand Business Council, which is a great place to go if you want to do stuff that’s international trade related, and it also helps your BNI Members. So I mean, it’s such a win-win. And I think there’s a real lesson here for BNI Members who want to expand and scale their business.

Jay:
Absolutely. I agree with you, Ivan. I think that’s exactly what I see from my perspective, as well as how do we actually leverage more, engage more, and connect more, and network more and give more?

Ivan:
Well Jay, any closing thoughts before we wrap up today’s podcast?

Jay:
Again, I just want to say thank you, Ivan. Fantastic. I really, really appreciate your time. And I really appreciate my great partner here, Graham Southwell, for giving me the opportunity to speak. I also want to engage more, and serve more, in whatever capacity I can. I really thank BNI for pretty much changing my life. So thank you so much.

Ivan:
Well, I appreciate you saying that a lot, Jay. Would you give our members, our listeners here, your website in case they want to follow up with you?

Jay:
Yes. So my website is www.orb360.co.nz.

Ivan: 
Great. Jay, thank you so much for being on BNI Podcast, you had a great message. I appreciate you, and I appreciate all the work you do with BNI.

Jay:
Thank you so much, Dr. Ivan.

Ivan:
Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Great, thank you both so much for the great information. I’d like you to know that this podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience and help you boost your business. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and use the promo code Ivan50 for 50% off all of the audio programs [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds are going to go to the BNI Foundation. So thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Jay Changlani shares what he learned at the BNI Global Convention about how a small business can leverage big global network connections.

title.rendered: Episode 735: If Seconds Were Dollars

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Mark Seery from BNI Hawaii joins Dr. Misner on the podcast to share ways to visualize just how much revenue BNI generates for its members.

In October 2021, BNI had generated $18.3 billion in Thank You for Closed Business (TYFCB) over the past 12 months. It’s hard to imagine what 18 billion of anything looks like, so Mark decided to translate dollars to seconds. If dollars were seconds, 1 billion seconds would be 31.7 years. 18.3 billion seconds would be 580 years.

If we stacked the BNI Thank You For Closed Business dollar bills up vertically, and then lay them down starting in New York City, they would stretch from New York City to Kansas.

In the United States, a family of four spends about $13,000 a year for their groceries. $18.3 billion would buy groceries for that one family for 136 years.

Always submit your TYFCB forms: they keep you in a mindset of gratitude and encourage your chapter members to pass you more referrals. If you’re not sure how to calculate and submit TYFCB, go back and listen to Episode 524. You can find statistics for global members, global chapters, member referrals, and member-generated business over the past 12 months on the BNI home page.

How many years’ worth of business has your chapter passed? Share your answers and analogies in the comments.

Complete Transcript of Episode 735

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, if all is going well, I’m still in the Antarctic. So this is a pre-recorded podcast. And hopefully we’ll be back soon into Texas, and I’ll definitely talk about how the Antarctic trip went.

Priscilla:
Yes, we want to hear all about it. Definitely.

Ivan:
Well, I have a guest today, Priscilla. His name is Mark Seery. Mark is a BNI Director Consultant in Hawaii, where he lives with his wife, BNI Executive Director, Joann Seery, and their dog, Rascal. I love the name of your dog, Mark, Rascal. Mark has been with BNI for 17 years. And I’ve known Mark for pretty much all of that time. And Mark is a class act. And Joann, of course, is an amazing woman. Mark, thanks for being on BNI Podcast.

Mark:
Dr. Misner, thank you so much for having me.

Ivan:
My pleasure. And Mark, we’ve known each other for so long, call me Ivan, please.

Mark:
Thank you very much, Ivan. I will.

Ivan:
So, I love the title of this, and I know the background to it. And I think Members are going to love this, “If Seconds Were Dollars.” But before we jump into that, how important is it, in your opinion, to submit your Thank You For Closed Business revenue in BNI?

Mark:
Wow. Well, I think that’s probably one of the most important things in BNI, and in life in general, is to demonstrate and show gratitude, and recognition. So when we submit those Thank You For Closed Business dollars, that is acknowledging to the rest of the chapter that this person has generated this much revenue on behalf of another Member. And I think that is extraordinarily important, for several reasons, another one of which is, it lets that person know that, hey, I submitted the thank you dollars, and it’s going to encourage them that they did something right, and that you would like them to do it again.

Ivan:
Mark, I couldn’t agree with you more. Thank You For Closed Business is so important. Submitting it is so important to recognize people who bring in business for you. But what I love is the way you position this. And that’s what got my attention, and why I wanted to do this podcast with you, was talking about the fact that, you know, what would it be like — because it’s hard to wrap your head around billions of dollars in business. We generated $18 billion in the trailing past 12 months in BNI. It’s hard to wrap your head around that. So if second is were dollars, what would that add up to? And you have this great mathematical outline that I would love for you to share with everyone.

Mark:
All right, well, thank you very much. I am a very curious person by nature. And I go on to www.bni.com and I see how many referrals are passed, how much revenue is generated in Thank You For Closed Business, and I look at that number and I share it with our Members. And you know, it is an enormous number; it’s hard for me, just as a human being, to imagine a billion of anything, or a million of anything. And so what I did is I wanted to break it down to something that I could relate to, and that is time. So, let’s see: how much time, if measured in seconds, do you think a million seconds is? And that would be a rhetorical question for you, Dr. Misner.

Ivan:
Yeah, you shared it with me, I know.

Mark:
So, if we broke down a million seconds, that is a week and a half – 1.65 weeks. So that is a lot if we were to think in dollars. Now, how much time, measured in seconds, do you think a BILLION dollars is? A billion dollars is 31.7 years! That is a lot, a lot of time, and actually a lot, a lot of dollars. And so 31.7 years. I mean, how many of our listeners are 31 years old? It’s just – wow! I’m trying to get my head around that.

And then I just took that out a little bit further. And I said, well, how much time, if measured in seconds, is 18.3 billion? And that’s the amount of revenue that was generated by BNI in the past 12 months. 18.3 billion [seconds] equates to just over 580 years!

Now, to give you an idea of what that means, going backwards in time, the year would be 1441, predating Columbus. That is how much revenue BNI has generated in the past 12 months alone. There is no other organization in the world of its kind that can come anywhere near that. And that is enormous.          

Now if we were going to go the other way and project forward, the year would be 2601. Now 2601 may very well be the next time that the United States wins the World Cup. I don’t know, I hope it’s sooner than that. But for our friends across the pond, I wanted to give you a reference there as well. So if seconds, were dollars, just think. And I can give you another example. And that is, if we were to stack the BNI Thank You For Closed [Business] dollar bills up vertically, and then lay them down starting in New York City, it would stretch from New York City to Kansas. That’s amazing.

Ivan:
That is absolutely amazing, Mark. And I love this analogy. 1 million seconds is 1.65 weeks. 18.3 billion, which is what we did in the trailing past 12 months, is 580 years. I remember when we did 1 million in Thank You For Closed Business, and thinking that was so big. But it’s really hard to wrap your head around 1 million versus 18 billion. And I think you’ve done it really, really well here.

Mark:
Thank you, thank you very much. It just blew my mind. And, you know Ivan, I’d like to take this down to the chapter level. When we look at this, and we can actually see and understand what a wonderful gift it is for our [BNI] chapter leadership teams, all of the folks that are involved in a chapter, and the chapter membership, to be a part of something awesome. Because we all live in our smaller economies of our towns and neighborhoods and such. And we are making a huge, huge contribution to that engine, that economic engine, moving all over the globe. It’s amazing.

Ivan:
Well, it is amazing. I think BNI is a great example of, you know, a private economic stimulus. We are absolutely helping to stimulate local community economies. And these numbers clearly show that. It’s really exciting to me to position it in that way.

And there’s just one other thing I want to say, and then I know you have some closing thoughts. You know, sometimes people say, “Well, that Thank You For Closed Business may not be accurate.” But I’ll tell you, I believe it’s very accurate because sometimes people — they don’t follow the system as to tracking the amount of business that you’re generating, which we’ve talked about in podcasts before — go back and look. But what is also forgotten is that many times people don’t enter the Thank You For Closed Business. Have you ever seen that, Mark, where they just forget to enter it?

Mark:
I have seen that.

Ivan:
Yeah, so it goes both ways. Sometimes people don’t use the proper calculation system, which you can find on BNI Podcast, but many, many times they don’t even enter it. So this $18 billion in Thank You For Closed Business, US dollars, I think is very accurate.

Mark:
And, you know, I was just looking at the average. In the United States, a family of four, spends about $13,000 a year for their groceries. And something else that people can understand, if we were to take that and play that out, that would buy groceries for that one family for 136 years. Staggering.

Ivan:
Wow, that’s amazing.

Mark:
So, you know, I guess my closing thoughts, Ivan, is that one, I think it’s so important that people do put in their Thank You For Closed Business. I think it’s very, very important to say thank you, to have a mindset of gratitude. And I think that our Leadership Teams and our Director Consultants know just how much they are impacting everywhere, families around the globe, whether they’re in BNI or not, this is an economic engine that is moving forward. And it doesn’t matter what comes. I’ve heard you say before, during challenges, BNI thrives. During the best of economies, BNI thrives. And I’m so blessed to have been a part of it for 17 years, and I just look forward to the future.

Ivan:
Well, thank you, Mark. I really appreciate you and you have been such a supporter of BNI, and a supporter of my content and material for years. And I recognize that, and I appreciate that. I mentioned the past podcasts that we’ve done on tracking business. Go to [BNI] Podcast episode 524, to make sure to put in the Thank You For Closed Business properly. And let’s see if we can, you know, surpass 1000 years’ worth of seconds. I just love this concept. Mark, thanks for being on BNI Podcast. I appreciate you a lot.

Mark:
Thank you so much, Ivan. I hope you guys have a very wonderful day. Aloha.

Ivan:
Over to you Priscilla. Aloha, my friend, aloha.

Priscilla:
Okay. Thank you both so much for the great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience and help you boost your business. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and use the promo code Ivan50 for 50% off of all audio programs [this code is good for a limited time]. All the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: How much business gets passed in BNI? If dollars were seconds, global TYFCB for the past 12 months would translate to 580 years.

title.rendered: Special Episode 5: Give Each Other Grace

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Dr. Misner has recorded this special episode because BNI is going through changes related to meeting in-person, online, or both. Because individual circumstances and feelings on the subject differ, patience and respectful, positive dialogue are critical. This is a time to give each other grace, to be patient in an impatient world.

  • Be there for each other. Work with your local Director to help with solutions that work for your membership.
  • Your chapter’s absolute top priority should be to be as inclusive as possible, and to accommodate everyone’s perspective.
  • Your chapter growing with every member is more important than the format in which it meets.
  • Keep doing One- to-Ones, keep learning, and most importantly, keep supporting each other and having fun along the way.

Complete Transcript of Special Episode 5

Ivan:
Hi, this is Ivan Misner, and this is Special Episode #5 of The Official BNI Podcast. I’m doing this Special Episode because the organization is going through change because of COVID, and the issues related to meeting in-person, online, or some combination hybrid.

I want to encourage members to be patient with each other. I want to encourage chapters to remain intact for the benefit of each member, and the overall chapter. This is a time to give each other grace, to be patient in an impatient world.

Today, more than ever, you need your network. Be there for each other. Work with your local Director to help with solutions that work for your membership. Each of you have done such a great job throughout 2020 and 2021. You’ve helped each other pass an incredible amount of business. You’ve saved businesses, supporting families and communities along the way. Great work, I’m really proud of you.

Building relationships is one of BNI’s Core Values. We know stronger relationships lead to more referrals, and more fun. As we begin to emerge from the disruption of the last 18 months, we know many chapters will be making decisions about their format: in-person, online, hybrid.

In that process, I encourage patience and respectful, positive dialogue. We know that individual circumstances and feelings on the subject may differ.

Communication, compassion, compromise, and collaboration can make all the difference in this process. Your chapter’s absolute top priority should be to be as inclusive as possible, and to accommodate everyone’s perspective. We are better together. So, your chapter growing with every member is more important than the format in which it meets. So keep doing One- to-Ones, keep learning, and most importantly, keep supporting each other and having fun along the way.

As we look to 2022, we are committed to helping each BNI Member be more successful than ever. In the meantime, thank you for continuing to support your fellow BNI Members.

Have a terrific New Year with family and friends and keep up the great work. And remember, give each other grace. Be a beacon of light in a sea of fear. Support each other and work together to find solutions.

I am really proud of this organization, and I am proud of you.

This is Ivan Misner, Founder of BNI. Thanks for listening to this special episode.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Because individual circumstances and feelings about BNI meeting formats differ, patience and respectful, positive dialogue are critical.

title.rendered: Episode 734: You Are Not Alone

content.rendered:
Synopsis

The longer you hold something, the heavier it feels. Today more than ever, your network can help you take some of the weight of your business and your life out of your arms and give you relief when there doesn’t seem to be any around you.

Today more than ever, you need BNI to help you through difficult times. Believe that you can pivot. Believe that your network can help you pivot.

It may come as a surprise to some, but it does not come as a surprise to me, that many of the people who believe they can pivot are doing so successfully. And all of the people who believe they cannot pivot are not doing so. If you think you can or you think you can’t, you will be right.

Ivan Misner

Set a vision that makes a difference to the people around you – then hold the vision, not the obstacles. Forget about the noise and distractions all around you.

Hope, plus a Plan, plus Taking Action, leads to Success. We’re going to succeed because we truly understand that Givers Gain.

Complete Transcript of Episode 734

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Oh, that’s a special question this time – how am I and where am I? So, if all goes well, this–full disclosure–this is a pre-recorded podcast. And if all goes well, I will literally be in Antarctica around this time. I’m doing an Antarctic expedition for several weeks, and I will come back with photographs and stories. And it should be a lot of fun.

Priscilla:
Oh, that sounds great. So we get to hear about it.

Ivan:
Yeah, yeah, absolutely. I don’t think I do anything exciting and don’t tell everybody. I try to tell everyone cool stuff that I do–both good and bad. You know, the good things that happen and the challenging things that happen.

Priscilla:
Right, you are pretty open about everything. Well, tell us, what are you going to be doing today?

Ivan:
Well, I want to talk a little bit about the way we view the world. We live in an age of sweeping conflict, widespread skepticism, and intense anxiety. Contention feels pervasive; balanced discourse is a thing of the past. And pundits tell us what’s wrong with society. People complain like it’s an Olympic event. And I’ve checked Priscilla, it’s not.

Gurus in the marketplace obsess over the massive problems they see in the world, and negativity seems to be the norm. The last couple years have been hard. It’s been very stressful, a very stressful time for almost everyone.

Some time ago, a psychologist walked around a room while teaching a stress management course to an audience and she raised a glass of water. Everyone expected they’d be asked the half empty or half full question. You know that question right, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
Right.

Ivan:
Instead, with a smile on her face, she inquired ‘How heavy is this glass of water?’ and answers called out ranged from eight ounces to 20 ounces. She replied that the absolute weight doesn’t really matter. “What matters is how long I hold it,” she said, “If I hold it for a minute, it’s not a problem. If I hold it for an hour, I have an ache in my arm. If I hold it for a day, my arm will feel numb and paralyzed. In each case, the weight of the glass doesn’t change. But the longer I hold it, the heavier it becomes.” She continued that the stresses and worries in life are like that glass of water. Deal with them for a little while and nothing happens. Deal with them a little bit longer, and they begin to hurt. And if you deal with them all day long, day in and day out, you’ll feel paralyzed, incapable of doing anything.

For the BNI Members, you are not alone. All the many months we’ve all been through, many people feel the way you feel…. I feel… we feel. But you’re not alone in another way. Today more than ever, your network can help you. It can help you take some of the weight of your business and your life out of your arms and give you relief when there doesn’t seem to be any around you. Two people sitting in a room at the same table having the same meal at the same time can actually be worlds apart. How? you might ask. Because they look at the world through a different lens.

Ivan:
I love astronomy and in [BNI Podcast} episode number 711, I talk about how I’ve learned that by choosing different lenses or filters for my telescope, I can literally observe different things in the night sky. By changing the lens, the things I view can appear or disappear before my very eyes. Objects can be overwhelmingly bright and painful to view, or they can be a beautiful sight to behold. And I believe that our lives are similar. The lens you choose to view the world through influences your life in ways that will determine your future. What you put in your brain shows up in your behavior.

Charles Swindoll once said, “I am convinced that life is 10% what happens to me and 90% how I react to it.” Now we can debate the percentages that he assigned to this powerful concept. But the truth of the concept itself seems self-evident to me.

You can’t control many things that happen to you in life, but you can control how you respond to them. How you frame your experiences in life influences your life in ways that will determine your future. I believe life has little to do with what happens to you and a lot more to do with what you DO with what happens to you. Today more than ever, you need BNI to help you through difficult times. Believe that you can pivot. I’ve talked about this a lot in the last couple of years. Believe that you can pivot. Believe that your network can help you pivot.

Ivan:
If you don’t believe me, believe the data. It took me more than 12 years to open 700 BNI Chapters.

During the last 21 months, BNI has grown by more than, more than 700 chapters. What took me more than 12 years to do, we’ve done in less than two. During the most challenging times I’ve ever seen, BNI grew. But not only grew, we helped more people along the way.

In the trailing past 12 months from last month, we passed 16 million referrals. 16 million referrals! We generated over 18 billion US dollars’ worth of business for our Members, just during the last 12 months! BNI is an incredible economic stimulus. I have a vision that BNI will become a platform to help businesses get to another level of fulfillment and success.

We are starting to see new ways to help Members access the full scale of the BNI platform, and to become an even stronger force for local economic development. In order to make that happen, you have to believe that it is possible. I see people in the exact same profession who believe it is possible to pivot, and people in the exact, the exact same profession who believe that it is NOT possible to pivot during challenging times. It may come as a surprise to some, but it does not come as a surprise to me, that many of the people who believe they can pivot are doing so successfully. And all of the people who believe they cannot pivot are not doing so. If you think you can or you think you can’t, you will be right.

Throughout my lifetime I’ve seen ordinary people do extraordinary things. I believe anyone can do extraordinary things with the right mindset, plan, and effort. I believe that our vision controls our perception, and our perception becomes our reality. Set a vision that makes a difference to the people around you – then hold the vision, NOT the obstacles. This is the thing that is so difficult for people, not just BNI Members, for people in general. They continuously focus on the obstacles. The truth is if you want to be successful, hold the vision, not the obstacles. Forget about the noise and distractions all around you. There have always been distractions. There will always be distractions, some bigger than others, but focus on your vision.

Ivan:
For me, it’s a focus on helping more people in the organization. I urge you to focus on helping more people in your community. Today more than ever, Members need their network. Today more than ever they need BNI. Today is the Tomorrow you were so worried about Yesterday. Maybe it’s time to set the worries aside and put the vision in front.

BNI truly is a beacon of hope in a sea of fear, and hope is more powerful than fear. Hope, plus a Plan, plus Taking Action, leads to Success. I believe in you, I believe in BNI Members, you can do this. We’re getting through these challenges, through these challenging times, because we have each other to help us believe and achieve. We’re going to succeed because we truly understand that Givers Gain. Those two words – Givers Gain. It’s more than a phrase to me, it’s a way of living one’s life. It’s a perspective to view and interact with the world. It’s an attitude, not an expectation. When it’s applied properly, it will change your life. And when it changes enough lives, it’ll change the world.

That’s my message for today, Priscilla. Any thoughts?

Priscilla:
Oh, gosh, that’s a great message, Ivan. Really special. I think it’s such good advice. And I really admire you for putting it together in such a beautiful way.

Ivan:
Thank you. I appreciate it. And, you know, I think it’s important for BNI Members to recognize that we all have challenging times, all of us, myself included. And the secret to getting through them is that lens that we look at life through, and the ability to pivot, and the ability to focus on the vision and not the obstacles. And the more we all can do that, the more successful we’ll be in our professional life, and I believe, in our personal life, as well. So thank you so much, Priscilla, I appreciate you. And that’s everything I have for today.

Priscilla:
That’s great, Ivan. Thank you so much. Well, I think that’s it for this week. I want to thank Ivan for this wonderful podcast, and also say that it’s sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience and help you boost your business. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com, and use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off all of the audio programs [this code is good for a limited time]. And all of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

excerpt.rendered: Your network can help you take some of the weight of your business and your life and give you relief when there doesn’t seem to be any.

title.rendered: Episode 733: Little Company, Big Vision (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 608, originally published on May 15, 2019.

Synopsis

Dr. Misner realized recently that there’s a story he’s been telling for decades but had never done a podcast on.

In 1986, a year after he started BNI, Dr. Misner was determined to follow Michael Gerber’s advice in The E-Myth and work on his business and not just in it.

He asked himself where he wanted to be and mapped out his vision for where he wanted BNI to be for the next 5-10 years. How many states would they be in? What additional support could they provide members?

All these questions came down to the need for a more comprehensive organizational structure. He sat down to draft an organization chart for the business he envisioned.

Based on everything he and his part-time employees were doing, there were 15 boxes in the chart. His employees’ names were in four of them. His name was in the rest.

It took seven years, but he eventually replaced himself in every role but Founder and CEO. BNI now employs more than 12,000 people.

Wherever you are in your business, this is a great exercise. Write out where you want to be and create your org chart.

If you’ve ever done anything like this, let us know about it in the comments.

Read more about this topic in Dr. Misner’s article on Entrepreneur.com.

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Complete Transcript of Episode 733

Priscilla Rice:
Hello, everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan Misner:
