I am happy to say I am in Italy this week for the Italian conference of BNI, and I love my visits to Italy. The Members here are amazing. And of course the food is incredible. And they have pretty good wine too, which as you know, I’m a fan of.

Priscilla:
Yeah. Oh, that sounds like so much fun. So tell us a little bit about this topic that you brought us.

Ivan:
So I realized, just recently, that there’s a story I’ve been telling for many decades, that I’ve never done a podcast on. And so I decided, I wrote an article on it, and I decided to do this podcast on it.

Little company, big vision. It was 1986. I had just started BNI a year earlier. And the concept of working on my business, not just in my business, really resonated with me. I read a book by Michael Gerber, who I’ve talked about a couple of times on the podcast, and he wrote a book called “The E-Myth”. And in that book, he talks about working ON your business versus working IN your business.

I wanted to work as much as possible on my business and I wanted to do some vision making for my business, and think ahead for where I wanted BNI to be for the next five to 10 years. How many members did I foresee? In how many states would I be operating in it? At that point, I wasn’t even thinking countries, but how many states would I be operating in? What additional support could we provide members? All of these objectives came down to the need for a more comprehensive organizational structure. So I needed a bigger operation to eventually handle that bigger vision of the company.

So I sat down, and I started my multi-year plan by drafting an organization chart. And this organization chart wasn’t for what currently existed, but it was for what I envisioned. And the organization chart was for what I thought the company could look like five to 10 years into the future. So I began by considering all the functions that I was currently doing, and that my employees were currently doing.

I had two part-time employees in 1986, two part-time employees for BNI. Both of them worked 20 hours a week. So it amounted to one employee basically. And I took a look at all of the functions that all three of us were doing. So there it was – I put their name in, like four boxes, I did 15 different boxes, 15 different positions that I thought could exist five to 10 years from now. Their name was in four of those. My name was in the other 11. So there was Ivan Misner – Founder, and CEO Ivan Misner, and I actually put our names in the boxes. And you’ll see a copy here of the organization chart that I did; it’s a replica of what I did in 1986.

So, Ivan Misner, Founder and CEO; Ivan Misner, Marketing Director; Ivan Misner, Complaint Department; Ivan Misner, Janitor. I was listed in all of these different boxes. So what’s really amazing is that that gave me a vision as to where I wanted to take the company. You see, when your company is small, everyone pitches in and does multiple roles.

So I worked at deconstructing everyone’s current roles and drafting an organization chart based on all the functions that we were currently sharing. Then, I put the three of us like I said, into every box that I drew relating to the functions that the people were currently covering, even if they were covering multiple functions, because it was a small office. This exercise, you see, gave me unwavering clarity on what I wanted to do to grow the company. And that was that I had to get out of all the boxes I was in, except for CEO.

Now, this is easier said than done. It was my five to 10-year plan, and I knew it would take at least five years to get there. That meant that I needed to start right now in order to achieve that within a five year period. So I began thinking about the things that I hated doing. And the accounting was at the top of that list. I knew that if I wanted to grow the company, I needed this, and other things, off of my plate.

And you’ve heard me say this before, Priscilla, in my podcast. I believe in entrepreneurs either working in their flame or working in their wax. When they’re working in their flame, they’re on fire. They’re excited about what they do. You can hear it in their voice, you can see it in the way they act. When an entrepreneur is working in their wax, they’re being drained and they’re tired. They don’t like what they’re doing. You can hear that in their voice, and you can see it in the way they act. So accounting was my wax, and I knew it. It was one of the first things that I needed to replace.

Now the beauty of business is that something that may be my wax happens to be someone else’s flame. And I remember the first bookkeeper I hired, she loved accounting. She loved it.

True story. One day, she came to me and she said “The books were off by five cents. But I found it.” Now, it took her two hours. It took her two hours, she said, to find it. But she did. And she was so proud. Now some people may be listening to this and thinking, “Wait a minute. You paid her two hours to find five cents.” Yes, I did. And I congratulated her on her hard work. Here’s why. For me, it could have been off $50. And I would have said, Oh, well close enough. You know, cuz, I hate this. Could you imagine if it were off $500? She wouldn’t go home and sleep until she tracked that money down. Bookkeeping was her flame. And I’m glad it was. So I was happy to have someone who that was their flame, and they would commit to the work to make sure it was done, right.

So each person that I hired, I would put a line through the name in the box that was there, and write in the name of the person who replaced me, or one of my first employees. It took seven years. But I have virtually replaced every single person in that original organization chart. Today, BNI has almost 100 employees at several corporate offices around the world. And it has over, I don’t think members realize this, BNI has over 12,000 as of today’s podcast, has over 12,000 franchisees, executive directors, national directors, director consultants, and employees working for the company in more than 70 countries – over 12,000 people. And it all began with that handwritten vision making organization chart.

Priscilla Rice:
That’s amazing Ivan. Really.

Ivan:
Every day, I look back and think, wow, you know, it’s amazing what this has become. But amazing things –  oak trees grow from a small acorn. The key, I believe, to creating a successful company, one of the keys, there are many, one of the keys to creating a successful company is creating a vision, and working towards that vision, and learning how to work on the business versus in the business.

And that was my vision back in 1986 when I did this original organization chart. And I think this is such a great exercise. If you’re listening to this podcast, wherever you are in business, whether you’re a year old or 10 years old, I think it’s a great exercise for you to sit down and write out where do you want to be five years from now. It doesn’t matter how long you’ve been in business, where do you want to be five years from now?

And here’s the other thing I’d recommend. And then if you want to talk about this idea, I’d be happy to but here’s one thing I’d recommend. Keep the first one that you did. I didn’t. What what you’re seeing here on this podcast, the graphic is a duplicate. I redid it for this podcast. But I didn’t keep the hand-written; it was a hand-drawn org chart. Yeah, if I had that today, thirty-some years later, I would have it in a frame and on my wall. But I didn’t. You know, it was like, YAY I did it! Last one! Scratch my name off, put in the new person. And I threw it out. I wish I’d kept it. I’d recommend that if you’re going to do this exercise, keep it. So that’s that’s my podcast for today. Priscilla, any thoughts?

Priscilla:
I just it’s really amazing what you’ve done, Ivan it’s just incredible that you’ve grown this little company into this very big company. And I’m always reminding the members, whenever I do the educational moment, I’m always reminding them that they are just a little cog in a very big machine. It’s you know, now it’s maybe in 73 countries.

Ivan:
Yeah, it’s over seventy countries. And you know, I like to say, because “a little cog in a big machine” sounds too much like a Charlie Chaplin movie. I would say it’s the tip of the iceberg.

Priscilla:
Yeah, that’s a good phrase.

Ivan:
Because that iceberg, you know, that’s big, it’s large. And oftentimes, members only see the tip, that there’s a massive infrastructure underneath that. I mean, 12,000 people as of this date, that’s not in my wildest dreams that I ever think that we’d have 12,000 people. That’s a pretty big org chart, by the way. I don’t think you can fit that on one page.

Priscilla:
Definitely not.

Ivan:
So if you’re listening to this podcast, I’d love to hear what you think about this idea. I’d love to hear if you’re going to try it or if you’ve ever done anything like it. Please share that with us here on the podcast.

Priscilla:
Thanks so much. It was a great podcast. Very interesting.
Okay. Thank you so much for that great information. For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: If you want to grow your business, create an org chart for all the roles the company needs–and start replacing yourself in those roles.

title.rendered: Episode 732: How You Stand Changes Who You Meet

content.rendered:
Synopsis

This week’s podcast focuses on a technique for in-person networking, and one that can make your meeting seem more welcoming to visitors.

The key to meeting people at a networking event is how you stand. When you walk into a room full of people, look for the open twos and open threes—groups of people that leave space for another person to join them. In an open two, the two people talking are not directly face-to-face, but stand shoulder-to-shoulder at an angle.

If you are hosting an event, remind people to open up their groups. If you have an open stance, you’re going to meet a lot of people that you may not have had an opportunity to meet in the past.

To help you visualize the difference between open groups and closed groups, we’ve included some helpful illustrations from Dr. Misner’s book Networking Like a Pro, Second Edition. Click the images to enlarge them.

Try changing your stance next time you’re at an in-person event, and share what happens in the comments.

Complete Transcript of Episode 732

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Still Zooming around the world. I have gone to a couple of actual locations. I was asked by Jack Canfield to speak at his event not so long ago and had a chance to see Jack again. I’ve done a US C.O.R.E. Zoom meeting – the regions from all over the United States that are run by BNI Global. I’ve been to [BNI] China for a live webinar, Malaysia for the [BNI] National Conference, and in Clubhouse again. I’ve been asked to speak in a number of Clubhouse events; the last one was with Dr. Finance on a great Clubhouse group. So still Zooming around a lot.

Priscilla:
Great. I don’t know what Clubhouse is.

Ivan:
Clubhouse is an online social media platform where people basically come into a room and you can listen to a speaker and raise your hand and ask questions, or you’re invited to be a speaker. And it’s an interesting program. And I do see a lot of BNI people on Clubhouse.

Priscilla:
Interesting. I never heard of it. Okay, great. Well, what are you going to share with us today?

Ivan:
Well, I’m going to share a concept that I have talked about for many years, if you’ve heard me do a presentation, this has been in my talk. And my assistant, Dana, just recently pointed out, “You don’t have a podcast on this.” And it’s a key element of my presentations. And this is aimed at people with an in-person meeting.

So you know, maybe a hybrid where occasionally you’re going in-person, or regular in-person BNI meetings. Or you go to a conference and it’s in-person, or any networking event and it’s in-person. So the technique I’m going to give you today is a technique that I’ve used and talked about in many of my books, about Open Twos and Open Threes. And it is for in-person meetings.

So sometimes you walk into a room full of people, you look around, and you have no idea where to start. None. You think who do I walk up to? Who do I talk to? It’s that moment where some people think, who do I know? And why did I even come here?

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
And what’s true is how you stand really does change who you meet. And so what you want to do is look around the room and figure out where do you start. So I want to make it a little bit easier for everyone. Now, I understand that many people listening to this are beyond this point. And I get that a lot of people really understand how to network and they go to networking events where they know a lot of people, but it’s important for you to recognize that there are many people that are presently having the experience I just gave. And if you recognize them, and that situation, you can actually be a better networker yourself, by understanding the concepts of Open Twos and Open Threes.

So let’s go back to the scenario of walking into the room. I’ve attached to this podcast transcript, a graphic to the podcast that you can download. It is from Networking Like a Pro, the book I wrote, Second Edition. And the first slide is Where do I start? Where do I start? And it’s a view from above, looking down. This could be you know, just maybe one small corner of the room where you entered. It could be many, many more people around you than what this shows. But the idea is, you walk in the room and you see from above all of these groups of people talking to each other, mixing and mingling.

Now the second slide is a slide called Closed Twos or Closed Threes. And if you’re listening to this podcast and you don’t have the slides in front of you imagine that you’re, you know, on a balcony looking down on everyone. And by the way, Richard Quest with CNN, I talked to him about this technique. And that’s exactly what he did on his CNN broadcast as he stood on a balcony looking down at the audience, and he said, “Oh, look at that, there’s a Closed Two, there’s an Open Three.”

And so you can see it looking down. But a Closed Two is two people standing perpendicular to each other having a conversation; a Closed Three is three people. So it’s like a triangle, looking down from above. And it’s really hard to break into those conversations, especially if you don’t know any of those people.

Ivan:
And so what I recommend is that if you’re going to a group, you don’t know a lot of people, you want to look for the Open Twos and Open Threes, which is the third diagram that I have in the transcript. And I’ll describe it for those of you listening, who don’t have access to the transcript. An Open Two looks more like a “V”. V as in Victor. There are two people standing askew, which makes it really easy to walk up and introduce yourself. An Open Three looks a little bit like a “U”, there’s an open spot. An Open Four would be like a huddle with an opening. Or a Five or a Six, the more people, just having an open spot is so incredibly important.

 And now if you go back to the first slide, if you look at slide number one, and you look down at it, the bottom left [image] is the Open Two, the top right [image] is the Open Three. Those are the kinds of things that you want to be looking for. And of course, [there are] individuals walking around. But those open groups are what you want to be looking for, because you can walk up and have a conversation with those people very easily.

Now, if you are running a BNI Chapter, or if you’re even a Member of a BNI Chapter, if you really want that group, the visitors who come to that group, at an in-person meeting, you want them to feel that you’re very open, and easy to talk to – always, always, always stand in Open Twos – Open Threes, and Open Groups.

I had a chapter who did a survey of visitors and asked them their honest opinion of the chapter. And the overwhelming opinion from visitors was: “They weren’t real friendly, they really seem to like each other a lot.” What they meant – they didn’t really reach out to the visitors. And so the Visitor [Hosts] are like, well, what can we do to improve that? I said, “Start with this, just do this; we’ll add layers to this down the road, but just do this.”

So the chapter really made an effort to always stand in Open Twos – Open Threes, Open Groups, so that when a visitor came in, and the Visitor Host was walking them around, or if the visitor broke loose from the Visitor Host and was walking around, it was easy to strike up a conversation. And the surveys, then, from the visitors came back significantly different. They had comments like, “You know, I can’t quite put my finger on it. But it was just so easy to talk to people in this group.” “Very, very friendly group. “And they hadn’t done anything else other than do the Open Twos – Open Threes concept.

So if you are new to a group and you walk in, look for those. If it is a group that you’re a member of, always try to make sure to stand like that, to stand in Open Twos – Open Threes. And that’s what I mean when I say, and it was your suggestion – I love the title, How You Stand Changes Who You Meet. Because if you have an open stance, you’re going to meet a lot of people that you may not have had an opportunity to meet in the past.

One time, it’s so funny, I did my whole keynote presentation, Priscilla. And at the end, I was signing books, meeting people. It was about a half hour later I met one member and we’re having a conversation and he looks at me. And he says, and he points down at our feet like someone’s looking from above, and we were standing perpendicular to each other. And he said, “We’re in a Closed Two.” And I’m like, oh yeah, you’re right, okay.” So we opened it up.

Here’s the amazing thing, Priscilla. Immediately, and I mean immediately, a third person came in. And as soon as the third person came in, we looked at each other and we said, “Closed Three” and we opened it up. And immediately a fourth person came in, we opened. A fifth, a sixth, a seventh, an eighth. It was a large group very quickly and all we did was open our stance and be friendly. And it really makes an incredible difference. This is such a subtle but significant technique in embracing a relationship-building component of your networking process. Open Twos – Open Threes. I highly recommend it. I’ll have graphics from Networking Like a Pro in this podcast. What are your thoughts Priscilla?

Priscilla:
It is incredibly subtle, but it obviously works. And you also have to greet them in some way, right? When they’re coming close.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Priscilla:
And entice them into your circle.

Ivan:

Yeah, it helps if you’re actually friendly when they do step into your group. No question about that. Here’s something that’s counter intuitive. You know, oftentimes introverts tell me they have a hard time striking up a conversation. And this is totally counterintuitive, but it works.

The really introverted people, I would recommend they look for the larger open groups, you know, an Open Four, an Open Five, Open Six. And that sounds counterintuitive. Why would someone walk into a larger group? You walk in, for the introverts here, because you are kind of unnoticed. It’s easy to just slide into a larger group. Eventually, someone’s going to say, “Hey, hi, nice to meet you. What’s your name?” and you have a chance to introduce yourself. But it’s not as abrupt as walking into an Open Two, and now you’ve got to speak because there’s only two of you.

Priscilla:
Yeah, right. I see that.

Ivan:
It’s a counterintuitive concept. But trust me on this, it works. And it works extremely well.

Priscilla:
That’s funny.

Ivan:
Well, that’s all I’ve got for today, Priscilla. Thank you.

Priscilla:
Okay, that sounds great. Well, thank you. That’s it for this week.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience and help you boost your business. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and use the promo code Ivan50 for 50% off all of the audio programs [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds are going to go to the BNI Foundation.

So thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: If you have an open stance, you’re going to meet a lot of people that you may not have had an opportunity to meet in the past.

title.rendered: Episode 731: Online and Hybrid Chapters

content.rendered:
Synopsis

By every measurement, BNI has flourished through the pandemic thanks to BNI online™. Now, as of October 2021, all BNI chapters are eligible to become Hybrid Chapters. Sam Spuhler, product manager for BNI online™, joins Dr. Misner to explain how Hybrid Chapters work.

When your chapter elects to become a Hybrid Chapter, the first meeting of the month takes place in person. The rest of the month, the chapter meets online.

51% of BNI members surveyed in July of 2021 preferred a hybrid meeting format. Another 16% of Members prefer to stay with BNI online™ permanently.

Hybrid Chapters are an option. If your chapter wants to continue as an online-only chapter, you can do that. If your chapter is in an area where it’s safe to meet in person every week, you can do that. But if you want both the connection of in-person meetings and the convenience of BNI online™, you can become a Hybrid Chapter.

Read the transcript below for some BNI online™ testimonials from BNI members.

Complete Transcript of Episode 731

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I have been Zooming around so many places, I can’t list them all. But I’ve done a live event in California with Steve Farber by Zoom, BNI Korea National Conference and other national conferences. And I’m headed to Necker Island again to meet with Richard Branson. And I hope to come back with some amazing stories, not too long from now.

Priscilla:
Great.

Ivan:
So it will be fun, and it’ll be very interesting as it has been in the past. Today, we have a guest on the show, and his name is Sam Spuhler. Sam is the Product Manager for BNI online™ for BNI Global. He has been involved with BNI Online since its inception in March 2020. And actually a little bit before that with some of the countries in Asia but globally, it expanded in March. He helped chapters around the world transition to the online meeting format at the start of the pandemic and has continued to provide day-to-day customer support to ensure a quality meeting experience. He conducts customer research and manages projects geared toward driving BNI innovation and increasing member value. I can’t think of anyone better to have on to talk about BNI online™ and Hybrid Chapters. Sam, welcome to BNI Podcast.

Sam:
Thank you very much, Ivan, for that introduction. It is fantastic to be here.

Ivan:
It’s great having you on. Talk a little bit about BNI online™ and why you think it is effective.

Sam:
Absolutely. So, you know, of course, BNI online™ was formed out of necessity after a few years of the organization recognizing the potential power of it for the future of BNI. But of course, you know, through the pandemic, we were able to enjoy a lot of success in a lot of ways using BNI online™ exclusively as the meeting format platform. And, you know, as we are now a year and a half into BNI online’s life, we of course have kept a very close eye on some of the KPIs, some of the metrics that drive our business.

Ivan:
And KPIs, for those of you who don’t know, is a Key Performance Indicator, the things that drive our business, as Sam just said.

Sam:
Yes, thank you. And, you know, so metrics such as overall membership, and member retention, member NPS scores, these are all metrics that we follow closely, of course.

Ivan:
NPS score is Net Promoter Score. That’s the happiness score, basically, are you happier or satisfied with what you’re getting? NPS.

Sam:
Yes, absolutely. And that’s, of course, something that every business owner should be keeping an eye on with their customer base. So all of these metrics that are listed, we’ve enjoyed an increase in them using BNI online™ largely as the operating engine. Other key metrics to the BNI model, of course, Thank You For Closed Business, and referrals. You know, they saw a small decrease at the start of the pandemic, of course, it’s a very large business disruption that took place last year and they quickly returned to those pre-pandemic levels, both Thank You For Closed Business and referrals, during this BNI online™ era. So, you know, in the last 12 months, BNI has generated $18.3 billion US dollars in Thank You For Closed Business.

Ivan:
That’s amazing.

Sam:
And again, using BNI online™ largely as the operating engine, so we’re quite proud of that.

Ivan:
Yeah, I mean, I get it with Members. You know, I created an in-person networking organization. And I get it that the online has some issues. But I ask everyone to think about this. What if this had happened 20 years ago? What if it had happened 30 years ago? There was no technology like this. We would virtually all be out of business. I mean, every one of our Members would be struggling, even more than many of them are. Either that, or we would be going out and about, and we’d have many more people ill and die.

And so yeah, you know, maybe the online is not quite as good as that handshake. But there are benefits. And I think you’re going to talk about some of those. But the alternative, the fact that this technology exists, is a blessing. And I had someone say to me not long ago, Sam, that she told the member who was whining about, she said, whining about “Zoom fatigue”. And she said “Buck up, Buttercup. You know, you could be out of business.” This is fantastic that we can do business online. Now, having said that, we do have chapters in areas that are gradually becoming safer. And there’s hybrid meetings that are taking place. And that’s what I know the Members want to hear about.

Sam:
Yes, absolutely. So what we do as a product team here at BNI, is we are very passionate about listening to our Members, we conduct a lot of customer research, a lot of surveys. And one thing that we found through a variety of surveys over the course of this year is there is a strong appetite for hybrid chapters, as you referenced Ivan. So, you know, we have officially launched this offering for chapters around the world as of October of 2021.

Ivan:
Is this for all chapters or just select chapters?

Sam:
So all chapters as of October 2021 are able to participate in the hybrid format. And what we define the hybrid format as, again, after a lot of extensive research, is that the first meeting of the month takes place in person. So the first week of the month meeting is in person. You enjoy your handshakes and your coffee at your venues as you usually would. And then the rest of the meetings of the month, take place using BNI online™. So the Members can enjoy that camaraderie, those handshakes, that building relationships that we’ve all known to love through the in person, traditional meeting setting. But also be able to enjoy the flexibility that a lot of Members prefer with BNI online™ too. And, you know, we really recognize that members want that ability to both build strong relationships, while not sacrificing a time savings.

Ivan:
Can I add something? Because I know there are some Members chomping at the bit here. We’re not saying that you have to go hybrid, we’re saying you can go hybrid. So if chapters, if it’s safe from a health perspective to meet in-person, you can continue to meet in-person. We have over 700 chapters, as of right now that started online, they’ve never met in person, they’re likely to not want to go to hybrid. So meeting online is okay, meeting in-person is okay. But I love the hybrid idea. And that’s not only okay, I think it’s a great idea.

Sam:
Yeah, and again, we listen to our Members, we follow what the data says. And 51% of Members that we surveyed in July of 2021, expressed that they preferred a hybrid meeting format that we’ve just discussed here. Another 16% of Members prefer BNI online™ permanently. So in my role and our team, the findings there is really exciting.

Ivan:
Yeah, that’s 2/3 of the membership wanted online or a hybrid.

Sam:
Yep.

Ivan:
What a change over the last couple years.

Sam:
I know and we’ll continue to poll our members and continue to be at the forefront of understanding what it is that they want. And, you know, our team will go act on that to provide a valuable experience.

Ivan:
So what are some of the reasons why some people prefer online?

Sam:
Yeah, through the surveys, we learned that this saves time and money for franchisees, for Directors, for Members. You’re not having to battle a commute or traffic jams or anything once a week. Of course, the flexibility that the offering has, I know you’ve expressed that you’ve been traveling around, as it is safe to do so, yourself, Ivan. And you could leverage BNI online™ to tune into your chapter meetings from wherever you are. And you know, it’s just become really extremely efficient with time and costs ultimately. And we’ve seen in some areas that inviting visitors is more effective, because the visitor themselves doesn’t have as much of a travel commitment. So these are a few reasons why many Members do prefer online, and we will continue to explore that.

Ivan:
So let’s talk for a moment, we’ve got another minute or so about the hybrid meetings. To me, it’s the best of both worlds. Because one of the things that I didn’t like about Zoom meetings or about online meetings is that you don’t see people 3-dimensionally. You don’t see all their body language, you can’t shake hands, in-person, you might not be able to shake hands right now either. But, you know, at some point, we’ll be back to that, and it’s just not as personal. So getting, you know, having one of those a month, to me, fills that void. And then the simplicity and the ease of not having to, you know, get up, prepare yourself professionally to get in a car and then drive whatever distance to a meeting, it just saves a ton of time. And so that combination to me is such a win-win. And it’s something that we couldn’t have done 10 or 20 years ago.

Sam:
Absolutely, it is definitely that best of both worlds. I always say that the in-person format is great for those that like business cards and handshakes, and the online format is great for those that would like to leverage the power of networking from their home or elsewhere, you know, anywhere that their busy life takes them. So it’s really an exciting offering that we are really proud of and really think will have a positive impact on the future of BNI.

Ivan:
Well, I know you have a lot of Member testimonials about the online and hybrid. Read two of them to us here, and then I’ll put the rest of them in the transcript so everybody will have that. Give us the first two that you’d like to share.

Sam:
Yeah, fantastic. So I’ll just read verbatim here: “Having virtual meetings is saving me about eight hours a month between getting ready, and driving time. I’d rather put that time into One-to-Ones.” And this was a Member in BNI India.

Ivan:
Brilliant.

Sam:
Absolutely. Next one: “I’ve been a member for 20+ years, and by far, meeting online has become extremely efficient with time and costs. The meeting runs smoothly and effectively. And it is also convenient to invite visitors.” That was from a [BNI] Member here in the USA.

Ivan:
And I’ll put a few more here in the transcript; one from Africa, UK, Canada, so you’ll get some from around the world.

“Online meetings are convenient and saves time and money.”

Member in BNI Kenya

“I’m fed up with wasting time in a car. If I want to have a 1-2-1 in person or socialise, then I can still do so.”

Member in BNI UK

“Since we have moved online, our attendance has been very consistent, the number of subs have dramatically decreased, and the number of visitors has increased.”

– Member in BNI Canada

“It just makes more sense to me both in saving time and money. Also, I think the real power of BNI is to be able to visit chapters worldwide via the online format…”

Member in BNI Canada

Ivan:
Sam, any closing thought before we wrap up?

Sam:
No. We’re just excited to continue to innovate and drive BNI forward. And, as always, Ivan, thank you for everything that you have done to build this fantastic and powerful network. We wouldn’t be here without you.

Ivan:
Oh, thank you so much, my pleasure. And everyone, I know these are challenging times. Those of us who know how, and are willing to pivot, will be successful and will get through these crazy times that we live in. That’s it for today. Sam, thank you so much. Let me turn it back over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, that’s great. Thank you both. That was great information. This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience and help you boost your business. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com, and use the promo code Ivan50 for 50% off of all the audio programs [this code is good for a limited time]. And all of the proceeds are going to go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: As of October 2021, all BNI chapters are eligible to become Hybrid Chapters, meeting both online and in person.

title.rendered: Episode 730: Time and Money

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Most visitors who aren’t ready to join aren’t sure they can invest the time and money required to make BNI work for their businesses. Shirley Towne from BNI South Carolina Midlands returns to the podcast to explain why those are exactly the reasons they should join BNI.

Before joining BNI, Shirley was spending $5000 per month on Yellow Pages ads for her ADT dealership…and knocking on a lot of doors. It took more than a year for the business to become profitable. Within two years of joining BNI, she had as much business coming to her from BNI as in her most profitable year of Yellow Pages advertising.

The 90 minutes you spend at your weekly BNI meeting are both more fun and more effective than spending the same amount of time cold calling or knocking on doors. Most members recoup the cost of their membership within the first few months. Referrals and personal introductions are a lot more likely to result in closed business than the leads generated by ads.

When you come right down to it, there’s no better use of your time and money than BNI.

Complete Transcript of Episode 730

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am still Zooming all over the world doing a lot of events. I’ve done a lot of live podcasts [one in] India. I did a couple of Clubhouse events – Breakfast with Champions I did at Clubhouse. And I popped into a Northern Virginia [BNI] chapter to give an award. They created The Ivan Award, like the Oscars except it’s called The Ivan, and so I had to just drop in and congratulate them on the first winners of The Ivan Award, and that was the Northern Virginia chapter with [BNI Executive Director] Leslie Hutchinson. So I’ve been going around.

Priscilla:
That sounds great.

Ivan:
Today, I have back a guest who has been on once before, Shirley Towne. Shirley also in the past has owned and operated an ADT Home Security franchise for 16 years. She joined BNI in 2014 to replace the business that she lost from the Yellow Pages – I want to hear a little about that, Shirley, and she never looked back. Shirley has been an Executive Director in the Midlands of South Carolina for four years and she doubled her region in the first two years. She’s launched 20 chapters; Shirley was named BNI USA Executive Director of the Year in 2020. And this is her second time as a guest on BNI Podcast. Shirley, welcome back.


Shirley:
Thank you. I’m excited to be here.

Ivan:
Tell me about that $5,000 a month you spent on the Yellow Pages.

Shirley:
So we spent $5000 every single month; we did it for probably five years. It took us a while to get to the first page in the Yellow Pages. I was so proud, but it was only profitable after that for like a year. We hung on to it you know but the calls just dwindled, and I needed something to replace it because that’s how we made a living.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Shirley:
And so you know I heard about BNI and I kind of invited myself, and yeah, I went to the chapter, there were 36 people there. I stood up to speak and my voice cracked like I was five years old. I was so nervous it was nothing but a bunch of suits just a few women in the room. That chapter today is now 150 members, so it is more intimidating now than it was then, for sure. Yeah, fun stuff but it totally replaced—BNI did what I wanted it to do. In two years, I was at the level of what my highest level of what I was doing when I was doing the Yellow Pages.

Ivan:
Well we’re going to talk a little bit about the concept of time and money and how it applies in your BNI membership. But here’s an interesting question I’d like to pose to the listeners, because I think you know the answer, Shirley.

The Yellow Pages cost you $5,000 a month. If a BNI membership cost you $5,000 a month, how would you be participating differently?

Shirley:
Ahh, golly. Can you imagine the intensity?

Ivan:
Yes! And it doesn’t cost that much. So, maybe we should be participating intensely so that we can be making that kind of money, and that’s your topic for today. So let me hand it over to you. What are the major considerations?

Shirley:
You know, Ivan, it is the topic. But when you put it like that, oh my goodness, my intentionality should have been even more than it was. Anyway, I guess on some level I knew it, because obviously I wrote that check every month. But when I think about that, it’s just crazy to think about. But yeah, that’s exactly it.

You know, I started being the visitor [host]. I started sitting in on Visitors Days on the visitor orientation and every time I did, you know, I could tell that hesitancy to join was over two major items: time and money, our limited resources. We all have limited resources when it comes to time and money. And so, you do want to be a good steward of both of those.

It took me maybe six or seven Visitors Days to realize that every time I heard the objection of time or money—it just dawned on me. Isn’t that ironic, because those two reasons are the reason why I do BNI today, six years after that whole Yellow Pages saga, seven years, going on seven years. Those are the reasons I do it, there’s no better use of my time or my money when it comes to BNI.

So when I’m talking to visitors, I say that. I just like to get it right out and say “Most of you are thinking, is this a good use of my time and money? And you know, those are the two hesitations or the considerations that we hear, and I want to address both of those. Because those, you know, seven years later, are the reason why I do BNI. There’s no better use of my time, and there’s no better use of my money.”

What’s the investment of time? So I always want to lay that out and say, “We’ve got this 90 minute meeting. A very smart person once told me that if you treat BNI like your best customer, BNI will become your best customer. Block it off on your schedule and count it as, you know, like the time with your best client.”

So I think that was a big mindset change for me. But you look at that, and you just think about the use of your time. And then obviously, in BNI we like to have what we call One-to-Ones. And, Ivan, as you have said a million times, that’s really where the magic of BNI happens. The meeting is amazing, and I always learn a tidbit here or there. But when I connect with an individual on a one-to-one basis, that’s how I learn how to be a better referral partner. I learn who their golden goose referral partners are, who can I connect them with that will feed them for life. So those are the two major time commitments: the meeting, and the One-to-Ones. And those are the times that I enjoy in my workweek. And those are the most productive times of my workweek.

And then when I look at the investment in BNI, I think Ivan said it well. I mean, you know, it is such a small investment. For most of us, it’s under $1,000 for the year. And it’s just crazy to think about the fact that I was paying $5,000 a month just to get leads. Because that’s what those were, they were leads, you know, they were calling two or three other people out of the Yellow Pages.

But when I get a referral from BNI, it’s a personal introduction, where somebody has already said, “This is the person you need to speak to.” So I would dare say that when you think about the use of your time and your money, there’s no better use than BNI. Like, you’re not gonna find anything that will compare to it. And you just want to think about what you do as a profession, and how many referrals it’s going to take to break even. And, you know, most of our members do that in a month or two.

Ivan:
Yeah, so you know, what do you say to the people, I’ve got a thought on this, but I’d like to hear what you say to people who say, “Yeah, but it’s a 90 minute meeting. And then if it’s in person, I’ve got a drive to it, I’ve got to drive back. And you guys – phone calls, and oh my goodness, then you want me to do One-to-Ones.” What do you say to those people?

Shirley:
Yeah, I say that you’ll learn that there isn’t a better use of your time. Where can you go and knock out a meal, and hang out with like-minded individuals, that are really kinda in the same position that you are?

Ivan:
Yeah.

Shirley:
It’s that, you know, business can be lonely when you’re an entrepreneur. And when you hang out with other people that are going through what you go through, it’s very uplifting. But also, where else can you hang out with 20 or 30 people or 40 people, whatever your chapter size is, that are going to be out there intentionally looking for opportunities for you? I mean, I think about social media. If somebody asked for, you know, somebody to take down a tree, and you look and there’s nine or ten recommendations, and they’re all BNI people, it’s like we dominate social media when it comes to that kind of stuff.

Ivan:
Yeah. You know, you may be in business alone, but you do not have to be alone in business, if you’re in BNI, a global powerful network.

Shirley:
Absolutely.

Ivan:
I think that’s so important. And I tell people look, you know, no matter what, it’s going to take you time. It takes time to build a business. You’re going to spend time cold calling; you’re going to spend time going out and maybe talking to your existing clients to get referrals. You’re going to do advertising. I’ve got nothing wrong with advertising, but advertising costs money and oh, how do you make the money? Well, you make the money by spending a lot of time doing direct selling. And so to me, networking is so much easier than direct selling. And it’s a great use of your time because it is, it truly is about farming not hunting. And when you’re direct selling, you’re out there hunting.

Shirley:
Yes. 100%.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Shirley:
I knocked on a few doors in my ADT business. And I will tell you, it was not fun. Coming to BNI was fun.

Ivan:
Yeah, look, I get it. I mean, I started BNI because of that. A lot of people don’t know this, and I think I mentioned it in a podcast many years ago, but one of my initial clients, I was hired to train a marketing department how to cold call.

Shirley:
I remember that.

Ivan:
I know how to cold call. I did it long enough to know, I don’t want to ever have to do it again for the rest of my life. Does it work? Yeah, it can work, although I think it’s getting harder. But I didn’t find it as fun. And I think if someone finds it fun, they have an emotional issue.

Shirley: They do – they’re sick. (chuckling)

Ivan:
There’s something wrong. Any closing thoughts, Shirley, on the time and money aspect?

Shirley:
No, I think that when we’re talking to visitors, and we want them to be a part of our network, we just have to be very upfront with them about those two things. And if we do that, you know, I think that we can definitely get them in our room, and they can be the best referral that you could give your chapter.

Ivan:
And I think it’s good to set expectations, too, and say it’s not a get-rich-quick scheme. It’s about building relationships. So it’s going to take time, but that time pays off over time.

Shirley:
Yes, very well said.

Ivan:
Shirley, thanks for being back on BNI Podcast. I appreciate it. And your region and website is bniofmidlands.com, www.bniofmidlands.com. Thank you so much, Shirley, I appreciate you.

Shirley:
Thank you so much. It’s good to get to talk to you.

Ivan:
Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, that’s great. Thank you both. That was great information. This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience and help you boost your business. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com, and use the promo code Ivan50 for 50% off all audio programs [this code is good for a limited time]. And all of the proceeds are going to go to the BNI Foundation. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Shirley Towne returns to the podcast to explain why some visitors hesitate to join BNI and how to address their concerns.

title.rendered: Episode 729: What a Brigadier General Taught Me about Communication (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 581, originally published on 10/24/2018.

Synopsis

This week Dr. Minser shares a story about a very important lesson about communication from the retired brigadier general who taught him management theory.

The general asked the students to write a 10-page paper and bring it back in two weeks. When they did so, he sat down with them and skimmed through them right there in class. After he finished, he told them to cut out all the fluff and come back in a week with a five-page version of the same paper.

When they did so, the general once again sat down and reviewed all the papers during class. He then told them to come back in a week with a two-page version of the paper. Incredulous, the students did so, only to be told to come back the next week with a one-page paper containing all the important information.

The general then explained the importance of including an executive summary with every report sent to a superior, because no one has time to read 10 pages just to figure out what the main points are. This is even more true now that everyone gets hundreds of emails per day. Start with a summary that includes bullet points of the most important items and offer to provide more detail.

What techniques for effective communication have you heard or learned over the years? Share them in the comments.

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Complete Transcript of Episode 581

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast, brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Miser and many other networking experts. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, I just got back from BNI Florida. I have been traveling a lot this month. Poland, Germany, Florida. It is always fantastic meeting BNI members

Priscilla:
That’s great. So what do you have for us today? This is an interesting topic.

Ivan:
Yeah it is. Here is the deal. I received an email from someone I didn’t know. He sent me this email that could only be described as the book, War and Peace. The original printing of the book was 1,225 pages long. His message felt like that to me. It was long. It was so long I didn’t read it all. It was so long that I sent it back to him and I told him this story.

One of the best lessons in communication I ever received as a young man was given to me by a retired brigadier general, taught in the Doctoral Program at USC where I did my graduate work. He was an amazing professor and he always shared the most incredible stories and taught the most valuable lessons. The lesson, of course, was on management theory. He asked us to write a 10 page paper on a specific topic relating to management and to turn it in within the next couple of weeks.

There were only 12 students in the class, and we all beautifully showed up with the paper in hand two weeks later. We watched as he collected all of our papers and sat down at his desk in front of the class and started skimming through all of the submissions right there. He then stood up and handed them all back to us. He told us to come back next week with a five page paper on the same topic. He said, “Take out all of the fluff. Go to the heart of the issue and turn it in next week.”

We were furious. We were absolutely furious, but we did it. Next week, we came back in with five page papers. Then he went through the same routine. He sat down, looked at them, stood back up, handed them all back to us and said, “You can cut more. Make it two pages and turn it in next week.”

As you might guess, Priscilla, we were incredulous, but we did as we were told and we came back the following week with a two-page paper. And as you might guess, he looked at them, gave them all back one last time and said, “Now make it one page and bring it back next week along with your original paper.”

We were beyond annoyed. We were so upset. But we did as we were told. We came back the next week and we turned in both papers. The one page summary and the longer paper. He then shared what I think was one of the most valuable lessons of my academic training. He said, “During your career, you will be working for people who are incredibly busy. They may ask you for a report on an important topic for the company. They may not have the time to read your long, drawn-out papers going through detailed minutia covering your brilliant recommendations. I think he might have been a little sarcastic, but that is what he said.

He went on to say “If you can learn to deobfuscate your writing and boil things down into a simple easy to digest document, busy people will respond better to your work. Always create an executive summary that bullet points the critical findings, the recommendations or advice and put that in the front of your longer report. So this will give the boss the chance to get an overview of the issue and then allow him or her to go deeper into your findings from the full report if they want to.” He also suggested that your summary bullet points make reference to the relevant page, pages or paragraph that this issue is covered in the full report in case they want to dive in further.

This was, to me, incredible advice, and it has served me well over the years. Your communication doesn’t have to be War and Peace to be effective. Heck, the Gettysburg Address was only 272 words long. This podcast is longer than the Gettysburg Address. So Priscilla, this was a great lesson. I would love to chat with you about it for a moment. I hope it is a lesson for everyone listening to this podcast that then they are communicating with someone, especially someone who they believe is very busy, if they are communicating up within an organization or to somebody they respect, who is really, really, really busy: summarize.

The truth is really, really busy people don’t want to read War and Peace in the form of an email. They want the bullet points. If bullet points don’t get their attention, trust me, the War and Peace version of email absolutely not.

By the way, I sent that email to the guy with the story. He didn’t get it. He didn’t get it.

Priscilla:
He was insulted?

Ivan:
He wasn’t insulted. You know, he said, “No, no. You needed to read it all. It was very, very, you know. “Every word I had is a gem.” He didn’t say that but he stood by his position that it was all extremely important.

The truth is I never went through it. I said I appreciate your opinion, but I just don’t have the time to go through it. If you can’t do a summary, I don’t have the time to read it. He wouldn’t do a summary.

Priscilla:
You know, I think that is especially true in these days because I can only imagine what your email looks like. I can tell you that my email, just to get through everything, eliminate, delete, do this, do that and then read what I need to read, we just don’t have that much time.

Ivan:
No. There was a point –  I am no longer the CEO. I am not running the day-to-day operation of the business – but I think the most emails I ever got in one year while I was actively running the company day to day, I am still working everyday, but in a different role as the spokesperson for the organization, the most emails I ever got in a year not counting spam was almost 40,000 emails.

Priscilla:
Yeah. That’s insane.

Ivan:
So I get a lot of emails. Bullet point it to me. This isn’t about me, though. This is really about you, the listeners. When you are communicating with others, give them a summary. Sometimes I will send just a summary and say if they want more details,I will send more details. Certainly when I am talking to somebody who I know is really busy, I say that here is a summary. I have a lot more information if you want some more and I am happy to send it to you.

Sometimes they will reply back. This is really true when I worked with other organizations and I was trying to connect with my boss or superiors. I would say, here is my summary. I will give you more details if you want it. Sometimes they would write back and say no this is good for me and I agree, thank you. Other times, they would say yeah, send me the details. I would do that. I always got their attention with the summary,

Priscilla:
I would rather talk on the phone if it is going to be involved, that is kind of how I feel.

Ivan:
Yeah. Sometimes you need to put things in writing. A phone conversation tends to- in some ways a phone conversation can go almost longer unless you have something to work from to begin with. Does that make sense?

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
So I would love to hear from BNI members as to what great recommendation or what experience do you have that has helped in your communication process with others? What techniques have you used to have your message heard effectively? For me, it was what this retired brigadier general taught me about always creating executive summaries, certainly in written communication. What techniques have you heard or learned over the years? I would love to hear it here on BNI Podcast. Any last thought, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
I think you covered it. That’s great.

Ivan:
We’re done then for today. It’s over to you.

Priscilla:
Okay, thank you, Ivan. Well, I think that is if for this week. Thank you so much for the great information. For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: The most important communication lesson Dr. Misner ever learned was to include an executive summary with every report.

title.rendered: Episode 728: Coaching a Chapter to Greatness

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Mark Breves, the chapter president of the Founder Chapter of BNI, joins Dr. Misner on the podcast this week. When Mark became the president, the chapter was down to 20 members and struggling. After some coaching from Dr. Misner, Mark and his chapter implemented the following suggestions to grow the chapter back to 35 members (and counting).

  • Talk to people you know who have had success in BNI.
  • Interview every member of your chapter.
  • Focus on member recognition and accountability.
  • Use BNI’s training materials and meeting stimulants.

Look for more podcasts later this year about the recommendations that Dr. Misner made to help the Founder Chapter grow.

Chapter Coaching Materials

Download the BNI Founder Chapter Coaching Materials (PDF).

Complete Transcript of Episode 728

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am about to head to Santa Barbara [California] for a training. Jack Canfield has asked me to be part of a training program that he’s got going in Santa Barbara and I’m really excited to be there and to work with Jack and many other people, helping to talk about how to be successful in business. So I’m really excited.

Priscilla:
That sounds great.

Ivan:
I’m also incredibly excited to have a guest on the podcast, Mark Breves. And Mark is the chapter President of the Founder’s Chapter of BNI. And when I say Founder’s Chapter, I mean The. Number. One. Chapter. I think it’s 10,363 chapters ago; it was the very, very first chapter of BNI. Let me just talk a little bit about Mark. Mark spent seven years in Thailand. His family traveled a lot. He returned to California when he was 14. He graduated high school in West Covina. I graduated from Azusa Unified so very close to you, Mark.

Mark:
Yes.

Ivan:
You went to Cal Poly Pomona to study electrical engineering. I went to Cal Poly and I taught at Cal Poly. And Mark quickly found out that he wasn’t real fond of physics, which I can totally get. So he took an entrepreneurial leap and opened a small custom t-shirt shop in a mall in Pomona. And I bet you I know which mall that is in Pomona that you opened up in.

Mark:
Which one?

Ivan:
Is it that walk-in mall that there’s like in the streets?

Mark:
Yes.

Ivan:
I remember it. Well, I remember it being redeveloped many, many years ago when my grandmother lived there. Yeah. So anyway, despite some initial difficulties, like he says he didn’t know how to run a business, he did make a success of it. And today his company is here some 35 years later and making some absolute, unforgettable, customized apparel in Irwindale [California]. And of course, I know where Irwindale is, as well. Mark, it’s such a pleasure to have you on this podcast. And you asked me in a note, if I recalled our meeting at Walters Restaurant in Claremont way back. And yes, I do. Do you want to give a quick review of what happened at that meeting?

Mark:
Well, that’s amazing. I mean, we found out you were going to be in Claremont and I couldn’t wait to go and check things out and see you in person and hear you talk. And I was very impressed with the way you worked the room, you went around and met everybody and shook hands and introduced yourself. And you showed everybody how to network, and I thought it was great. I was very impressed. And there was a great Q&A session afterwards, and I left kind of like, “Wow! What can a chapter be if we follow some of these strategies?” It was great.

Ivan:
Well, thank you. I really enjoyed doing it and I love meeting BNI members. And I remember this event very clearly because it wasn’t huge; I think there was like 30 people, maybe 40 people at this.

Mark:
Something like that.

Ivan:
Yeah. And it was a blast. So you reached out to me afterwards and asked when you became chapter President, if we could talk and if I could help in any way. And I think I actually even made the offer at that meeting that I’d be glad to help because I know the Founding Chapter was struggling. And by the time we spoke, you had about 20 members. It’s now 35 members. And you asked if I would help and I said yes. And so I coached the Founding Chapter because I really want this chapter to continue to do well. And so what we’re going to do today, Mark, is talk about some of the things that you implemented into your chapter to turn it into a 35 member chapter. So, the first one was talking to people who you know. You want to talk about that?

Mark:
Yes, one of the strategies was to realize that we don’t know anything or everything and it would be better to talk to people who’ve had success in BNI and find out what their strategies were. So my Vice President coming in, Marc Vigil, and I did that. We talked to different chapter leaders, and we had you helping us and our Executive Director, Mike Adray, and the wonderful Christine Bond in Southern California.

Ivan:
Love Christine.

Mark:
She’s great, yeah, she was very helpful. So we just asked questions, you know: What are some of the things we need to do? How can we do them? How do you get people to buy in? That kind of thing.

Ivan:
And love you too, Mike; both great people. Mike Adray. What is the next thing that you did?

Mark:
Well, what we wanted to do is, in order to get a feel for where the chapter was with the Members, we interviewed every single Member, and we asked some specific questions to just get a general idea. What do you like most about the chapter? What do you like least about the chapter? Where would you like to see the chapter in six months? In a year?

And the answers were similar. And it was just like, well, there’s not enough members. There’s not enough referrals. We want more Members. We want more Thank You For Closed Business. And I stole one of Christine Bond’s signature questions, which was, “If your chapter membership cost you $100,000 a year, what would you be doing differently?” And they would say things like, “I would do more One-to-Ones.” or “I prepare my presentations a little better.” And I would say, “Well, why aren’t you doing that now?”

Ivan:
That’s a great question. I didn’t know she asked that. The next time I see her…

Mark: 
Isn’t that a great question? I love that question.

Ivan:
It is, it absolutely is because it makes you think “Well, yeah, I’d be working a lot harder.” Why aren’t we now?

Mark:
Exactly.

Ivan:
Great, great question. All right. Give me your next point about helping members realize the chapter’s potential.

Mark:
Yeah, what we wanted to do is basically focus on the core values of BNI, specifically the member recognition and accountability, which are two of my favorites. I mean, there’s so many that are my favorites, but I thought that one might be a good one. So what we started to do was we did a weekly best speaker trophy award where we would award that to the member who gave the best weekly presentation, they would hold on to it for a week, come back in a week, and then they would give it to the next speaker. So that was a way to encourage people to up their presentation skills a little bit.

Ivan:
Love it. Love it.

Mark:
Yeah, we also wanted to continue with the notable networker theme, what we did was we created almost like a certificate, like a diploma from a university, where we got a simple frame, framed it, put their name in it in Old English letters, so it looked real official. We brought them up to the front of the room said, “How were you able to get that many One-to-Ones and that much [Thank You For] Closed Business?” and gave them a chance to shine. So the goal was, they could be recognized because nobody ever recognizes entrepreneurs, and they could hopefully inspire some other Member to do the same.

Ivan:
You know, the Notable Networker awards were started in your chapter. And we actually in the very early days, had a small award, a standing award, and it would sit in front of the member who won the notable networker award for the month, and it would sit in front of them for the month. Over time, they just kind of grew legs and disappeared. So we went to certificates. But that concept of Notable Networker started in your chapter, as did many, many other things.

Mark:
Nice.

Ivan:
Talk about the top achievers. Is that what you just covered?

Mark:
Sort of, yeah; and we also did a perpetual plaque that we sat in front of the visitor table. So whenever anybody came in, there was this nice walnut framed plaque and it had everybody’s name for each month, you know, in terms of Thank You For Closed Business, referrals, CEUs, etc. So they were all recognized as well. So it gave a little extra I think, and it seemed to work.

Ivan:
You mentioned recognition and accountability. Is there anything else you want to say on that? Or do you want to talk about education?

Mark:
Accountability was going to be a little bit challenging. So we implemented, we started basically following BNI rules a little more closely, and started talking about tardiness and why you want to be on time. And despite a little initial resistance, everybody kind of fell in line and people were showing up on time and doing the right things.

Ivan:
It’s amazing how that effort yields such great results, and people are really hesitant to do it.

Mark:
Absolutely.

Ivan:
Education and meeting stimulants. You want to talk about those real briefly?

Mark:
Sure, sure. We basically wanted to make sure that our Members were well trained because well trained Members pass more referrals and ultimately everybody wins when they do that. So we had some chapter in-house educational themes we had, we tried to encourage people to go to trainings, and ultimately more people participated and the results – it’s kind of a no-brainer. BNI has prepared the roadmap, all you have to do is follow it. The other thing we did in the meetings were stimulants, where we tried to make the meetings a little more fun. One of the last ones we did in-person was tie your weekly ask [presentation] to a Beatles song. And it was fun to watch people’s faces; and the tax guy did Tax Man. And it was funny, it was really good.

Ivan:
And just for clarity, he said “weekly ask” – ask with a K. And yeah, that is a fun meeting stimulant. And we’ve got a lot; if you don’t know what we’re talking about, we’re not talking about good drugs. We’re talking about these meetings stimulants that have been developed by BNI over the years. And you can find them, the Leadership Teams have access to them. And that’s a fun one. I wouldn’t do a meeting stimulant every week, but doing them you know, once every month or six weeks, I think works great.

Mark:
It was once every other month.

Ivan:
Yeah, I think that’s great. Yeah. You wanted to talk about COVID briefly, hit upon that.

Mark:
Yes. When COVID came, it sort of changed everything. We really didn’t know what was going to be happening. But fortunately, BNI came through; we just missed one week. And the initial challenge, once the tech issues were resolved, was getting people to focus on the meeting.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Mark:
One of our members, Josh Fischer, sent me a PowerPoint wheel that we use in the meetings to this day. It basically is a round wheel, it has everybody’s name on it. Everybody writes down, everybody’s ask. We spin the wheel, the first person has to recite is up on deck. And then they have to recite the ask of another member. If they are able to do that, they get another ask. So everybody’s focused, and it works really, really well.

Ivan:
And Mark, you’ve been stable or grown during COVID, right?

Mark:
Yes, absolutely.

Ivan:
All right. I want to say this to all the listeners, the Members: I recently just had a BNI Director tell me, “What are you thinking, Ivan? You can’t possibly grow; everything is going to go backwards during COVID.” And I had to tell him, if you think you can, or you think you can’t, you’ll be right. And people, COVID is not the problem. COVID is a problem. But people need BNI more than ever. It can be an opportunity, you can in fact, grow during COVID. We’ve opened more than 700 chapters. I don’t know if you knew that, Mark, during COVID!

Mark:
Amazing.

Ivan:
We’ve opened net 700. Our membership has gone up. It’s all about belief. And what I love about you, Mark, is that you believed.  When you and I talked at Walters Restaurant, you believed it was possible. And you helped other people in your chapter believe it was possible. And I’m just really proud of the Founders Chapter. And I’m proud of you. And I’m so pleased to have you on BNI Podcast today.

Mark: Thank you, it’s a privilege.

Ivan:
Well, before I leave, I know the question that chapters are going to ask is, “Hey, can you do this for our group, too?”  (Chuckling) With, you know, 10,400 groups worldwide, I can’t. However, what I’m going to do is take all of the communication that I had with you, Mark, and with your permission, I’m going to do podcasts before the end of the year, it’ll probably take about three podcasts to go through all of the different recommendations that I made to the chapter. And so you’ll get the same coaching, basically, that I gave to the Founders Chapter. And, Mark, thank you so much. Again, it’s a pleasure having you on.

Mark:
Thank you. I appreciate the opportunity. And ‘Founders’ rules!

Ivan:
Founders rules – absolutely. Well done. Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, perfect. Thank you both, that was just great. Well, I’d like to tell you that this podcast is sponsored by wwwmisneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs are going to provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience and help you boost your business. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and use the promo code Ivan50 for 50% off all audio programs [this code is good for a limited time]. All the proceeds are going to the BNI Foundation. So thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: How can your chapter benefit from coaching? Mark Breves shares actions the BNI Founder Chapter took to grow from 20 to 35 members.

title.rendered: Episode 727: Visitor Contact and Experience

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Jenni Butz joins Dr. Misner on the podcast today to share some best practices for BNI Visitor Hosts, with a timeline and touch points to help visitors have a better experience:

  • A pre-meeting phone call to ask for a headshot and inquire about any special needs such as dietary requirements for in-person meetings.
  • A Visitor Host testimonial script so the Visitor Host can share their own BNI experience during orientation.
  • An easy-to-follow Visitor Host timeline with the best times for different pre-and-post meeting visitor contacts.
  • Visitor slide decks that show how BNI fits into a member’s overall marketing plan.

You’ll find all this material on the BNI Business Builder mobile app, which you can get in the Apple App Store and on Google Play.

More Resources

Download Ivan’s two-hour training The BNI Visitor Experience from Misner Audio Programs.

Complete Transcript of Episode 727

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, I am doing the international BNI Convention this year, it starts this week, I’m doing pre-event of things currently. If you haven’t signed up for the convention, and you still can, please do. Go to BNI.com and sign up for the convention. It’s all online this year, and we will have probably the largest turnout, thousands and thousands of members and directors from all over the world.

I’ve got a guest again this week. Her name is Jenni Butz. And Jenni is a great follow up to the podcast we had last week with Shirley Towne, about the visitor experience. Jenni’s going to talk about the visitor contact and experience. Jenni is a former high school French teacher. I’m so sorry, Jenni (chuckling). I taught high school as a substitute for a while and that’s tough. She was introduced to BNI in 2012 when the owner of a tutoring company she worked for had her join a BNI chapter. She was a Member in the Seattle area for several years before taking on the Director roles for C.O.R.E. – company-owned regions. And she now serves as the Director of Sales and Marketing for BNI in the US. Jenni is also a speaker, writer, and podcast host of her own. Let me know how I did when we’re all done here.

Jenni:
That’s right.

Ivan:
It’s great to have you on thank you so much for being on BNI Podcast.

Jenni:
Well, thank you for having me. I’m excited about this topic, even though I have to follow Shirley Towne. I think we’ll have something to talk about.

Ivan:
Well, good. You guys should chat. So you have several talking points that I’d love for you to cover. The first is the visitor experience and how it can be greatly improved. Do you want to talk about that?

Jenni:
Yes, we have been, probably for the last six or eight months, we’ve been talking about how to incorporate the individual practices of chapters into the overarching process to make the experience better. Starting with contacting the visitor even before they come to the meeting. And so we have produced a timeline to help Visitor Hosts make a phone call. We have lots of automated things in BNI Connect and they get automated emails with lots of information. But we love the idea of encouraging chapters to reach out with a phone call and say, “Hey, we’re preparing for you to come tomorrow. Do you have any food restrictions? Or could you send us a headshot because we want to feature you as our visitor in our PowerPoint deck?” And so we think there are lots of touch points for the visitors that will enhance the visitor experience overall, as well as make the chapter more aware of the value that visitors have by coming and making those strategic connections.

Ivan:
Yeah, and you bring up a couple of points that Shirley didn’t cover. And I think that’s important, like the headshot and food restrictions, particularly for those chapters that are going back, gradually, to meeting in person, obviously, but those are those are great points. So, talk to me about testimonials from current Members, and how that impacts your connection with visitors.

Jenni:
Well, we have been talking to lots of salespeople and successful Visitor Hosts and Directors to talk about the connection of stories. You know, in BNI we talk about launching, filling, retaining, and that ‘plus one’ is stories. Stories are so compelling, and they allow us to make connections with people without feeling threatening or overwhelming. And so we’ve incorporated into some new visitor orientation scripts, a place for the visitor host to talk about their own experience in BNI because, we know, visitors want to know that BNI works for people like them. And so we’ll have….  My husband was a BNI member for several years and we were talking about one referral he got that turned into 13 transactions and over $250,000 worth of business. And when we let our visitors hear that that’s the kind of consistent and intentional introductions and referrals that they can expect, because of the relationships in BNI, it’s very powerful and compelling in that orientation process that makes them want to fill out an application right away.

Ivan:
Yeah, I agree. And the more we can use testimonials from current Members, the more effective it is. I’d like to see the organization, we’ve talked about having one resource, one place, where people can see testimonials based on profession. And I know that there are some regions that have done that regionally, but nothing that’s been done, really, nationally or globally around the world. And I think that’s a value for any Member.

Jenni:
Yeah.

Ivan:
So what kind of materials are available for chapters to really have the visitors have a great experience?

Jenni: Well, one thing that we developed is the ideal contact timeline. So it’s very easy to refer to. And our Visitor Hosts trained on it this year are going to be able to go back in future meetings to find What is the best thing I can do for an online chapter and an in-person chapter a day before the meeting? What’s the best thing I can be doing 30 minutes before the meeting? What’s the best thing for an hour after the meeting? And so they can constantly refer back to that timeline. And it’s formatted so easy for them for reference. So we have that.

We also have some new PowerPoint decks that highlight the importance of how BNI fits into a larger marketing plan. So we want to set the expectations with the visitors right away – this is not something that will take away from your business, this is something that’s going to enhance your business, and dovetail perfectly with the rest of your marketing to grow your business and generate new leads. So all of these things have been put together: PowerPoint, we have a short two minute video that visitor hosts can show, all of this has been loaded up into BNI Business Builder. So whether they’re Visitor Hosts or Presidents, any members of the Leadership Teams can resource, a lot of these documents and videos and PowerPoints to use in their chapters online and on Zoom. Because we know for a while we’re going to be dealing with both.

Ivan:
Yeah. Now you’re talking about materials. And I was gonna say that begs the question as to Where can I get these materials? So can you describe specifically, if possible, where on BNI Business Builders, people can find this material? And is it available to every Member or just Leadership Teams?.

Jenni:
