title.rendered: Episode 698: Referral Flow in Power Teams

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Making a plan for referral flow is the key to the success of your Power Team. Spencer Reynolds, Executive Director of BNI Tampa Bay joins Dr. Misner on the podcast this week to show you how.

To develop a profitable referral flow, it’s important to build your Power Team from people who serve your ideal customers. In their Power Team meetings and their One-to-Ones with each other, they work out who in the team is the logical person to send them referrals, who is the logical person to send them to, what to listen for, and what to say. You need to teach each other how to sell your business.

Example Referral Flows

  • CPA -> mortgage broker -> estate attorney.
  • Landscaper -> HVAC contractor

Why This Works

  1. The Power Team has a plan for handoffs from one person to the next.
  2. Each team member makes the introduction to the next while they are with the client and encourages them to set up an appointment then and there.

You don’t have to have a natural referral partner to make this work–you just have to have someone who’s committed and works with your ideal clients.

Complete Transcript of Episode 698

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you? And where have you been?

Ivan:
Oh, I have been Zooming all over the world. The latest one was in China, but I’ve been in other countries around the world. That is one of the few advantages of not doing in-person, but doing via Zoom, is that I get to talk to a lot of people. So it’s been fun. China was the most recent.

Priscilla:
Oh, that’s great. Okay, so who’s here today?

Ivan:
I have a guest today. His name is Spencer Reynolds. He is the Executive Director of BNI Tampa. Spencer has been with BNI for over 13 years. He started day one in BNI as a Director Consultant. And he tried to get into BNI chapter for three and a half years as a mortgage broker. He finally did find a way in, and that was to start his own chapter, which a lot of people have had to do. He’s now launched over 35 chapters in BNI between Utah and in Florida. And in 2017, Spencer and his wife Tricia moved their family from northern Utah to Tampa, Florida – a little warmer, and purchased the amazing BNI region from Tom Fleming, and they are now the ninth largest region in the United States. And it is truly a pleasure to have you here on the podcast today, Spencer, welcome.

Spencer:
Thank you very much. We started as the ninth largest, we’ve become the largest in the United States.

Ivan:
Oh, wait a minute, did I miss that? Oh, you started as the ninth, there it is. You know what, I misread it. You’re right. He is the number one region in the United States. That is quite an achievement, Spencer. And Tom ran a good region. So, to have you go from number nine to number one is truly amazing. Well done.

Spencer:
That’s done with a team. Thank you.

Ivan:
Yeah, yep. And it always starts from the top, I know how that goes. So, well done. You’re here today to talk about, I think, a really important topic, and that is Power Teams, and referral flow with Power Teams. So tell me what you mean by that, Spencer. Referral flow?

Spencer:
Sure. At a very high level referral flow is who does the referral come from? And then once you get it, where do you send it to? So you have this connection of referrals that happens, and inside of a Power Team is really a great way to see what that’s like. So in other words, you have a referral that comes to you from a specific person, every time, and then who do you send that referral to every time in the flow? So I’ll tell you an example of this. I was in my own home chapter that I started years ago in Utah. And we had a Power Team of folks that were in that group. Well, as a mortgage person, I really wanted to meet with high net-worth individuals. And let’s say that this thing, before we even get into the whole conversation, you’ve got to be clear on who your ideal target market is. I think a lot of people take Power Teams and think that that means that we go with everybody in the trade. That’s a contact sphere. Power Team is really all about who serves the right client. So we got very clear that all of our clients were a specific demographic, they were the same age, we came up with a very clear, call it an avatar, of the perfect client.

And then we designed who would refer who next in the sequence, so it would make it around the group. And our Power Team was five people, and that Power Team with that five people set all of us a six-digit income for many, many, many years. So as we go through this process, I’ll tell you, the person that would refer me was the CPA. So as you have a CPA, they’re talking to a client and they say, “Look, here is all this mortgage interest that you’re paying. Do you have a plan to pay your mortgage off in 10 years or less?” And they normally go, “Uhh, no.” They normally think 15 or a 30 year. And now the CPA says, “I need to book you an appointment with our trusted advisor that can make sure that you have a plan for that. Let me do it right now and get a three-way text going, or a phone call right now, any ways that work for you inside of your business, and how you want to communicate.”

Ivan:
Right. I love making that connection right then and right there. That is so powerful.

Spencer:
Yeah, well, that way they leave with an appointment right there, they’re actually meeting. This is level four in your five levels of referral; this is a level four, you’re not going there with them, but it’s real good. Now, when they’re talking with their mortgage broker, now what happens is I say, “Okay, I want you to be very clear that as you are closing this transaction, that you’re going to be able to put this in your trust.” And they go, “Trust? We don’t have a trust.” And that’s when I pause, and you put the pen down. And you say, “Now, in order to put you in a mortgage with that much responsibility, and not protect it with a trust, I believe, is mortgage malpractice on my part. So I want to introduce you to Rachel. Rachel is our estate attorney, and she can help you set up a trust, if that’s what works for you. Let me get Rachel on the phone right now, and we can set a time when she can talk about the positives and negatives of what that means to set up a trust. And we can even pay for that with closing costs at the end when we’re done with your transaction.” And so that flow has come from CPA through the mortgage person to the attorney and it happens the same every time. There’s no question of where that person is going to be in the process. So you can take this into so many different aspects and avenues and in different types of classifications in your chapter.

I have a very great landscaper. Well, that landscaper knows that when he goes to the job site, the first thing he’s going to look at is, he’s going over to the air conditioner condenser unit outside. In Florida, it’s always hot, it’s running all the time. The first thing he looks is, is there a tree over the top of the AC? If there is, there’s going to be problems over time. And he tells the homeowner, “I would love to have my AC guy come out and make sure that this has been okay, because we’re going to trim the tree back. But it’s been over this for a long time, we want to make sure that you’re not stranded and hot on a day that you don’t want to be stranded and hot. So I’m going to have Gil come over and he’s going to check out your AC.” And that’s his AC guy, and that’s the flow inside of their Power Team.

Ivan:
So you’re just providing additional value to the client by referring other people, which, to me seems so obvious. But a lot of people don’t think about that.

Spencer:
Yeah, they don’t put the flow together. They’re just saying, “Let’s all refer each other.” But instead of saying “let’s refer each other”, let’s have a plan. Because if someone’s going to get a mortgage, they need a trust; that needs to happen as a handoff every single time. If a CPA is going to meet with somebody, they’re going to talk about those things. And so each one of us have that opportunity to say, who sends me referrals regularly? Or who is the person that’s the natural person to send me that? Now I need to build a relationship because here’s the thing, I didn’t know how to say those words, that it’s ‘mortgage malpractice’ on my part. That’s Rachel, the attorney, who’s teaching me exactly the right words to say to refer her. That’s what we spend our time in, enhanced One-to-Ones.

Ivan: Yeah, right. Yeah. Well, talk about that. The One-to-Ones are really important for the Power Teams.

Spencer:
Well, they’re vital. And we do them so often with them, but what we’ve got to do is have a plan and strategically decide what we’re doing inside of those meetings. We’re going to actually teach each other what are the words to say, what are the things that you’re going to notice? Maybe you’re someone who goes into a home. Maybe you have clients come in to you. What are the things that you’re going to be looking for? And teach me, the estate planner, tell me exactly what to talk about and tell me how exactly to introduce you to say it so well, that it’s as though I’m selling you. Because that’s what we are doing in essence. And we wanted to learn from the pro, because here’s the thing, we’re six-year-olds in everybody else’s business, but we’re geniuses in our own. I can’t take credit for that, because a great member in our region, he teaches our Member Success Program and says that to every member, remember to make it simple. If the six-year-old can get it, you’re doing it right. So teach me on language that I can use with anyone to absolutely get that next person in line with the flow to happen so that those referrals continue to happen.

Ivan:
So how do you get to level five referrals?

Spencer:
Well, for level five is when you actually go with the person and introduce each other. So in this time, it’s a little difficult. It was absolutely a piece of cake in the past, because you would go to lunch or go to a meeting point, you’d meet at somebody’s office. We were professionals that all had offices, and so we had a very clear way we would go about that. Right now, you know, a Zoom – that’s as close as we’re gonna get, literally, to them, as close as we’re gonna get to be able to do it. So as we do it and we go and meet with them online, that’s a level five, we’re actually putting it together. And we’re getting them applied. Right? Like the whole point of this is, this is not me now having to sell this client on whether or not they want a mortgage. This is about that person already coming, saying, “So tell me what I need to do”. And when they go to the attorney, “How do I set up my trust?” It’s not, “What’s this? What’s that?” It’s a, not a done deal, but it’s as close to a done deal as you can get with a referral.

Ivan:
And, you know, certainly meeting in person is probably better. But the Zoom thing, you know, I keep thinking back to when I started BNI in 1985. If COVID had hit in 1985, or in 1990, there would be no BNI, it would have completely been shut down. So the technology allows us, it’s maybe not quite as good as in person, but man without it, we just wouldn’t be where we are. And so I’m glad you’re doing it by Zoom.

Spencer:
Yeah.

Ivan:
We’re almost out of time. But I have one more question for you.

Spencer:
Sure.

Ivan:
How can members who may not have a natural referral partner use this referral flow process that you’re talking about?

Spencer:
Well, I think for one, you’ve got to get out of the box. And when we say that, you know, people use that term all the time, but really looking for – Who are the people who are talking to your same client? Even though you may be in financial services, the perfect person that may send you the most amount of clients could be your hairdresser in your chapter. It could be the dentist in your chapter that’s having conversation with your exact clientele, your person, your ideal target market. And here’s the thing, if we’re not good at identifying and painting the picture, if people can’t see exactly the person we’re talking about, and be specific, as you’ve said before, then they can’t see that person to be able to refer them to us. So you don’t have to have a natural referral partner, you have to have somebody who’s committed, who will do whatever it takes to help you succeed. And that’s what we find in BNI, and that’s why we do Power Teams, right? We have this group of people who have got the buy-in to say, “I’m going to support you, we both serve the same client. Now how do we learn each other’s methods to introduce so powerfully that they can’t not do business with you?”

Ivan:
Well, this is great content. I hope everyone who’s listening to this has been taking notes. Of course, we’ve got the transcript – use it, share it, Education Coordinators definitely share this. It’s, I think, the effective use of Power Teams that can really, really have chapters succeed at a much higher level. Spencer, thank you so much for being here today. Do you have any last thought before we wrap?

Spencer:
Well, I just, I love BNI because it’s a growth environment. And your activity equals your success. And so, you know, I think we all need to make BNI a verb and do BNI-ing very well and get into action with it.

Ivan:
I love it. Spencer, your website for BNI is www.bnitampa.com, the number one region in the United States, largest region in the U.S. Spencer, thanks so much for being here today. I appreciate you.

Spencer:
Thank you.

Ivan:
Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, well, thank you both so much for the great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Making a plan for referral flow is the key to the success of your BNI Power Team. Spencer Reynolds shows you how.

title.rendered: Episode 697: The Connector Effect

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Why would you join BNI? To become a Master Connector. A Master Connector is the person that people come to when they have a problem.

This week Robert Skrob, who is also the author of Retention Point, joins Dr. Misner on the podcast to talk about the new BNI book the two of them wrote with Graham Weihmiller. It’s called The Connector Effect and its purpose is twofold:

  1. To help your chapter grow by improving retention because members have a better expectation of what BNI membership is all about.
  2. To help business owners grow their business, because BNI is the single most effective business growth strategy out there today.

The Connector Effect is your ability to attract people to you. It grows in proportion to your referral network and your ability to solve problems for your friends, family, and your best customers by referring those people to members within your network.

The more members that you have within your network, the more problems you’re able to solve. The bigger your network gets, the more referrals everyone in that network generates. The Connector Effect is “Givers Gain” in action.

Get your copy of The Connector Effect at www.theconnectoreffect.com.

Complete Transcript of Episode 697

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hi Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, I’m still doing, I’m still Zooming around the world like most other people are, but I’ve been to a number of countries including the United Kingdom and Singapore for big events. And I’m happy to be able to talk to so many people around the world using Zoom. It’s at least one of the benefits of this crazy world we live in right now.

Priscilla:
I know. And that’s great. It sounds like fun.

Ivan:
It is, actually it is fun. So I’ve got a guest today, Priscilla, it is a good friend and co-author of a book. His name is Robert Skrob. Robert is a marketing expert, and he’s been in the field for more than 20 years. He’s helped thousands of businesses attract and retain their best target customers. Robert’s superpower is simplification. I love that, Robert. I tell people my superpower is persistence. So good combination.

He simplifies people’s messages to attract customers, simplifying what you deliver to improve long- term retention, and simplifying how you deliver it to increase your profits as you scale. And while he’s worked with entrepreneurs in more than 100 business categories, today he specializes in helping businesses grow recurring subscription revenue.

And Robert is the co-author with our CEO of BNI, Graham Weihmiller, and myself for a book called The Connector Effect. And we’ll have a link here in this podcast. I believe it will be theconnectoreffect.com, but it will also be here, you can click on it. Robert, welcome to BNI Podcast.

Robert:
Thank you so much. I’m honored, Dr. Misner, been a longtime fan and absolutely it’s a pleasure to have the opportunity to chat with you again and be a guest on your show. Thank you so much.

Ivan:
Well, listen, it is great having you here. And just for full transparency, Robert did the heavy lifting for this book. He deserves most of the credit. And you know, we certainly contributed a lot. But Robert, this is something that Robert really took the ball with and ran. And it’s a brand new book. And it’s really aimed at not just BNI members, but people that are thinking about joining BNI. Why would I want to come to BNI? and the reason is to become a Master Connector. So Robert, what is The Connector Effect? And why did we create the book?

Robert:
Yeah, I thank you for that. The Connector Effect is a fresh look at BNI, [which] has, you know, been around for 36 years now. And being able to look at it with a new prism of how, really, BNI chapter membership is actually kind of like a disruptor for a small business because it changes everything about how they generate customers, how they connect with other entrepreneurs, how they get information about growing their business. And The Connector Effect was a way of showing Givers Gain in action, that by passing referrals, is the secret key to growing your business.

The Connector Effect, specifically, is your ability to attract people to you. It grows in proportion to your referral network and your ability to solve problems for your friends, family, and your best customers by referring those people to members within your network. And that as your network grows, so does your ability to solve problems and attract customers to you.

Ivan:
Yeah, someone told me once, it’s like a force multiplier, belonging to BNI, which I think is a great term. I know it’s a military term, but I think it’s really true. It’s a force multiplier.

Robert:
Totally. And there’s so much that goes along with BNI membership that we really don’t think about, and me coming from the outside, looking at it and going “you know what?”

You know, there’s a huge movement in the tech world today to try to create gamification. And everybody’s trying to figure this out, you know, similar to Waze, [which] you know, helps you find your particular destination and connects you to other users of the app along the way. So does BNI with your scoreboards, and your referral counting, and your being able to track, it makes making money fun. And so that, not only are you doing it in order to grow each other’s business, but there’s a friendly competition. And the winner is everybody because we grow our businesses. And so being able to bring forward these sorts of, you know, ways that in the systems that BNI gives you as a small business, I was really thrilled and excited about bringing that forward in this book.

Ivan:
You mentioned gamification. And you mentioned Waze, and I think that’s a great example. It’s, I’m almost embarrassed to admit this, I’m admitting it on recording here. On Waze, I wanted to be Waze Royalty. There’s Waze Baby, and there’s Waze Knight. I have three college degrees, but my big goal was I wanted to be Waze Royalty.

Robert: (Chuckling)

Ivan:
I’m very happy to say I am now. But the gamification really is effective, because it’s about, you know, kind of competing with yourself to be amongst the best.

Robert:
Of course. And when, within your BNI chapter of course, the score is based on how many referrals you pass, and the BNI systems that, for somebody coming from the outside, watching members of the chapter kind of keep score by passing referrals, that can feel kind of mercenary. Because outside of BNI, that’s very uncommon. And so really framing something that might turn somebody off, instead really helping to illustrate that that’s a real benefit to them, and that you can enjoy it, and it’s fun, was really the kind of the key of taking each of these elements of this ingenious system that together is worth well more than the sum of its parts, and helping illustrate how they work to somebody that’s new, so that they can really appreciate it.

Ivan:
Yeah, and measuring things, you know, we measure things in addition to the referrals, thank you for closed business, and of course One-to-Ones. But these are all measuring things that make a difference in the success of membership. So it’s not just measuring things to measure things, it’s measuring the right things to get great results.

Robert:

Well, gamification for a purpose, rather than simply becoming the king of Waze.

Ivan:
Yes.

Robert:
Well, that certainly is important, it will not diminish your status, your highness.

Ivan:
Thank you, thank you.

Robert:
But if, when you’re the king of thank you for closed business, you have changed some lives in your chapter. And so having a friendly competition of who gets to wear that crown, everybody in that chapter wins.

Ivan:
You know, that’s so important, what you just said there, I want everyone to listen to that, again. When you [compete] based on, you know, keeping track of thank you for closed business and referrals and One-to-Ones, that as you do those things, you’re changing people’s lives. And that’s the goal here, it’s to transform lives, to change the way the world does business, and to do that through our referral marketing platform. And that’s really what I see as the benefit of being a great connector. So can you explain a little bit for us, Robert, what is The Connector Effect as we describe it in the book?

Robert:
Yeah, absolutely. You know, the kind of the key point of the book is your BNI chapter gives you a network that you can trust to refer your friends, family, and best customers. A group of people who trust you to do what they promise. And because they trust you to do the same, they pass referrals of their friends, family, and customers to you. One of the things that I heard from a lot of BNI members was that it can take a couple or three years before you fully experience the benefit of receiving referrals.

And what I thought is that really discounted a tremendous amount of value that you can get from your BNI chapter, the very second that they accept you into your business category in order to join. And that is the ability to solve problems for your friends, family, and your best customers. And that is really what becoming a, what we talk about in the book, becoming a Master Connector is all about. The person that people come to when they have a challenge, when they have a problem. And many of them, you’re going to be able to refer to yourself and be able to help them within their own business and provide your services.

And other times you’re providing your referrals to your BNI chapter members. And the key is that the more members that you have within your network, the more problems you’re able to solve. And because if you don’t have a category taken within your referral network and within your chapter, that means that there’s problems that you’re encountering, that you don’t even know that you can solve. And once you have those folks in those slots, and giving weekly presentations, it creates in your mind an open loop that you can close when you encounter individuals with those types of problems. And the bigger your network gets, the more referrals everybody generates. It’s really, The Connector Effect is Givers Gain implemented.

Ivan:
You know, the technology that exists today is just mind boggling to me compared to what it was when I started BNI. And when I started BNI, I wanted to be a Master Connector. And I don’t know if I ever told you this, Robert, but this is what I did. Once every two or three months, I would send a letter to like hundreds of people that were in my database, which was mostly a Rolodex, and for those of you who are Gen Xers, I’m not talking about an expensive watch. It’s a little paper document that, you know, that you turn and you get these cards with all the addresses.

And anyway, I would mail to all of my contacts, a list of business professionals that I did business with. That’s it; not their names, not their phone numbers, and then I’d have a cover letter that said, “You know, I really believe in connecting people who need referrals. And so here’s a list of professions, if you need one of these professions call me.”

And, you know, it took about six months or a year, but people started calling me, Robert, and asking, “Hey, do you know this person? Do you know that person?” I didn’t give the phone number because I wanted to make the connection personally. And that was sort of a, you know, paper-based Master Connector. But now with technology, we can just do so much more, wouldn’t you agree?

Robert:
Absolutely. And I think that’s the real power. You know, whereas you had to assemble all of those names yourself and vet those individuals and have meetings with them and really work to curate that list that you were, you know, that you were making available. Your BNI chapter is a system that plugs in to your existing business that gives you access to that power, all together really at the power of your BNI Connect app. And so, it’s fast, it’s effective, and makes you a Master Connector, so much easier than it was for you out on your own 36 years ago.

Ivan:
Absolutely. Any last thoughts before we wrap up? We’re over time. Any last thoughts before we wrap?

Robert:
Yeah, the reason why we built the, or we created, The Connector Effect were two things. To help your chapter grow by improving retention because members have a better expectation of what BNI membership is all about, and really the most important, helping business owners grow their business. BNI is the single most effective business growth strategy out there today. And I want to do everything we can in order to help business owners around the world appreciate it, use it more effectively, and grow their business for the benefit of their families.

Ivan:
Robert, thank you so much for doing this book with me. I really appreciate it. Thanks for being on the podcast today. You’re helping to make a difference in a lot of people’s lives. The book is called The Connector Effect. It’s written by Robert Skrob, Graham Weihmiller, and myself. You can go to www.theconnectoreffect.com or find it on Amazon. Thank you, Robert.

Robert:
Absolute honor to be here. Thank you, Dr. Misner.

Ivan:
Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, thank you both. That was some great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

title.rendered: Episode 696: Addition by Subtraction (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 143, recorded in 2010. Please note that the old SuccessNet website no longer exists. The link to the article has been updated in the synopsis.

Synopsis

Today’s episode is inspired by a letter Dr. Misner got from the vice-president of a struggling BNI chapter who wanted to know how to keep the long-term goal of building the chapter in mind when facing personal attacks from members?

Dr. Misner’s response was to refer this VP back to an article he wrote in SuccessNet in 2006, called “Addition by Subtraction.” The chapter in that story was facing a problem with absenteeism.

In the article, Ivan and Beth Misner take turns explaining that the way to grow healthy rose bushes is to prune them back—and the way to grow a healthy BNI chapter is to get rid of the members who don’t show up or don’t participate. BNI thrives on accountability.

This episode is brought to you by our new sponsor, Ask Ivan Misner.

Complete Transcript of BNI Podcast Episode 143

Priscilla:
Hello everyone, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast brought to you by AskIvanMisner.com, which is a new Web site where you can ask any question you have about networking.

I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the founder and the chairman of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner.

Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I’m doing great, Priscilla. This week I’m an BNI headquarters doing leadership team training – or I should say director training. We bring in directors from all over the world several times a year and do about three full days of training. And then after the training, we bring them over to my house for dinner, and that’s what we just did last night. It’s all about education, and that’s why we do these podcasts, trying to teach people how to do this effectively, and we make sure that our directors get really good training with a 700-800 page manual and go through the whole program.

Priscilla:
Wow! That’s impressive. So what are you going to share with us?

Ivan:
I’ve got a fun topic today, an interesting topic. Maybe “fun” is the wrong word; I like it, but it is really based on a letter I received from somebody. The topic is Addition by Subtraction. And I’m talking about this today because I received a letter – actually an e-mail from a BNI member who’s a vice president, and he talks about his frustration and the burnout relating to rebuilding his chapter. And he said they are, for the first time, following the rules and guidelines for BNI, and he really feels like he is hitting a wall.

He’s the VP, and the VP gets all the dirty work; he recognizes that in his e-mail. And he said he has a tough problem, how do you keep the long term goal in mind of building the chapter while there are so many attacks personally. He said, “I’ve been called a tyrant by a member of my chapter who was late nine times and had missed several meetings.” And he had a conversation with the guy, and he didn’t take it well and called the VP, the poor VP a tyrant.

So this really is for members everywhere but particularly for you, VP, who sent me this letter. I hope you listen to this podcast because I want to share some information that’s really based on an article I wrote a few years back called Addition by Subtraction. And if you’re listening to this podcast and you want to see the original article, go to BNISuccessNet.com, BNISuccessNet.com. Do a search on “Addition by Subtraction,” and you’ll get this article. We’ll also try to put a link to this article on the podcast so you can go directly to it.

Note: the old SuccessNet website has been replaced by “The Latest” on the main bni.com website.

But here’s basically how the article goes, and this is my answer to the VP, how do you handle people that get this upset because you’re doing the right thing, you’re trying to turn the chapter around. So to him, I would say, “Have faith.” The best way to build the chapter, you want to add to your chapter is to subtract from your chapter. And I say that because this article that I wrote, and it’s done as a “he said/she said” article. I write a little bit, and then my wife writes a little bit. And I talk about the fact that I was in a local chapter and I asked how things were going, and she said, “Great. We’re up to 35 members, but only 25 are coming every week.”

I was like, “Whoa, wait a minute. That’s not great. If 25% of your membership is missing, that’s not good at all.”

Then we had my wife talk a little bit, and she talks about gardening. Big transition, but she talks about gardening and how, if you really want – we’ve got like 75 rose bushes at my house. If you really want the rose bushes to grow, you have to prune them back. You have to cut the rose bushes back in order for them to bloom.

Priscilla:
Right.

Ivan:
And that’s what the whole article is about, is talking about the fact that if you want a chapter to grow, sometimes you have to prune the chapter, and people aren’t going to be happy about that. “Oh, my goodness! You actually have expectations of them? They actually have to show up on time? They can’t miss meetings?”

Look, this is a business organization. It’s okay to have rules and regulations that help raise the bar and the quality of participation. BNI is not for everybody; I get that; it’s okay. We want people who are committed, and the truth is this vice president is doing the right things by focusing on accountability. And Priscilla, we’ve talked about this in a lot of podcasts.

One of the strengths of BNI is that all the members, or many of the members, are friends. One of the weaknesses of BNI is that many of the members are friends.

Priscilla:
Right.

Ivan:
It is both a strength and a weakness. And the truth is, if your chapter is struggling and hasn’t been following the program, the best way to turn it around is to prune it back, to let everybody know that we’re going to start following the program again, get everybody on board. And those people who aren’t willing to do what everybody else is willing to do, it might not be the right group for them. And so he’s doing the right things by starting to focus on absences and tardies.

The one part of this article that I end that I think is really important is a study that I did many years ago with two chapters, and it started because this one chapter had 14 members. And they came to me and they said, “Attendance is nonsense.” It’s what they said to me, Priscilla. “Attendance is nonsense. Whether people show up or not is not going to make a difference in the amount of referrals, and we’re going to prove it to you.”

I said, “Okay.”

“We’re going to start following the attendance policy, and we’re going to track it and we’re going to show you that it doesn’t make a difference.”

I said, “Okay. That’s fair. If you show me it doesn’t make a difference, I’ll stop bothering you about attendance.”

Originally, this was published in 1994, and it was republished in this article.

So they had an average of 2.1 absences per member per quarter. That’s a lot of absences. Fourteen members and they were passing 188 referrals a quarter. Once they started focusing on absenteeism, they dropped absenteeism down by 52 percent, so it want to one absence per member per quarter. Their number of members went up 29 percent, and their number of referrals went up 43 percent to 269 referrals.

They did it in another quarter, absenteeism dropped to .6, .6 absences per member. The number of members jumped up to 21 percent, a 50 percent jump in membership, and referrals went up 62 percent to 305 referrals. A 71 percent drop in absenteeism, a 62 percent increase in referrals. That is a direct correlation.

Here’s the funny part of the story. The chapter came up to me when we originally published that back in 1994 in a printed version of SuccessNet, and they said, “We don’t believe these numbers.” Oh, by the way, the [original] chapter started following the attendance policy all the time. They said, “Never mind. It is correct.” And so they followed the program.

I had another chapter come to me and said, “We don’t believe these numbers.”

I said, “Why?”

He said, “These guys are in your pocket. They’ll just do whatever you say.”

“They’re an independent chapter. They did this on their own.”

He didn’t believe me. He said they wanted to do it, but they wanted to do it one month longer – or one quarter longer; they wanted to do it for three quarters instead of two quarters.

I said, “Sure, fine.”

And he said, “You have to publish our results, because I don’t believe it’ll make a difference over a nine month period.”

So I said, “I promise I’ll publish the results.”

He was right, it didn’t – he said, “I don’t believe that they’ll be the same.” And he was right; they weren’t the same. They had a 53 percent drop in absentees and a 164 percent increase in referrals.

Priscilla:
Wow!

Ivan:
A 90 percent increase in membership.

Priscilla:
Wow!

Ivan:
And all they did was follow the attendance policy. And that’s not all. They also focused on the visitor host and they did a few other things right, but they just focused on following the program.

So to every chapter out there that’s made the decision, the tough decision to follow the system, go read this article because it shows that there’s a direct correlation between attendance and referrals. 53 percent drop in absenteeism led to a 164 percent increase in referrals over a nine month period.

That, vice president who wrote me the e-mail, that’s the reason why you are doing the right thing by focusing on the fundamentals in your group. Keep it up. Don’t let it bother you that people are resistant to the change, because they will be. People don’t like change, but this is good change. And it will make a difference for your chapter.

And that’s my message for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
It’s a good message. I just have a question as to why he had to take all the heat and why it wasn’t just a leadership team decision.

Ivan:
That’s a good question, and the truth is, the VP is the one who usually does take the heat because they’re the spokesperson for the membership committee; they’re the chair of the membership committee. But I think it would be good if an entire chapter, if the leadership team can get behind the vice president, it makes it a little bit easier. The worst thing a leadership team can do, and he did not say that his group was doing this, but the worst thing they could do would be to say, “Hey, look, it’s not my responsibility; it’s the VP doing this,” to wash their hands of it.

What they really need to do is to say, “We support this. This is in the chapter’s best interest. Go read that article, Addition by Subtraction. You’ll see why it’s important to do this. And so if you get a leadership team who is supporting the VP, you’re going to have less of this. But there’s always going to be – the person who brings the bad tidings is the person that’s going to get most of the anxiety from the chapter.

So I understand. Just hang in there. You’re doing the right thing, and I promise you, if you continue to do it and you do it in a tactful way, don’t be a fascist about it, be polite, be professional, but be tough, tough love. And you will absolutely build your chapter.

I urge people listening to the podcast, go read that article, Addition by Subtraction. It was written by my wife and I a few years ago. We’ll include the link here.

Thanks, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
You’re so welcome. And, Ivan, I was wondering if you could tell the listeners a little bit about our new sponsor.

Ivan:
You know, we do have a new sponsor, AskIvanMisner.com. This is a Web site that was developed by Alex Mandossian. He is like the world’s expert on Ask Web sites, and Alex has put this site together, and we’re working with him on it. Just think about any question you have about business networking, go to that Web site, and type in your question. It’s free! When you type in your question, you’ll get a link that will tell you when to the phone call is. You may go and listen to the phone call live for free, no cost. If you want a recorded copy of the phone call, it’s $15 or less for the recorded copy of it, but free, listen to the telebridge for free live. And it’s once a month – at this point, it’s once a month. That may change over time.

But go to AskIvanMisner.com, throw a question at us. It can be either about BNI or about networking in general. This is not just a BNI Web site; it’s a networking Web site. Go check it out, see what you think.

Priscilla:
Great! Okay, then.

Well, listeners, I’d like to remind you that this podcast has been brought to you by AskIvanMisner.com, new Web site where you can ask any question you have about networking. Thanks so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we hope you’ll join us next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Ivan and Beth Misner explain that the way to grow a healthy BNI chapter is to get rid of the members who don’t show up or don’t participate. BNI thrives on accountability.

title.rendered: Episode 695: Finding the Good in Bad Times

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Finding the positive in difficult situations is hard, but not impossible. Dr. Misner shares 7 takeaways from 2020 to get you started reflecting on the good that came out of a difficult year.

  1. Micro-dose the news to avoid negativity.
  2. Look for opportunities, like a chance to spend more time with your children.
  3. Hope + a plan + action will lead you successfully through challenges.
  4. During difficult times, your network will be there for you.
  5. If you wait until tough times are over, your business will be over.
  6. You can travel the world without getting on a plane.
  7. Friends are more important than ever.

So, what are your takeaways from 2020? What was something positive that you learned or took away from the most challenging year in recent history? Share your observations in the comments.

Complete Transcript of Episode 695

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner.

Hello Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I have recently been to BNI Mid America, that is the central part of the United States, where I did a large online event. So, still visiting regions all around the world.

Priscilla:
Oh, that’s great. Well, tell us a little bit about this topic.

Ivan:
Yeah. Well, as you might suspect it, I came up with this content because of 2020, the year 2020. And it’s all about really looking for the good in a bad situation.

I ended 2020 with a humorous graphic on my social media that said this, it said, “Dear 2021: I don’t want any trouble from you. Just come in, sit in the corner, don’t touch anything, and keep your mouth shut.”

You know, that was pretty, I thought it was pretty pithy, and kind of funny. But there’s an important flip side to this sentiment. And that flip side begs the question, “what did we personally learn from 2020?” And I think that’s an important question to consider.

Complaining about things that go horribly wrong is easy. Finding the positive in difficult situations is not. And I believe that finding the gems in life is the best way to move forward successfully.

I learned a great deal from 2020. And I have a lot of valuable takeaways for me out of 2020. And here are seven takeaways that I got from last year.

  1. I’ve talked about this in a podcast, so I won’t go into much detail, and that is micro dose the news. I learned that to get what I need to know from news apps and not to obsess about the problems by watching the news hour after hour. Negative news sells and the media has become a master of sharing negative news. Technically, I think it’s opinions much more than actual news, but they’ve become a master of it.
  2. A really valuable lesson I observed involved a man that told me that he was able to keep his job, and I think I may have mentioned this in a podcast, but that he had to work from home and he also needed to care for his children and help them do their schoolwork. And he told me, he was really stressed out about doing that double duty. And during our conversation, he came to the realization that this was actually an opportunity to spend much more time with his children. And although it was challenging, it might be a good time period where they all look back with fondness because of the experience they had together. And when he embraced that idea, his vision of the situation completely changed. And that was a lesson that I hope that many others with children experienced.
  3. Third is I witnessed focus beating out fear. And I know I talked about this in podcasts and a lot of my writings, focus beat out fear during 2020 for many entrepreneurs. When people are frozen by fear, they had no hope. But when that fear focused them, they had hope and they were much more likely to weather the storm. Hope is more powerful than fear. Hope is that little voice inside you whispering to you about what “can be” when everyone around you is screaming “what can’t be.” Hope plus a plan, plus action, will lead you successfully through challenges.
  4. Fourth, I learned that during difficult times your network will be there for you. I saw people support each other emotionally and professionally. I witnessed people give one another ideas that kept their business alive and thriving. Two of the best examples included the furniture re-upholstery shop that became a COVID mask manufacturing company with all of the material they had, and a brewery that became a COVID hand sanitizer company with the alcohol that they had for the brewery.
  5. I learned that if you wait until tough times are over, your business will be over. During difficult times you need your network more than ever. You need BNI more than ever, Social distancing is the wrong phrase. Physical distancing is what’s necessary at this time, and hopefully, this will change this year. We need to be more social than ever, though. And I saw successful businesses activate their network and not abandon their network.
  6. I learned that I could travel the world without getting on a plane, I love that. I have 2.3 million miles on one airline alone. And it was a gift to be able to spend months with my wife at home last year, particularly last year. It was the longest period of time that we had together in our 31 years of marriage, and I’ll forever be thankful for that.
  7. I learned that friends are more important than ever; that the quality of the people in your life, or in your room as I call it in one of my books, will determine the quality of your life. And the quality of the people determine the quality of your life.

For me, that last lesson was profound. It was a tough year for me as it was for many people. But I came out of 2020 with a clearer vision of who my friends are. And I understand who truly cares about me as an individual, and as a person, as a result of 2020. It was something that I learned. And I will forever be grateful for that.

So I suggest that if you’re listening to this podcast, you don’t completely write off 2020 and erase the year from your memory banks. Instead, I suggest that you consider what you learned from last year and what your positive takeaways are from that experience. You’ll be a better person for it.

So, what are your takeaways from 2020? What was something positive that you learned or took away from the most challenging year in recent history? Give some thought to this question and write down your observations here on this podcast. I’d love to read the things that you come up with, that were takeaways for you in 2020. Positive takeaways, it’s easy to come up with the negative ones, we all have a big lesson, just watch the news. The positive things that you came out of 2020. And I believe that reflecting on this, that kind of reflection, will help you recognize the good that took place during one of the toughest years on record. And so Priscilla, I’m gonna put you on the spot here. What good came out of this for you? Anything come to mind?

Priscilla:
Well, I have to go along with what you said in terms of how people were really there for each other. And I saw that in my BNI group. I saw that in our Nextdoor neighbors group, you know, there’s a online Nextdoor group that you can be a part of. I saw people helping each other through that. And then you know, just in my own circle, you know, people being extremely kind and loving, even though we were maybe, had to be on Zoom or whatever. And that’s a fun thing. Being on Zoom with your friends, and having birthday parties on Zoom, and being able to connect all over the country and pull in your friends and your family to situations that before you would never think of doing that.

Ivan:
Right.

Priscilla:
And so that was something really positive.

Ivan:
Yeah, listen, that’s exactly what I’m talking about. And so if you’re listening to this podcast, I’d like to ask you to do what Priscilla just did. And name a few things that were positive that came out of 2020. Because if all we do is look at how bad 2020 was, and it was bad for all of us, it certainly was for me, I lost my wife last year. And so, it was tough on all of us. It’s still good to reflect on what we got out of 2020 moving forward. And I think that’s the best way to be successful in life. So thanks for your suggestions, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Yeah, it’s so nice to look at the positive. I always love, I always try to do that.

Ivan: Yeah, as do I. Because it’s so easy, we live in such a negative world, that it’s so easy to just obsess about the problems and challenges, but generally, the people I’ve met in life, who are really successful, tend to be, not everyone, but they tend to be a lot more positive and look for a positive future, as opposed to obsess about a negative past. So that’s everything I’ve got for today, Priscilla.

Everyone, make sure to post your comments. I promise you, I will read them all. Thank you very much. Back to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla: Oh, perfect. Thank you so much, Ivan. I really, really love this podcast.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Dr. Misner shares 7 takeaways from 2020 to get you started reflecting on the good that came out of a difficult year.

title.rendered: Episode 694: Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Claudia Thompson joins Dr. Misner on the podcast this week to talk about why diversity, equity, and inclusion are good for BNI chapters in business worldwide.

  • You build a stronger, more robust and more effective network when you broaden your horizon.
  • Being a truly culturally inclusive leader is the only way to be effective and create positive change.
  • The more that we interact with those who appear different than us, then the more comfortable we become with them.
  • When you diversify your network, you have people who connect you to those groups of people that you don’t have a clear link with.
  • If you want your network to be diverse, you need to be inclusive. That means making a deliberate effort to reach out.

Complete Transcript of Episode 694

Priscilla:
Hello everybody, and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I love visiting regions around the world via online and this week I’ve, or just recently, I’ve been in Malaysia. And so I’m unable to tell you all the places I go every week, now there’s so many, but that was one of the largest that I’ve been to recently.

I have a guest today. Her name is Claudia Thompson and Claudia started her career on the BNI director team as a Launch Director five years ago and she’s launched nine chapters in the Boston area.

(And by the way, Claudia, I kicked off Boston with the original Director there, Patty Salvucci, I was at the very first launch.)

So for over a year she served as the Area Director of Suffolk County and most recently was appointed to lead the Diversity, Equity and Inclusion panel for BNI U.S. Claudia is a proud BNI member for seventeen years, Claudia, you probably joined when you were like 18.

Claudia:
(laughing) Okay.

Ivan:
Claudia currently serves as Board Chair of the Boston Women’s Fund. She’s the Founder and Organizer of Single Mothers United and is a Founding Director of Boston Business Women. In her spare time, Claudia loves spending time with her son Michael, and she also loves staying active by performing with the dance company, Boston Bollywood—I love it—running marathons and hiking trails. Claudia, welcome to BNI Podcast.

Claudia:
Thank you so, so much, Ivan. Thank you for having me here.

Ivan:
Well, it’s my pleasure. And this is a really important topic. And we all know in the U.S. how important this is. But the truth is, this is important globally. And maybe for different reasons. It might be that chapters are highly represented with one gender or another, or it might be ethnicity, it’s often age. So this is a topic, although it’s front and center in the United States, it’s a topic that I think is valid worldwide. And I’m really glad that you are here. So talk to us about why Diversity, Equity and Inclusion is good for our chapters in business worldwide.

Claudia:
Absolutely. So I’m going to break this up, you know, first, I’m going to talk about the diversity piece. You know, so obviously, diversity creates strength, right? And power. You know, there’s that old phrase that the whole is greater than the sum of the parts. And this is most accurate, obviously, when all the parts aren’t exactly the same. So you build a stronger, more robust and more effective network when you broaden your horizon. You know, similar to like, you mentioned this actually, networks are clumpy in that, I forgot when you had released that.

Ivan:
I wrote it in a book that they’re “clumpy”, that’s the technical term, but we hang out with people like ourselves too much.

Claudia:
Exactly, exactly, right? And then, you know, so when it becomes, so that is just more of like, okay, the look of it, right, like the aesthetics are the diversity piece of it.

But then when we go into the equity and inclusion piece, you know, they’re intertwined with our core values, right? Particularly building relationships, lifelong learning, positive attitude, recognition, you know, and being a truly culturally inclusive leader is the only way to be effective and create positive change. So anything less than that, and you look like you’re actually putting on a show, it becomes more performative.

So, you know, and again, thinking about contact theory, which you talked about, you know, the more that we interact with those who appear different than us, then the more comfortable that we become with them as well. So, I know for myself, you know, the guys in my group, they love it. We were more women when we launched. I’ve been a member for a long time, but then I launched a new group that I joined, and so now, it seems to be more men, currently. And so a lot of them will say, “Oh, I had a One-to-One. We got together, nine of us, you know, at the golf course.” And I’m like, really? Like, okay.

Ivan:
I’m not sure that’s a One-to-One.

Claudia:
Exactly, but like, presumably as a black woman, they don’t know that I own my own clubs. So then I’ll have to say, “Well, hey guys, if you want to, ask me,” and so a lot of times, that’s who they’ll bring, are people that they golf with, or, you know, I was part of a chapter that, you know, everybody belonged to the same religious, you know, temple. So like there’s different, you know, groups that they stay together. And that’s totally fine. But I think when you start expanding and getting to know people, you find that you actually have more in common than you realize.

Ivan:
You do. And the truth is that you when you diversify your network, and you have people in there that don’t look like you, sound like you, aren’t the same age, don’t have the same educational background, you have people who are connectors who connect you to those groups of people that you don’t have a clear link with. And that’s one of the reasons why it’s much more powerful to have a diverse network. I mean, it’s good business. And it’s the right thing to do.

Claudia:
Correct, absolutely. Yep.

Ivan:
So diversity, inclusion, equity. Talk a little bit about that, in terms of how that fits within a chapter. I mean, you talked about the golfing, which I think is a great example. Any other examples?

Claudia:
Absolutely. So we actually did, you know, on the CORE corporate-owned side of BNI, we have CORE talks every Friday, at 12 noon, Eastern, and we did one, we did like a panel on diversity. This was like, last year, you know, after the, you know, the uprisings of racial injustice after George Floyd. And we had a really frank conversation, right? Something that we didn’t know was needed. And I think 90% of people that signed up for it, like attended the event, it was kind of wild. But we had a panel of a lot of different Directors. So we had one Latin Director from, you know, a gentleman, and then the rest of us were black women.

And oftentimes, even I’ll join like an Area Director call. And the first time, you know, when we went online, I’ll say, oh, like we don’t say it. It’s just we just automatically think there’s another one. Right? When we have these conversations on that panel, we talked about, “Oh, wow. Yeah, my experience of BNI is when I joined, it was mostly—fill in the blank: you know, mostly older men, older white men or a white chapter. But whatever it is, you know, like a lot of women say that, you know, had said that over the years, I think that we’ve actually sort of leveled that off.

But that sense of, you know, is there a lot of people in positions of like being a Director or even members? Are they there? Are they out there? So I think, you know, I think it’s important to make sure that there’s representation because it really matters. And even one of the Area Directors had said, when I first attended, I loved what BNI was about, you know, the structure of it all. But does this work for people like me, who look like me? And oftentimes, as people of color, we are asking ourselves that question. Or if you’re in any marginalized group, you’re often asking, since I don’t see other people like myself, are they going to be interested in doing business with me?

Ivan:
Yeah, so I want to, I want to make a statement, which at first may sound controversial, and that is “Diversity is a fact, inclusiveness is a choice.” Now, what I mean by that is, either a group is diverse or it’s not, you know, there’s nowhere in between. They either are or they aren’t diverse. But what is a choice is inclusiveness.

And I think sometimes we look at our group, and we say, “Well, you know, look, we’re open to anybody,” but we don’t include them. We don’t reach out to them, just like your golf story, which I think is great. And so you know, you either are or you aren’t diverse, but if you want to become diverse, you have to be inclusive, and inclusiveness is a choice. I recommend people go to episode number 672, where I talk about that at length. So how do you create equitable opportunities for other demographics, abilities within an organization?

Claudia:
Yeah. I mean, so you know, obviously, this BNI system, the structure is for everyone, you know, and it needs to be accessible to all business people who identify with our mission and our core values. So one of the best parts of BNI is that it grows, you know, by word of mouth. And that’s one of the hardest parts about BNI at the same time, of course, right? So like, only people in the quote-unquote “in the know,” find out about BNI and most people interact with people who look like them. So, like I mentioned, they might go to the same, like religious, you know, place of worship or live in the same geographic area. And as an organization, we have to make a deliberate effort to go to the places of business, you know, like those organizations, maybe reach out, you know, or create affinity groups, and then create visibility, credibility and profitability for those folks so that more people know about the opportunities.

So I did create, in Boston there is a predominantly black area that I wanted to launch a chapter in. I knew that, actually, a lot of commerce was popping up there. And I knew this because of the people that I had been networking with, I belong to, you know, black business associations and so forth. And people, some people, you know, not people directly from BNI, but some other directors were like questioning if it could work there.

And it really took me time, I honestly had to really get the trust of them because again, of what we spoke to before, but they saw that it works. And now they are like my most thriving chapter in Boston. So they, but it took time, you know, because they had to know, it had to be word of mouth, it had to be, this is gonna work for you, the system works. And they’re loving BNI. They love it. It’s really fantastic. They got on TV with the group as a result, they were in the newspaper, like it was really very special. And again, for myself, in particular, like it was all my launches are wonderful, I love them all. But that one in particular was really, really amazing to do in that area.

Ivan:
Yeah, it’s an amazing story. And, you know, BNI does work everywhere. We’re in 70 countries in different cultures. It works everywhere, it just really comes down to having the commitment to make it work in various communities all around the country and around the world.

Well, we’re running out of time, talk to me briefly about why the BNI Diversity, Equity and Inclusion panel, which you’re on in the U.S., is working. And if you’re listening to this podcast, and you’re not in the U.S., I want you to think about how what Claudia is going to share with you here, how that might apply in your country, and maybe talk to your Executive Director or your National Director about doing something like what’s being done in BNI U.S. in your country. So Claudia, talk a little bit about the panel.

Claudia:
Absolutely. So we had interviewed people from all aspects, like all areas of BNI U.S. So both on, you know, the CORE side and the franchise side, Executive Directors, members, and everyone in between. We wanted to have something again, just as diverse as what we wanted to represent. So we have many, you know, races, gender, sexual orientation, all types of different, you know, identities. And it really is, we’ve been working on, we meet bi-weekly, and it has been amazing just to find these folks. They’re truly talented. I really feel honored to be leading this amazing group of folks. And what we’ve been working on is really, there’s four subcommittees.

So there’s like the training committee. So working on different trainings. So we’ve been really fortunate to work with Laura del Rosal, I believe her name is, in Ireland, on like, some of the Business Builders stuff, updating that, making sure that it’s accessible for both hearing impaired and visually impaired, making sure some of the language is more inclusive, gender wise. So things of that nature.

Working on, then we have another committee, marketing communications, so making sure there’s more representation online, you know, we’ve been working with, you know, folks like Terry Atkins, who’s the CMO and Meena as well, Srinivasan, who’s the CDO. So like, it’s been amazing, because a lot of times you hear about these panels and tasks force, and they don’t have to be able to connect with people that can actually do something about what they’re working on.

Ivan:
And what you’ve just described, Claudia, for those of you listening, these are people all over the world in BNI that she’s talking about.

Claudia:
Yeah. Yeah, it’s amazing. So like, when you talk about a global impact, it does matter. You know, like, I do believe that it’s not just the U.S. and I do feel and hope that other, you know, and we’ve been working, like, and getting ideas from like BNI South Africa and other, you know, organizations, so it’s been really, like, amazing.

And then we have the, you know, the 3+1, you know, Launch, Fill and Retain folks working on different efforts there. And then we have, you know, hiring, like looking at our hiring practices, are they transparent? Is there even footing, you know, so it’s been really awesome, and I really like we’ve made a huge difference.

Ivan:
Claudia, this is great content and I’d love to have you back to talk about it more when you have some more ideas that you’ve come up with your panel and hopefully we’ll have some other countries create a diversity panel as well. You’re doing really, really important work, Claudia, and I appreciate what you’re doing for BNI very much.

For those of you who are interested in connecting with Claudia you can connect with her at ClaudiaConnects on Instagram or Facebook. Thank you, and Claudia, would you thank the panel for everything that they’re doing? Thank them on my behalf personally, I appreciate it.

Claudia:
Absolutely, Ivan. Thank you so much.

Ivan:
Over to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, perfect. Thank you, Claudia, it sounds really amazing what you’re doing.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.
