excerpt.rendered: Claudia Thompson joins Dr. Misner on the podcast this week to talk about why diversity, equity, and inclusion are good for BNI chapters in business worldwide.

title.rendered: Episode 693: Givers Gain and Storytelling

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Greg Davies from BNI London SE (a.k.a “The Storyfella”) joins Dr. Misner on the show today to share more material from Infinite Giving. Greg is one of the co-authors of the book, together with Dr. Misner and Julian Lewis, who appeared in Episode 682.

Greg shares the story of James Harrison, which illustrates Givers Gain Principle #2, Giving without Expectation. He also tells a story to illustrate how he learned that stories are much more effective than charts and graphs when it comes to persuading people.

Find out more about Greg at https://www.thestoryfella.com/.

Complete Transcript of Episode 693

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you? And where are you?

Ivan:
I have just recently been to BNI Mainland China.

Priscilla:
Wow!

Ivan:

Now okay, it was by Zoom, but still, I was there. Wow. They are just doing amazing things. They actually had a kickoff just recently with over 200 members joining in one chapter. Blows me away, I never thought that would be possible. It’s incredible. So Mainland China for BNI is doing incredibly well. And that’s why I visited recently.

However, today, I have a good friend who is on with me, and his name is Greg Davies. Greg is known as the “Storyfella” because he uses his passion for telling stories to help businesses and communities thrive, including BNI.

After working with both large corporations and owning his own small business, he really understands that working hard is something that’s unavoidable. But it’s okay as long as we understand why we’re doing it—I couldn’t agree more—and if we surround ourselves with those people who inspire us to be better every day.

Greg is a best-selling author. (I think I may have helped him with that particular one.) He’s a multi-award-winning director (he did that on his own) and a corporate trainer and international inspirational speaker. Greg is a not-such-a-secret Disney fan, and he and his two daughters – it’s their happy place.

Greg, my friend, it is a pleasure to have you here. Greg is the co-author with me and Julian Lewis of Infinite Giving: The Seven Principles of Givers Gain. Greg.

Greg:

Thank you very much for having me on. I really appreciate it.

Ivan:

It’s my pleasure to finally have you on. This is your first visit on BNI Podcast, I’m pretty confident it won’t be your last. So you’re known as the Storyfella. And you really had some amazing stories that you contributed to Infinite Giving, which really helped make the book. Talk a little bit about The 7 Principles of Givers Gain and the stories that are within.

Greg:
So the book was just that, an amazing project for us. And it came from trying to codify Givers Gain. And it was a huge undertaking for Julian and myself. And of course, we came to you and we didn’t even want to go close to the core value and philosophy of BNI without including you. And it was just a wonderful experience from start to finish. But one thing that we knew right from the start was that we were going to have to have some pretty substantial stories in there.

One thing that I learned very early on in my journey in BNI is the fact that there are ways of growing people’s businesses, our members’ businesses, and their referrals that they receive, and also the groups that they’re in. And now like you just said, one in China launching with over 200 members and getting more people and keeping people for longer—but wrapped around every activity that we do is telling stories. And it’s like, if BNI was a block of wood and we were taking slices off, it would be a line that runs right the way through it.

Telling stories is so important that we knew that Infinite Giving had to include some amazing stories. I know a number of them didn’t make the book but I think we got a good cross section of real kind of BNI member life experience stories in there, and some inspirational motivational stories. One of my favorites is the one we opened the book with, which is a story of James Harrison, the ultimate giver who dedicated his life to helping others. I won’t spoil the story but it revolves around one of my…


Ivan:
Give us 30 seconds though, because it is a powerful story.

Greg:
Well, that was an interesting one for me because I have two phobias in life; one is spiders, and one is of needles. And this story is about giving blood. Now luckily, the nurses weren’t dressed as spiders otherwise, that would have been a lot worse. But it tells the story of how James Harrison, who’s an Australian citizen, was in a car accident in his late teens. And his life was saved because people donated blood for his operation. And as he lay in the hospital bed recovering, it kind of hit on him this gift that had been bestowed on him by strangers that he would never meet. So he decided that he would give back; he decided he would give blood.

Ivan:
But he had unusual blood, didn’t he?

Greg:
Amazing, yeah, he had a very rare genetic bacteria in his blood, which led to a vaccine for newly pregnant women, which they believe has now saved over 1 million lives.

Ivan:
That’s just incredible. It’s incredible. And just so everyone knows, he gave 1,173 blood donations during his lifetime.

Greg:

And what was amazing about the story, yes, it’s a remarkable feat. I mean, he’s such a humble man; on his achievement, he was quoted in saying, “Well, I hope someone beats it, you know, because that means they’ve dedicated their life to helping others like I have.” But the reason it was the first story in our book is because it really did encompass Givers Gain. So he went out of the way to help others. But the vaccine that he developed, or that scientists developed from his blood, was administered to his daughter and it allowed him to have grandchildren. So it really was just a beautiful kind of cyclical nature of how Givers Gain works. And this story summed it up just absolutely perfectly.

Ivan:

And he’s a great example of giving with no expectation, because he certainly didn’t expect that giving blood would help his daughter years later.

Greg:
No, absolutely not. And it’s one of our principles. And in fact, it’s our second one. So giving without expectation, he laid down on that bed, he actually to repay the gift that he had received. So we mentioned in the book that, yes, we’re making a huge deal of what James had achieved. But the kind of unsung heroes of that story where they can’t really tell how many people gave blood initially, but it would have been between four and five people would have given the amount of blood for his initial operation. And those are the real heroes. Because without that, he wouldn’t have realized just what a gift was bestowed on him and wanted to repay that.

But you’re 100% right, he had no expectation. And there’s some brilliant news footage out there of moms with newborn babies coming over in little pink romper suits, and he gets poked in the eye at one point, which I think is just a beautiful image. And you can see, he’s very embarrassed by it. He’s, you know, he’s very humble. I mean, he sums up principle number six for us, which is to stay humble. For someone to be walking around, he’s described as a man with a golden arm, and for someone to be walking around having saved over a million lives.

Ivan:
All right, so we can go one of two directions here. We can either talk about why stories are powerful, or we can maybe share another story or two out of the book. I’m good with either one. What would you like to do?

Greg:
Oh, well, I think we’ve got to go for why stories are powerful.

Ivan:
Okay. Let’s get some tangible stuff that will help our members understand, because that’s a powerful story, give some more information about why stories are powerful.

Greg:
Well, the first time I realized how powerful stories were, when I was managing a group on the south coast of England. The group was called Worthing Rocks and it was the first group in the region of West Sussex ever to go platinum. And this was on their journey to platinum. So they just got some really good members in, and on the visitors event, I would run a feature presentation.

I’d take 10 minutes and explain how BNI works and how BNI could work for the people in the room. And on their first visitor day experience on their journey to being 50 plus members, which is what the level Platinum means, I did this formula where you could work out the amount of relationships in the room and I still remember it. It’s n2 – n/2 where n represents a number of people in the room.

And I’ll admit to you, you know, I felt like some kind of Harvard scientist going for it and I was working out all these figures and at the end of it, I had a few smiles and nods. And a number of people joined the chapter on that day.

And then a few months later, we had another visitors event and this fantastic marketeer, a lady called Louise Stevenson who runs Tiger Marketing, she said, “Oh Greg, great to see you again today. You’re here because of visitors day?”

“Yeah, yeah, I’m here because of visitors day.”

“Brilliant. So you did that horrible formula last time. You won’t be doing that again, will you?”

And of course, what had I planned to do? I planned on doing the exact same thing again.

Ivan: (chuckling)

Greg:
So what I had to do—and you know, people have said to me, “Well, you shouldn’t have changed, it worked. You know, you got Louise as a member, you shouldn’t have changed it.” But, probably in my naivety, and luckily for me, I did change it.

And I thought about the lady who lived down my street, who when we were growing up, my parents would always go and speak to if they needed anything. She knew the best mechanic, the best heating engineer, the best insurance guy, the best car salesman. And I realized that that’s what chapters were, and I kind of weaved it in with a bit of nostalgia.

And after two or three minutes of the presentation, I had the majority of the room smiling and nodding along about playing soccer in the park and neighbors with large dogs. And I realized right then, that what stories do is rather than telling people what they need to do to make a decision, they influence people.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Greg:
Allows them to come to their own conclusion to make the right choice.

Ivan:
Yeah, very true. I mean, there’s the old saying that facts tell but stories sell. And I think that is a truism. And it’s one of the ways that I think that you explain ‘why’ to people, because people don’t care about ‘how’ until they understand the ‘why’. And when they understand the ‘why’ then the ‘how’ becomes a lot easier. And the way to get to the ‘why’ is through an effective story, in my opinion. Would you agree?

Greg:
Absolutely, I totally agree. Yeah.

Ivan:
Well, Greg, it was a pleasure working with you on Infinite Giving. We’re working on another book on the legacy that you leave in life. And I love that concept; not sure when that will come out, but we’re working on it. And you had some great contributions to this book.

For anyone that’s interested in getting some information about Greg, you can go to www.TheStoryFella.co.uk, that’s www.TheStoryFella.co.uk. Greg, thanks for being such a great co-author on Infinite Giving and thanks for being on BNI Podcast today.

Greg:
Thank you very much for the opportunity in both aspects. Thank you.

Ivan:
Over to you Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. Well, that was wonderful. I love that story. It was fantastic. I think that’s it for this week. Thank you for the great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Storyfella Greg Davies joins Dr. Misner this week to share a story that illustrates Givers Gain Principle #2.

title.rendered: Episode 692: Just Ask

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Andy Lopata returns to the BNI Podcast this week to talk about his new book, Just Ask: Why Seeking Support Is Your Greatest Strength. (Andy last appeared in Episode 376.)

There are three reasons people hesitate to ask for help:

  1. We want to look strong.
  2. We don’t want to be a burden.
  3. We assume.

Andy outlines a strategic approach to asking for help and emphasizes the importance of being open to support of different kinds from across your whole network. Remember to say “thank you” and to look for ways you can help others in your network–and be sure to check out Andy’s book.

Complete Transcript of Episode 692

Priscilla:

Hello everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you? And where are you?

Ivan:
Well, I have, appropriately so, just recently been to the UK via Zoom, well actually via Facebook, in a Facebook Live for the United Kingdom, which was open to members and visitors. And it was great. That’s just one of the many places I’ve been jumping into on Zoom. I love being able to literally travel the planet in minutes and do lots of presentations.

I say appropriately so because my guest today is from the United Kingdom. He’s a specialist in professional relationships and networking for more than 20 years. His name is Andy Lopata. And he was called “One of Europe’s leading business networking strategists” by the Financial Times and “A true master of networking” by both the Independent and Forbes.com. He is a very experienced international speaker. And he’s the author of five books. He’s been quoted in a number of other books and is regularly quoted in the international press. He’s a fellow and board member of the Professional Speaking Association of the UK and Ireland (PSA), and a fellow of the Learning and Performance Institute, as well as a Master of the Institute for Sales Management. He’s just one on 26 recipients of PSA’s top honor, the Award of Excellence recognition.

There are only a handful of people, I mean, literally, I could count them on one hand, the number of people that I would recommend to speak to an organization on networking that are outside of BNI, and Andy, you are one of them. You truly are a networking expert. And it is a pleasure to have you on the BNI Podcast the second time, your first visit was episode number 376. Welcome back.

Andy:

Thank you very much. And what an introduction, Ivan, that’s very kind of you to say so.

Ivan:
Well, and I mean it. There literally is just a handful of people outside of this organization that I would recommend. You’re definitely one of them. So you’ve just done a new book. I love the book, it’s called Just Ask; the subtitle is “Why Seeking Support is your Greatest Strength.” This is perfect for BNI members; it’s perfect for anyone who wants to build a powerful personal network. So let me start off by asking you, why do we struggle to admit we face challenges and don’t know the answers?

Andy:
There are a number of reasons. I think that you can summarize them into three. Number one is that we want to look strong, we want to look good in front of our peers, in front of our colleagues. And we see vulnerability as a weakness, which in my mind is wrong. You know, vulnerability is a strength. And I tip my hat to anyone who’s got the courage to turn around and say, “I don’t know”, and not worry about how they look. But we stop ourselves for that reason. And that’s something I explore a considerable length in the book.

The second is that we don’t want to be a burden to others, which I think is a really interesting one. And BNI members will know this very well, when you pass a referral to a member of your chapter, and they come back and they say, “Thank you for that referral, that worked out really well for me,” you get a feeling of delight. You don’t feel that person has been a burden to you. And yet, we don’t ask for help because we’re worried about being a burden. There’s a real disconnect there. And we’re denying people the pleasure of helping us when we don’t ask them for help.

And then the third is we assume. We assume that people can’t or don’t want to help us. And you know what, maybe they can’t, but that’s not for you to decide. Let people decide for themselves. Make it okay for them to say no, make it okay for yourself, and understand that just because they say no, it doesn’t make it about you. There may be many reasons why they can’t help you.

Ivan:
Can I just comment on your vulnerability comment? Because I think that’s an important one. I think it’s important to understand the context of who you’re talking to. If you’re talking to somebody you just met, maybe you don’t want to be vulnerable. But if you’re talking about somebody you have a relationship with, like these people were coming to a BNI meeting every week, or they’re part of a network and they know these individuals, we are talking about close friends and associates, there’s nothing wrong with being vulnerable and nothing wrong with asking for help. I agree completely with that. So it’s just about context. Would you agree or disagree with that?

Andy:
I would agree. I went into writing the book with one premise that I wanted to test. And that is, is it easier for people to talk to strangers and open up to strangers, rather than to people they know? You know, going on the looking good factor, when you’re talking to someone you don’t know you don’t have that reputational risk you might carry otherwise. But apart from one person in Russia that I talked to, pretty much everyone was adamant that in most cases, they prefer to share, when they did share, with people they trusted. I did speak to a counselor in Chicago, and she said, “You know, this is why counseling is there, why there’s, you know, psychiatric support for people, so you have someone who can be objective, but generally people want to talk to people they trust and confide in people they trust.”

Ivan:
You also talked about people wanting to help, and I want to let you continue with your conversation. But I want to comment on that. I think the gratitude effect is powerful, and when you ask for help, and then show gratitude, that works both ways, I think, and it’s a powerful part of what you’re talking about, I believe.

Andy:
Completely. I mean, for years, one of the key things that I’ve been teaching in terms of referral strategy is the importance of the thank you. The simplest thing that most people forget. But every time you say thank you to someone, whether the referrals turned into business or not, not only is it the right thing, the courteous thing to do, but you’re giving them a little shot of feeling good. An endorphin shot. And every time someone feels good, they want to repeat that. So, that gratitude is key.

Ivan:
Yeah, very true. All right, what else? Tell me some more about the book.

Andy:
So in the book, I look at what holds us back, which is what we’ve talked about. And then what we need to do, and look at, moving forward. If we’re going to be strategic about the support that we seek, and I do look at it from both a professional and a personal perspective, but if we’re going to be strategic, we need to be more thoughtful about the approach that we take. So, what support do we need right now? Where do we need to look for it? Who’s going to support us? So I explore that, and then I move on to, actually how do you take action?

Ivan:
And doesn’t your support really come from your network? And I’m not talking about just a chapter of a network like BNI, but it comes from your personal network, yeah?

Andy:
It does. I mean, support can come both formally and informally. So you can have chapters of organizations like BNI. I have, over the years, I’ve been in a number of mastermind groups, which are peer group mentoring sessions, and then you have one to one mentoring as well. And I explore both of those options. But then there’s just the informal support, who can I pick up the phone to? Who can help me with this issue? You know, who can I check in with? And it works both ways. You know, I’ve had two people message me today and just say, “How are you doing? How are you coping with everything?” And I think we need to be doing that for each other as much as we possibly can. And so it can be both formal and informal, and from your personal network, and professional.

One thing I would add in there is, I think that it’s a natural thing to look to where the challenge is and seek the support from people who have expertise and experience in that challenge. And that makes absolute sense. And there’s a space for that. But there’s also a strong argument for diversity of thought, and actually getting people who haven’t experienced that challenge, necessarily, but might have been in a similar position in a different context. And then you bring new ideas in as well. So being open to support from across your network is key.

Ivan:
Well, this is good stuff. You want to talk any more about managing? You’ve talked about managing how, when, and where. Anything else on that topic?

Andy:
Yeah, I think that you have to, there are certain areas of balance in terms of how much you ask people as well. If you’re constantly going to the same people for help, unless you have that very strong mentoring relationship, you have to be careful, it can wear thin.

And you get into the area of givers gain and understanding that everything’s not one way. Now it might well be that there’s someone who’s in a position to help you. You can’t necessarily support them, but they know that you would, if you could. And then there’s also the issue of paying it forward. So they are confident that you’re able to do that.

My favorite quote, and I’m sure I’ve shared this with you before, Ivan, I know I have, is from a lady called Elizabeth Asquith Bibesco, who was the daughter of the former British Prime Minister, Lord Asquith, who said, “Blessed are they who give without remembering and receive without forgetting.” So we’ve talked about the receive without forgetting from the gratitude. But the giving without remembering, this is a two-way process. So I want to encourage people to ask for help. But I also want to encourage people to be there for others in their network, and proactively seek to help them as well.

Ivan:
Good advice. All right, we’re running out of time. But there’s one other thing I want to bring up, you have this crazy phrase in the book called semantic differential questioning. You want to talk about that a little bit?

Andy:
Well, I wonder where that came from? That actually came from you. And it’s something that I’ve repeated in a number of interviews, including one today, funnily enough, because I think it’s such a key point. And since you shared it with me, Ivan, I found myself using it a lot. It works. Absolutely, it works. Now my take.

So when you ask someone, how are you? They’re gonna say ‘I’m fine.’ But you asked three times, and in three different ways. And my spin on it is to ask the last time, as an open question. So it might be “How are you, Ivan?” ‘I’m fine.’ “How are you coping with the pandemic?” ‘Yeah, it’s okay.’ “In what ways is it impacting you? How are things different?” And it’s that last question where people will open up to you.

Ivan:
Yeah. This is such an effective technique. And by the way, for those of you listening to this, I talked about it in a number of podcasts: podcast 603, podcasts 443 and 230. I talked about semantic differential questioning, it’s a really effective technique. And, Andy, thank you so much for quoting me in your book and mentioning me and BNI. We appreciate it very much. So who is this book for, in your opinion, Andy?

Andy:
It’s a difficult one, because I always teach people to be specific about, you know, where they target things. But it really is a book for many people, there’s a strong, business bent. And if you’re running a small business, you need that support. If you’re at the top of that business, and people are looking to you for guidance, then you need that support around you as well. So I think that’s a key group. But really, a lot of people are struggling right now. And a lot of people need support but need to feel confident that they can ask, so anyone who, I always say never say ‘anyone who’, but it really is people who need to be open more and who are always saying, “Yeah, I’m fine. Yes, business is great.” and not elaborating.

Ivan:
Let me help narrow down even a little more. If you’re listening to this podcast, you’re interested in networking. This book is for you. You want this book. Trust me, you want this book. You can get the book by going to Andy’s website, lopata.co.uk/justask. Andy, thank you so much for being on the BNI Podcast again. You’re, truly an expert in the field.

Andy:
Thank you, Ivan. Thank you for inviting me back.

Ivan: 
Over to you Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, thank you so much for some great information.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

excerpt.rendered: Do you need support? Just ask your network. Andy Lopata shares a strategic approach to asking for and offering help.

title.rendered: Episode 691: Giving Is Transformational

content.rendered:
Synopsis

This week Dr. Misner focuses on the idea that giving is transformational. This topic comes from the book Infinite Giving, which Dr. Misner wrote with Greg Davies and Julian Lewis.

Here are some of the way that giving transforms us, according to studies:

  • When people give to others, it activates regions of the brain associated with pleasure, social connection and trust, creating a “warm glow” effect.
  • People who volunteered to multiple organizations were 44% less likely to die over a five-year period than non-volunteers.
  • The more extensive the reciprocal altruism born of a social connection, the greater the advance towards health, wealth, and happiness.

Consciously giving has a profound ripple effect, as BNI members around the world demonstrated in 2020. The beginning of the year is a great time to be a role model for the people around you. Start the year by really being there to support and help your fellow BNI members and others. You can start small, but start today.

Complete Transcript of Episode 691

Priscilla:

Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice, and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you? And where are you?

Ivan:
Oh, Priscilla, I have been to so many countries, online, over the last few weeks, I can’t even keep count anymore. I mean, one of the advantages of doing so many meetings online is that you can be in one part of the world in the morning and a completely different part of the world in the afternoon. So, that part has been really fantastic. So visiting a lot of regions around the world.

Priscilla:

That’s really amazing. I think that’s great. So tell us what you’re offering us today. What do you have?

Ivan:

Yeah, so my topic today is Giving is Transformational. The material, the concept, is from a book that I’ve talked about here on BNI podcast, and I’ve had my co-authors on, the book is Infinite Giving. And I co-wrote it with Greg Davies and Julian Lewis, it’s a great little book, and you’ll see it on the side here of BNI podcast. If you have a chance, pick up a copy. It’s a quick read. And so, I want to talk about the transformational aspect of giving. You know, the world can be a pretty dark and contentious place. We’ve got illness, famine, poverty, hostility, there’s desperation all around us. But it doesn’t have to be that way for us. The world can be a better place, and we can make a choice, a choice to be a voice of change. And that change can transform the world we live in. Business people, I believe, are well positioned to help make that change.

Years ago, I implemented the phrase into the fabric of the core values of the company, that simple phrase is Givers Gain, and it was really a game changer in giving people a different framework relating to the way they network with others. You know, Givers Gain is so integrated into the DNA of BNI, we, I think, sometimes forget that this was a completely different strategy in 1985. I mean, to most people networking was about hunting, it was about, you know, finding someone and closing deals. And the idea of Givers Gain was truly a transformational idea.

And I love the way that it has been so incredibly embraced throughout the organization. I think that giving is an idea that proclaims that we can be something bigger than ourselves. It’s a reaffirmation that our lives have significant meaning and that through community we can be our best selves. For people who get that, this philosophy, I think, unlocks a whole new world of meaning and opportunity. So our job in BNI, I believe, is to help people get it. More importantly, we can be intentional about helping people get it to reach that place.

In 2010, Greater Good magazine wrote that the National Institutes of Health found that when people give to others, it activates regions of the brain associated with pleasure, social connection and trust, creating a warm glow effect, they called it. Scientists also believe that altruistic behavior releases endorphins in the brain producing the positive feeling known as the helpers high. Researchers at the University of California Berkeley, your neck of the woods, Priscilla, found that people who volunteered to multiple organizations were 44% less likely to die over a five-year period than non-volunteers. That blew me away. The author of Loneliness: Human Nature and the Need for Social Connection, says that the more extensive the reciprocal altruism born of a social connection, the greater the advance towards health, wealth, and happiness. And I think that’s really the foundation of what BNI is, and certainly should be, around the world. So there is, I think, a profound ripple effect that takes place through the act of consciously practicing a giving philosophy. And I’ve seen the dramatic impact that one person can have on another, never any more than last year. The 2020 year, I was just so proud of BNI and BNI members, and what they did for each other.

I’ve observed the surging ripple that takes place as an act impacts one person, and then the next person and the next person and so on. Within a community or a network, that ripple effect that takes place from this philosophy can influence hundreds, and it can influence thousands of people. In fact, I really believe we can influence the world. That’s why our vision statement is Changing the Way the World Does Business. I think I’ve said this in a previous podcast, but it’s worth repeating: Giving is contagious. Conscious giving can be infinite. There’s a Chinese saying, ”If you want happiness for an hour, take a nap. If you want happiness for a day, go fishing. If you want happiness for a year, inherit a fortune. If you want to happiness for a lifetime, help somebody.”

For centuries, I think, the greatest thinkers have suggested the same thing. Happiness is found in giving and helping others. The results from this philosophy cannot be observed as a straight line in life. It’s a complex zigzag of interconnected relationships that lead to a place of contentment and fulfillment, and it begins with the right mindset. It’s about taking off your bib and putting on an apron. It’s not about what’s in it for you, or how much you can get. It’s about serving and helping others in a very conscious way. Not doing so in a way that ignores the fact that there are takers in the world. But in a way that consciously chooses to find your fellow givers in the world. It’s easy to be cynical, and to think that the help you can offer is negligible. But it only takes a small gesture to, sometimes, change people’s lives.

I’ve talked about this in podcasts before and I think it’s so important. We’ve all had people who are in our story. They’re people that we talk about who have changed our life in some way. However, I think something’s even more important. To me, the real question is not who’s in your story, but whose story are you in? Whose life have we made a difference in? This is what creates a meaningful life. We are in the beginning of the year; it’s a great time to be a role model for the people around you. To start the year really being there to support and help your fellow BNI members and others.

We often, I think, look at the world and hope that someday things will be different. But I have a good friend, as matter of fact, I just had dinner with her a few nights ago, Sam Horn, and Sam says something that I really love. She even has a book by this name: Someday Is Not a Day of the Week. I love that expression. Someday is not a day of the week. If we wait for someday, we’re waiting for others to make the first move. I believe someday can be today. And it can start with us. Today is as good a day as any to be our Someday. We can all make a difference in people’s lives starting today. And that difference starts with a transformational change within us, and how we deal with people around us, and the world beyond them.

We would be giving without a transactional expectation, giving to people in all parts of our life, appropriately. We would understand that we give what we can afford and that it’s okay to gain. Give what you can afford, give from your saucer, not your cup; it’s a phrase that we use in Infinite Giving. And that it’s absolutely okay to gain in such an environment. I think it’s important that we remain humble, where saying thank you is the norm. And I think that we can start small. But we can start today. Begin local but think global. If we influence one life, you begin to influence the world. So just start with that person next to you. And know that you may not make a world of difference, but you can make a difference in the world. And you do that one person at a time. Priscilla, I thought this was an important message to start off the new year after having such a challenging year last year. It’s a great message to begin this year with.

Priscilla:
I totally agree, Ivan. I think it’s beautiful. I don’t know how to state this exactly. But giving is something that gives me huge pleasure. So I love this podcast.

Ivan:
Yeah, thank you. And I would urge people to take a look at the book, Infinite Giving. It’s a quick read, you can read it in two hours. We talk about so many things in there, about what the 7 Principles are for Givers Gain, and that, you know, being an infinite giver doesn’t mean you’re an infinite victim. There are lines, you have to have discernment. We talk about that. But it has to start from a position of being willing to give and to help and support other people. And if you start with that in mind, and have discernment as you go through life and through your business, I think you’ll find, you know, that at the end of your career you’ll look back and really feel like you’ve made a difference in people’s lives. And more importantly, they’ll look at you and feel like you’ve made a difference in their lives. And I think that’s a life well lived.

Priscilla:
Mm-hmm, me too.

Ivan:
So that’s it for today, Priscilla. I hope you enjoyed that message.

Priscilla:
I totally enjoyed that message. Thank you so much. It was wonderful.

Ivan:
Thank you.

Priscilla:

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

title.rendered: Episode 690: Networking Strategies for Introverts

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Matthew Pollard returns to the podcast this week to talk about his new book, The Introvert’s Edge to Networking. Matthew’s first appearance on The Official BNI Podcast was in Episode 554, shortly after his first book came out.

There is a system out there for every single introvert to learn how to do anything that they believe is an extroverted kind of behavior. The Introvert’s Edge to Networking: Work the Room. Leverage Social Media. Develop Powerful Connections outlines networking strategies for introverts.

This strategy relies on planning, preparation, and practice, three areas where introverts excel—and on listening, which introverts generally do better than extroverts. And like the strategy for sales, it relies on the power of story.

Go to www.theintrovertsedge.com/networking to download a free chapter of the book.

Complete Transcript of Episode 690

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you today?

Ivan:
I am doing great Priscilla, thanks for asking. And I have a good friend on today. Matthew Pollard, his second time on the BNI Podcast. For those of you who find this topic interesting, go to podcast number 554. That was Matthew’s first visit to the BNI Podcast and a lot of great background information, particularly about his best-selling book, his first book, The Introvert’s Edge.

Matthew has been called the “real deal” by Forbes. I love that Matthew. And as I said, he has a best-selling book, The Introvert’s Edge. And he has a soon to be released second book, The Introvert’s Edge to Networking, something that I am very interested in. He has received endorsements on his book from Harvard, Princeton, Neil Patel, Michael Gerber, Marshall Goldsmith, and some guy named Ivan Misner. So I was happy to do this endorsement on Matthew’s book. As I said, this is his second time on BNI Podcast, go to Episode 554. Matthew, welcome back.

Matthew:
Man, I’m ecstatic to be here. I can’t believe it’s been almost 150 episodes since our last interview.

Ivan:
(chuckling) It’s been a fair amount. Yeah, well over 100 for sure.

Matthew:
Well, that’s impressive. You put so much value out for your audience. I think the podcasting these days is such a great medium for people to really get the information that they need.

Ivan:
Yeah, and we do it in bite-sized pieces. You know, we have Education Coordinators in all the chapters, and they’re very, very active on the Podcast. And they do five-minute presentations, up to five minutes, at a chapter meeting. And so these 12-minute podcasts are perfect for them to take content and do it at a BNI meeting. So you’re going to be talked about in thousands of BNI groups all around the world, Matthew.

Matthew:
(chuckling) Well, you know that we discussed my personal story in the last podcast with my mother being a BNI fan and getting so many clients from BNI activities, and the dance cards most specifically. So you know, I’m ecstatic to be something that is used as a resource for everyone in BNI groups across the world.

Ivan:
Absolutely. And you know, you talk a lot about being an introvert and networking, and I think that’s important for a lot of people. Because oftentimes people think that, you know, an introvert isn’t going to be as good at networking. And we both know that that is absolutely not the case. So let me jump in to your new book. Your first book was a runaway success. Can you tell us what motivated you to write that first book and why it was so successful?

Matthew:
Yeah, I think it was a few things. I think that I was the first sales leader that just got up and said, “I’m a proud introvert and introverts can sell.” I mean, it’s not new information. I mean, it’s new information to a lot of people that people like Zig Ziglar, people like Jeb Blount, were introverted. The truth is people felt like there was a stigma there, that they just couldn’t talk about their introversion publicly. And I think this book really allowed us to confront that stigma head on and I was so excited to see so many introverted sales leaders come out and say, “I’m introverted as well.” But I think it was the topic and the dialogue we created around it. I mean, you know, Ivan, you’ve featured on my Introvert’s Edge Podcast, which we built around the book launch. And you know, just bringing that topic where we sort of highlighted introverts aren’t second class citizens, we can network, we can sell, we can speak publicly.

Ivan:
Absolutely. And we talked about this on your show, that I took a test and found out I was also an introvert who was a situational extrovert. And that was news to me.

Matthew:
I think that introverts, specifically, we project extraversion onto anyone that we see as successful. I know I do it myself from time to time, and then you know, you’re always surprised. I mean, I remember I was doing some research on you after you endorsed my first book. And I just typed your name in and the word ‘introvert’ because I’d just given up guessing, and I remember seeing that blog post, oh my god, I can’t believe I’m an introvert. And you know, I remember reading through it. Now it makes so much sense and, you know, it’s such a great parallel with my second book.

Because, you know in BNI, you talk about process and systems, and for introverts that’s great. Because if you know systems, you know what’s coming. If you can practice your little infomercial beforehand, BNI is an amazing way to get in front of your ideal clients. And I just wanted to take that a step further and say, everything that I do in the success that I had in sales in the first book, and the success that I’ve had in networking, which I talk about in the second book, is all a systematic, methodical process that I can practice beforehand. So I don’t get stuck in my head worrying about what I’m going to say. For me, it feels almost like Groundhog Day, while to the person I’m speaking to it feels like an organic conversation.

Ivan:
Yeah. So you’ve got a second book. And it sounds like you’re going to make this into a series, The Introvert’s Edge. I love that concept, that’s really good. Why do you think that, and this one’s on networking, why do you think so many introverts struggle with, or even hate, networking?

Matthew:
I think it’s because of the way people see it. I mean, we all see those transactional networkers walk around the room. Do you want to buy something from me? No. What about you? What about you? and we don’t want to be that person. So we end up then trying to be what I call the aimless networker, which is the person that kind of sits to the side or gets involved in conversation, but then ends up having all of these very un-detailed conversations, because they try to almost avoid talking about what it is that they do, because they don’t want to come across as that transactional sales person. But what happens is, that means they end up coming home with all these business cards with people that they’ve had relatively shallow conversations with. And they just go, ‘you know what, if they reach out to me, then I’ll work with them’. Of course, they never do. So we always have this view, that networking just doesn’t work.

The truth is it does work. They’re just doing it wrong. So in the book, I talk about what I call strategic networking, and that is going in with a plan, going to the right rooms to make sure that your niche of clients is there, knowing that just clientele is not the only thing you are going there for. I mean, you know, if I met you in a networking event and I tried to sell you something, that would have been a horrible way to leverage the relationship that you and I now have. So there are different types of relationships you can make in the networking room. Being prepared for all of that, all comes down to some things that introverts are great at, which is planning, preparation and practice. And then once we have that, we can leverage our natural skills, which I mean, we’re great listeners, we’re very empathetic, we’re all about the other person. These are qualities that are so in demand in a networking environment.

Ivan:
Yeah. Right. And extroverts, you know, they tend not to listen real well, they tend not to, and I’m not talking about everybody, but they tend not to listen very well. They love to talk, and what’s their favorite subject?

Matthew:
Themselves. You know, it’s interesting, though. I think there’s a big difference, though, if we think about organizations. If somebody in the HR department saw somebody that wasn’t listening very well, they’d send them to an active listening class. If they weren’t empathetic, they might send them to empathy training. If it was an extrovert and they were running their own business, they might go and do training like that for themselves, because they believe it to be a learnable skill. I think the big divide is that introverts believe that because of this thing called the gift of gab, learning how to sell, how to network, how to speak from stage, is just not possible for them. And that’s the big thing that we need to start to work with, that introverts need to realize that their path to success is just different to that of an extrovert. And when they embrace that, they can actually not only do well, they can actually dominate in every one of the so called extroverted arenas.

Ivan:
Yeah, no question about it. Any other suggestions for introverts, that they can do things differently? Other things they can do differently?

Matthew:
Yeah, I think that the number one thing that an introvert does when they’re in a networking room is they tend to get into a conversation, and then they get into the jargon and all the technicalities of what they do. And they just overwhelm the person that they’re speaking to. It’s like, they love what they do and they’re trying to download 30 years of information into the person they’re speaking to’s head in two minutes, while they’re politely listening, hoping probably to get to the restroom or to the bar to get away. The difference is, what I suggest, is that firstly the introvert realizes that generally if they’re selling to somebody, those people, they need to define them as a niche and then say, Okay, what are the three major problems that that person would have? And then look for one story for each one of those problems. So when they’re in a networking event, and they’re having a dialogue about what they do, and that conversation comes up, then what they should be doing is saying, “Actually, you know what, you remind me of…”, or “Oh, that reminds me of…”, and then going into a story about someone that they worked with that had a problem, and that they then got to a desired response. And the reason why that’s so important is that stories firstly, it short circuits the logical mind. People love listening to stories, but people also find stories much more tangible.

Ivan:
Yes, absolutely. So what do you think is one of the biggest mistakes that people make, introverts make, in networking?

Matthew: You know, I think it’s the time when somebody actually says to them, you know, that’s really interesting. You know, tell me a little bit more about that, I’d like to buy from you, or what does working with you look like? And then they get into, oh my gosh, finally, I’ve been waiting for this, I’ve gone to 10 networking events, this is the first person that asked me! So they then start talking about their product or service. And networking is never about sales. When something like that comes up, what’s going to happen, and I do this in roleplays with introverts all the time, is I’ll ask them that question after they explain what I call the unified message, which I explained in Episode 554, and then how to explain their passion and mission in this story, I’ll then say, “Oh, that’s great. Tell me, how would I work with you?” And then they will go into explaining how what working with them looks like. And I say in the middle of their conversation, “Oh, Bob, I’m so glad that you popped in, it’s great to see you.” And of course, we’ve been interrupted in a networking event, right? And I simulate that for them. And I help them realize that even when somebody’s really interested in working with them, you have to make sure that you appreciate them saying that, but saying that there’s more that you need to know to make sure that you’re the right fit. Plus, now’s not really the time for that. I’m sure that both they and you came to meet people and to network, and then to set up a time to have a dialogue with them later. I think the power of that is that they can also, if they’re with a partner, have a conversation with them about whether or not they’re willing to pay for you. Also, whether or not they got different things out of the conversation. You know, I have worked with one guy that literally because I said that, he said to his partner afterwards, ‘if he’s under this price we’re definitely going to work with him.’ We met up about three days later. And as soon as I explained the pricing and what it looked like, they were like, yep, let’s move forward. But if I hadn’t jumped on it like a cobra at a networking event, I know I would not have got that deal.

Ivan:
Right. Great advice. Any last thoughts before we wrap up?

Matthew:
You know, I think the most important piece of advice that I can give everybody that’s listening, is that there is a system out there for every single introvert to learn how to do anything that they believe is an extroverted kind of behavior. And more than that, I think you’ll be blown away to discover that introverts actually dominate in most of those things. I mean, you think about people like Bill Murray, and Leonardo DiCaprio. Even in that forum with somebody supposed to be on stage or creatively in comedy, those people also, both of them are introverts. So one of the things I want everyone to know is there is a system out there for you, to help you become successful as an introvert. My publisher hates me when I say this, but you don’t need to buy my book, The Introvert’s Edge to Networking, to do that. You know, you can go to www.theintrovertsedge.com/networking and there I’ll help you get over the fact that you think you can’t network as an introvert in the first chapter, which I give away for free there. And I’ll also map out what I call the Introvert’s Edge Networking System to really help you understand why everything you’ve been doing up till now feels so inauthentic and incongruent, and how you can turn all that around in a really simple way.

Ivan:
Well, I’ll help promote your book, Matthew, if you don’t. I urge members to pick up a copy of The Introvert’s Edge to Networking. You can get it at the website that Matthew just gave, www.theintrovertsedge.com/networking.  A lot of great content there. A fantastic book, and I was happy to endorse it. And thanks for coming back to BNI Podcast, Matthew. It’s great having you.

Matthew:
My pleasure. I’m always happy to be on this show.

Ivan:
Over to you Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. That was some great information, thank you, Matthew and Ivan.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.
